title: SEFANet: Spectrum-Enhanced Feature Aggregation Network for Salient Object Detection in Hyperspectral Images
authors: Kaihong Chen, Zhen Wang, Xuqi Wang, Shan-Wen Zhang
abstract: Salient object detection in hyperspectral images (HSIs) is critical for applications in remote sensing, national defence, and environmental monitoring. However, the high dimensionality of HSIs, background interference, and scale variations pose significant challenges for traditional methods in effectively extracting spectral features and fusing spatial information. To address these issues, we propose a novel Spectrum-Enhanced Feature Aggregation Network (SEFANet) for hyperspectral salient object detection (HSOD). SEFANet adopts a dual-channel, dual-encoder architecture: the main channel leverages a High-Resolution Attention (HRA) module to model spatial and channel dependencies, while the auxiliary channel integrates a Feature Aggregation and Selection (FAS) module to progressively aggregate spectral features. Additionally, the Spectral Sensing Enhancement (SSE) module deeply fuses spatial and spectral information, enhancing robustness against background interference and scale variations. Extensive experiments on the HSOD-BIT dataset demonstrate that SEFANet achieves state-of-the-art performance, with an accuracy of 96.6%, mean intersection over union (mIoU) of 85.3%, mean Dice coefficient (mDice) of 88.1%, and a mean absolute error (MAE) of just 0.039. Compared to existing methods, SEFANet achieves significant improvements in both detection precision and robustness. The source code and dataset will be available on https://github.com/darkseid-arch/SEFANet.

title: FEMS-DETR: A Frequency-Enhanced Transformer for Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images
authors: Xuanyu Ling, Zhijie Lin, Zhaoshui He, Yangru Yu, Jiakun Gong, Huankun Chen
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: The detection of objects in remote sensing imagery faces numerous challenges, primarily due to cluttered backgrounds, significant scale variations, and densely distributed small objects. To address these issues, this paper presents an object detection framework named FEMS-DETR, specifically designed for remote sensing imagery. The core of the framework consists of two main modules: a Frequency-enhanced Multi-scale Perception Module (FMPM), which improves feature extraction for multi-scale and small objects through parallel multi-scale convolutional processing and frequency-domain transformations, and a Detail-Preserving Downsampling (DPD) module, which employs a three-branch architecture integrated with an attention mechanism to retain critical features during downsampling. Experimental results on the NWPU VHR-10 and RSOD datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive results compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches, with mAP scores of 95.00% and 96.22%, respectively. It performs particularly well in detecting small objects and maintains robust performance in complex scenarios.

title: Global Modeling Matters: A Fast, Lightweight and Effective Baseline for Efficient Image Restoration
authors: Xingyu Jiang, Ning Gao, Hongkun Dou, Xiuhui Zhang, Xiaoqing Zhong, Yue Deng, Hongjue Li
abstract: Natural image quality is often degraded by adverse weather conditions, significantly impairing the performance of downstream tasks. Image restoration has emerged as a core solution to this challenge and has been widely discussed in the literature. Although recent transformer-based approaches have made remarkable progress in image restoration, their increasing system complexity poses significant challenges for real-time processing, particularly in real-world deployment scenarios. To this end, most existing methods attempt to simplify the self-attention mechanism, such as by channel self-attention or state space model. However, these methods primarily focus on network architecture while neglecting the inherent characteristics of image restoration itself. In this context, we explore a pyramid Wavelet-Fourier iterative pipeline to demonstrate the potential of Wavelet-Fourier processing for image restoration. Inspired by the above findings, we propose a novel and efficient restoration baseline, named Pyramid Wavelet-Fourier Network (PW-FNet). Specifically, PW-FNet features two key design principles: 1) at the inter-block level, integrates a pyramid wavelet-based multi-input multi-output structure to achieve multi-scale and multi-frequency bands decomposition; and 2) at the intra-block level, incorporates Fourier transforms as an efficient alternative to self-attention mechanisms, effectively reducing computational complexity while preserving global modeling capability. Extensive experiments on tasks such as image deraining, raindrop removal, image super-resolution, motion deblurring, image dehazing, image desnowing and underwater/low-light enhancement demonstrate that PW-FNet not only surpasses state-of-the-art methods in restoration quality but also achieves superior efficiency, with significantly reduced parameter size, computational cost and inference time. The code is available at: https://github.com/deng-ai-lab/PW-FNet.

title: LearnMat: Semantic-Aware Self-Supervision Fine-Grained Visual Recognition
authors: Shuaiheng Li, Qing Cai, Fan Zhang, Yangyang Shu, Guanbin Li, Junyu Dong, Lingqiao Liu, David Zhang
abstract: Self-supervised learning has shown potential in fine-grained visual recognition (FGVR). However, existing self-supervised learning methods are often susceptible to irrelevant patterns during training and lack the ability to capture the critical subtle differences in FGVR, leading to suboptimal performance. Moreover, existing approaches focus primarily on unimodal visual concepts. Despite the emergence of powerful vision-language models (VLMs) in various high-level vision tasks, their potential in self-supervised FGVR remains largely unexplored. To this end, we propose a novel self-supervised learning (LearnMat) framework, that effectively filters out irrelevant feature interference and extracts more important and subtle discriminative features during training. Specifically, LearnMat consists of two key modules: the semantic awareness module (SAM) and the insight extraction module (IEM). In the SAM, we introduce a novel vision–language–grounded semantic distillation strategy using a corpus of generic, category-agnostic textual attributes, that injects explicit semantic constraints into self-supervised training and improves robustness to background interference. Complementarily, the IEM exploits gradient-based signals from the input image to highlight subtle differences and localize key discriminative regions, mitigating inter-class similarity and intra-class variation, and enhancing fine-grained discrimination. Extensive experiments across multiple popular FGVR datasets show that LearnMat significantly outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its marked effectiveness.

title: Revisiting the Adversarial Transferability: Towards a Perspective of Semantic Preservation
authors: Zhuangzhuang Wang, Chengze Jiang, Jie Gui, Xinli Shi, Yuan Yan Tang
abstract: Transfer-based attacks pose a significant threat to deep neural networks, as they can attack without accessing the target model. Recently, input transformation-based attacks have emerged as a research hot-spot in transfer-based attacks. This is attributed to their efficiency in enhancing the transferability of adversarial examples. In this work, we revisit and reveal that recent input transformation-based attacks mainly introduce a large variation, to the extent that they inadequately preserve the semantic information. Such semantic inconsistency results in a misalignment between the gradient information for generating perturbations and the true decision boundaries of target models, intensifying overfitting to the surrogate model due to inaccurate gradients, which limits the transferability. To tackle this problem, we propose a novel input transformation-based attack method called Selfmix, which employs a localized mixing paradigm to mix various regions of the input image itself. Our Selfmix enhances the diversity of transformed images while effectively preserving semantic consistency, thereby improving the transferability. Moreover, Selfmix demonstrates effective compatibility with other input transformation-based attacks due to enhanced semantic preservation. Comprehensive experiments on the ImageNet dataset verify the competitiveness of Selfmix in enhancing transferability on both normally trained models and defense models, and the generality to various deep network architectures, especially when attacking transformer-based models. The code is publicly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/bookman233/SelfMix</uri>.

title: Tiga: Autonomous Hybrid Active-Passive Communication for Ambient IoT System
authors: Jiazhen Lei, Bingbing Wang, Fengyuan Zhu, Tianze Cao, Wenhui Li, Linling Zhong, Xiaohua Tian
abstract: Power consumption continues to pose a fundamental challenge for large-scale IoT tag deployment. While conventional active communication strategies struggle to mitigate peak power demands due to energy-intensive components, passive alternatives achieve microwatt-level consumption but suffer from limited operational range. This paper introduces Tiga, an innovative hybrid active-passive communication system for Ambient IoT that autonomously selects optimal transmission modes. Recognizing the ambiguity inherent in the distance-based mode switching boundary, we instead adopt energy level as our boundary criterion. Tiga employs passive communication when channel energy suffices and seamlessly transitions to active transmission when required. Our design features a novel microwatt-scale dual-function detector that performs concurrent downlink signal demodulation and RSSI computation. We further propose an adaptive four-phase blind boundary search algorithm capable of efficient execution on any low-power controller. We prototype Tiga on a four-layer PCB and evaluate its performance across various environments. Experimental results demonstrate that compared to duty-cycling active communication, Tiga reduces dynamic power consumption by 1.85× to 9.42× across various distances from the gateway.

title: VR-Based Teleoperation Framework: Integration of Haptic Feedback and Singularity Management
authors: Seungnam Yu, Geegum Lee, Jeongmok Kim
abstract: This study presents a VR-based teleoperation framework enhancing collaborative robot stability and manipulability via hand-tracking, adaptive control, and dual-modality haptic feedback. It addresses critical synchronization challenges (singularity avoidance, tracking responsiveness, and workspace constraints), which are especially problematic in first-person VR where kinematic limits are not directly perceivable. The framework employs Adaptive Damped Least Squares (A-DLS) to maintain manipulability near singular configurations, workspace impedance control to enforce boundary constraints, and vibrotactile feedback delivered through a haptic glove to convey both workspace limits (fingertip vibration) and path deviation information (wrist vibration) to operators. Key features include real-time hand-tracking, workspace calibration, and adaptive controls to ensure seamless coordination between virtual and real robot workspaces. Experimental validation through two complementary studies demonstrates the system’s effectiveness. Experiment 1 evaluated singularity management and workspace stability, showing that the A-DLS algorithm maintained manipulability above critical thresholds for 92% of operational time versus 78% without adaptive damping. Experiment 2 assessed trajectory tracking accuracy through a path-following task with 10 participants. Results demonstrate that haptic-enabled control achieves a 24.7% reduction in mean path-following error (from 10.03 mm to 7.55 mm, p = 0.001) compared to haptic-disabled conditions, indicating improvements in both accuracy and consistency. Although haptic guidance modestly increases task time due to higher precision focus, the resulting gains in accuracy and stability make this framework ideal for precision-critical tasks. By ensuring stability near workspace boundaries, the system effectively facilitates VR-based teleoperation for applications like painting, polishing, and contour-following.

title: On the Efficiency of a Misspecified Contaminated Nonlinear Regression Model: Application to Time-Delay and Doppler Estimation
authors: Hamish McPhee, Lorenzo Ortega, Stefano Fortunati, Jean-Yves Tourneret
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Nonlinear regression models play a crucial role in signal processing and multi-sensor applications. Traditionally, performance bounds for these models assume independent Gaussian observations. In practice, the Gaussian assumption fails in multi-sensor systems if some proportion of sensors are corrupted by non-Gaussian noise and outliers. In this context, we extend the Misspecified CramérRao Bound (MCRB) framework to the contaminated Gaussian noise model, where observations are generated from a mixture of nominal Gaussian noise and occasional outliers. Building on previous work with Complex Elliptically Symmetric noise models, we derive analytical MCRB expressions under the mismatched Gaussian assumption and study the asymptotic behavior of the corresponding Misspecified Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MMLE). To demonstrate practical relevance, we apply the theory to joint time-delay and Doppler estimation in GPS signals under contamination. Numerical simulations confirm that the MMLE root mean squared error converges to the theoretical MCRB, which aligns with the classical Gaussian CRB.

title: Control of artificial ecosystems: A model for environment-entity regulation
authors: Mohamed ElZayyat, Denis Mušić
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Recent efforts in artificial life research (Alife) have leveraged cellular automata (CA) to probe emergence across different organizational scales, yet most models lack adherence to thermodynamic laws and thus artificial entities do not represent real life processes accurately. Extending our earlier analysis of CA for Alife and critiques of thermodynamic omissions [1] [2], we present a closed loop control model that jointly governs environmental fields and entities under explicit energetic budgets. The environment comprises reaction-diffusion processes and resource reservoirs while entities are endowed with metabolically constrained policies and internal stores.

title: Real-Time Behavioral and Physiological Monitoring via mmWave Radar
authors: Shih-Pang Tseng, Wun-Yang Wu, Jhing-Fa Wang
abstract: This study proposes a non-contact driver monitoring system based on millimeter-wave FMCW radar. It integrates temporal convolution, spatial attention, and a cross-gated fusion mechanism in a multi-modal deep learning model called RaTSNet to recognize driver behaviors and estimate heart and respiratory rates in real time. The system features an adaptive sensing mechanism that adjusts the monitoring range based on driver position and signal quality, enhancing the precision of physiological signal detection. Deployed on the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano platform, the system achieves over 94% accuracy in detecting high-risk driving behaviors, demonstrating strong practical potential.

title: Green Clean Software Sustainability
authors: Luis Cruz, Patricia Lago, Henry Muccini, Eoin Woods

title: Tradeoffs in Energy Efficiency for Safety Supervisors for Adaptive Cruise Controllers
authors: Jonathan Sprinkle, Daniel Work, Matthew Bunting, George Gunter
publish: 2026 IEEE Forum for Innovative Sustainable Transportation Systems (FISTS)
abstract: This paper compares a collision-avoidance controller with a common factory-configured adaptive cruise control system, with respect to the speed controller’s acceleration signal energy. While many vehicles manufactured today include an option with adaptive cruise control, those controllers are typically designed to enhance the comfort of passengers, and are not specifically designed for energy efficiency. Similarly, controllers designed for purposes of dampening traffic waves typically require supervisory solutions that avoid collision with the car ahead, and are not designed to be energy efficient. The approach of this paper is to compare the desired acceleration signal for the factory controller, and the supervisory controller, to assess whether they significantly differ in the characteristics of their signals. The foundations for the two controllers are outlined, and an open-road test is performed to validate the approach. The results demonstrate that the safety supervisory controller is not necessarily going to be more energy efficient, especially if the nominal controller is aggressive in its driving.

title: A Comparative Finite Element Analysis of Lightweight Sandwich Structures for Packaging Applications
authors: Sikandar Khan, Abdul Wahab, Aamer Nazir
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: This study deals with the numerical evaluation of sandwich structures including various core geometries like, Corrugated, Hexagonal, Diamond, and Octagonal using finite element analysis (FEA) in ANSYS software. The main objective of the current study is to determine how core geometry impacts the mechanical behavior of sandwich structures under compressive loading. Three-dimensional models of each core type were developed in ANSYS SpaceClaim, meshed with appropriate refinement, and simulated under controlled boundary conditions to ensure consistency. Corrugated cardboard was employed as the material, modeled with orthotropic elastic and plastic properties derived from experimental data. Important parameters for the simulation included deformation, stress distribution, and reaction forces. This methodology allowed a systematic comparison of different core shapes while maintaining consistent material properties and loading scenarios. This study helps to the understanding of how geometric design impacts structural performance and offers a foundation for optimizing sandwich structure in lightweight and packaging applications. Results indicated that the diamond design obtained the best balance of stiffness, low deformation, and stress distribution, while the octagonal structure exhibited strong load resistance. whereas, hexagonal and corrugated structures exhibited higher deformation and stress concentrations, indicating lower strength.

title: Artificial Intelligence In Cognitive Impairment:Research Landscape and Emerging Fronts in Early Detection and Diagnosis
authors: Shiying Zhou, Shihua Cao, Xinyu Zhang, Yuyang Jia, Xule Zhu
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Objective: This study aims to reveal the global progress and trends of artificial intelligence technology in the early screening and auxiliary diagnosis of cognitive impairment through a comprehensive bibliometric analysis, understand its research focus, and predict its future development trends. Method: Relevant lit eratures in the field of “Early Screening and Auxiliary Diagnosis of Cognitive Impairment by Artificial Intelligence” were retriev ed from the core database of Web of Science, and software such as Citespace, VOSviewer, and RStudio were used to conduct visu al analysis on the number of publications, research institutions, and countries. Result: A total of 663 literatures were included in this study. The research results show that the number of articles published in this field has shown a significant upward trend, and 2018 has been identified as a key turning point. At present, the high-frequency keywords in this field mainly focus on “Alzheimer’ s disease”, “deep learning” and “machine learning”, etc. The research hotspots are shifting towards multimodal data fusion, digital biomarker mining and lightweight algorithm development. Conclusion: Globally, the application scope of artificial intelligence technology in the early screening and diagnosis of cognitive impairment is expanding increasingly, attracting more and more coun tries to participate in research in this field.Future research should be dedicated to addressing these challenges and promoting the better transformation of artificial intelligence technology into clinical applications.

title: Ensembled Deep Learning (DL) Methods for Detecting Skin Cancer Using CNN Algorithm with Multi Model
authors: Fatima Taj, Ranya Muqaddas, Adnan Yousaf, Muhammad Fawad Nasim, Maham Naeem, Eisha Nawaz
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Skin cancer is a common and possibly fatal condition. Effective treatment results are greatly influenced by early identification. Deep learning (DP) algorithms have demonstrated encouraging outcomes in skin cancer detection computer-aided diagnostic systems. This article investigates the many forms of skin cancer, such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma (BCC), and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and offers a system for detecting skin cancer utilizing convolutional neural network (CNN) approaches, particularly the multi-model ResNet (M-ResNet) architecture. We present a ResNet architecture that is capable of handling deep networks and has increased skin cancer detection performance. The proposed approach uses a thorough pipeline to find skin cancer. The dataset first goes through pre-processing (PP) procedures, such as picture resizing, normalization, and augmentation approaches, to improve the model's capacity for generalization. The multi-model assembles, leading to improved accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity in skin cancer LEARNING CLASSIFICATION SYSTEM (SC-LCS) tasks. In this study FINAL highlight the effectiveness of deep learning (DL) techniques, specifically the multi-model ResNet architecture, AND skin cancer LEARNING classification SYSTEM (SC-LCS) for skin cancer detection. The proposed system shows promising results in accurately identifying different types of skin cancer, aiding in early diagnosis and treatment. Further research and development in this field can potentially contribute to improving healthcare systems and reducing the global burden of skin cancer-related effects and death rate. Keywords— Deep Learning, Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Skin Cancer Detection, Multi-Model ResNet, Learning Classification System, Early Detection melanoma, Ensemble Learning, Performance Evaluation Analysis

title: Graph Your Way to Inspiration: Integrating Co-Author Graphs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Model Based Scientific Idea Generation
authors: Pengzhen Xie, Huizhi Liang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate potential in the field of scientific idea generation. However, the generated results often lack controllable academic context and traceable inspiration pathways. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes a scientific idea generation system called GYWI, which combines author knowledge graphs with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to form an external knowledge base to provide controllable context and trace of inspiration path for LLMs to generate new scientific ideas. We first propose an author-centered knowledge graph construction method and inspiration source sampling algorithms to construct external knowledge base. Then, we propose a hybrid retrieval mechanism that is composed of both RAG and GraphRAG to retrieve content with both depth and breadth knowledge. It forms a hybrid context. Thirdly, we propose a Prompt optimization strategy incorporating reinforcement learning principles to automatically guide LLMs optimizing the results based on the hybrid context. To evaluate the proposed approaches, we constructed an evaluation dataset based on arXiv (2018-2023). This paper also develops a comprehensive evaluation method including empirical automatic assessment in multiplechoice question task, LLM-based scoring, human evaluation, and semantic space visualization analysis. The generated ideas are evaluated from the following five dimensions: novelty, feasibility, clarity, relevance, and significance. We conducted experiments on different LLMs including GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-8B, and Gemini 2.5. Experimental results show that GYWI significantly outperforms mainstream LLMs in multiple metrics such as novelty, reliability, and relevance.

title: Enhancing STEM Education Through an Augmented Reality-Integrated Hybrid Learning Model and Learning Management Systems
authors: Aeron Vince Fajardo, Geewel P. Dariagan, Channen Kyle S. Cruz, John Luiz G. Rebancos, Joaquin Rashid Jaranilla, Jaypy T. Tenerife
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: This study explores the adoption of a hybrid learning model that integrates Augmented Reality (AR) with a Learning Management System (LMS) to enhance STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) education in Philippine schools. Aimed at addressing long-standing challenges such as limited access to quality instructional materials, insufficient teacher training, and low student engagement, the paper introduces an immersive AR-enhanced learning kit embedded within an LMS platform. The hybrid approach seeks to deliver interactive, personalized, and scalable e-learning experiences that align with the 21st-century educational needs. Using thematic analysis of qualitative data from 263 STEM educators in the Greater Manila Area, the study identifies strong institutional support for AR-LMS adoption alongside key barriers, including infrastructural limitations and cost concerns. Findings highlight that integrating AR into LMS-supported instruction significantly improves conceptual understanding, learner motivation, and instructional engagement in STEM-related subjects. The research underscores the potential of AR-enabled e-learning tools to transform traditional teaching methodologies, promote active learning, and support digital transformation in education. The study offers a scalable and inclusive framework for enhancing the STEM competencies of learners in the country, and it perfectly aligns with global educational innovation goals.

title: An Idle-Stroke Underactuated Gripper with Independent Double-Parallelogram Linkages for Pinching and Adaptive Grasping*
authors: Yizhe Chen, Haotong Luan, Haokai Ding, Wenzeng Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Traditional parallel grippers often require repeated pose adjustments of the robot arm when grasping objects lying on a table, which complicates reliable handling of planar objects with varying sizes. This difficulty mainly arises from the circular fingertip trajectories generated during conventional parallel motion. To overcome this limitation, this paper presents an underactuated idle-stroke gripper that integrates linear parallel and adaptive grasping modes in a single design, called the GPL hand (Grasshopper Link Gripper). A grasshopper-inspired linkage is employed to linearize the fingertip motion, while an independent two-stage parallel four-bar double-parallelogram linkage mechanism constrains the terminal finger segments to maintain a constant orientation during pinching. Furthermore, an idle-stroke transmission with delayed engagement enables a smooth transition from precision pinching to self-adaptive enveloping grasp once contact is established. Both theoretical analysis and experimental validation demonstrate that the proposed gripper achieves robust planar object grasping, wide size adaptability, and reliable performance, making it suitable for industrial grasping applications.

title: Design and Optimization of Multi-Stage WiFi Antenna Arrays at 2.45 GHz: A Comparative Study of Electrical and Radiation Performance
authors: Ahmed Laiche, Hamza Ben Hamadi, Mohamed Salah Karoui, Said Ghnimi, Ali Gharsallah
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: This research demonstrates the development and performance enhancement of an antenna array operating at 2.45 GHz for Wi-Fi applications. The process began with a conventional rectangular patch antenna, which achieved an initial reflection coefficient (S11) of –12.66 dB and a gain of 6.88 dBi. To improve impedance matching and overall performance, two horizontal rectangular openings and two vertical rectangular slots are on either side of the patch. These modifications enhanced the S11 to –24.78 dB and increased the gain to 7.08 dBi. Subsequently, two array configurations were developed: Array 1 achieved a gain of 7.42 dBi, while Array 2 demonstrated a significantly improved gain of 10.20 dBi. This progressive design approach highlights the effectiveness of structural enhancements and array integration in optimizing antenna performance for wireless communication applications.

title: Environment-Aware Channel Measurement and Modeling for Terahertz Monostatic Sensing
authors: Yejian Lyu, Zhiqiang Yuan, Henk Wymeersch, Chong Han
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) at terahertz (THz) frequencies holds significant promise for unifying ultra-high-speed wireless connectivity with fine-grained environmental awareness. Realistic and interpretable channel modeling is essential to fully realize the potential of such systems. This work presents a comprehensive investigation of monostatic sensing channels at 300 GHz, based on an extensive measurement campaign conducted at 57 co-located transceiver (TRx) positions across three representative indoor scenarios. Multipath component (MPC) parameters, including amplitude, delay, and angle, are extracted using a high-resolution space-alternating generalized expectation-maximization (SAGE) algorithm. To cluster the extracted MPCs, an image-processing-based clustering method, i.e., connected component labeling (CCL), is applied to group MPCs based on delay-angle consistency. Based on the measurement data, an environment-aware channel modeling framework is proposed to establish mappings between physical scenario attributes (e.g., reflector geometry, surface materials, and roughness) and their corresponding channel-domain manifestations. The framework incorporates both specular and diffuse reflections and leverages several channel parameters, e.g., reflection loss, Lambertian scattering, and intra-cluster dispersion models, to characterize reflection behavior. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach can reliably extract physical characteristics, e.g., structural and material information, from the observed channel characteristics, offering a promising foundation for advanced THz ISAC channel modeling.

title: A Preconditioned Hessian-Like Proximal Gradient Algorithm for Sparse ML-DOA Estimation
authors: Thomas Aussaguès, Anne Freréol, Alice Delmer, Pascal Larzabal
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Maximum Likelihood (ML) Direction-of-Arrival (DoA) estimation under the Vectorized Covariance Matrix Model (VCMM) offers improved performance but remains computationally intensive. To alleviate this issue, sparse formulations have been adopted and shown to be asymptotically equivalent to the ML using a proper regularization parameter choice. Yet, the resulting sparse criterion remains intricate and is typically minimized using slow first-order methods such as the Proximal Gradient Algorithm (PGA). To improve convergence speed, an Hessian-like preconditioned PGA is introduced. As the associated proximal operator lacks a closed-form expression, an IRL1 scheme, known to converge to a critical point of the $\ell_{0}$-regularized criterion is employed, enabling its numerical evaluation via the Chambolle-Pock (CP) algorithm. This approach yields faster convergence and enhanced performance in low-SNR scenarios by exploiting the local geometry encoded in the Hessian.

title: Can Agentic AI Match the Performance of Human Data Scientists?
authors: An Luo, Jin Du, Fangqiao Tian, Xun Xian, Robert Specht, Ganghua Wang, Xuan Bi, Charles Fleming, Jayanth Srinivasa, Ashish Kundu, Mingyi Hong, Jie Ding
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Data science plays a critical role in transforming complex data into actionable insights across numerous domains. Recent developments in large language models (LLMs) have significantly automated data science workflows, but a fundamental question persists: Can these agentic AI systems truly match the performance of human data scientists who routinely leverage domain-specific knowledge? We explore this question by designing a prediction task where a crucial latent variable is hidden in relevant image data instead of tabular features. As a result, agentic AI that generates generic codes for modeling tabular data cannot perform well, while human experts could identify the important hidden variable using domain knowledge. We demonstrate this idea with a synthetic dataset for property insurance. Our experiments show that agentic AI that relies on generic analytics workflow falls short of methods that use domain-specific insights. This highlights a key limitation of the current agentic AI for data science and underscores the need for future research to develop agentic AI systems that can better recognize and incorporate domain knowledge.

title: CAGNet: HVI-based Dual-Path Underwater Image Enhancement
authors: Huikun Huang, Siyu Lin, Hongxuan Yang, Kaiying Wang, Renzhang Chen, Zixin He, Xinzhong Liu, Zhongchun He
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Underwater Image Enhancement (UIE) is plagued by severe color distortion and contrast loss. Existing methods face a critical dilemma: processing in sRGB space conflates distinct degradation patterns, while color space decoupling often overlooks the persistent interplay between illumination and chrominance in degraded scenes. To resolve this, we introduce the Cross-Attention Guided Network (CAGNet). Our framework employs a dual-path U-Net architecture within the HVI color space, dedicating specialized branches to luminance (I) and chrominance (HV) enhancement. The centerpiece of our design is the Cross-Attention Block (CAB), a module that establishes a dynamic bridge between the two pathways. This mechanism facilitates a synergistic dialogue, allowing illumination features to guide color restoration and, conversely, chrominance cues to refine detail and brightness. By explicitly modeling this crucial interdependency, CAGNet achieves a more holistic and accurate restoration. Extensive evaluations on public benchmarks confirm that our method surpasses state-of-the-art techniques, delivering superior results both quantitatively and qualitatively.

title: Accelerating Machine Learning using RISC-V Vector Extension in a Manycore Platform
authors: Willian Analdo Nunes, Antônio Vinicius Corrêa Dos Santos, César Marcon, Fernando Gehm Moraes
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: This work addresses the acceleration of convolutional neural network (CNN) inference in manycore architectures using coarse and fine-grain parallelism. Prior approaches focus on dedicated accelerators or modified NoCs, limiting flexibility. This work proposes integrating a RISC-V processor extended with the vector extensions (RVV) as general-purpose processing elements in a NoC-based manycore. The implementation applies depthwise convolution mapped across PEs and uses autovectorization provided by the compiler. Experiments on a $4 \times 4$ manycore running the first AlexNet layer achieved up to 5.70x speedup and reduced execution cycles by 82.45% compared to a scalar single-core baseline.

title: LiC: Low-Cost Cache Replacement Algorithm for All Cache Levels
authors: Varun Venkitaraman, Tejeshwar Thorawade, Mitul Tandon, Keerthisagar Kokkiligadda, Virendra Singh, Janak Patel
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: Modern processors use caches to reduce memory access time. However, their limited size leads to frequent misses, requiring an efficient replacement policy. The Least Recently Used (LRU) policy is widely adopted for its effectiveness but becomes impractical in highly associative caches due to its high area and power costs. To address inefficiencies in the last-level cache (LLC), researchers have proposed sophisticated replacement policies. However, their complexity and hardware overhead make them unsuitable for level-one (L1) and leveltwo (L2) caches, which require fast and lightweight decisionmaking. Additionally, low-cost microcontrollers demand simple and efficient replacement mechanisms. This paper introduces the Lightweight Cache Replacement Policy (LiC) as a low-cost, power-efficient alternative to LRU. Unlike conventional policies that focus on eviction decisions, LiC prioritizes protecting the last accessed block. This approach significantly reduces hardware complexity and power consumption while maintaining performance. We evaluate LiC through simulations in both single-core and multi-core environments. Results show that LiC matches LRU’s performance while drastically reducing storage overhead. Compared to sophisticated LLC replacement policies, it reduces storage costs by up to $28 \times$. Against low-cost policies like NRU and PLRU, it achieves $4 \times$ and $3.75 \times$ lower storage demands. Additionally, LiC reduces area overhead by $16 \times$ compared to LRU. With its low hardware overhead and strong performance, LiC emerges as an efficient and scalable solution across all cache levels.

title: Air Quality Prediction with Bi-Directional Gated Recurrent Unit (Bi-GRU)
authors: Tahmida Majumder, Michael Goh, Nor Hidayati Abdul Aziz
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Air pollution is a global health concern due to the higher concentration levels of pollutants (PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2.5</inf>, PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">10</inf>, SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, NO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, CO, O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>) found in the air. Accurate prediction of the concentration levels plays a crucial part in mitigating public health concerns. To resolve this problem, we propose a Bi-GRU-based deep learning model to aid in forecasting PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2.5</inf> concentration levels. The proposed method utilizes a sliding window to retain past information to forecast for the future. The Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (Bi-GRU) model was developed using a multi-station dataset from Beijing. The model achieved an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score of 0.95, MSE of 185.51, MAE of 9.7, and RMSE of 13.62. Compared to baseline models our model has shown significant improvement, suggesting its effectiveness in real-time air pollution monitoring and forecasting. Future studies can focus on analyzing broader datasets and hybrid architecture.

title: Multi-Rail Flyback Auxiliary Power Supply with 9–70V Input for Bidirectional DC-DC Converter Control
authors: Mohamed Boudokhane, Sonia Eloued, Firas Chabbah, Adel Bouallegue, Christian Mader, Otto Kreutzer
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: This paper presents the design, implementation, and experimental validation of a multi-output isolated flyback auxiliary power supply intended for controlling a bidirectional DC-DC converter. The system operates across a wide input voltage range of 9 V to 70 V, delivering three regulated outputs: 12 V, 5 V, and 3.3 V, which are essential for logic circuits, gate drivers, and communication modules. The flyback topology was selected for its galvanic isolation, safety features, and adaptability to input voltage variation. The converter was modeled, simulated using LTspice, and implemented on a two-layer PCB. Experimental results demonstrated reliable output regulation with a maximum efficiency of 65% under nominal conditions. Key performance metrics, including thermal behavior and component layout, were analyzed to assess suitability for compact, embedded power applications. The system delivers a total output power of approximately 34 W, making it a practical solution for isolated auxiliary supply in power electronic platforms.

title: Multi-Modal Latent Diffusion using Information Disentangling Autoencoders
authors: Daniel Siromani, Pier Luigi Dragotti
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: While real world data frequently comprises multiple distinct modalities, most generative AI operates primarily with a single modality. Moreover, existing multimodal generation works primarily tackle either text-image generation or conditional modality generation. We extend the latent diffusion framework into the multimodal image domain, facilitating consistent joint generation of two or more modalities. Based on the idea that two modalities comprise some shared information and some information unique to each modality, we develop a variational autoencoder architecture and training scheme to generate an effective latent space for diffusion generation. We demonstrate generation of both RGB & semantic segmentation and RGB & depth map modality pairs. We find our disentangled latent space structure to improve generative quality and ensure consistency between modalities.

title: Bipolar Joint Energy: Energy-Based Multi-Label OOD Detection with Bipolar Networks
authors: Sourav Mishra, Keshav Moorthy, Suresh Sundaram
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is essential for deploying vision systems safely, since test-time inputs may not follow the training distribution. In real-world settings, inputs are inherently multi-labeled containing multiple correlated and overlapping concepts, which makes OOD detection particularly challenging across interdependent label spaces with single output layered networks. Despite its practical importance, OOD research has primarily addressed single labeled classification, only recently turning to multi-label settings by designing energy based OOD measures like Joint Energy (JE) for single output layered networks. On the other hand, bipolar networks have shown better multi-labeled classification performance over single output layered networks by encoding both positive and negative evidence for each label, yielding well-separated margins for in-distribution inputs. This raises the question - can bipolar networks improve multi-label OOD detection performance? This work addresses this research gap by proposing Bipolar Joint Energy (BJE), an energy-based scoring function that operates on bipolar margins for better OOD detection in multi-labeled settings. Experiments on PASCAL-VOC, MS-COCO, and NUS-WIDE show that BJE improves AUROC by up to 2.5 % and reduces FPR95 by up to 39 % compared to existing methods, demonstrating the advantage of bipolar architectures for multi-label OOD detection.

title: Variable-Gain RISE Control for Human Upper-Limb Exoskeleton in Passive Rehabilitation
authors: Afef Hfaiedh, Nahla Khraief, Safiya Belghith
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: In this paper, we propose a Variable-Gain Robust Integral of the Sign of the Error (VRISE) control strategy for a human upper-limb exoskeleton, with the aim of enhancing passive rehabilitation. The proposed controller integrates a variable-gain mechanism to enhance tracking accuracy and disturbance rejection, outperforming the traditional RISE control approach. The stability of the closed loop system is formally analysed using Lyapunov theory, which guarantees asymptotic convergence of the tracking error to zero. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated through numerical simulations, demonstrating improved trajectory tracking and enhanced robustness against external disturbances. These results highlight the potential of VRISE control in optimizing exoskeleton-assisted rehabilitation.

title: Design and Validation of an Autonomous IoT Collar for Bovine Monitoring in the Bolivian Altiplano
authors: Miguel Angel Silva Plata, Nayara Carminia Lara Ramos, Diego Samuel Guzmán Riveros, Sharly Marian Ballón López, Leyla Bionda Lipa Huari, Gabriel Antonio Rojas Silva
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This work presents the design and validation of an autonomous IoT collar for livestock monitoring in the Bolivian Altiplano. The device integrates motion, temperature, and environmental sensors into a compact platform powered by a solar energy system. A low-power wireless link enables continuous data transmission to a local gateway for remote visualization. The mechanical enclosure was 3D-printed with a bio-inspired internal pattern that improves impact resistance and overall durability. Compression, vibration, and water-resistance tests confirmed the structural reliability of the collar, while field trials demonstrated stable operation under real conditions. The embedded motionanalysis algorithm achieved a mean absolute error of 1.5 steps <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{1. 1 {\%}})$</tex> and a correlation of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=0.969\left(R^{2}=0.939\right)$</tex>, effectively distinguishing locomotion from non-advance movements. These results confirm the system's robustness and accuracy, which provides a low-cost solution for continuous livestock monitoring in remote rural areas.

title: Mapping Research Trends in Extended Reality for Dementia and Cognitive Impairment
authors: Yuyang Jia, Shihua Cao, Xinyu Zhang, Shiying Zhou, Xule Zhu
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Objective: This study aims to provide an overview of the current status and emerging trends in extended reality (XR) applications for dementia and cognitive impairment, analyze the research hotspots and frontiers in this field, and provide a reference for further development and in-depth research of XR in dementia and cognitive impairment in the future. Methods: The methodology involved searching the Web of Science core database for relevant literature on XR and cognitive impairment up to October 15, 2025. Visualization and analysis were performed with the help of CiteSpace (6.4.R1), VOSviewer (1.6.20), R (4.5.0) software and Scimago Graphica. Results: A total of 1,070 papers on extended reality (XR) in the field of cognitive impairment were included in this study. Overall, the number of publications has grown exponentially, indicating the increasing academic interest and technological maturity of XR research. Among XR modalities, virtual reality (VR) is the most widely used, while augmented reality (AR) and mixed reality (MR) are emerging as complementary technologies. Keyword and cluster analyses reveal that research hotspots are mainly concentrated in cognitive assessment, immersive rehabilitation, and dementia care. The most studied target population is individuals with dementia and mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and long-term care facilities represent the primary application settings. Conclusion: The growth of XR research in the field of cognitive impairment is extending into different stages of cognitive impairment. Future studies can advance the role of XR in cognitive disorder management by enhancing ecological validity, promoting interdisciplinary collaboration, and integrating XR with artificial intelligence and biosensing technologies.

title: Towards a Child-Appropriate LLM for Child–Robot Conversation
authors: Irina Rudenko, Utku Norman, Lukas Hilgert, Jan Niehues, Barbara Bruno

title:
A DC–65-GHz Modulator Driver With 4-V
                    <sub>ppd</sub>
                    Linear Output Swing for 112-GBaud Optical Transceivers in 180-nm SiGe BiCMOS
authors: Han Liu, Bolun Cui, Guike Li, Zhao Zhang, Jian Liu, Nanjian Wu, Wim F. Cops, Tao Chen, Liyuan Liu, Nan Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This work presents a 112-GBaud wideband linear driver designed to drive Mach–Zehnder modulators (MZMs) for intensity-modulation direct-detection (IM-DD) and coherent optical transmitters. Designed in 180-nm SiGe BiCMOS, the driver employs a distributed driving stage to overcome gain–bandwidth limitations. A low-impedance input artificial transmission line (ATL) is designed to enhance the cutoff frequency, impedance matching, and group delay performances. Equalization (EQ) is embedded within each drive cell in the distributed driving stage to compensate for MZM electrodes and channel losses. A capacitive-division structure is integrated in the drive cells to reduce the input impedance variation of the BJT-based cells across frequencies and EQ configurations. M-derived topologies improve the matching bandwidth between ATLs and the terminations. Trimming shields incorporated into the ATL design tune the high-frequency response and provide a better-matched impedance. The driver attains a 4-V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ppd</sub> linear output swing with 2.9%-THD, 65-GHz typical −3-dB bandwidth, and 11.3-dB maximum gain peaking at 50 GHz. Time-domain measurements demonstrate clear eye openings for all NRZ, PAM-4, and PAM-8 modulation formats at 112-GBaud rate and beyond. The driver achieves 3.62-pJ/bit energy efficiency with 112-GBaud PAM-4 and 2.41pJ/bit with 112-GBaud PAM-8, which can be further optimized with co-packaged MZM.

title: A 4-Port 1×8 MIMO Antenna Array for 24 GHz Radars with Enhanced Gain and Isolation Using a Decoupling Metamaterial
authors: Mahmoud Shaban, Abdullah. M. Almohaimeed
abstract: This work offers the design, analysis, and experimental verification of high-performance 4-port MIMO antenna, designed for use within the Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) band. The antenna operates at a frequency of 24 GHz and is mainly targeted high-resolution automotive radar sensing applications. The proposed design is based on a 4-ports 1×8 microstrip patch array configuration, that is carefully designed to ensure high radiation efficiency with a compact form factor. The design includes an optimized metamaterial decoupling mechanism placed strategically between radiating elements to address the most important mutual coupling challenge in densely packed MIMO antennas. By successfully stopping surface wave propagation, this decoupling produces an exceptional inter-port isolation of over 31.4 dB, which is noticeably higher than that of traditional MIMO designs. At an operating frequency of 24.15 GHz, the antenna exhibited a high peak gain of 15.6 dBi, which is essential for long-range detection in radar application. The radiation pattern analysis showed a narrow E-plane half-power beamwidth (HPBW) of 9° and a wider H-plane HPBW of 54°, ensuring accurate beam steering and spatial selectivity. Additionally, in a cluttered environment, the side-lobe levels were reduced to −22.4 dB, reducing interference and ensuring accurate target detection. In addition to improving port isolation, the use of metamaterial decoupling structure was quite helpful in designing efficient MIMO system. These achieved specifications show that the proposed antenna can be used in next-generation millimeter-wave sensing, especially in industrial sensing, high-density wireless communication networks, and automotive radars, where the high gain, low interference, and reliable channel capacity are importance characteristics.

title: Phase Drifts and Signal Dispersion in Coaxial Cables
authors: Andžej Šerlat, Maciej Grzegrzółka, Maciej Urbański, Bartosz Gąsowski, Krzysztof Czuba, Dominik Sikora, Radosław Papis, Paweł Jatczak, Igor Rutkowski, Jeremiah Holzbauer, Nathan Eddy
abstract: Phase reference distribution is a critical challenge in modern linear particle accelerators, such as European Spallation Source (ESS), European X-ray Free Electron Laser (E-XFEL), or Proton Improvement Plan II (PIP-II). Similar issues may also arise in other RF systems that need to synchronize many independent circuits. Achieving low phase drift of the reference signal requires careful selection of cables and their environmental operating conditions. Due to the limited data provided by cable manufacturers, it is necessary to characterize the cable performance. This article investigates the phase drifts in coaxial cables and presents a dedicated measurement methodology. The cables’ dispersion is also examined. The dispersion characteristics of the cables are also examined. The measurement results of various coaxial cables are presented, along with conclusions and practical guidelines for designers of phase reference distribution lines.

title: Unlock the Potential of Large Language Models for Predictive Tabular Tasks in Data Science With Table-Specific Pretraining
authors: Yazheng Yang, Yuqi Wang, Yaxuan Li, Sankalok Sen, Lei Li, Lin Qiu, Qi Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: In data science, predictive tasks such as classification, regression, and missing value imputation are fundamental challenges in tabular data analysis. This research investigates the application of Large Language Models (LLMs) to these tasks. While LLMs excel in natural language understanding, their effectiveness on structured tabular data remains limited due to minimal exposure during pretraining. To address this gap, we construct a large-scale corpus of annotated tables and introduce a tailored pretraining framework. Our trained model achieves significant improvements over baselines, with an average gain of 8.9% in classification and 10.7% in regression tasks. We further evaluate its performance in zero-shot and few-shot prediction, as well as in-context learning scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial gains over existing benchmarks, highlighting the potential of LLMs for tabular data processing. Additionally, we apply our approach across multiple open-source LLMs and demonstrate its generalizability. This work establishes a new benchmark for enhancing tabular intelligence through LLM-based pretraining.

title: Optimization Design of Bearing Insulation for Traction Motors of High-Speed Trains under Overvoltage Impulses
authors: Song Xiao, Zijian Zhan, Hui Li, Yujun Guo, Ye Cao, Tiangeng Li, Junjie Zeng, Qifeng Wang, Wenbo Xu, Taifan Zhou, Yu Liu, Peng You, Guoqiang Gao, Xueqin Zhang, Guangning Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: The increasing speed and power of high-speed trains have intensified the occurrence and amplitude of overvoltage impulses caused by the operation of vacuum circuit breakers (VCBs) during the phase-changing process. These overvoltage impulses result in an abnormal rise in bearing voltage, far exceeding normal operational levels. With hundreds of overvoltage impulses occurring daily, the electrochemical corrosion of bearings is significantly deteriorated. Herein, the propagation paths of overvoltage within the traction system and the damage mechanisms affecting bearings are systematically investigated. Via launching a simulation model encompassing the traction power supply system, train body coupling paths, and bearing equivalent circuits, the propagation patterns of overvoltage through the train body, bogie, and motor coupling system is revealed. An experimental platform simulating transient overvoltage impulses under practical operational conditions is established, as the scanning electron microscopy of bearing cross-sections validates the correlation between insulation layer thickness and electrochemical damage. Based on the improved RIME optimization algorithm, an optimized method has been proposed to balance the electric field strength within the insulation layer and the voltage across the bearing. These findings offer solid theoretical support and experimental data for the optimization design of bearing insulation with the development of overvoltage suppression strategies in traction motors.

title: ICRNet: Semantic Segmentation of Remote Sensing Images Based on Integrating Global and Local Class Representations
authors: Yifei Wu, Liang Chen, Haibo Huang, Ke Wang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Remote sensing images are characterize by complex backgrounds, small inter-class variations and large intra-class variance. Although existing class-guided methods have achieved impressive results in addressing these issues, they still suffer from reliance on predefined geometric priors and loss of spatial information. To address this, we propose ICRNet, a novel network integrating global and local class representations for semantic segmentation of remote sensing images. Specifically, a Global Class Gather Module (GCGM) is applied to aggregate global contextual information of same class into channels. Then, we build a cascaded decoder based on Class-Guide Transformer Block (CGTB), which contains Global and Local Class Attention (GLCA) and Multi-Scale Dilated Convolution (MSDC). GLCA leverages class information to guide context modeling by integrating global and class representations through bi-level routing attention and MSDC is proposed to capture multi-scale contextual information. The extensive experimental results demonstrate that our proposed ICRNet can achieve superior performance compared to current representative semantic segmentation methods. Code is available at https://github.com/mrwuyf/ICRNet.

title: A Multi-Band Spectral Information and Multi-Scale Center-Guided Spatial Information Learning Framework for HSI Few-Shot Classification
authors: Yong Wang, Jiaxin Zheng, Yuyang Wang, Pei-Qiu Huang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Deep learning has been widely applied for hyperspectral image (HSI) few-shot classification tasks. However, 1) existing methods directly process whole spectral bands, ignoring the diversity among bands and thus weakening the spectral representation capability; and 2) they often fail to adequately focus on the central pixel information that represents the target, leading to poor classification performance. To alleviate these two issues, we propose a multi-band spectral information and multi-scale center-guided spatial information learning framework (MBS-MSCS), which includes two parts: multi-band spectral information learning (MBSIL) module and multi-scale center-guided spatial information learning (MSCSIL) module. Specifically, MBSIL employs both intra-band and inter-band attentions to assign adaptive weights to different spectral bands, thereby extracting multi-band spectral features and enhancing the representation ability for different spectral bands. Afterward, MSCSIL makes use of multi-scale cropping and central filling on the multi-band spectral features to fully leverage the central pixel information and output multi-scale center-filling patches. Theses patches are subsequently processed via feature extraction, fusion, and decoding to obtain discriminative spectral-spatial features for classification. Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrate that MBS-MSCS outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
