title: Thermal Dispersive Variation in Quality Factor of Resonances for Broad and Multiband THz Absorber
authors: Kamlesh Kishore, Gaurav Varshney
abstract: An absorber is implemented with the perturbed leafy structure of resonator made of phase change material, vanadium dioxide (VO<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) to obtain the multiple resonances in terahertz (THz) frequency domain. The quality factor of resonances can be controlled through thermal variation in dispersive properties of VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>. The resonances with high-quality factor can be excited in insulating phase of VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> to achieve the multiple narrow absorption bands. The quality factor of excited resonances can be thermally tuned to lower values in the metallic phase of VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> which merges their spectrum to provide a broad absorption band. Thus, absorber can provide a thermally switchable absorption spectrum from broad to nine narrow absorption peaks within frequency range from 3 to 8 THz using ultrathin geometry of thickness around <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda /8$</tex-math> </inline-formula>; <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda $</tex-math> </inline-formula> is free space wavelength. The absorber structure is numerically analyzed and operation is validated through transmission line method prepared using circuit theory approach.

title: Redistribution Layer Routing for Fan-Out Wafer-Level Packaging Considering Multiple Advanced Design Rules
authors: Zepeng Li, Zhen Zhuang, Genggeng Liu, Bentian Jiang, Weihua Sheng, Tsung-Yi Ho
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Advanced fan-out wafer-level packaging (FOWLP) has become a key enabler for high-performance computing (HPC) and artificial intelligence (AI) systems, offering high-density and heterogeneous integration of chips. As the interconnect density of signal nets in redistribution layers (RDLs) continues to increase, ensuring reliability and manufacturability becomes increasingly challenging. To ensure reliable power delivery, signal nets are required to minimize their wire usage within fan-in regions to reserve routing space for power delivery networks (PDNs). Meanwhile, specific fan-out routing patterns in keep-out-zones (KOZs) and chip boundaries are essential for improving manufacturing yield. While these advanced design rules improve packaging reliability and manufacturability, they also increase routing difficulty due to constrained fan-out space and specific fan-out routing patterns in the KOZs and chip boundaries. To address these challenges, we propose a redistribution layer routing method for fan-out wafer-level packaging considering multiple advanced design rules that offers improved reliability and manufacturability by implementing five major techniques: 1) A KOZ-aware channel generation technique and a well-designed directed 2D global routing graph are proposed to analyze routing resources of limited fan-out regions and avoid the violations in KOZs and chip boundaries. 2) A bipartite matching algorithm is employed for global net-to-bump assignment, minimizing wirelength while avoiding locally suboptimal solutions. 3) We generate multiple candidate 2D global routing paths using a K-shortest path algorithm, enabling each net to explore candidate wirelength-minimized and congestion-avoiding routing paths. 4) We formulate the path selection problem as an integer linear programming (ILP) model to globally assign minimum-length routing paths while satisfying routing capacity constraints. 5) We propose a 3D routing framework to perform intra-channel routing using an octagonal-tile-based 3D global routing and a geometry-based detailed routing to minimize the final routing wirelength while avoiding conflicts and satisfying spacing constraints. The experimental results show that our algorithm achieves 100% routability without violations in KOZs or chip boundaries, while reducing fan-in usage by 25.13× and 14.31× on average compared with two leading RDL routers.

title: Saliency-Aware Adversarial Attacks on Visual Trackers
authors: Xingsen Huang, Yuqing Huang, Hongpeng Wang, Yaowei Wang, Xin Li
abstract: Object tracking is a fundamental task in computer vision, and ensuring its security is crucial for reliable and safe real-world applications. Similar background distractors are commonly present in tracking scenarios and often result in tracking failures. However, tracking attack methods leveraging background similarity have yet to receive adequate attention. In this paper, we propose a saliency-aware guided adversarial attack method for tracking models. The proposed method aims to evaluate the performance of advanced trackers under adversarial attacks, targeting interference from similar objects to reveal their vulnerabilities. Specifically, we introduce a text-guided similar region localization module that leverages semantic features of coarse-grained category attributes to select regions from multiple frames that are both visually and semantically similar to the target, overcoming the limitations of random selection methods. In addition, we develop a saliency-aware search region feature constraint module that incorporates foreground-background saliency information. By dynamically filtering feature weights through the joint use of fine-grained target appearance and text descriptions, this adaptive mechanism reduces the confidence of the tracker in foreground-background distinctions, progressively leading to target drift. We conduct extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets over multiple types of trackers, and the results demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively attack existing trackers and reveal their potential security risks.

title: Surface Stress Characterization and Trends in Thermally Strengthened Photovoltaic Module Glass
authors: Elizabeth C. Palmiotti, Timothy J Silverman, Michael G. Deceglie, Ingrid L. Repins, E. Ashley Gaulding
abstract: Simultaneous changes in photovoltaic (PV) module architecture—such as increased area, replacing the polymeric backsheet with a glass backsheet, and reducing glass thickness from 3.2 to 2.0 mm—have resulted in a novel failure called spontaneous glass breakage. Spontaneous glass breakage results in front and/or rear glass failure without any obvious cause. The resulting low-energy fracture patterns have raised concerns about the thermal strengthening in 2.0-mm glass. We present validation of a nonde structive method for measuring the glass surface stress in PV modules. We use a scattered light polariscope to examine the glass properties of 11 modules from 6 solar fields, with and without spontaneous glass breakage. Based on a Mann–Whitney test p-value of 1.4 × 10−6, we conclude that there is a correlation between lower surface stress and susceptibility to spontaneous breakage. A Kendall's Tau test p-value of 2.0 × 10−4 indicates a correlation between increased module area and lower surface stress. We find that 2.0-mm glass can be, and often is, fully tempered (surface stress ≥69 MPa), but inspecting the fracture pattern is not a reliable way to assess the amount of thermal strengthening. A combination of factors, not just surface stress, influences glass breakage.

title: DynNet: A Lightweight Distributed Framework for Topology Obfuscation in Dynamic Networks
authors: Xuanbo Huang, Kaiping Xue, Lutong Chen, Zixu Huang, Jian Li, Hyundong Shin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Network Topology Obfuscation (NTO) has emerged as a promising scheme to conceal the physical layout of networks, thereby preventing adversaries from targeting critical nodes or links. By constructing deceptive virtual topologies, NTO schemes can obscure essential components and deploy honeypots to mislead and detect malicious behavior. However, existing NTO methods suffer from two major limitations. First, their high computational overhead makes them impractical for dynamic environments such as vehicular networks, where rapid topology adaptation is essential. Second, most current solutions depend on centralized controllers, typically within Software-defined Networking (SDN) architectures, which limits their applicability in decentralized or ad hoc settings. To address these challenges, we propose DynNet, a distributed and probabilistic NTO framework that adapts to dynamic topologies. Each node in DynNet independently handles probing packets (e.g., packets with low time-to-live (TTL) values) by probabilistically choosing to respond truthfully, return deceptive data, or remain silent. This probabilistic framework allows nodes to rapidly update their behavior in response to topology changes with minimal overhead. To ensure consistency, we propose a bucket-based probabilistic mapping to ensure that packets from the same flow consistently trigger the same response. Our evaluation shows that DynNet maintains effective topology concealment in highly dynamic scenarios while significantly reducing computational and communication overhead. The results demonstrate its suitability for deployment in distributed, rapidly changing network environments.

title: Energy Management Strategy for Fuel Cell Hybrid Electric Vehicle Based on Driving Pattern Recognition with Fuzzy C-means Clustering
authors: Zhanfeng Zhu, Zhendong Sun, Yingfang Liu, Zonghai Chen
abstract: Fuel cell vehicles have broad prospects in the field of transportation electrification, while their operating characteristics limit their application in complex working conditions. As a result, fuel cell vehicles are generally equipped with Li-ion batteries or ultra-capacitors as auxiliary power sources. An energy management strategy (EMS) is essential to maintain the efficient, stable, and reliable operation of fuel cell hybrid electric vehicles (FCHEVs). In this work, a driving pattern recognition (DPR) method is introduced to improve the adaptability of the EMS. First, fuzzy C-means (FCM) clustering is applied to classify different driving patterns, facilitating the construction of representative driving cycles. Under each driving cycle, the parameters of fuzzy logic controllers are optimized using a genetic algorithm (GA). Finally, the corresponding fuzzy logic controller is invoked based on the DPR results for power allocation in this hybrid power electronic system. The proposed method reduces the overall cost by at least 9.94% compared with a fuzzy logic control strategy, while remaining within 1.83% of the dynamic programming strategy, demonstrating its near-optimal performance with real time feasibility.

title: A 134-MHz UGB Capacitorless GaN-Based LDO With Wide Load Range and High-Temperature Stability
authors: Cheng Sung, Yu-Sheng Hsiao, Pin-Shiuan Kuo, Tian-Li Wu
abstract: This paper presents the first experimental demonstration of a fully integrated GaN-based output capacitorless low-dropout regulator (LDO), implemented in a 0.5 μm GaN-on-silicon platform. Owing to the low parasitic capacitance and high-speed nature of GaN HEMTs, together with a gain-enhanced error amplifier, the regulator attains a 134 MHz unity-gain bandwidth. It supplies 0–300 mA while preserving phase margin > 77° for loads above 10 mA and 46° at no-load. Measured power-supply rejection is −34 dB at 1 MHz and −22 dB at 10 MHz, and retaining a robust −13 dB rejection at 10 MHz even at 150°C. A 1 mA → 300 mA step produces a 223 mV deviation and settles in 276 ns. The LDO operates from 8 V to 17 V and remains stable up to 150°C yet occupies only 0.018 mm² (LDO core).

title: A comparative study of low-level jets in different climate regions in China using multi-site Doppler wind lidar observations_supp1-3672972.docx
authors: Tianwen Wei
abstract: Low-level jets (LLJs) play a crucial role in modulating regional weather, air quality, and wind energy resources. In a topographically diverse country like China, the characteristics and driving mechanisms of LLJs are expected to vary significantly. However, a comprehensive understanding of these regional differences has been limited by the lack of coordinated, high-resolution observations. This study bridges this gap using a year-long observational experiment with Doppler wind lidars at three representative sites: Minfeng (temperate continental climate), Beijing (temperate monsoon climate), and Hefei (subtropical monsoon climate). Our analysis reveals clear contrasts in LLJ regimes: boundary-layer jets (BLJs) dominate in Minfeng and Hefei, while synoptic-system-related LLJs (SLLJs) prevail in Beijing. LLJ-1 category is dominant at all three sites. A double-nose structure of BLJs is observed at 0.5 km in Beijing and SLLJ peaks at 1.4 km in Hefei. Key properties such as jet core height, diurnal phase, and seasonal prevalence exhibit clear regional dependencies. The most frequent jet core heights are 0.7 km in Minfeng, 0.4 km in Hefei, and 1.5 km in Beijing, with peak seasonal activity occurring in May, July, and October, respectively. Jet core height increases with more stringent jet speed criteria at all sites. The time of the lowest LLJ occurrence frequency shows a longitudinal time lag, occurring at 14:00 local time in Hefei and Beijing, and at 16:00 local time in Minfeng, which aligns with the time of peak mixing layer height at the corresponding site. The occurrence of nocturnal LLJs is positively correlated with a deeper mixing layer height, with occurrence frequencies exceeding daytime values during most of the night. The roles of inertial oscillations and synoptic forcing in shaping LLJ characteristics are investigated. The most intense inertial oscillations occur near the jet core heights and are synchronized with frontal passage events at each site. Case studies of Minfeng and Hefei suggest that frontal passages can amplify the inertial oscillation signal, and the diurnal cycle of inertial motions associated with nocturnal LLJs is consistent with Blackadar's evening-transition hypothesis. Under strong synoptic forcing, such as in Beijing, the inertial oscillation signal becomes less pronounced. Analysis of synoptic forcing reveals distinct regional influences: in Minfeng, nocturnal LLJs are favored by cold fronts, the Tarim easterly jets, and terrain-induced baroclinicity; in Hefei, they are influenced by the East Asian monsoon and frontal systems; and in Beijing, the Northeast China Cold Vortex is the primary synoptic driver with significant influence from complex terrain. This study provides valuable observational insights into the regional variability of LLJs and their associated boundary-layer processes.

title: The True Cost of AI Assistance to Programming of Software
authors: Daniel M. Berry

title: Path Departure Risk Assessment and Hierarchical Longitudinal Control for Autonomous Mining Trucks
authors: Xiao Chen, Guizhen Yu, Peng Chen, Qi Xia, Han Li, Haoyuan Ni
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Autonomous Mining Trucks (AMT) are extensively deployed in open-pit mining areas. When AMT experience path departure, collisions with road boundaries or obstacles may occur, leading to severe consequences. This necessitates specialized path departure risk assessment and safety control strategies. In this paper, an enhanced Time to Lane Crossing (TLC)-based departure risk quantification method and a collision detection-based collision risk quantification method are proposed, and a hierarchical longitudinal control strategy is developed building upon these quantifications. Experiments using actual AMT operational data from mining sites demonstrate that the proposed safety protection control strategy computes appropriate deceleration commands based on risk quantification results, enabling timely safety interventions before path departure or collisions occur, thereby ensuring AMT operational safety.

title: Optimizing EEG Signal Quality and Streaming Performance in BCIs Using ADS1299EEG-FE
authors: Yslam Orazov, Mukam Ekayev, Kerim Ryzayev, Gurbanmyrat Mezilov
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Reliable and noise-free electroencephalography (EEG) acquisition is a key factor in building effective braincomputer interface (BCI) systems. This paper presents an evaluation of methods for optimizing EEG signal quality and realtime streaming performance using the ADS1299EEG-FE platform. The study compares hardware-based noise reduction strategiessuch as active shielding and grounding-with software-level filtering and adaptive preprocessing techniques to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) under varying environmental conditions. In addition, an edge-based streaming setup using a Raspberry Pi was implemented to test the performance of realtime EEG data transmission to a cloud analytics platform. Experimental results showed that the optimized configuration achieved up to a 35% reduction in power line noise and a 40% improvement in signal stability during continuous recording, while maintaining low data latency suitable for BCI feedback applications. The outcomes demonstrate that ADS1299EEG-FE can serve as a reliable, low-cost, and scalable solution for EEG data acquisition in both research and educational environments. These findings contribute to ongoing efforts to make EEG-driven intelligent systems more accessible and efficient for neurotechnology applications.

title: Credit Risk early warning Research based on Multisource Heterogeneous Data
authors: Wei Guo, Yushen Lu, Bocheng Wang, Qinghong He, Xuan Liang, Yuting Wen
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Accurate early warning of credit risk is crucial for financial stability. Existing models mainly rely on financial indicators, while unstructured data is used less frequently, which limits their predictive performance. This paper constructs multi-source heterogeneous data (MHD) that integrate financial variables, market-attention measures, and corporate-governance attributes, and employs both econometric and machine learning models to systematically examine the gains in credit-risk early warning performance. The SHAP method is further used to quantify feature importance and enhance model interpretability. Results show that, compared with traditional methods, the early warning model enriched with MHD achieves significant improvements in precision, accuracy, F1-score, and AUC. While financial indicators remain the most influential, market attention and corporate-governance factors also play a critical role.

title: Evaluating Urban Livability through Aesthetic and Environmental Index: A Spatial Framework for Bandung City
authors: Robby Yussac Tallar, Ferlina Sugata, Tantri Oktavia, Andi A. Hamzah, Grace Carolline, Wenny Anggraini Natalia
abstract: Bandung City faces challenges in maintaining a balance between physical development and the preservation of aesthetic values and environmental safety. Therefore, this paper introduces an Aesthetic-Environmental Index that integrates four parameters-population density, green open space, average temperature and frequency of natural disasters with adjusted weights. This study uses a Geographic Information System (GIS)-based spatial analysis approach to assess urban spatial welfare in Bandung City by integrating environmental and aesthetic aspects. Based on the weighting results between green open space and building density variables, 23 subdistricts have an aesthetic parameter score of 0.6 with a cumulative value combination of 0.1 for population density and 0.5 for green open space, so it can be concluded that subdistricts with this score are greatly influenced by the excellent green open space value. Most of area in Bandung City were categorized on quadrant II (High Aesthetics and Low Safety). This study also showed that Bandung City has spatial disparities in livability, therefore for recommendation, the government should have priority intervention zones identified through spatial gaps for example in North Bandung Area, the government can have target for industrial emissions and river restoration. Further, the government can build a pilot livability area by integrating green infrastructure and aesthetic upgrades then launch a public livability dashboard for real-time monitoring and participatory governance.

title: A Comparative Study on Deep Learning Models for Facial Expression Recognition
authors: Daniel Setiadikarunia, Riko Arlando Saragih, Robert Oloan Rajagukguk, Roshan Gobind
abstract: Facial expression recognition is a computer vision task that support intelligent action such as surveillance, robotic applications and medical systems. This paper describes a comparative study on two deep learning (DL) models, namely DeepFace (DF) model and Mini-Xception (MX) model to predict emotions based on facial expressions. This work is preliminary work for the counselling process. Both models were tested on the FER dataset that is publicly available. The simulation shows that the MX model outperforms the DF model in almost every emotion category

title: Financial Transmission Rights–Based Economic Instruments for Congestion Management in Electricity Markets
authors: Majed A. Alotaibi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: This paper addresses the use of economic instruments for congestion management in competitive electricity markets. It reviews prominent financial mechanisms applied in congestion control and introduces a mathematical model designed to minimize the total generation cost within a single-auction market framework. Financial Transmission Rights (FTRs) are employed as a hedging tool to mitigate risks arising from congestion. The results demonstrate the significant effectiveness of FTRs in alleviating congestion and supporting efficient market operation.

title: FairSCOSCA: Fairness At Arterial Signals — Just Around The Corner
authors: Kevin Riehl, Justin Weiss, Anastasios Kouvelas, Michail A. Makridis
publish: 2026 IEEE Forum for Innovative Sustainable Transportation Systems (FISTS)
abstract: Traffic signal control at intersections, especially in arterial networks, is a key lever for mitigating the growing issue of traffic congestion in cities. Despite the widespread deployment of SCOOTS and SCATS, which prioritize efficiency, fairness has remained largely absent from their design logic, often resulting in unfair outcomes for certain road users, such as excessive waiting times. Fairness however, is a major driver of public acceptance for implementation of new control systems. Therefore, this work proposes FairSCOSCA, a fairness-enhancing extension to these systems, featuring two novel yet practical design adaptations grounded in multiple normative fairness definitions: (1) green phase optimization incorporating cumulative waiting times, and (2) early termination of underutilized green phases. Those extensions ensure fairer distributions of green times. Evaluated in a calibrated microsimulation case study of the arterial network in Esslingen am Neckar (Germany), FairSCOSCA demonstrates substantial improvements across multiple fairness dimensions (Egalitarian, Rawlsian, Utilitarian, and Harsanyian) without sacrificing traffic efficiency. Benchmarked against Fixed-Cycle, Max-Pressure, and standard SCOOTS/SCATS controllers, FairSCOSCA significantly reduces excessive waiting times, delay inequality and horizontal discrimination between arterial and feeder roads. This work contributes to the growing literature on equitable traffic control by bridging the gap between fairness theory and the practical enhancement of globally deployed signal systems. Open source implementation available on GitHub.

title: Deep Learning-Based Estimation of Li-Ion Battery State of Charge: Comparative Analysis of CNN, LSTM, and GRU Approaches
authors: Marwa Gaich, Sabeur Jemmali, Bilal Manai
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: The growing demand for accurate battery state monitoring in electric vehicles has driven significant advancements in data-driven estimation methods. In recent years, deep learning-based approaches have emerged as powerful alternatives to traditional model-based techniques for State of Charge (SoC) prediction. This study systematically evaluates three neural network architectures : Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) for lithium-ion battery SoC estimation under varying temperature conditions using the LG 18650HG2 dataset. The normalized input features (voltage, current, and temperature) are directly fed into each model, and their performance is assessed using standard regression metrics. The experimental results show that all models achieve high estimation accuracy, with the GRU model demonstrating superior robustness and precision. Notably, at 40°C, the GRU achieves an RMSE of 0.4%, MAE of 0.7%, and an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score of 99.8%, outperforming the CNN and LSTM. These findings confirm the effectiveness of recurrent models in capturing the temporal dependencies inherent in battery data. The GRU architecture, in particular, it effectively navigates the trade-off between prediction precision and computational load, which renders it appropriate for deployment in real-time battery management systems.

title: A Systematic Classification Study for Single-trial EEG-based Target Detection during RSVP Task
authors: Jingjia Yuan, Chenyun Lu, Linze Qian, Yu Sun
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Target detection using brain-computer interfaces (BCI) based on rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) offers a promising solution for high-throughput visual search. However, practical application is hindered by challenges such as the low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of single-trial EEG, the ambiguity of optimal classifier selection, and severe class imbalance. This study addresses these issues through a systematic evaluation and optimization of a complete classification pipeline using data from 34 participants. Our findings first validate that xDAWN spatial filtering significantly enhances P300-based features, boosting classification performance. A subsequent comprehensive comparison of six classifiers identifies the linear support vector machine (SVM) provides the best balance of accuracy, stability, and computational efficiency across both offline and pseudoonline scenarios. Most critically, a dual strategy combining ADASYN data augmentation with cost-sensitive learning is shown to effectively mitigate class imbalance, substantially increasing the hit rate for rare targets while maintaining high overall performance. In conclusion, this work provides a validated, step-by-step methodology with optimized parameters, offering a robust and practical framework for developing effective realworld RSVP-BCI systems.

title: AI Enhanced Data Privacy in Retail CRM Systems
authors: Sathish Kumar Velayudam, Nidhi Sharma
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Customer Relationship Management (CRM) software is one of the contemporary business processes but a grave concern as far as data privacy is concerned since it is filled with personal details of the customers. The paper examines the blend of Artificial Intelligence (AI) to enhance data privacy in such software. We propose a three-level AI solution that includes three pillars: dynamic data anonymizer, a mechanism for detecting unauthorized access anomalies, and an intelligent consent management engine. The system will safeguard Personally Identifiable Information (PII) without compromising data utility for rightful business analytics. Our experiment uses a synthetic 476-entry customer dataset mimicking actual CRM entries of contact, transactional, and user preference data. Models created were simulated and proved valid through Python, with libraries such as Scikit-learn to enable machine learning and Pandas to work with data. The outcome demonstrates how the AI system identifies intrusion of privacy at 98% and reduces re-identification risk by 85%compared to baseline systems. The research derives AI success as an active approach towards enhancing data privacy in CRM utilization, sheltered under an extensible solution that adapts learning to avoid emerging attacks without largely impacting the system and data consumption efficiency.

title: Inter-chip Clock Network Synthesis on Passive Interposer of 2.5D Chiplet Considering Transmission Line Effect
authors: Tai Yan, Yiyu Wang, Zhan Li, Ning Xu, Yuanqing Cheng
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: With the slowdown of technology node scaling, 2.5D Chiplet technology has emerged as a promising approach to sustain Moore’s Law. When routing clock signal on the passive interposer layer, transmission line effect must be considered, as signal rise/fall delay becomes comparable to propagation delay, significantly impacting signal integrity and system performance. Additionally, since active devices cannot be placed on the passive interposer, buffers can only be inserted in the chiplets mounted on the interposer. This paper investigates the problem of clock network synthesis for a 2.5D Chiplet with a passive interposer. Firstly, we propose to use a transmission line model to evaluate inter-chip clock skew and delay accurately. Then, we propose a clock network synthesis method considering transmission line effect and impedance matching. Finally, we propose a buffer insertion method with minimum clock wire detouring on passive interposer in order to optimize the inter-chiplet clock network. Experimental results show that our fast transmission line model can calculate clock skew more accurately compared to conventional Elmore model and only results in 2.4% error compared to HSPICE simulations. The clock network syntheses on several 2.5D Chiplet benchmarks show that our proposed algorithm is effective to construct zero-skew clock network for a 2.5D Chiplet in terms of clock skew and buffer area.

title: IoT and AI-Based Approach for Smart Hydroponic pH Control and Lettuce Disease Detection
authors: Manel Souaf, Ibtihel Nouira, Chamsedinne Baazaoui
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: The main objective of this study is to optimize the growth and yield of hydroponic crops, particularly lettuce, while maintaining a healthy and controlled environment. This project involves the development of an automated system for monitoring and regulating the hydrogen potential (pH) level in a hydroponic culture of the Nutrient Film Technique (NFT) type, in order to ensure an optimal pH value in the nutrient solution circulating within the horizontal channels. The system use a pH sensor, an ESP32 microcontroller to collect and process data, a stepper motor and solenoid valves to deliver precise and timely dosing according to the detected needs. The daily measurement results are recorded on a cloud server then, displayed via a mobile application allowing a real-time monitoring of crop conditions. This work also aims to detect the majority of diseases affecting lettuce by relying on an artificial intelligence model based on a convolutional neural network (CNN) and a dedicated database, guaranteeing precise and efficient identification. The obtained results are promising, showing that this system offers a precise pH water, which lead to a reliable and sustainable solution to improve agricultural productivity while reducing water resource consumption.

title: Identification and Tracking of Multiple Objects on a Peristaltic Conveyor System
authors: Sai Preetham Sata, Markus Weidmann, Sebastian Frasch, Michael Albrecht, Hanna Westphal, Matthias Pusch, Benjamin Noack, Andreas Scholz, Elmar Woschke, André Katterfeld
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: The courier express parcel industry has demonstrated considerable volumetric growth in shipment activity throughout the preceding years. In order to enhance the performance and throughput of the material flow technology without compromising on space requirements at distribution centers, various methods are being developed, and their feasibility is being investigated. One such method includes the development of a robust object identification system that is able to identify and track objects for longer duration. Artificial intelligence based methods that utilize You Only Look Once (YOLO) deep learning architecture have been shown to reliably detect and identify objects in several applications provided that there is enough highquality training data. The aim of this work is to detect and track multiple objects on the Peristaltic Conveyor System (PCS) using YOLO-based detection along with existing tracking algorithms, and to estimate the real-time location and dimensions of these objects. The reliability of the detection and tracking approaches is evaluated by comparing their performance using appropriate detection and tracking metrics.

title: Tree-Structured Parzen Estimator-Based Optimization in Stacking Ensemble Learning with Neural Network Meta-Learner for Heart Disease Prediction
authors: Azhar Anas, Naufal Altaf, Nadia Zalfa Arief, Andi Nugroho
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) cause 17.9 million deaths annually, making accurate early detection critical for prevention. While machine learning, particularly Stacking Ensemble Learning (SEL), has shown great promise in this domain, optimizing its complex architectures remains a significant challenge. This study introduces a novel framework, a Stacking Ensemble Learning with a Neural Network Meta-Learner (SEL-NNML), optimized using the Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) algorithm to enhance heart disease prediction. Our approach leverages a neural network's capacity to model non-linear relationships between base learner predictions and leverages TPE to efficiently navigate the vast hyperparameter space. The model's efficacy was rigorously validated on two public benchmarks: the Kaggle Heart Failure Prediction Dataset (KHFPD) and the UCI Cleveland Heart Disease Dataset (UCHDD). The TPE-optimized SEL-NNML framework achieved state-of-the-art performance with accuracies of 94.0% on KHFPD and 91.7% on UCHDD, outperforming the traditional Stacking Ensemble by approximately 2.9% on the KHFPD dataset and surpassing other established classifiers such as XGBoost, CatBoost, and BayesNet.

title: Importance Of Gap Analysis: A Case Study of Webtrust Audit In Government Body Certificate Authority
authors: Muhammad Ihsan Fathurrahman, Abba Suganda Girsang
abstract: Compliance with audit schemes is critical for Certificate Authorities (CAs) to ensure customer trust and operational integrity. Balai Besar Sertifikat Elektronik (BSrE), Indonesia's government CA, must comply with Webtrust for Certification Authority v2.2.2 standards. This study conducts a comprehensive gap analysis to identify compliance gaps before formal auditing. Using a three-phase methodology (Preparation, Field Work, and Reporting), we examined BSrE's controls across seven Webtrust domains. Results revealed twenty-eight gaps, with 82% (23 gaps) concentrated in CA Environmental Controls, particularly in personnel and access management. Technical and procedural gaps were minimal in key lifecycle management domains (1 gap in CA Key Lifecycle, four in Subscriber Key Lifecycle). Early gap identification enables proactive mitigation, reducing audit findings and ensuring continuous compliance. We recommend implementing a centralized findings database, enhancing internal audit functions, and upgrading physical security infrastructure. This proactive approach demonstrates how systematic gap analysis strengthens CA operational readiness and maintains certification integrity.

title: Strategies for Enhancing the Work Experience of the New Generation Workforce from the Perspective of Positive Psychology
authors: Yi Cheng, Linlin Cao
abstract: In contemporary enterprise management practices, a growing number of HR professionals are recognizing the critical value of Employee Experience Management. The prevailing perspective suggests that creating a positive employee experience is fundamental to enhancing the value of corporate products and services, thereby driving business growth. Currently, the new generation of employees constitutes half of the workforce. Effectively managing this cohort, enhancing their work experience, and improving their loyalty have emerged as critical concerns for both corporate managers and academic research. Based on an empirical investigation targeting Post -90s employees in the Yangtze River Delta region (Jiangsu, Zhejiang, and Shanghai) and utilizing SPSS for analysis, this study explores the key factors influencing their workplace experience. Furthermore, it proposes actionable strategies for enhancement from the lens of Positive Psychology, aiming to provide evidence-based recommendations for modern enterprise management.

title: Multimodal Visual and X-Ray Machine Learning Features for Art Authentication - A Case Study in the Analysis of Paintings by Goya
authors: Hassan Ugail, Ismail Lujain Jaleel
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Art authentication of Francisco Goya’s works presents complex computational challenges due to his heterogeneous stylistic evolution and extensive historical patterns of forgery. We introduce a novel multimodal machine learning framework that applies identical feature extraction techniques to both visual and X-ray radiographic images of Goya paintings. The unified feature extraction pipeline incorporates Grey-Level Co-occurrence Matrix descriptors, Local Binary Patterns, entropy measures, energy calculations, and colour distribution analysis applied consistently across both imaging modalities. The extracted features from both visual and X-ray images are processed through an optimised One-Class Support Vector Machine with hyperparameter tuning. Using a dataset of 24 authenticated Goya paintings with corresponding X-ray images, split into an 80/20 train-test configuration with 10-fold c ross-validation, the framework achieves 97.8% classification accuracy with a 0.022 false positive rate. Case study analysis of "Un Gigante" demonstrates the practical efficacy of our pipeline, achieving 92.3% authentication confidence through unified multimodal feature analysis. Our results indicate substantial performance improvement over single-modal approaches, establishing the effectiveness of applying identical computational methods to both visual and radiographic imagery in art authentication applications.

title: Design and Development of In-Situ Microwave Curing Technology for Construction 3D Printer
authors: Bakbergen Temirzakuly, Dias Nurgaliuly, Fadhlan Hafizhelmi Bin Kamaru Zaman, Essam Shehab, Md. Hazrat Ali
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: This study presents the design and development of a compact in-situ microwave curing module for a customized 3D printer. The device converges electromagnetic radiation at the nozzle to allow localized and volumetric heating of freshly extruded geopolymer materials. Microwave energy transmission is through dipolar polarization, ionic conductivity, and interfacial polarization mechanisms, and thus accelerates early-stage hydration and geopolymerization reactions. It is found that selective exposure enhances buildability, inter-layer bonding strength, and geometric stability by reducing slump and deformation during the printing process. Unlike conventional thermal curing, microwave-assisted curing minimizes losses by direct radiation-to-matter coupling, achieving greater energy efficiencies and life-cycle cost metrics. Uniform internal heating is used to reduce temperature gradients and shrinkage-induced cracking, whereas microstructural consolidation is integrated by adjusting thermal and non-thermal microwave action. Preliminary results show a promising outcome in enhancing mechanical strength in geopolymer samples. The findings project a future direction towards scalable, energy-efficient, and structurally superior 3D construction printing technology with low-carbon binder chemistries.

title: Sequential Rotation Feeding Networks for Circularly Polarized Patch Antenna Arrays in the ISM Band
authors: Meriem Drisi, Neila Kammoun, Ferid Harabi, Ali Gharsallah
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: The escalating demand for high-speed wireless communication, crucial for modern applications such as multimedia streaming and data transfer, necessitates the development of advanced antenna solutions. This article presents the design and characterization of an optimized printed circuit antenna array specifically tailored for WiFi and ISM band applications, aiming to address the inherent limitations of narrow bandwidth and modest gain typically associated with cost-effective, lightweight antenna designs. Through an optimized array configuration, we demonstrate enhanced performance metrics vital for future wireless systems.A compact 2×2 circularly polarized (CP) patch antenna array is detailed, which is not only suitable for general wireless communication but also for emerging applications like wireless power transfer in the ISM band. A key innovation lies in the implementation of a sequential rotation feeding network, which integrates a sequential phase shifter. This sophisticated feeding technique enables the design to achieve significantly improved circular polarization purity, wider impedance and axial ratio bandwidths, and an optimized reflection coefficient when compared to existing antenna array structures.

title: Synchrophasor-Based Monitoring System for Ramp Detection in Photovoltaic Plants
authors: Victoria Arenas-Ramos, Isabel Santiago, Rafael Real-Calvo, Miguel Gonzalez-Redondo, Victor Pallares-Lopez
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The large volume of renewable energies such as photovoltaic energy in the electricity grid presents a significant challenge in terms of management and optimization, especially when these new sources of generation are distributed throughout a wide area. To adequately control the grid and reduce the effects of short-term phenomena, monitoring has improved considerably in recent years. Equipment such as Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs), which transmit data every 10 ms or 100 ms, has become increasingly common. In this work, a monitorization system based in PMUs was developed and a strategy commonly utilized in transmission and distribution networks was adopted and applied within a photovoltaic plant, to investigate, among others, the abrupt fluctuations in power generation resulting from cloud transients. The methodology presented here enables the collection of data with significantly higher requirements for synchronism, temporal accuracy, and scalability than typically achieved. This enhanced data quality will support the development of algorithms dedicated to the control of power ramps.

title: The Impact of Digital Intelligence Technology Application on Online Consumption Behavior of Elderly Consumers in China
authors: Lebing Wang, Yihan Liu
abstract: With the rapid advancement of digital technologies and the growing demographic shift toward an ageing population, addressing the digital divide for older adults and promoting their integration into digital lifestyles have emerged as pressing societal challenges. This study empirically investigates the impact of digital smart technologies on the online consumption behavior of older adults by leveraging data from the China Household Finance Survey (CHFS). The findings indicate that the adoption of smart technologies significantly increases online spending among older adults, with particularly notable effects in lifestyle-related services. This impact demonstrates heterogeneity across subgroups, being more pronounced among male individuals, those residing in central and western regions, and rural populations. The research contributes novel empirical evidence to understanding elderly consumption patterns in the era of digital intelligence, offering valuable insights for advancing age-friendly digital policies and fostering the development of the silver economy.

title: UAV-to-Ground Communication Channel Characterization with Frequency Dependency in Built-Up Environments
authors: Irfan Khan, Mardeni Roslee, Siti Maisurah Mohd Hassan, Farman Ali, Yasir Ullah, Fardin Kabir
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The modeling of UAV-to-Ground (U2G) Communication channels is important for reliable connectivity in frequency-selective built-up environments. Existing models are limited by scattering structures, static UAV configurations, and the absence of frequency-dependent multi path characterization. This work introduces a channel characterization model that integrates geometric features of built-up environment with a data-driven framework. A Graph Neural Network (GNN) is trained to capture spatial-temporal dependencies in channel impulse responses, while the Grasshopper Optimization Algorithm (GOA) is employed to tune channel parameters including delay spread, coherence bandwidth, and angular dispersion. The proposed model achieves high accuracy in predicting frequency-selective fading patterns under UAV mobility across diverse urban layouts, outperforming traditional analytical and semi-empirical approaches.

title: Outsourcing Selection Optimization Through Neuro-Symbolic AI Approach: Learning Integration and Reasoning for Fair and Efficient Recruitment
authors: Dian Alamanda, Derry Destian, Synthia Atas Sari
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Outsourcing has become a dominant employment model in Jakarta; however, outsourcing recruitment often suffers from inefficiency, implicit bias, and a lack of transparency, raising concerns about fairness and compliance with labor regulations. Conventional machine learning (ML) approaches have been usually applied to candidate classification, yet their black-box nature limits interpretability and accountability. This study aims to address these shortcomings by proposing a neuro-symbolic artificial intelligence (NSAI) framework that integrates the predictive capacity of neural networks with the interpretability of symbolic reasoning. Drawing on theories of explainable AI and fairness in algorithmic decision-making, the research employs a quantitative and computational design. Primary recruitment datasets from Jakarta outsourcing firms were analyzed, and fairness-related HR policies were encoded into symbolic rules. The model’s performance was evaluated using predictive metrics (precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC) as well as fairness measures (demographic parity, equalized odds, and disparate impact ratio). The results show that NSAI outperforms conventional ML models by maintaining high predictive accuracy while significantly improving fairness. The originality of this research lies in applying NSAI to Indonesia’s outsourcing sector, demonstrating the dual capacity of NSAI to enhance both efficiency and ethical compliance. This provides a replicable framework for responsible AI the in HRTech sector.

title: Hydraulic Soft Gripper for Human-Safe Manipulation: Design, Modeling, and Validation
authors: Dong Anh Khoa To, Mai Thanh Thai
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Soft robotic grippers enable safe, adaptable manipulation of irregular and fragile objects, yet achieving precise force control and repeatable performance remains challenging. This paper presents a hydraulically actuated soft finger composed of a TPU kerf-patterned elastomer base and four soft actuators, each formed by a silicone tube laterally constrained by a linear spring. A continuum mechanics model based on incompressible Mooney-Rivlin theory is developed to relate internal pressure to actuator stretch, enabling controlled elongation via a linear slider. By tuning the relative length of opposing filaments, the finger achieves desired bending angles. The model demonstrates strong agreement with experimental results (average <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}=0.9912$</tex>) and is further validated through Finite Element Analysis (FEA). Finally, strategies for precise configuration control of the gripper and its potential applications are discussed.

title: Enhancing Robustness of Transformer Networks for Complete Road Geometry Detection under Adverse Conditions
authors: Chien-I Chang, Din-Yuen Chan, Jhing-Fa Wang, Chun-Hsiang Chen
abstract: LSTR (Lane Shape Prediction with Transformer Network) is a curved road shape detection model based on the Transformer architecture. It provides robust road shape detection capabilities and a sound context consistency structure. This study introduces RE-LSTR (Robustness-Enhanced LSTR), an improved variant designed for greater stability and reliability in autonomous driving. RE-LSTR addresses challenging conditions including low-light environments, severe occlusion, degraded markings, and heavy shadows. It integrates a Shuffle Attention mechanism to enhance inter-feature dependencies and adopts a more expressive nonlinear activation in the feed-forward network to improve feature learning. Additionally, a Semantic-Pattern Placement Data Augmentation (SPDA) strategy is proposed to increase training data diversity and generalization. Experiments confirm RELSTR outperforms LSTR across multiple metrics, particularly under adverse scenarios, highlighting its potential to enhance safety in intelligent transportation and autonomous driving.

title: Implementation of a 16:1 Multiplexer and 1:16 Demultiplexer on a Single Chip Using Sky130 PDK and Open-Source EDA Tools for Silicluster
authors: Uriel Jaramillo-Toral, Susana Ortega-Cisneros, Emilio Isaac Baungarten-Leon, Erick Jaramillo-Toral, Héctor Emmanuel Muñoz Zapata
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a 16-to-1 multiplexer (MUX) and a 1-to-16 demultiplexer (DEMUX) integrated onto a single chip using the SkyWater 130 nm process design kit and open-source electronic design automation (EDA) tools. These components are part of the Silicluster project, a scalable architecture aimed at integrating 256 independent projects, including digital, analog, and mixed-signal circuits, on a single silicon die. The design flow employs Xschem for schematic capture, Magic for layout, and Netgen for simulation and layout-versus-schematic (LVS) verification, providing costeffective, accessible, and flexible solutions for circuit design. The implemented circuits achieved a cut-off frequency of 398.1 MHz and a power consumption of 1.488 mW, demonstrating the correct functionality and efficiency of the proposed design. These results highlight the potential of open-source tools for creating functional and scalable circuits, contributing to a sustainable and modular approach to semiconductor development by reducing chip fabrication costs.

title: Adversarial Semantic and Label Perturbation Attack for Pedestrian Attribute Recognition
authors: Weizhe Kong, Xiao Wang, Ruichong Gao, Chenglong Li, Yu Zhang, Xing Yang, Yaowei Wang, Jin Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR) is an indispensable task in human-centered research and has made great progress in recent years with the development of deep neural networks. However, the potential vulnerability and anti-interference ability have still not been fully explored. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes the first adversarial attack and defense framework for pedestrian attribute recognition. Specifically, we exploit both global- and patch-level attacks on the pedestrian images, based on the pre-trained CLIP-based PAR framework. It first divides the input pedestrian image into non-overlapping patches and embeds them into feature embeddings using a projection layer. Meanwhile, the attribute set is expanded into sentences using prompts and embedded into attribute features using a pre-trained CLIP text encoder. A multi-modal Transformer is adopted to fuse the obtained vision and text tokens, and a feed-forward network is utilized for attribute recognition. Based on the aforementioned PAR framework, we adopt the adversarial semantic and label-perturbation to generate the adversarial noise, termed ASL-PAR. We also design a semantic offset defense strategy to suppress the influence of adversarial attacks. Extensive experiments conducted on both digital domains (i.e., PETA, PA100K, MSP60K, RAPv2) and physical domains fully validated the effectiveness of our proposed adversarial attack and defense strategies for the pedestrian attribute recognition. The source code of this paper will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/OpenPAR.

title: Semi-Negative Contrastive Subclass Discriminative Network for Compositional Zero-shot Learning
authors: Yang Liu, Xinshuo Wang, Xinbo Gao, Jungong Han, Ling Shao
abstract: The goal of compositional zero-shot learning (CZSL) is to train a model to recognize images containing known attribute-object pairs. This reduces the reliance on extensive training data and enables the model to identify unseen combinations. Current CZSL methods face several challenges, including multiple attributes for a single object, disconnected training and test sets, long-tailed distribution of visual categories, and substantial differences in state representation between different objects. These factors collectively impede the precise identification of new combinations. In response to these challenges, we propose a Semi-Negative Contrastive Subclass Discriminative Network (SN-CSDN) based on contrastive learning. Firstly, we propose a semi-negative sampling strategy that incorporates carefully selected negative samples into the training process. This approach enables the model to effectively distinguish between different classes while enhancing its ability to capture fine-grained subclass features. By improving the model’s sensitivity to inter-class differences and refining its recognition of subtle intra-class variations, this strategy significantly boosts overall discrimination performance. Additionally, we introduce a decoupled network branch designed to capture the intricate relationships between attributes and objects by generating more representative compositional embeddings. This branch leverages subclass information to ensure an accurate classification of synthesized embeddings while preserving the inherent visual distinctions of the original decoupled embeddings across different combinations. By improving feature representation capacity and mitigating sample imbalance, this design effectively improves model performance in long-tailed distributions. Our method has been comprehensively evaluated on three benchmark datasets, with results showing significant performance improvements that demonstrate the method’s effectiveness and reliability.

title: Analytical Model of Charge Transfer Potential Barrier Based on Doping Distribution in Pinned Photodiode CMOS Image Sensors
authors: Jinghua Ao, Quanmin Chen, Wanbin Zha, Chen Chen, Jiangtao Xu
abstract: In this paper, an analytical model of charge transfer potential barrier is proposed by analyzing doping distribution. The actual ion distribution in the doping process is considered to include Gaussian distribution and ion-channeling effect. Based on this, the doping concentration distribution in the pinned photodiode (PPD) is represented as functions of doping dose and energy. By solving the Poisson equation with the derived doping distribution, the electric field and potential distribution are calculated to determine the barrier height. The model has been validated by technology computer-aided design (TCAD) simulations and measurements, with the test devices fabricated in a 0.11 μm CMOS image sensor (CIS) process. The potential barrier estimated by our model can be used for the design, simulation, and optimization of PPDs in CMOS image sensors.

title: Lag-Llama-based Remaining Useful Life Prediction for Lithium-ion Batteries of Electric Vehicles with Auto-Correlation Analysis
authors: Muaaz Bin Kaleem, Heng Li, Zeyu Zhu, Xiaolong Chen, Yunsheng Fan, Lisen Yan, Weirong Liu
abstract: The accurate prediction of the remaining useful life of lithium-ion batteries is essential for applications like electric vehicles, but current methods often struggle to generalize across different battery types and operational conditions due to their reliance on large labeled datasets. This paper proposes a novel approach that integrates the Lag-Llama foundation model with auto-correlation analysis for remaining useful life prediction. Firstly, the tokenization scheme of Lag-Llama is enhanced using auto-correlation to identify degradation patterns in historical capacity sequences, improving the model’s ability to capture capacity fluctuations. The model is pre-trained on the large RWTH Aachen University dataset to learn degradation trends and fine-tuned using smaller, target-specific datasets, including the Center for Advanced Life Cycle Engineering (CALCE), National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), and Battery Analytics with Artificial Intelligence (BAWAII) datasets. Compared to the baselines, the proposed method reduces mean absolute errors by 43.2% on CALCE, 55.2% on NASA, 78.9% on MIT, and 91.1% on BAWAII. In terms of relative error, improvements of 48.7%, 54.9%, 80.2%, and 91.2% are achieved on the same datasets, respectively. These results demonstrate the model’s superior accuracy and generalizability for data-driven remaining useful life prediction of lithium-ion batteries, even with limited fine-tuning data.

title: Recurrent Neural Network Based on DNA Strand Displacement Circuits and Its Application in Location Prediction
authors: Yanfeng Wang, Pengpeng Zhao, Junwei Sun
abstract: With the rapid development of science and technology, various intelligent devices continuously generate large amounts of real-time location data. Efficiently utilizing this data for accurate location prediction has become a critical issue in fields such as intelligent transportation and smart logistics. To realize low-power location prediction, this paper constructs a molecular recurrent neural network (RNN) model based on DNA strand displacement (DSD) technology. The RNN model can process sequence data and accurately predict the next position. Firstly, multiple modules are designed based on DSD circuits, including dual-channel weighted summation module, dual-domain data module and Tanh activation function module. Secondly, a RNN model for processing sequence data is constructed using the above modules. Finally, the constructed RNN model successfully achieves position prediction for multiple inputs and a single output. The robustness and accuracy of the neural network are verified through data experiment. It has been demonstrated that DNA molecules can effectively process complex sequence data. This method holds significant potential in the field of path planning. This method holds significant potential in the field of path planning. This paper uses MAE and RMSE to evaluate the experimental data. The results prove that the RNN model constructed in this paper demonstrates strong accuracy and stability

title: Signal Compression for Wireless Communication and Sensing: A General Approach Utilizing Pretrained Wireless Foundation Models
authors: Liwen Jing, Tingting Yang, Han Zhang, Yuxuan Shi, Chi Zhang, Bowen Zhang
abstract: Artificial intelligence is expected to play a central role in enabling future 6 G networks. Developing foundation models that support a wide range of downstream tasks is critical for advancing 6 G standardization. This paper proposes a general framework for compressing wireless channel state information (CSI) using pretrained wireless foundation models. The foundation model is pre-trained using self-supervised learning with a masked reconstruction objective, achieving a normalized mean square error on the order of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{-3}$</tex-math></inline-formula> during pretraining. The model is evaluated on a range of wireless communication and sensing tasks, including classification tasks where compressed CSI is directly used for prediction, and regression tasks that require full CSI reconstruction. For regression tasks such as massive MIMO CSI feedback, the pre-compressed output from the foundation model is used as an auxiliary input to the downstream compressor, effectively enhancing the reconstruction quality. Compared to the Type-I codebook with comparable number of feedback bits, our method improves SGCS by 16.22% and reduces NMSE by 93.24%. Additionally, it achieves comparable SGCS performance to the Type-II codebook while using only 29% of the feedback bits. Comparison with existing research further confirms the contribution of the foundation model's compressed output in improving CSI compression performance. For classification tasks such as WiFi-based human activity recognition and human identification, the compressed representations produced by the foundation model can be directly utilized without additional fine-tuning. These representations achieve over 97% accuracy, outperforming conventional AI-based methods even under higher compression ratios. These findings demonstrate that leveraging a pretrained wireless foundation model consistently enhances performance across both classification and regression tasks, underscoring its versatility and potential in wireless CSI processing.

title: Target-Heading-Coordinate-Frame Formation Tracking Control for Fixed-Wing UAVs in 3D Space Under Asymmetric Constraints
authors: Lei Tian, Hao Chen, Haozhe Wu, Ming Chen, Xiangke Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper investigates the target-heading-coordinate-frame formation tracking control problem of fixed-wing UAVs in 3D space. Conventional fixed formation tracking typically requires that the formation configuration remains invariant in inertial space during the tracking process. However, in practical applications, it is often desirable for the formation configuration to adapt dynamically according to changes in the heading angle of leader, which can be seen as tracking target, leading to a more general formation configuration termed target-heading-coordinate-frame formation, which is more suitable for encirclement and interception missions. This paper employs 3D UAV motion models and proposes a robust controller based on sliding mode theory, which ensures resilience to disturbances while explicitly accounting for asymmetric constraints on linear and angular velocities. The convergence of the formation tracking is rigorously established via Lyapunov stability theory. Meanwhile, when the formation configuration simplifies to either conventional fixed formation tracking or trajectory tracking, the angular velocity information of the leader is not required, providing an advantage over existing approaches. Simulation results including numerical simulations hardware-in-the-loop (HiL) experiments validate the effectiveness and correctness of the proposed theoretical approach.

title: Differential Evolution-Based Event-Triggered Observer Design for Adaptive Control of Markov Jump Systems Via Sliding Mode Technique
authors: Wei Gao, Baoping Jiang, Xin Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper presents a differential evolution (DE)-optimized event-triggered sliding mode control framework for Markovian jump systems subject to nonlinear disturbances. A key innovation is the development of a DE-based optimization strategy for the dynamic event-triggering mechanism, where a novel hyperbolic-type objective function is formulated and optimized to achieve optimal bandwidth utilization. The control scheme integrates an observer-based sliding mode controller with an adaptive compensator designed to counteract unknown disturbances. Finite-time convergence to the sliding surface is guaranteed, while stochastic stability and H∞ performance are established through rigorous Lyapunov analysis. Practical simulations demonstrate that the DE-optimized approach significantly reduces communication frequency while maintaining control performance, outperforming conventional fixed-parameter and static event-triggering implementations. The results highlight DE’s effectiveness in solving complex event-triggered control optimization problems.

title: Synergistic Enhancement of Mechanical and DC Electrical Properties of mPE/IPC HVDC Cable Materials via Dual-Phase Grafting Modification
authors: Manzhi Shao, Jiaming Yang, Xu Yang, Chengcheng Zhang, Hong Zhao, Chunyang Li, Junqi Chen, Xuan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: To address the limitation of traditional elastomer blending methods that often reduce the dielectric strength in toughening polypropylene (PP) cable insulation materials, this study proposes a separately grafted and blended method by grafting maleic anhydride (MAH) onto impact polypropylene compound (IPC) and metallocene polyethylene (mPE). Unlike conventional elastomer-modified systems or single-phase MAH grafting approaches reported previously, the independent grafting of MAH onto each phase significantly enhances the non-bonding energy and interfacial interaction, promoting deep interpenetration of molecular chains. This effectively addresses the problem of poor mechanical properties in the blend system while maintaining low hardness and storage modulus. Simultaneously, MAH optimizes the two-phase distribution and introduces uniformly dense deep traps that act as charge capture sites. The isothermal surface potential decay (ISPD) and simulation results indicate that grafting MAH introduces deeper trap energy levels into the material. The mPE-M/IPC-M exhibits superior DC electrical performance compared, achieving a synergistic improvement in the mechanical and DC electrical properties of IPC. This study breaks through the bottleneck of mutual constraint between mechanical and electrical properties in traditional blending modification, providing important theoretical and technical support for the development of recyclable insulation materials for HVDC cables.

title: Overdispersion Parameter Estimation For Negative Binomial Optimization For Low-Photon Imaging
authors: Yu Lu, Roummel F. Marcia
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Low-photon imaging is widely applied in many fields such as medical imaging, astronomy, and fluorescence microscopy. Compared to Poisson noise, the negative binomial (NB) distribution provides a more flexible and accurate model for over-dispersed photon-limited data. While NB-based reconstruction and denoising methods have demonstrated improved performance across various scenarios, accurately estimating the over-dispersion parameter r remains a significant challenge. Traditional approaches, such as the method of moments and maximum quasi-likelihood estimation, can struggle with accuracy and robustness. To address this limitation, we propose a deep learning-based framework to predict the over-dispersion parameter r in a data-driven manner. Experiments show that our method is both accurate and generalizable, enabling more effective applications of NB models in real-world imaging tasks.

title: Searching Positive-Incentive Noise from Optimal Consensus in Continuous Action Iterated Dilemma
authors: Dengxiu Yu, Haojing Li, Litong Fan, Zhen Wang, Xuelong Li
abstract: In this paper, an analysis-definition-processing (ADP) framework is proposed to search positive-incentive noise in continuous action iterated dilemma (CAID). We analyze the influence of communication noise on the cooperative behavior of players in the system and introduce the concept of positive-incentive noise in CAID. We design a global cost function to ensure convergence of the system can be achieved and strive to improve the final level of cooperation. An optimal CAID control method is proposed to derive the deterministic optimal learning rate in analytical form, avoiding the variability and uncertainty brought about by neural network fitting or parameter adjustment. On this basis, the convergence of the dynamic model is further analyzed by using the Lyapunov function instead of the Jacobian matrix. Additionally, an adaptive filtering mechanism is designed to dynamically ensure that only positive-incentive noise affects the system, effectively reducing the impact of negative noise and enhancing system stability. The framework is validated through simulations involving triple classical game models, including the hawk-dove game, the stag hunt game, the chicken game on networks, and a straightforward illustrative example.

title: Free-Space Optical Communication With Optical Intelligent Reflecting Surfaces Under Phase Errors
authors: Emna Zedini
abstract: Free-space optical (FSO) communication systems enhanced with optical intelligent reflecting surfaces (OIRS) offer promising solutions to overcome line-of-sight limitations and mitigate atmospheric turbulence effects. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of OIRS-assisted FSO systems with phase errors utilizing both heterodyne and intensity modulation with direct detection (IM/DD) methods, which provides a more tractable alternative to existing methods. We first derive exact closed-form formulas for the mean and variance of the squared sum of products of independent, identically distributed Gamma-Gamma random variables, incorporating both pointing errors and IRS phase errors. Based on these results, we approximate the square envelope of the OIRS-assisted FSO channel with a Gamma distribution, with shape and scale terms determined from the derived mean and variance. This approximation allows us to obtain closed-form expressions for key performance metrics, including the average bit-error rate for different modulations, the outage probability, and the ergodic capacity, expressed using the Meijer’s G function. Our approach demonstrates excellent agreement with Monte Carlo simulations while offering superior computational efficiency compared to methods based on multivariate Fox’s H functions. Furthermore, we conduct asymptotic analysis at high signal-to-noise ratios and investigate the system’s diversity order for both heterodyne and IM/DD techniques.

title: Fractional Parameter-Varying Nonlinear Systems Identification
authors: Soumaya Marzougui, Saïda Bedoui, Kamel Abderrahim
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: This work addresses the combined estimation of the states and the parameters for nonlinear systems in a parameter-varying framework, particularly the fractional-order parameter-varying Hammerstein system. A novel Levenberg-Marquardt estimation approach based on auxiliary models is developed. The developed method combines fractional calculus theory with nonlinear optimization to ensure an accurate estimate of the model parameters. The simulation results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method of estimation.

title: Partitioned EEG Model for Emotion Recognition and Lightweight Deployment
authors: Zhanyi Ruan, Bowen Yang, Sicheng Zhou, Jiaheng Li
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Mental health disorders, especially depression, pose a growing challenge to global public health. However, early detection of depression remains difficult because current diagnostic approaches largely rely on subjective assessments. In this context, electroencephalogram (EEG) signals have attracted considerable attention, as they provide objective and quantifiable neural biomarkers that can capture subtle brain activity patterns associated with emotional states. Different emotions generated by the human brain can, to a large extent, be represented by the distinct characteristics of EEG signals. The aim of this study was to design neuronetworks to recognize brain emotions, in particular three types including positive, neutral and negative emotions, then applying it to the microcontroller (Rasberry Pi) after model optimization. A three partitioned model was constructed to capture the corresponding three types of emotions. A ONNX model was created with the use of dynamic quantization method to help run the model in a light devices as easy as possible. The accuracy of 99.65 % with only 0.83 ms inference latency tested on the microcontroller has shown model's great capability in lightweight devices deployment. This indicates that there is a possibility of achieving real-time emotion recognition in resource-constrained environments, making it possible for preventing the bad impact of negative feeling in a early time.

title: Single-Operator Cancer Vision Goggles for Quantitative Near-Infrared Fluorescence-Guided Oncologic Surgery
authors: Xiao Xu, Haini Zhang, Christopher Ta, Ian Zurutuza, Nicole Blasi, Krishna Sharmah Gautam, Cody Hongsermeier, Varun Trivedi, Jaden Jovan, Zohar Nussinov, Alexander Seidel, Fangchen Li, Mutian Shen, William Leu, Henry Hite, John Dunn, William Buras, Jinming Gao, Baran Sumer, Walter J. Akers, Samuel Achilefu
abstract: Objective: Near-infrared fluorescence (NIRF) imaging systems often require multiple operators and lack standardized acquisition constraints, limiting reproducibility across users and sites. We present a single-operator, wearable Cancer Vision Goggles (CVG) platform for hands-free NIRF guidance while preserving a radiometrically faithful reference stream for quantitative analysis. Methods: The head-mounted binocular CVG integrates synchronized visible/NIR cameras, real-time co-registration to an optical see-through display, green alignment lasers converging at a preset 50-cm distance to standardize geometry, and a posture-dependent laser safety interlock. A Bluetooth foot pedal and graphical user interface enable hands-free paired laser-on/laser-off snapshot capture. Performance was characterized using USAF targets and ICG Intralipid phantoms, and validated in vivo in a 4T1 murine tumor model using LS301-HSA and in the operating room by ex vivo imaging of head and neck cancer specimens from patients injected with Pegsitacianine. Results: At 50 cm, spatial resolution was 281 μm; the excitation field exhibited peak irradiance of 26.4 ± 2.2 mW/cm2 with 73.8 ± 3.2 mm FWMH. Phantom studies achieved signal-to-background ratio (SBR) >1 at 100 pM (raw Bayer) and 300 pM (Y-luminance), with linear behavior at low-to-moderate concentrations. Murine tumors and human specimens demonstrated consistent tumor-associated NIRF localization. Real-time dynamic thresholding enhanced tumor–background delineation and on-display reporting of fluorescence metrics for data-driven guidance. Conclusion: This CVG platform offers a wearable, single-operator NIRF imaging system that combines distance-enforced acquisition, integrated safety, a hands-free workflow, and dual-spectral imaging, preserving a radiometrically linear reference for quantitative analysis. Significance: Standardized acquisition supports reproducible fluorescence imaging and analyzable translational datasets.
