title: GALC: Guided Amplified Learning With Lipschitz Constraint for Robust Trajectory Generation
authors: Zhiliang Lin, Zhuangzhuang Chen, Guanming Zhu, Jiaxian Chen, Jianqiang Li
abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) demonstrated remarkable performance in learning valid policies by benefiting from high-quality offline datasets. However, collecting such a dataset is labor-intensive, especially for humanoid locomotion. For this reason, many data augmentation techniques have been proposed to improve the quality of offline datasets through noise injection or data synthesis. However, existing data augmentation methods are noise-sensitive, resulting in limited capability in complex robotic environments. To address these issues, we propose guided amplified learning with Lipschitz constraint (GALC), a novel trajectory augmentation method that employs the reward-amplification-guided conditional diffusion model for noise-insensitive data augmentation. Specifically, we introduce a local Lipschitz continuity constraint to regulate the reverse denoising process from the offline dataset. Consequently, the exploration of the diffusion model can be restricted within the local continuity region of the original dataset, thereby generating high-reward trajectories. Moreover, the generated trajectories are also enforced to be noise-insensitive to perturbations, thus enjoying robustness. Notably, our proposed method can prevent the generation of unsafe actions that do not align with the environment dynamics. Extensive experiments on sparse reward scenarios and high-dimensional robotic tasks show that our proposed GALC achieves significant improvements in both the augmented trajectories and policy performance.

title: Leads-Adaptive Fetal Electrocardiogram Extraction Using Attention-Based BiLSTM_supp1-3672909.xlsx
authors: Yi Zheng
abstract: Extracting the fetal electrocardiogram (fECG) from the abdominal electrocardiogram (AECG) will  help clinicians accurately discern fetal cardiac rhythm patterns. Nevertheless, the intricacies inherent in the clinical setting often precipitate signal anomalies within AECG recordings, thereby rendering traditional extraction methodologies suboptimal. This work proposes a deep learning-based fECG extraction method designed for the adaptive extraction of fECG signals from multi-lead AECG inputs. The proposed methodology is predicated upon a bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) architecture, augmented with a deep supervision subnetwork and an attention mechanism module. The attention module quantifies the inter-channel relevance of the input AECG through the computation of attention weights, facilitating subsequent feature fusion along the channel axis. This process effectively mitigates the impact of defective channels on the output. Comprehensive evaluations were conducted on publicly available datasets, encompassing scenarios with channel loss and benchmarked against established models. The results demonstrate the proposed model's efficacy in extracting fECG signals under diverse channel defect conditions. Ablation studies further validate the critical role of the attention module in enhancing the model's resilience to channel anomalies. Additionally, the reliability of the extracted fECG signals was corroborated through experiments involving input signal masking. The method proposed in this work is helpful for the clinical deployment of the fECG extraction in fetal cardiac rhythm assessment.

title: Wireless AI Evolution: From Statistical Learners to Electromagnetic-Guided Foundation Models
authors: Jian Xiao, Ji Wang, Kunrui Cao, Xingwang Li, Zhao Chen, Chau Yuen
abstract: While initial applications of artificial intelligence (AI) in wireless communications over the past decade have demonstrated considerable potential using specialized models for targeted communication tasks, the revolutionary demands of sixth-generation (6G) networks are propelling a necessary evolution towards AI-native wireless networks. In particular, the arrival of large AI models (LAMs) paves the way for the next phase of Wireless AI, where pre-training on universal electromagnetic (EM) principles equips wireless foundation models (WFMs) with the essential adaptability for a multitude of demanding 6G applications. However, existing LAMs face critical limitations, including pre-training strategies disconnected from EM-compliant constraints, a lack of structural adherence to wave propagation physics, and the inaccessibility of massive labeled datasets for comprehensive training. To address these challenges, this article presents an electromagnetic information theory-guided self-supervised pre-training (EIT-SPT) framework designed to systematically inject EM physics into WFMs. The EIT-SPT framework aims to infuse WFMs with intrinsic EM knowledge, thereby enhancing their physical consistency, generalization capabilities across varied EM landscapes, and overall data efficiency. Building upon the proposed EIT-SPT framework, this article first elaborates on potential applications of WFMs in 6G scenarios, then validates the efficacy of the proposed framework through illustrative case studies, and finally summarizes critical open research challenges and future directions for WFMs.

title: A Zero-overhead Flow for Security Closure
authors: Mohammad Eslami, Ashira Johara, Kyungbin Park, Samuel Pagliarini
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: In the traditional Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC) design flow, the concept of timing closure implies to reach convergence during physical synthesis such that, under a given area and power budget, the design works at the targeted frequency. However, security has been largely neglected when evaluating the Quality of Results (QoR) from physical synthesis. In general, commercial place & route tools do not understand security goals. In this work, we propose a modified ASIC design flow that is security-aware and, differently from prior research, does not degrade QoR for the sake of security improvement. Therefore, we propose a first-of-its-kind zero-overhead flow for security closure. Our flow is concerned with two distinct threat models: (i) insertion of Hardware Trojans (HTs) and (ii) physical probing/fault injection. Importantly, the flow is entirely executed within a commercial place & route engine and is scalable. In several metrics, our security-aware flow achieves the best-known results for the ISPD’22 set of benchmark circuits while incurring negligible design overheads due to security-related strategies. Finally, we open source the entire methodology (as a set of scripts) and also share the protected circuits (as design databases) for the benefit of the hardware security community.

title: AnalogCoder-Pro: Unifying Analog Circuit Generation and Optimization via Multi-modal LLMs
authors: Yao Lai, Souradip Poddar, Sungyoung Lee, Guojin Chen, Mengkang Hu, Bei Yu, Ping Luo, David Z. Pan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Despite recent advances, analog front-end design still relies heavily on expert intuition and iterative simulations, which limits the potential for automation. We present AnalogCoder-Pro, a multimodal large language model (LLM) framework that unifies the stages of circuit topology generation and device sizing optimization. The framework features a multimodal diagnosis-and-repair feedback loop that uses simulation error messages and waveform images to autonomously correct design errors. It also builds a reusable circuit tool library by archiving successful designs as modular subcircuits, accelerating the development of complex systems. Furthermore, it enables end-to-end automation by generating circuit topologies from target specifications, extracting key parameters, and applying Bayesian optimization for device sizing. On a curated benchmark suite covering 13 circuit types, AnalogCoder-Pro successfully designed 28 circuits and consistently outperformed existing LLM-based methods in figures of merit.

title: Reflectance Consistency between MERSI-II and MODIS in Northeast China under varying Conditions
authors: Rui Xie, Weimin Ju, Dongge Liu, Anxin Ding
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The FY-3D satellite, launched by the China Meteorological Administration, carries the second-generation Medium Resolution Spectral Imager (MERSI-II). With the channel configuration similar to that of the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS), MERSI-II is one of the important data sources for global medium-resolution multispectral observations. For better usage of MERSI-II data, this study analyzed the differences in top-of-atmosphere (TOA) reflectance and land surface reflectance (LSR) observed by these two sensors under different conditions of solar zenith angle (SZA), land cover types, and aerosol optical depth (AOD) through a cross-comparison of synchronous imageries. The results show that MERSI-II and MODIS TOA reflectance exhibit strong correlations across visible bands (R > 0.7, p 0.4, MERSI-II LSR-based NDVI becomes lower than that of MODIS, especially in densely vegetated regions. The findings provide an important reference for the product upgrade of MERSI-II data and the collaborative inversion of vegetation parameters using multi-source remote sensing data in the future.

title: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems Publication Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems

title: Cross-Modality Feature Fusion for Forward-Looking Sonar Image Segmentation in Complex Underwater Environments
authors: Hu Xu, Ju He, Changsong Pang, Yang Yu, Xudong Jiang
abstract: Forward-looking sonar (FLS) image segmentation enables underwater robotics autonomous exploration in marine engineering. However, the inherent characteristics of sonar systems such as weak semantic information, complex backgrounds, and high environmental noise, pose significant challenges and cause performance bottlenecks for traditional FLS image segmentation algorithms. Moreover, relying on single-modal features further exacerbates these limitations, restricting the segmentation accuracy and robustness. To address these limitations, this paper proposes FLSFusionNet which models the multi-modal relationships between sonar space and vision modality, capturing complementary features and enhancing segmentation performance through adaptive cross-modal interactions. Specifically, FLSFusionNet comprises four key modules: (1) sonar and camera backbones that extract distinct features from each modality; (2) a camera-sonar spatial transformer module designed to bridge the representation gap between sonar and visual features; (3) a cross-modal adaptive fusion module that maintain the semantic consistency between the dual features for effective integration; and (4) a fusion-based segmentation head that predicts the final segmentation results. Experiments conducted on the multi-modal benchmark dataset demonstrate that the proposed fusion approach significantly outperforms existing single-modal and multi-modal sonar image methods in segmentation accuracy, especially under complex underwater conditions. The results underscore the potential of sonar-vision fusion strategy to enhance the robustness and precision of FLS-based applications.

title: Scene Graph Knowledge Enhanced Hashing with Contrastive Learning for Image-Text Retrieval
authors: Hui Meng, Huaxiang Zhang, Li Liu, Lei Zhu, Xiaojun Chang, Xinru Guo
abstract: Cross-modal hashing retrieval has emerged as a prominent research focus due to its advantages of high efficiency and low storage requirements. Most of the existing works predominantly focus on aligning objects or entities across different modalities, often ignoring the critical semantic relationships between them. However, a thorough understanding of these semantic relationships is pivotal for achieving fine grained cross-modal alignment. To address this issue, we pro pose a novel method called Scene Graph Knowledge Enhanced Hashing (SGKEH) with contrastive learning for cross-modal retrieval. Specifically, SGKEH orchestrates textual scene graphs to generate semantic hard negatives through entity swap and antonymic predicate replacement strategies, thereby simulating structurally plausible yet semantically divergent text variants. These hard negative samples are then used to train a fine tuned CLIP encoder, enhancing its ability to discriminate subtle relational semantics through contrastive learning. Moreover, a Multi-level Attention Fusion (MAF) module is proposed to bridge structural scene graph knowledge with global CLIP features via cross-modal attention, enabling explicit modeling of relation aware semantics. It ultimately facilitates cross-modal semantic alignment through the implementation of semantic consistency constraints. Extensive experiments conducted on the Flickr30K and MS-COCO datasets demonstrate that SGKEH achieves excellent retrieval performance, which verifies the effectiveness and superiority of our method.

title: PhET Interactive Simulations Significantly Improve Student Learning Outcomes in Physics Education
authors: Jarot S. Suroso, Hadi Suprayitno, Nur Hadisukmana, Thomas Thomas
abstract: This quasi-experimental study investigates the effectiveness of PhET Interactive Simulations in improving eighth-grade students' understanding of electric circuits at SMP Gracia, Jakarta. Sixty students were assigned to experimental (PhET-based, n=30) or control (conventional instruction, n=30) groups. Results showed the experimental group significantly outperformed the control group (M=76.3, SD=8.2 vs. M=64.3, SD=9.1; t=5.42, p<0.001), with medium N-gain (0.62) indicating substantial learning improvement. PhET simulations effectively transformed abstract concepts into interactive, explorable phenomena, significantly enhancing conceptual understanding and student engagement. These findings provide empirical support for integrating interactive simulations into physics education, demonstrating their superiority over conventional lecture-based methods for teaching abstract concepts.

title: Towards Modelling Military Disadvantage in Military Operations
authors: Clara Maathuis
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: This article presents the development of a computational ontology model for military disadvantage built using a Design Science Research (DSR) methodological approach to support the definition, representation, and automated reasoning when conducting military operations. Drawing on domain analysis, field expertise, and operational case studies, the model is constructed to capture the multi-faceted dimensions of military disadvantage such as space, force, and temporal factors as well as the implications experienced by the target itself as well as allies, friendly, and neutral actors in operational settings. Furthermore, to reflect the effectiveness of proposed model, a demonstration is conducted on a use case designed on an AIbased target by exploiting existing security vulnerabilities. To this end, this research contributes to existing efforts that are dedicated to building intelligent and adaptive AI-driven decision-making support and to building responsible and trustworthy military AI systems.

title: LPGE:Learning Private Graph Embedding by Extracting Sensitive Information
authors: Shaobo Du
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Learning graph embeddings simplifies graph structure representation, enhancing the efficiency of graph data analysis and providing powerful feature representations for machine learning tasks. It compresses graph data into a low-dimensional space, reducing computational complexity and storage costs, benefiting tasks like node classification, link prediction, and clustering. However, graph embeddings are vulnerable to attribute inference attacks, where sensitive node attributes can be inferred from embeddings. Privacy-preserving methods, such as adversarial learning, differential privacy, and mutual information, have emerged to address this issue. Existing methods often overlook the impact of irrelevant attributes on privacy leakage during training, despite the high-dimensional, sparse nature of graph data. This paper proposes a method for Learning Privacy Graph Embeddings (LPGE) to extract sensitive information. Our approach consists of three modules: (i) an encoding module that learns privacy node embeddings without relying on edge structure, reducing privacy leakage risks; (ii) a privacy extraction module with two decoders to extract sensitive information, optimize sensitive attributes, and preserve non-sensitive attributes; and (iii) a classification module that performs node classification without using edge structure information. Experimental results on four real-world datasets demonstrate that LPGE outperforms existing methods in terms of both utility and privacy preservation.

title: Real-Time Implementation of a Deep Learning-Based System for Road Sign Detection and Classification on Edge Devices
authors: Yasmine Fekih Hassen, Mehdi Hermassi, Mohamed Ali Hajjaji
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Road accidents continue to be a leading cause of mortality worldwide, highlighting the urgent need for advanced technologies to enhance road safety. This paper presents a real-time traffic sign detection and classification system implemented on a Raspberry Pi, utilizing the YOLOv5 deep learning model. Designed for deployment on low-power edge devices, the system achieves high performance and efficiency, making it suitable for integration into Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). The Jordanian Arabic Traffic Sign dataset was used for training, providing a highly relevant and representative set of images that closely match road conditions and signage in North African and Middle Eastern countries, particularly Tunisia. Experimental results show that the trained YOLOv5s6 model attains a precision of 97.8%, a recall of 96.1%, a mAP@0.5 of 98.2%, and a mAP@0.5:0.95 of 76.5%, demonstrating its effectiveness in real-time traffic sign recognition for intelligent transportation applications.

title: A Near-Infrared Face Liveness Detection Algorithm Fusing Multi-Task Learning and Dual-Scale Models
authors: Xianwei Zhang, Ziru Bao, Jierui Kang
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: A proposed near-infrared face liveness detection method fuses dual-scale model integration and multi-auxiliary task learning. This addresses the vulnerability of traditional face recognition systems to presentation attacks, such as photos and videos, in specific environments, ensuring the system’s normal operation. The method expands the sample library for liveness detection by combining existing datasets with self-collected data. This increases the number of near-infrared images with different lighting conditions and angles to improve the model’s generalization ability. The process begins by using a face detector to locate the target area, employing a dual-scale cropping strategy to simultaneously extract local and global features. Following this, an improved MiniFASNetV2 network is constructed by integrating multi-task learning, which includes liveness classification, reflectance reconstruction, and LBP reconstruction. Finally, a dual-model fusion strategy synthesizes the prediction results from different scales to enhance robustness. Experimental results indicate that fusing models of different scales can achieve higher accuracy. The improved MiniFASNetV2 network ensures the algorithm’s effectiveness and practicality in complex attack scenarios.

title: Conceptualization and Validation of Parent-Child Tourism Motivations in School-Age Children Families: From Education to Nurturance
authors: Jiang Bing-Rui
abstract: This study investigates the motivations for parent-child tourism in school-age children families. Through exploratory factor analysis, a three-dimensional motivational structure was constructed, comprising "Child Nurturing" "Family Relationships" and "Relaxation". The findings identify "Child Nurturance" as the core motivation, whose conceptual scope extends beyond traditional knowledge acquisition to encompass multidimensional goals, including cognitive, physical, psychological, and social development. Concurrently, the "Family Relationships" highlights a significant function of emotional compensation. The high consistency between this motivational structure and the results of multiple-choice questions demonstrates that tourism is primarily perceived by these families as a domestic practice aimed at promoting the comprehensive development of their children. This study provides a clear theoretical framework to inform subsequent research and market-oriented practices.

title: LiteFL: A Lightweight Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning Framework for Non-IID Distributed Wearable IoT Devices
authors: Fan Yang, Billy Chiu, Jing-Yu Ji, Wenhui Yu, Jeff Tang, Zusheng Tan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The rapid adoption of Wearable Internet of Things (WIoT) devices in smart cities has enabled a wide spectrum of applications. Although Federated Learning (FL) was once considered the silver bullet for privacy-preserving systems, it falls short in WIoT settings. This is primarily because deep learning (DL) models typically require extensive computation, which exceeds the limited processing capabilities of resource-constrained WIoT devices. Additionally, the non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) nature of WIoT data hinders model convergence and generalization. To this end, we propose LiteFL, a lightweight privacy-preserving FL framework tailored for non-IID WIoT environments. LiteFL partitions the model into extractor, processor, and classifier modules, offloading the computation-intensive processor to the server while retaining lightweight components on clients. It integrates differential privacy (DP) for data perturbation and fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) for the proposed secure aggregation mechanism. Experiments on a real-world WIoT dataset demonstrate LiteFL achieves an AUC of 0.939 and F1-score of 0.845, outperforming baselines by over 5%. It also mitigates the cold-start problem in real-world applications by enabling knowledge transfer across heterogeneous distributions, advancing scalable, secure FL for resource-constrained WIoT systems.

title: A Modified U-Net Based Approach for Human Contour Extraction and its Hardware Realization
authors: K. Suryanarayana, N. Ramsai, T. Murali Eshwar, K. Hruthik, K. Sridharan
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: We present an approach for accurate extraction of human contours from an image in this paper. This is motivated by applications in computer graphics, medical diagnosis and robotics. The U-net architecture designed for image segmentation is studied. A variation of the architecture is developed which is lightweight and adequate for contour extraction amidst a non-empty background. Ablation studies are performed to establish the efficacy of the proposed solution. The architecture proposed is also studied for hardware realization. Preliminary results based on Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) are reported.

title: Optimization of Ciliary Magnetic-Driven Soft Robot Based on Gradient Magnetization
authors: Yaoyuan Xu, Jingzhi Jia, Hao Zhang, Hui Xie
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Inspired by ciliate microorganisms such as Paramecium, this study designs and optimizes a cilia-driven magnetic soft robot based on gradient magnetization. The robot is primarily composed of a non-magnetic body and magnetic ciliary legs embedded with hard magnetic particles, aiming to achieve efficient and controllable locomotion. Finite element simulation was employed to systematically analyze the influence of external magnetic field parameters and the connection method of the hinge-like structure on the robot's leg deformation and locomotion efficiency. Through experimental optimization of the driving magnetic field parameters, the optimal driving conditions were determined to be <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7 \text{mT}, 20 \text{Hz}$</tex>, and a duty cycle of 0.6, under which the robot's locomotion speed increased by approximately 40%. Furthermore, trajectory planning experiments verified that the robot can achieve stable and controllable movement at the air-liquid interface under these optimized conditions, with a mean trajectory tracking error of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$429.8 \pm 289.2 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex>. This demonstrates the effectiveness of the structural optimization and magnetic field control methods.

title: An AI-driven Framework for Screening and Severity Stratification of Obstructive Sleep Apnea from Non-Respiratory Features
authors: Sujie Wang, Linze Qian, Peifeng Wang, Yu Sun
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: To address the discomfort associated with respiratory sensors in polysomnography (PSG), which hinders the diagnosis of obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), this study aimed to develop and validate machine learning models for OSA screening using only non-respiratory features. We conducted a retrospective analysis of 6,999 PSG reports, utilizing twentyfive features encompassing demographics, sleep architecture, and EEG-derived arousal metrics to train four models (SVM, XGBoost, TabNet, and TabKANet). The models were evaluated across two clinically relevant scenarios: general screening (apneahypopnea index, AHI $\geq 5$) and the identification of moderate-tosevere cases (AHI $\geq 15$). The results demonstrated strong predictive capabilities, with TabKANet and XGBoost performing best. Notably, performance was significantly higher for the moderate-to-severe screening task (highest AUC $=0.9467$) compared to the general screening task (highest AUC $=0.8762$). Feature importance analysis revealed that for moderate-to-severe OSA, the model’s decision was overwhelmingly dominated by EEGderived arousal metrics and BMI, whereas for general screening, feature importance was more distributed, with factors like age and gender playing a larger role. In conclusion, machine learning models leveraging non-respiratory features can effectively screen for OSA and are particularly powerful in identifying patients with moderate-to-severe disease. These findings support the development of simplified, more comfortable screening tools that can enable a tiered diagnostic strategy for OSA management.

title: Trojan Attacks on Graph Convolution Neural Networks for Circuit Analysis
authors: Rupesh Raj Karn, Ozgur Sinanoglu
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: Graph convolutional neural networks (GCNNs) have become a powerful tool for circuit analysis in VLSI design. This work systematically studies Trojan attacks on neural networks, first demonstrating their impact on a simple CNN trained on MNIST to illustrate trigger insertion and misclassification. We then extend this approach to GCNNs for node classification in circuit netlists (ISCAS’85, EPFL), introducing node features and graph connectivity triggers to force misclassification to a target class. Experiments show that while Trojanized models maintain high accuracy on clean data, they reliably misclassify triggered samples, highlighting the need for robust architectures in circuit analysis. The attacks exhibit high success rates and clean-data fidelity across diverse trigger types and gate classes, revealing structural vulnerabilities in GCNN-based EDA pipelines.

title: Management Strategies in Media Communication for Happiness: Exploring the Effects of Audience Interaction, Message Framing, Emotional Conditions, and Emoji Visuals
authors: Rudi Prasetyo Ardi, Krisnawati NugraheniSetyaningrum
abstract: Emojis have become integral to digital communication, yet the factors driving their usage remain underexplored. This study investigates how audience interaction, message delivery, and emotional states influence visual emoji use among social media users. Using a quantitative approach, data were collected via online questionnaires from 70 young adults (ages 18-30) in Indonesia. Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) was employed to test the hypothesized relationships. Results reveal that emotional states significantly predict visual emoji use (α = 0.586, p < 0.001), explaining 43.4% of the variance. However, audience interaction (α = 0.068, p = 0.599) and message delivery (α = 0.121, p = 0.340) showed no significant effects. These findings suggest that emotional expression is the primary driver of emoji adoption in digital contexts, with implications for social media marketing, digital communication design, and human-computer interaction. Future research should explore additional factors accounting for the remaining 56.6% of variance and examine cross-cultural differences in emoji usage patterns.

title: A Study on the Coupling Coordination Degree of Cultural and Tourism Integration in Jiaxing
authors: Yue Ning
abstract: The tourism and cultural industries are intrinsically linked and mutually reinforcing, exhibiting a natural synergy. This study quantifies the degree of cultural-tourism integration in Jiaxing City from 2011 to 2021. Findings indicate that, in most instances, the tourism industry in Jiaxing has demonstrated superior development levels and growth rates compared to the cultural industry, with the latter predominantly exhibiting a lagging developmental pattern. Nevertheless, the degree of coupling between the two sectors has been steadily increasing.

title: Adaptive Cluster-Based Rate Control for Opportunistic Routing in Wireless Mesh Networks
authors: Christopher Lehmann, Jennifer Schwardt, Jennifer Gabriel, Frank H.P. Fitzek
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Wireless Mesh Networks (WMNs) require efficient rate selection to exploit variable channel conditions, yet traditional approaches often use fixed or heuristic-based rates that underutilize available capacity. This paper introduces a dynamic Rate Control (RC) algorithm for Opportunistic Routing (OR) that leverages cluster-based rate probing to reduce overhead and improve adaptation. The proposed method replaces a fixed rate set with dynamically selected cluster-heads, each representing a group of transmission rates with similar Packet Loss Ratio (PLR) and Airtime (AT) characteristics. An Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) mechanism updates the PLR, while two AT estimation methods, namely the Expected Transmission Time (ETT) and the Minimum Acceptable Airtime (MAAT), capture both direct transmission costs and retransmission overhead. The experiments show that our approach maintains a PLR below 10% and reduces channel occupancy by over 6% compared to a fixed base rate. The AT−PLR cluster-head selection achieving up to 10% higher reliability and approximately 16% better efficiency compared to empirical selected rate set.

title: An Edge-Cloud Collaborative Anomaly Detection Method for Rotating Machinery Based on Perceptual Vibration Hashing and Autoencoder
authors: Shiwei Yan, Fengli Zhang, Jinjiang Wang, Tiantian Liu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Condition monitoring of machinery health is essential for ensuring operational safety and maintaining production efficiency. To address the challenge of delayed anomaly detection caused by high data throughput during realtime, high-frequency vibration monitoring, this paper proposes an edge-cloud collaborative anomaly detection method based on perceptual vibration hashing and autoencoder. On the edge side, a perceptual vibration hashing algorithm is employed to compress raw vibration signals into fixed-length machine condition hashes, thereby enabling compact feature representation. On the cloud side, an autoencoder is utilized to compute the reconstruction error of the machine condition hashes, which is then processed using a Kalman filter to construct a health indicator for degradation assessment. Furthermore, a residual contribution analysis is introduced to identify the parameters responsible for detected anomalies. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through experiments on a benchmark bearing dataset, demonstrating its capability for timely and accurate anomaly detection in high-throughput monitoring scenarios.

title: Optimization of Temporal Convolutional Networks Using Harris Hawks Algorithm for Efficient Embedded Speech Recognition
authors: Mnassri Aymen, Nasri Sihem, Mansouri Nouha, Cherif Adnane
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Recent advances in embedded speech recognition require models that balance accuracy, efficiency, and low resource consumption. This paper presents a novel approach using Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN) optimized by Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) to enhance speech recognition performance on Raspberry Pi devices. The HHO algorithm dynamically tunes key hyperparameters of the TCN models such as convolutional filter sizes, dilation rates, and learning rates—in order to achieve high accuracy while reducing computational overhead. Experiments on benchmark speech datasets demonstrate that the proposed HHO-optimized TCN achieves higher recognition accuracy and faster inference compared to baseline models (e.g., standard LSTM, GRU). The work illustrates the potential of combining bio-inspired optimization with efficient convolutional sequence models to enable real-time speech recognition on resource-constrained edge devices.

title: A Unified Framework for Absolute and Relative Pose Estimation of Spherical Cameras
authors: Liliana Lo Presti, Giuseppe Mazzola, Marco La Cascia
abstract: Spherical cameras are polydiopter cameras that use multiple lenses with overlapping field of view to capture the scene. A spherical view of the scene is reconstructed through stitching algorithms after correcting the distortion introduced by the lenses. These types of cameras are becoming very popular nowadays and are of great interest in several fields ranging from production and distribution of content to virtual reality and autonomous robotics. This paper aims to provide a simple and complete understanding of the spherical camera model by decoupling the model and the equirectangular projection to gain insight into the geometry underlying spherical images. The article assumes that a spherical image has been captured and, delving into the most relevant geometric aspects, proposes algorithms to estimate the pose of a single camera (extrinsic parameters) and the relative position of pairs of cameras (epipolar geometry) without a priori knowledge of the characteristics of the specific device (i.e. optics) used to acquire the image. The article demonstrates that both proposed algorithms can be derived from a common mathematical framework, and the experimental results show that the method for estimating the relative camera pose outperforms the state-of-the-art 8-point algorithm.

title: Adaptive Interference Mitigation in FMCW Radars using 2D AR
authors: Majid Joshani, Bruna G. Palm, Mattias Dahl, Mats I. Pettersson
abstract: In this paper, a two-dimensional (2D) autoregressive (AR) model is employed as a mitigation algorithm for interference in the Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radars. The AR model, due to its simple structure, can perform super efficiently in multi-dimensional estimation problems and can be a suitable replacement for complex Neural Network (NN) based algorithms. This study addresses the advanced requirements of the 2DAR algorithm for mitigating interference in actual frames collected from real-world experiments. Three approaches to interference generation were incorporated to reproduce and study the most common and likely circumstances of mutual interference. A dynamic sampling direction selection framework is developed to tackle unpredictable shapes of interfered segments in a frame. An iterative signal reconstruction algorithm is proposed to reconstruct the damaged areas using clean samples. Finally, the parallelizable processes were vectorized to make them implementable in the real world. The 2DAR mitigator’s performance was assessed using a diverse dataset of frames collected from real-world experiments, each containing unique target, noise, and interference attributes. The derived mitigator improved the signal in all experimental cases, down to the noise floor, and increased the Signal to Interference plus Noise Ratio (SINR) to almost 15 dB. Finally, the performance of different order models was compared in an identical hardware and software environment to provide a scaled indicator of the computation escalation in different model orders.

title: From Radar Cardiography to Electrocardiograms: Conditional Diffusion Model Enabled Contactless ECG Monitoring Using mmWave Radar
authors: Hanwen Zhang, Peichun Li, Liping Qian, Zhiguo Shi, Yuan Wu
abstract: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is one of the foremost causes of mortality globally, and cardiac arrhythmias constitute a major contributing factor. Continuous monitoring of cardiac signals plays a vital role for early detection and prevention. However, traditional electrocardiogram (ECG) devices require skin contact, which can be uncomfortable and inconvenient for long-term usage. In contrast, contactless cardiac health monitoring technologies, such as Wi-Fi and millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar, present a promising alternative. Millimete-rwave radar provides high range resolution, strong immunity to ambient light, and high sensitivity to small vibrations, making it ideal for contactless monitoring. However, mmWave radar primarily captures cardiac mechanical vibrations, known as radar cardiography (RCG) signals, which differ from the electrical activity recorded by clinical ECGs. To bridge this gap, we propose a contactless framework that uses mmWave radar and a conditional diffusion model to reconstruct ECG signals and then utilizes a deep learning model to classify arrhythmias. Specifically, mmWave radar captures RCG signals associated with cardiac activities. Leveraging the nonlinear relationship between cardiac mechanics and electrical activities, we design a Residual Network (ResNet)-based conditional diffusion model to convert these RCG signals into ECG signals. Finally, we develop a CNN-BiLSTM-SE network for arrhythmia classification. Experimental findings demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed approach for signal conversion as well as arrhythmia classification, offering a promising pathway toward contactless cardiac health monitoring.

title: SoK: Software Defined Networks Security with Machine Learning
authors: Alperen Örsdemi;r, Utku Tefek, Ertem Esiner, Alptekİn Küpçü
abstract: Software-Defined Networks (SDN) have revolutionized modern networking by enabling flexible, programmable management of network resources. This flexibility facilitates the effective design and deployment of Machine Learning (ML)-based defense mechanisms, including Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) and anomaly detection. However, the validity of existing SDN-based threat detection solutions for systems that use SDN utilities remains unresolved. This work presents a Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) that synthesizes the literature on ML-based SDN security. The study aims to: (i) analyze and strengthen the validity of reported success in SDN security with ML by reviewing 50 recent high-ranking papers, using a taxonomy-driven analysis that categorizes evaluation metrics and the use of ML models, datasets, controllers, and SDN frameworks; (ii) critically assess the state of the literature by comparing these findings with primary surveys and questioning reported accuracy rates; and (iii) identify future perspectives and key takeaways for security framework deployment, to propose solutions to address validation challenges, and to outline a hybrid model. The outlined hybrid model combines passive DL-based traffic monitoring with triggered active mitigation, mapping datasets, ML model families, and programmable enforcement mechanisms into a layered SDN defense to improve validity, efficiency, and real-world deployability.

title: A Dynamic Gradient Modulated Barrier Neural Network for Collision Avoidance of Mobile Dual-Manipulator Humanoid Robot
authors: Zhijun Zhang, Chenke Huang, Zhongwen Cao
publish: IEEE Systems, Man, and Cybernetics Magazine
abstract: In order to generate collision-free motion of two redundant manipulators for mobile humanoid robots under complex constraints, a novel dynamic gradient modulated barrier neural network (DGM-BNN) is proposed to track end-effector path accurately and prevent potential collisions. For example, the mutual collisions between two manipulators and the collisions between manipulators and obstacles. The kinematic model of the mobile dual-manipulator humanoid robot is first established, and the trajectory tracking problem is formulated as a timevarying quadratic programming (TVQP) problem. Then, collision avoidance constraints are incorporated into the TVQP problem as inequality constraints through a shortest-distance-based angular velocity control strategy. The proposed Karush-Kuhn- Tucker (KKT) conditions with dynamic gradient modulation can overcome the possibility of convergence failure of the traditional KKT method in solving the optimal solution. Subsequently, A DGM-BNN solver model is designed based on neural dynamics formula. The excellent performance of the DGM-BNN scheme for reducing the position error and improving the collision avoidance capability is verified by simulation experiments. The results show that the mobile dual-manipulator humanoid robot can achieve higher-accuracy trajectory planning and collision avoidance than other methods, even under complex constraints.

title: Blockchain-Mediated Forensic Control Plane for Secure Isolation and Investigation of Malware-Infected Devices
authors: Sultan Asiri
abstract: When devices are isolated after a suspected compromise, investigators can no longer perform secure remote forensics. Current approaches lack immutable logging, multi-party authorization, and cryptographic verification, making it challenging to maintain evidence integrity and chain of custody during incident response. We present BMFCP (Blockchain-Mediated Forensic Communication Protocol), a system for applying secure forensic investigations on isolated devices using blockchain coordination. BMFCP uses smart contracts to manage device isolation states and enforce multi-party authorization, requiring approval from multiple organizations before executing sensitive forensic actions. Agents submit cryptographically signed execution proofs, and all evidence is verified through hashes recorded on the blockchain. Every action is logged with timestamps and digital signatures, creating a complete chain of custody. In this paper, we built a prototype on Hyperledger Fabric.We tested our method with 2 and 3 organizations. Results show transaction latencies of 1-3 seconds and throughput around 4 transactions per second, which is sufficient for forensic work. Our security analysis shows that BMFCP prevents evidence tampering, enforces multi-party oversight, and creates audit trails.

title: Layout-Based Design Approach for Sub-6 GHz RF-SOI LNAs with Enhanced Matching and Noise Performance
authors: Min-Su Kim, Il-jun Kim, Sang-Sun Yoo
abstract: This work presents a design methodology for improving the input matching and noise performance of low-noise amplifiers (LNAs) through transistor layout optimization. As transistor scaling is inevitable for low-power and high-frequency operation, layout-dependent parasitic components limit the achievement of optimum impedance matching and low-noise performance. Among these, the gate-to-source capacitance (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gs</sub>) and gate resistance (R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">g</sub>) are critical design parameters that directly affect input impedance and noise characteristics, respectively. The proposed layout-based technique selectively controls layout-induced parasitic capacitances to maintain sufficient C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gs</sub> for better input matching while reducing metal-related R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">g</sub> to improve noise performance. As a result, both input matching and noise characteristics are enhanced without relying on excessive source inductance or large transistor size. Implemented in a 0.13-μm RF-SOI process, the fabricated LNA achieves a gain of 19.5–18.8 dB over 5–6 GHz, a minimum noise figure (NF) of 2.53–2.65 dB, and an input return loss (S11) better than 10 dB, while consuming 13.5 mA from a 1.2-V supply. These results validate that layout-based optimization effectively enhances RF performance in scaled RF-SOI technologies.

title: Online Convex Optimization-Based Resource Allocation in Dynamic Edge Computing-Enabled Networks with Time-Varying Cost and Constraints
authors: Yuxia Cheng, Chengchao Liang, Qianbin Chen, F. Richard Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Efficient resource allocation in dynamic edge computing-enabled networks is crucial for cost reduction and enhancing user satisfaction. Such networks comprise diverse resources, with both their parameters and system models being inherently uncertain. Despite the inherent uncertainty in dynamic edge computing-enabled networks, comprehensive online solutions remain essential for coordinating wireless, wired, and computing resources. This study considers time-varying cost and constraints under uncertain cost function, optimizing AP selection, computing node association, computing resource allocation, and bandwidth allocation to minimize long-term cost. To tackle the complexity of decision-making under uncertainty, the proposed problem is reformulated as an online convex optimization (OCO) problem with bandit feedback. The modified online saddle point algorithm is employed to iteratively approximate the global optimal state of the system at each time step. To perform the projection in the modified online saddle point algorithm, each variable update is formulated as a quadratic programming problem, which can be efficiently solved using convex optimization techniques. We evaluate the algorithm's performance using dynamic regret and accumulative constraint violation metrics, demonstrating its potential to achieve sub linear results in both if the drift remains sub-linear. Simulation results confirm efficacy and superiority of the proposed scheme.

title: A Fault-Tolerant Strategy for Parallel Inverters in Grid-Connected Power Conversion System
authors: Xiaobiao Wang, Junwei Liu, Huafeng Xiao, Yi Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Renewable energy power generation has been extensively developed. Fault-tolerant operation is required for power conversion system to maximize power delivery and system availability. This paper presents a fault-tolerant operation strategy for parallel inverters in grid-connected power conversion system that enables continued operation under multiple power switch failures without additional redundant hardware. Two distinct reconfiguration modes based on fault distribution are presented. When the faulty switches within a phase are located within a single inverter unit, the entire affected bridge leg is deactivated while healthy phases maintain normal operation. When the faulty switches within a phase are distributed across different inverter units, only the half-legs containing faults are deactivated, preserving the remaining healthy switches for system reconfiguration. The healthy half-legs and the inductors operate in every half-cycle of grid in faulty phase. The parallel architecture inherent redundancy is fully exploited to achieve fault tolerance under various faulty scenarios. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed strategy is verified by simulations and experiments.

title: LAURA: LLM-Assisted UAV Routing for AoI Minimization
authors: Bisheng Wei, Ruichen Zhang, Ruihong Jiang, Mugen Peng, Dusit Niyato
abstract: With the rapid growth of the low-altitude economy, demand for real-time data collection via UAV-assisted wireless sensor networks (WSNs) is increasing. This paper studies the problem of minimizing the age of information (AoI) in UAV-assisted WSNs by optimizing the UAV flight routing. We formulate the AoI minimization task and propose a large language model (LLM)-assisted UAV routing algorithm (LAURA). LAURA employs an LLM as the intelligent crossover operators within an evolutionary optimization framework to efficiently explore the solution space. Simulation results show that LAURA outperforms benchmark methods in reducing the maximum AoI, especially in scenarios with many sensor nodes.

title: Research on Vibration Characteristics of Amorphous Alloy Three-Dimensional Wound Core Transformer Considering Insulating Oil
authors: Zhanyang Yu, Mengyu Zhan, Bing Xue, Yan Li, Yongteng Jing, Junqing Wang, Chengxiang Liu, Hao Li, Pengzhe Zhuang
abstract: amorphous alloy transformer has energy-saving advantages, but its magnetostrictive effect is higher than that of silicon steel transformer, causing severe vibration and noise, which makes it very easy for amorphous alloy debris to be produced, thereby leading to insulation faults. Additionally, transformer is important power equipment for power delivery. Therefore, in order to reflect the vibration information of the core under different external environments, an analysis model for the transmission of core vibration to the tank is established, to analyze the vibration characteristics of the tank of the amorphous alloy 3D wound core transformer under both insulating oil and non-insulating oil conditions. Firstly, to obtain fundamental magnetization and magnetostriction characteristics, a single-frame amorphous alloy three-dimensional wound (3D) core magnetic property testing platform is constructed. Subsequently, drawing on the principles of elastic mechanics, a magneto-mechanical coupled vibration model is established for amorphous alloy 3D wound core. Meanwhile, a vibration test platform is constructed to obtain the distribution trend of core vibration under different operating conditions, which the accuracy of the finite element model for core vibration is validated. Finally, the investigation is carried on a 50kVA amorphous alloy wound 3D transformer to evaluate tank's vibration characteristics under the conditions with and without insulating oil, obtaining vibration intensity and frequency distribution characteristics of the tank, and the accuracy of the tank's vibration characteristics is validated for transformer model. On this basis, the influence of insulating oil temperature on the tank's vibration characteristics is considered to reveal the relationship among the vibration characteristics of insulating oil, core, and tank. To break through the limitations of traditional electrical detection methods and provide a theoretical basis for the online fault diagnosis of core.
