title: Reinforcement Learning-Based Prescribed Performance Tracking Control for Autonomous Vehicles with Input Saturation and State Constraints
authors: Xin Zhou, Heng Wang, Zhonghua Miao, Weiwei Che, Qing Li
abstract: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning-based prescribed performance control framework for the path tracking of autonomous vehicles. The Actor-Critic neural networks architecture is introduced in the design of the path tracking controller, which provides a solution for accurately approximating unknown nonlinear terms, obviating the requirement for a priori knowledge of tire cornering stiffness. Compared with traditional prescribed performance methods, the proposed control scheme can quantitatively adjust overshoot, settling time, and steady-state accuracy by integrating a set of boundary functions. Furthermore, an auxiliary function is designed to regulate the tracking error within a prescribed set regardless of the initial conditions, thereby removing their inherent feasibility constraints. To guarantee operational safety, a unified barrier function-based controller is designed to ensure state constraints (longitudinal/lateral velocity and yaw rate) are not violated, which eliminates the feasibility conditions imposed on virtual control laws. Meanwhile, a first-order filter is introduced to avoid the complexity explosion involved in the design of adaptive backstepping control strategy. Simulation results illustrate the effectiveness and superior performance of the proposed control strategy.

title: Operational Space of Solid-State Amplifiers and Matching Network Performance in the NBTF RF System
authors: Riccardo Casagrande, Mauro Recchia, Marco De Nardi
abstract: The Neutral Beam Test Facility (NBTF) plays a crucial role in advancing plasma heating and current drive technologies for International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER). It aims to develop and test the high-performance 1-MeV neutral beam injector (MITICA) and its negative-ion source (SPIDER). To achieve the required negative-ion density, each experiment within NBTF employs four radio-frequency (RF) generators that supply as many couples of plasma drivers, for a total rated power of 800 kW at 1 MHz. The RF power supplies are currently being upgraded from tetrode-based oscillators to solid-state amplifiers and are at the final procurement stage. This transition aims to address the instabilities observed during experimentation with the oscillators, which prevented the achievement of nominal power. Solid-state technology is expected to improve the operation of individual RF generators; however, other side effects related to the simultaneous operation of the four units exist. To enhance the operational efficiency and reliability of the new solid-state amplifiers, we conducted an extensive analysis to identify and address potential optimization areas. In particular, this study aims to expand on our previous considerations on mutual coupling among the ion-source RF circuits, which induces oscillations of the impedance seen by the RF generator. This concept emphasizes the importance of precise frequency and phase alignment to optimize power delivery to the load. Given the complexity of achieving this with the existing fixed-elements and narrowband matching network, we explore various strategies to enable effective iso-frequency operation.

title: Phonocardiogram Classification through Averaging Transforms and Domain-Balanced Cross-Mix Approaches
authors: Ebrahim A. Nehary, Sreeraman Rajan
abstract: Deep learning models have recently emerged as a promising tool for the classification of phonocardiogram (PCG), but their training requires large and accurately annotated datasets. Unfortunately, such datasets are not readily available. So, datasets that are collected using different sensors and those collected in controlled and uncontrolled environments are amalgamated and used as a training dataset. This introduces heterogeneity in the dataset. Further to increase the training samples, each PCG record is segmented into cycles, and a cycle-based training model is employed with cycle labels inherited from record labels. Although these labels are accurate for normal records, they are not for abnormal ones; thus leading to mislabeling of abnormal cycles. Heterogeneity and noisy labels reduce classification accuracy because of inaccurate training. Therefore, this work proposes averaging of record cycles of PCG to address noisy labels and a novel domain-balanced cross-mix (DBCM) approach to handle dataset heterogeneity. Various deep learning models such as MobileNetV2 , gMLP, and MLP-Mixer are trained to classify PCG signals as either normal or abnormal. Additionally, different 2-D transforms such as spectrogram, scalogram, MFCC, and Stockwell are considered for creating input to the deep learning models. A comprehensive comparison between the proposed method and existing methods in the literature that only address dataset heterogeneity using the domain-balanced training (DBT) approach shows that the proposed method is superior by at least 10% in terms of mean accuracy. These results underscore the effectiveness of addressing noisy labels and dataset heterogeneity and utilizing 2-D representations of PCG cycles.

title: Revealing the Dual Impact of Data Elements on Supply Chain Risk via Supply Chain Configuration
authors: Jiasen Sun, Jiaxue Wang, Guo Li
abstract: Data elements (DE) refer to economic resources owned or controlled by firms that can generate economic value. China is the first country to formally recognize data as a factor of production and has actively encouraged firms to leverage DE to enhance their supply chain risk (SCR) management capabilities. However, whether DE can consistently and effectively mitigate SCR remains contentious, and it remains unclear whether the strategic choices of core firms toward suppliers and customers can continuously play a role in data-driven risk management. To address these questions, this study develops a nonlinear analytical framework that links DE, supply chain configuration (SCC), and SCR. By constructing a panel simultaneous equation model, this study systematically investigates the heterogeneous transmission pathways through which DE influences SCR from the holistic supply chain and unilateral perspectives. The results reveal a significant U-shaped relationship between DE and SCR, indicating a “too much of a good thing” effect when firms excessively rely on DE. This finding remains robust across a series of rigorous robustness checks. Moreover, as DE indirectly affects SCR by influencing SCC, the factors of enterprise innovation and artificial intelligence adoption act as “shock absorbers” that moderate this indirect pathway, albeit with notable differences between the holistic supply chain perspective and the unilateral perspective. Finally, the study finds that state-owned enterprises and firms located in cities with data trading platforms can achieve earlier optimization of their supply chain structures, thereby influencing risk transmission. However, data trading platforms also amplify the intensity of risk transmission under highly concentrated SCC. Overall, this study extends theoretical understanding of DE in the field of supply chain management, uncovers the nonlinear transmission mechanism through which DE affects SCR, and provides empirical evidence to guide firms in leveraging DE for SCR management.

title: The Importance of Exposure to Robotics and Its Future for Nursing Education
authors: Marta Romeo, Alessandra Bossoni, Daniel Hernández García, Matthew Forsyth, Catherine Mahoney, Martina Fiori
publish: Companion Proceedings of the 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction
abstract: None

title: DACESR: Degradation-Aware Conditional Embedding for Real-World Image Super-Resolution
authors: Xiaoyan Lei, Wenlong Zhang, Biao Luo, Hui Liang, Weifeng Cao, Qiuting Lin
abstract: Multimodal large models have shown excellent ability in addressing image super-resolution in real-world scenarios by leveraging language class as condition information, yet their abilities in degraded images remain limited. In this paper, we first revisit the capabilities of the Recognize Anything Model (RAM) for degraded images by calculating text similarity. We find that directly using contrastive learning to fine-tune RAM in the degraded space is difficult to achieve acceptable results. To address this issue, we employ a degradation selection strategy to propose a Real Embedding Extractor (REE), which achieves significant recognition performance gain on degraded image content through contrastive learning. Furthermore, we use a Conditional Feature Modulator (CFM) to incorporate the high-level information of REE for a powerful Mamba-based network, which can leverage effective pixel information to restore image textures and produce visually pleasing results. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the REE can effectively help image super-resolution networks balance fidelity and perceptual quality, highlighting the great potential of Mamba in real-world applications. The source code of this work will be made publicly available at: https://github.com/nathan66666/DACESR.git.

title: NESS-Net: An NAMLab Edge-Guided and Scribble-Supervised Swin-Transformer Net for RGB-D Salient Object Detection
authors: Yunping Zheng, Hao Li, ShiQiang Shu, Ke Xu, Mudar Sarem
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: The advent of scribble-supervised learning has opened new frontiers in weakly supervised RGB-D salient object detection (SOD), representing a novel paradigm that significantly reduces annotation costs while maintaining detection efficacy. The SOD methods predominantly based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs) exhibit notable limitations in capturing global contextual dependencies and effectively utilizing hierarchical multiscale representations. Furthermore, conventional edge extraction approaches based on Canny edge detection demonstrate inherent limitations in preserving structural coherence across diverse object boundaries. To address these critical challenges, we propose an NESS-Net model, an innovative weakly supervised architecture that synergistically integrates our previously developed NAMLab framework with Swin-Transformer-based feature learning. The core contributions of our work are threefold: Firstly, building upon our prior work in perceptual-aware image segmentation, we employ the NAMLab algorithm—a hierarchical segmentation framework inspired by Nonsymmetry and Anti-packing pattern representation Model in the Lab color space (NAMLab)—to generate edge maps that better align with human visual perception. Secondly, we devise a U-shaped Edge Construction Module (ECM) that systematically refines edge features through progressive refinement of the NAMLab-generated edge priors. Thirdly, leveraging Swin-Transformer’s hierarchical attention mechanism, we put forward a lightweight Cross-Attention Fusion Module (CAFM) that establishes long-range dependencies across modalities while maintaining computational efficiency. This architectural innovation naturally extends to our Cross-Modal multi-scale Attention-weighting Module (CMAM), which explicitly models inter-modal relationships through transformer-based attention weighting. The extensive experimental results on nine benchmark RGB-D SOD datasets demonstrate that our proposed NESS-Net model outperforms all the current state-of-the-art scribble-supervised models and even achieves performance competitive to leading fully supervised models. The source code and pre-trained models will be made publicly available at https://github.com/EricLie-c/NESS-Net.

title: The Redundancy of Emotion Features: A Comparative Analysis of MBERT and XLM-RoBERTa for Code-Switched Hate Speech Detection
authors: Susan Lady, Abba Suganda Girsang
abstract: Social media platforms have amplified the spread of hate speech, particularly in multilingual contexts where code-switching complicates detection. While recent studies suggest emotion features enhance hate speech detection, their effectiveness in code-switched environments remains unclear. This study investigates whether fusing explicit emotion features with contextual text embeddings improves hate speech detection accuracy in Indonesian-English code-switched text. We fine-tune two transformer models (MBERT and XLM-RoBERTa) on 13,169 hate speech samples and 10,000 augmented emotion samples, then evaluate three classification scenarios: text-only baseline, basic concatenation fusion, and attention-based fusion. Each model incorporates Bi-LSTM layers for sequence aggregation. Results demonstrate that emotion feature fusion provides no significant improvement over baseline: MBERT achieves 86% weighted F1-score in baseline versus 85% with fusion, while XLM-RoBERTa maintains 86% across all scenarios. Both fusion mechanisms (concatenation and attention) yield identical results, and both transformer models demonstrate equivalent peak performance (86% F1-score, 0.93 AUC). These findings indicate that for code-switched Indonesian-English hate speech detection, modern transformers already capture sufficient emotional context from text alone, rendering explicit emotion features redundant. This has important implications for model efficiency and resource allocation in multilingual hate speech detection systems.

title: A Multimodal Biosensing System for Real-Time Driver Monitoring and Adaptive Feedback
authors: Youssief Khalifa, Majd Abo Kalam, Kinan Ghaalrebat, Abhilasha Singh, Jinane Mounsef
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This paper presents a multimodal biosensing driver monitoring system for real-time detection of driver drowsiness and psychological stress. The system leverages facial expression recognition, simulated EEG signal analysis, and EMG-based stress detection to assess the driver's physical and mental state. Three different machine learning models are used: a CNN for facial emotion classification, a Random Forest classifier for EEG-based drowsiness detection, and an MLP for EMG-based stress recognition. Each model is developed with a robust preprocessing pipeline and trained using either real or simulated physiological data. The outputs are analyzed by a central decision-making engine that autonomously adjusts the vehicle's music playback via the Spotify Web API, enhancing driver alertness and comfort. This architecture exemplifies the use of affective computing to support road safety, offering an intelligent and adaptive vehicle environment. Experimental results demonstrate reliable performance across all modalities, highlighting the system's potential for practical deployment.

title: A Real-Time Cybersecurity Monitoring Framework Using SIEM and Honeypot: Implementation in BUMN Bank
authors: Edwin Wiyanto Saputra, Muhammad Fahri Hibatullah Suroso, Jarot S. Suroso
abstract: As the digital transformation of banking services accelerates, cyber threats have become a critical risk for financial institutions such as BUMN Bank. This study aims to design and implement a real-time cybersecurity monitoring framework that integrates Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) and honeypot technologies to enhance early threat detection and response. The proposed framework employs the Wazuh SIEM platform to aggregate, correlate, and analyze logs from various critical assets, while Cowrie and Dionaea honeypots act as decoy systems that capture attacker behaviors and generate threat intelligence. Data collected from honeypots are fed into the SIEM to enrich correlation rules and improve detection accuracy. Experimental testing using simulated attacks—such as brute force, port scanning, SQL injection, and DDoS—demonstrated the framework’s ability to detect threats within 2–7 seconds with high accuracy. The resulting framework provides centralized visibility, early alerts, and actionable intelligence, enabling organizations to transition from reactive to proactive cybersecurity strategies.

title: Robust Classification under Noisy Labels: A Geometry-Aware Reliability Framework for Foundation Models
authors: Ecem Bozkurt, Antonio Ortega
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Foundation models (FMs) pretrained on large datasets have become fundamental for various downstream machine learning tasks, in particular in scenarios where obtaining perfectly labeled data is prohibitively expensive. In this paper, we assume an FM has to be fine-tuned with noisy data and present a two-stage framework to ensure robust classification in the presence of label noise without model retraining. Recent work has shown that simple $\mathbf{k}$-nearest neighbor ($\mathbf{k}$-NN) approaches using an embedding derived from an FM can achieve good performance even in the presence of severe label noise. Our work is motivated by the fact that these methods make use of local geometry. In this paper, following a similar two-stage procedure, reliability estimation followed by reliability-weighted inference, we show that improved performance can be achieved by introducing geometry information. For a given instance, our proposed inference uses a local neighborhood of training data, obtained using the non-negative kernel (NNK) neighborhood construction. We propose several methods for reliability estimation that can rely less on distance and local neighborhood as the label noise increases. Our evaluation on CIFAR-10 and DermaMNIST shows that our methods improve robustness across various noise conditions, surpassing standard k-NN approaches and recent adaptive-neighborhood baselines.

title: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Sensor Fusion for Accurate and Real-Time Smart Grid Current Monitoring
authors: Melek Walha, Joma Aldrini, Inès Chihi
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Cyber-Physical Systems depend on precise, reliable, and real-time sensing to enable advanced monitoring, secure operations, and safety. While classical sensors are characterized by high responsiveness, they are limited to noise and drift faults. Whereas, quantum sensors offer high precision, yet suffer from slow update rates, expensive, and real-time implementation. Existing studies have highlighted the superior accuracy and stability of quantum sensors over classical sensors. However, the integration of classical and quantum sensors is vital to tackle the limitation of individual sensing technologies and to develop more resilient sensing systems. To address this gap, this article proposes a hybrid quantum-classical data fusion framework based on a Kalman filter. The Kalman filter efficiently handles heterogeneous signals with different dynamics and sampling rates, combining the rapid responsiveness of classical sensors with the accuracy of quantum devices. Furthermore, a smart grid system is presented as a proof of concept to demonstrate the practical methodology for the deployment of the proposed framework. Finally, experimental validation is performed to evaluate the performance of the hybrid fusion approach, achieving real-time, robust measurements with an accuracy of up to 94%.

title: Dynamic Event-Based Model Predictive Control for Networked Systems with Hybrid Cyber Attacks
authors: Fan Wei, Xiongbo Wan
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This paper studies the dynamic event-triggered mechanism (DETM)-based model predictive control (MPC) problem for networked systems with false data injection (FDI) attacks and denial of service (DoS) attacks. A DETM is used to save network resources. To reflect the impact of both FDI and DoS attacks on networked systems, a new model describing hybrid cyber attacks is proposed based on indicator functions. A solvable auxiliary optimization problem is designed to get the control gain. The DETM-based MPC algorithm is presented and the recursive feasibility of this algorithm is ensured. The mean-square input-to-state stability of the closed-loop system is guaranteed. The validity of the developed DETM-based MPC algorithm is illustrated by a case study of a networked two-area smart grid.

title: Mapping the Intellectual Landscape of Self-Congruence Research: A Bibliometric Analysis of Business and Marketing Literature (2015-2025)
authors: Fiorello Muhammad Haidar Putra Suwandi, Helena Hanindya Kartika Putri
abstract: Self-congruence theory has become increasingly important in understanding consumer behavior and marketing strategies, yet the field lacks a comprehensive bibliometric analysis of its intellectual structure and evolution. This study addresses this gap by conducting systematic bibliometric analysis of self- congruence research in business and marketing literature from 2015 to 2025. Using Biblioshiny and VOSviewer to analyze 569 publications from the Scopus database, we map the intellectual structure, thematic development, and keyword relationship. Co-citation analysis reveals three major research clusters: (1) branding and consumer behavior, (2) theoretical foundations of self-congruence, and (3) methodological and demographic perspectives. The thematic map highlights strong motor themes such as technology adoption and consumer behavior, while also identifying emerging and niche areas including moderating effect such as ideal versus actual self-congruence, social influence. Keyword co-occurrence analysis further confirms the dominance of brand-related contexts, but also shows a lack of integration with digital and interdisciplinary domains. These findings provide researchers and practitioners with a comprehensive intellectual landscape of self-congruence research and point to promising direction for future research, especially in digital marketing and organizational contexts.

title: Minimizing Losses and Improving Voltage Profile in Power Systems through Wind Energy Integration
authors: Mohamed Amir Labed, Mohamed Benidir, Hocine Sekhane, Slah Sbika, Abderrahmane Zaafouri
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of wind power integration in the IEEE 14-bus system, focusing on the impacts of variable wind generation on system performance. Using MATPOWER, we simulate the integration of up to three wind generators with realistic characteristics, including output variability and reactive power limitations. The study evaluates changes in system losses, voltage profiles, and power flows resulting from wind power integration. Our results demonstrate that wind integration can lead to significant changes in system behavior, including reduced overall losses under certain conditions and altered voltage profiles across the network. This work contributes to the growing body of knowledge on renewable energy integration and offers insights for power system planners and operators dealing with increasing wind power penetration.

title: Mission Profile-Driven Transistor Aging Modeling and Simulation Flow
authors: Firas Ramadan, Maayan Ella, Freddy Gabbay
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: The impact of transistor aging on reliability has become increasingly critical with the rising trends of miniaturization and thermal density in modern integrated circuits (ICs). Aging simulations during the design stage are essential for predicting degradation over an IC’s lifetime. However, conventional aging simulations typically assume fixed, worst-case operating conditions, leading to overly conservative aging margins. In this paper, we introduce a new model and a simulation flow that incorporate variable operating temperatures, accurately reflecting the mission profile of ICs in the field. By capturing the dynamic nature of real-world operating environments, our proposed approach enables more precise aging predictions and potentially reduces overdesign, thereby improving design efficiency and lowering power consumption.

title: Towards Distribution-Shift Uncertainty Estimation for Inverse Problems with Generative Priors
authors: Namhoon Kim, Sara Fridovich-Keil
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Generative models have shown strong potential for use as data-driven priors in solving inverse problems, such as reconstructing medical images from undersampled measurements. Although these data-driven priors can improve reconstruction quality while reducing the number of required measurements, they also introduce the risk of hallucination when the image to be reconstructed falls outside the distribution of images used to train the data-driven prior. Existing approaches to uncertainty quantification in this setting (i) require an in-distribution calibration dataset, which may not be readily available, (ii) provide heuristic rather than statistical uncertainty estimates, or (iii) quantify uncertainty arising from model overparameterization or limited measurements rather than uncertainty arising from distribution shift. We propose an instance-level, calibration-setfree uncertainty indicator that is sensitive to distribution shift, requires no prior knowledge of the training distribution, and incurs no retraining cost. Specifically, we posit that reconstructions of in-distribution images will be more stable with respect to variation in random measurements compared to reconstructions of out-of-distribution images, and that we can use this stability as a proxy for detecting distribution shift. This uncertainty indicator is efficiently computable for any inverse problem in computational imaging; we demonstrate it with preliminary experiments on tomographic reconstruction of MNIST digits, where the generative prior is a learned proximal network trained only on digit “ 0 “ and evaluated on all ten digits. These experiments show that our uncertainty indicator, high variation among reconstructions from different measurement subsets, indeed shows higher uncertainty for out-of-distribution (OOD) digits compared to indistribution digits. Moreover, this higher uncertainty accurately predicts the higher reconstruction error we observe for these OOD digits. Our results motivate a deployment strategy that pairs generative priors with lightweight guardrails, to enable aggressive measurement reduction in computational imaging for in distribution images while automatically warning when the generative prior is operating out of distribution. Code is available at https://github.com/voilalab/uncertainty_quantification_LPN.

title: Analysis of Protein Dynamics and Flexibility with AlphaFold
authors: Faisal Asadi
abstract: AlphaFold has been one of the critical breakthroughs in structural biology. This tool can predict protein structures that would be formed from an amino acid sequence, providing resolution down to the atomic level. It allows us to analyze and interpret how interactions of individual amino acids form a specific structure. However, AlphaFold has some limitations, such as the effect of mutations on the final structure. Additionally, it has difficulties in determining a protein structure that is dynamic and flexible, capable of changing under certain conditions. This is mainly because AlphaFold is trained only to correlate one data to one structure. However, information obtained from predictions can infer dynamic regions at the level of individual amino acid residues. By implementing various methods and combining the information from AlphaFold's predicted Local Distance Difference Test (pLDDT) confidence score, dynamic features of a protein can be captured and estimated.

title: Virtual Power Plant in Modern Power System: A Review
authors: Marwah Ezzulddin Merza, Myasar Salim Alattar, Omar Sharaf Al-Deen Alyozbaky
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: A Virtual Power Plant (VPP) is a cloud-based platform implement to collect and control decentralized power resources like photo voltaic arrays, thermal units, wind turbines, battery storage units (BSU), as well as electric vehicles (EV). VPP task is to delivers grid services and capitalize on the value of these distributed mentioned sources to costumers and grid operators. The idea of these systems established in the early 2000s, matching with the raising integration of renewable energy resources as extra inputs to the power grid and the increasing demand for fast, flexible responses inside power systems. Early VPP tested were achieved in Germany, with the objective of assessing their technical and economic feasibility, beside their potential gifts to grid reliability and stability. Ended the last years, VPPs have extended significantly, pushed by development in digital technologies. This work introduces a complete review of recent advances in Virtual Power Plant (VPP) technologies, as well as their structural design, control strategies, and overall feasibility in modern energy systems. It explores how VPPs integrate diverse distributed energy resources—such as solar photovoltaics, wind turbines, energy storage units, and demand response into a unified, cloud-based operational platform capable of delivering reliable grid services.

title: Hybrid Recommender System for Mutual Fund Investment Using Demographic Filtering and Fund Performance Metrics
authors: Siti Maesaroh, Emil R. Kaburuan, Ahmad Faizal Amri Annur, Ratna Mutu Manikam
abstract: Mutual fund investment recommendation systems play a crucial role in assisting investors in making more informed decisions based on investor demographics, risk profiles, and mutual fund performance history. This study proposes a Hybrid Recommender System that combines Demographic Filtering and Fund Performance Metrics to provide mutual fund recommendations aligned with investors' demographics and risk profiles. In this research, investor data is grouped by income level, age group, and risk profile. Meanwhile, mutual fund performance is measured using the Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) over a 3-year period and the Compound Monthly Growth Rate (CMGR) over the last 12 months. Based on the test results, optimal, balanced performance was achieved with 2 of 20 funds, with a precision of 0.5038, a recall of 0.9878, and an F1-score of 0.6673. This indicates that 98.78% of mutual fund purchases by investors matched the recommendations, even though only 50.38% of the recommended funds were purchased, meaning investors bought at least one of the two recommended funds.

title: Cross-Layer Approximate Hardware Design of Interpolation Filters for Fractional Motion Estimation in Versatile Video Coding
authors: Rafael Da Silva, Ricardo Reis, Mateus Grellert
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: The fact that we can stream video on multiple devices in our homes, on the go using mobile devices, or even while video chatting across the globe, even with low bandwidth, is owed to video coding. Versatile Video Coding (VVC) is the latest video coding standard, which introduces a range of innovative tools. One example is adopting an alternative fractional motion estimation (FME) filter that is part of the Advanced Motion Vector Resolution (AMVR) extension. This work introduces a lowpower hardware architecture accelerator specifically designed for Fractional Motion Estimation (FME) with support for the AMVR extension of VVC.

title: Linear Time-Varying Model Predictive Control for Emergency Collision Avoidance of Four-Wheel-Steering Vehicles
authors: Xiaochuan Zhou, Lin Li, Rui Wang, Chunyan Wang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Emergency collision-avoidance maneuvers demand rapid and coordinated vehicle responses, especially when both longitudinal and lateral dynamics become highly coupled. Conventional trajectory tracking controllers-typically based on simplified single-track or constant-speed models-often neglect these coupling effects, resulting in degraded control accuracy and stability under high-speed evasive conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a linear time-varying model predictive control (LTV-MPC) framework for emergency collision avoidance and trajectory tracking of four-wheel-steering (4WS) vehicles. The controller is derived from a nonlinear seven-degree-of-freedom vehicle dynamics model and linearized around the current operating point to ensure real-time solvability. It jointly regulates longitudinal driving force and steering angle to minimize tracking errors along a preplanned evasive trajectory. A closed-loop simulation platform based on Carsim and MATLAB/Simulink is established to validate the control performance under double lane-change and sudden obstacleavoidance scenarios. Results demonstrate that the proposed LTVMPC achieves superior trajectory tracking accuracy, stability, and computational efficiency compared with conventional DPID-LQR and DPID-MPC controllers, providing a feasible real-time solution for safety-critical vehicle maneuvers.

title: Research on Short-Term Load Forecasting for Distinct Heating Systems Based on CNN-LSTM
authors: Wang Yao, Long Xiaohan, Gao Liang
abstract: An autocorrelation analysis was first conducted on historical operational load data from the heating system to identify short- and medium-term autocorrelation characteristics. Subsequently, a correlation analysis was carried out across multiple datasets influencing demand-side load, from which variables with correlation coefficients above a predefined threshold were selected as input features for the forecasting model. A hybrid CNN-LSTM model was employed to develop a forecasting model capable of capturing both short-term fluctuations and medium-to-long-term trends. The model was applied to predict the hourly heating load for each heat exchange station over a 12-hour horizon. Results demonstrate that for heating loads affected by short-term disturbances and medium-to-long-term trends, the CNN-LSTM model achieves high-precision future load predictions. This offers reliable load forecasting data that supports precise regulation in distinct heating systems, particularly in the context of predictive control.

title: Acoustic Measurements of Dysphonic Speech in New Zealand Women
authors: Maryam Erfanian Sabaee, Hamid Sharifzadeh, Jacqueline Allen
abstract: This study investigates the acoustic characteristics of dysphonic speech in New Zealand women, comparing 13 dysphonic participants with 7 healthy controls. From sustained vowels, we extracted perturbation measures (jitter, shimmer), phonatory features (F0), noise metrics (HNR), resonance measures (F1, F2), and timing (VOT). Participants were clinically rated using the GRBAS-CI scale; most dysphonic cases exhibited low overall severity with preserved intelligibility. After assessing normality and variance, group comparisons were conducted using independent-samples t-tests or non-parametric alternatives. Dysphonic speakers demonstrated higher jitter and shimmer and lower HNR, indicating increased aperiodicity and irregular vocal fold vibration. In contrast, no statistically significant differences were observed in formant frequencies across the target vowels, suggesting broadly preserved vowel articulation. These findings show that source-related acoustic parameters can detect subtle voice changes even when perceptual severity is mild. The results support objective acoustic analysis as a sensitive tool for profiling dysphonic voices in this population and provide a foundation for future diagnostic applications, treatment monitoring, and speech reconstruction.

title: Machine Learning Algorithm and Practice in Artificial Intelligence Marketing
authors: Jingling Cai, Yi Zhu
abstract: In recent years, AI marketing's application and development have advanced significantly, drawing wide attention in the market and academia, and becoming a research hotspot with increasing related studies. Regarding machine - learning algorithms in AI marketing, this research clarifies their practical domain and proposes insights and future research directions for AI marketing practices. It does so through systematic literature review, overview of mainstream marketing algorithms, and analysis of technical matching between algorithms, marketing applications, and cases. The research shows that algorithms in supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning have distinct technical matches for specific marketing aims, establishing an "algorithm - marketing" matching analysis framework. Under this framework, algorithm practices in marketing are clarified, mainly in five aspects: human - computer interaction, cognition & identification, analysis & mining, monitoring & evaluation, and auxiliary decision - making, and they penetrate different industries. In various industry scenarios, AI marketing has developed suitable algorithms and models to meet industry - specific marketing needs.

title: 3D Seismic Acquisition Design Optimization Regularized by FWI: A Deep Learning Approach
authors: Ana Mantilla-Dulcey, Javier Torres-Quintero, Henry Arguello Fuentes
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Seismic acquisition design is crucial for high-quality subsurface imaging and accurate target identification. Its primary objective is to ensure adequate subsurface illumination. Traditional seismic pattern designs are random, uniform, or jittered. Still, they are usually unfeasible due to physical restrictions or the addition of incoherent noise caused by the aliasing of irregular patterns. Currently, seismic acquisition geometries are designed using deep learning (DL) methods within a data-driven framework; however, they overlook the subsurface illumination quality. This work proposes a DL-based method for designing receiver placement in 3D seismic acquisition geometries through regularization with full waveform inversion (FWI) used as an imaging operator. By comparing 3D velocity models from dense and undersampled patterns, the method optimizes receiver placement to acquire the most relevant information. This enables the identification of acquisition geometries that maximize illumination while minimizing the number of receivers. The results showed that our method outperformed state-of-the-art baseline patterns, demonstrating that considering subsurface imaging for design optimization provides a valuable framework for improving the performance of real-world acquisition scenarios.

title: Toward Multi-Person Breath Rate Estimation via mmWave Radar
authors: Cristian Turetta, Christian Farina, Chiara Bozzini, Morteza Varasteh, Graziano Pravadelli
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: The measurement of breath rate (BR) is essential for comprehensive human health monitoring across a wide range of scenarios. Several studies in the literature have explored the estimation of BR using millimeter wave (mmWave) technology. However, these approaches typically focus on a single subject at a time. To enable multi-person estimation, researchers have often relied on data fusion with camera systems or employed specialized hardware configurations. On the contrary, this paper proposes a methodology that employs only one Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar to estimate the BR of multiple subjects stationary in the environment. The proposed methodology includes a pre-processing pipeline to refine the radar-captured signals, followed by frequency-domain analysis to distinguish between subjects. Finally, phase variations in the reflected signals caused by chest movements are analyzed to estimate the BR. Advantages and limitations of the approach are discussed on the basis of an experimental campaign.

title: A Study on the Government-Industry-University collaborative Innovation Mechanism in the Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Industry of Changzhou
authors: Yun Shi, Yao Wang
abstract: This study examines the government-industry-university collaborative innovation mechanism within Changzhou's Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) industry. Drawing on the triple helix theory and collaborative innovation mechanism as theoretical foundations, it develops an evaluation index system for government-UAV industry-university collaborative innovation. Utilizing a government-industry-university collaboration model, the research quantitatively assesses the orderliness degree of three subsystems—Changzhou Government, UAV industry, and universities —and their overall collaborative innovation performance. It further identifies key influencing factors through Grey relational analysis. Results indicate that while government-industry-university collaborative innovation in Changzhou's UAV industry is accelerating, the orderliness degree of university subsystem lags significantly. The absolute value of overall collaborative efficiency remains moderate, with collaborative effect between government and industry, as well as between universities and industry, yet to be fully realized.

title: Construction of the Trait Model for High-Potential Scientific Research Talents: An Empirical Study Based on Private Colleges and Universities
authors: Minjie Yang, Shibo Yang, Jun Tong
abstract: To explore the current status of scientific research literacy of teachers in private colleges and universities, as well as the core trait model and support needs of high-potential scientific research talents, this study adopted a mixed research method combining questionnaire survey and in-depth interview. From June to July 2025, a questionnaire survey was conducted among 610 teachers from private colleges and universities in multiple provinces across China, and in-depth interviews were carried out with 12 high-potential scientific research talents (who have obtained provincial/ministerial-level projects or published high-level papers) and 4 non-high-potential but high-output teachers. The results show that: 1) The overall scientific research literacy of teachers in private colleges and universities is at a medium level, with weak scientific research output (only 12.3% have obtained provincial/ministerial-level projects, and 8.7% have published ⩾3 core journal papers in the past five years), but they perform well in scientific research norm awareness and stress resistance; 2) High-potential scientific research talents have a "four-dimensional core trait model" including "intrinsic motivation, professional ability, executive ability, and collaborative ability"; 3) Teachers generally face four core dilemmas: shortage of funds and resources, heavy teaching and administrative tasks, platform disadvantages, and lack of high-quality students. Based on these findings, this study proposes suggestions for constructing a support system from three levels (government, colleges/universities, and individual teachers), providing empirical references for the cultivation of scientific research talents and the improvement of scientific research strength in private colleges and universities.

title: Enabling Contextual Awareness for Automated Systems through Machine Learning on Electromagnetic Emissions
authors: Zhuoran Jiao
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Effectively sensing device states from complex electromagnetic EM emissions often relies on computationally expensive black box models. This paper proposes a systematic framework that prioritizes efficiency and interpretability over algorithmic complexity. Instead of deploying complex algorithms, this work demonstrates that the most critical factor is optimal feature selection. The framework begins by extracting 30 candidate features from raw EM signals and then employs Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) to identify a minimal optimal subset. The analysis reveals that a compact set of just 10 features is sufficient for peak performance. When trained on this subset, a classical Support Vector Machine (SVM) delivers exceptional accuracy and robust generalizability across different devices. The results clearly show smart feature selection is more important than model complexity. This principle offers a blueprint for building lightweight, efficient, and interpretable monitoring systems for future AI and automation applications.

title: A Study on the Digital Teaching Model, Learner Engagement, and Learning Outcomes of Tourism Scenario English Courses in Applied Undergraduate Universities
authors: Zhaohui Wu, Xiaoyu Jiang, Zhihong Zhang
abstract: This study aims to investigate the impact of <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>digital teaching perception and learner engagement on the learning outcomes of students in application-oriented universities. A questionnaire survey was conducted among tourism majors at an application-oriented university in Shanghai, with 180 questionnaires distributed and 160 valid responses collected, yielding an effective response rate of 88%. Through comprehensive application of descriptive statistics, correlation analysis, and multiple regression analysis, the findings reveal that: teaching perception significantly and positively predicts learning outcomes; learner engagement plays a significant partial mediating role in this relationship; the benefits of digital teaching are universal across genders, but students with stronger English proficiency derive greater advantages from digital resources and interactions, while those with weaker foundations face a potential "digital divide." Accordingly, universities should prioritize the enhancement of teaching objectives and resource design, and improve learner engagement through process evaluation and immediate feedback. Simultaneously, tiered support strategies should be implemented for students with limited English proficiency to achieve inclusive benefits of digital teaching for all student groups.

title: Optimizing Stream Editing Performance
authors: Diomidis Spinellis

title: Long-Tailed and Inter-Class Homogeneity Matters in Multi-Class Weakly Supervised Tissue Segmentation of Histopathology Images
authors: Siyang Feng, Xipeng Pan, Huadeng Wang, Zhenbing Liu, Weidong Zhang, Rushi Lan
abstract: Using image-level weakly supervised semantic segmentation (WSSS) techniques to segment tissue regions in giga-pixel histopathological whole slide images (WSI) has garnered widespread attention, as it can reduce many annotation workloads for pathologists. Most recent studies are based on class activation mapping (CAM) to generate pseudo masks, which are then used to train segmentation model in a fully supervised manner. However, it is still a challenge to accurately segment non-predominant tissue categories due to the existence of long-tailed and inter-class homogeneity matters. For these matters, we propose three designs to solve them: 1) Diffusion-based Data Generation to synthesis new images of tail class to expand data distribution; 2) Feature Recalibration to reassign the logits in CAM to narrow the feature-level prediction gap between predominant and non-predominant classes; 3) Grade-skip Learning to correct the under-fitting tendency of hard samples during the segmentation phase. Moreover, we also design a powerful pipeline LoHo for histopathology tissue segmentation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method not only achieves new state-of-the-art performances but also significantly improves segmentation of tail classes. In addition, our methods are plug-and-play, making it easily integrable into many mainstream WSSS frameworks.

title: The Role of Gamification and Social Value in Increasing Consumer Engagement on TokTok Platform
authors: Theresya Fideliana Celine, Lasmy
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: In today's digital economy, social media platforms, such as TokTok, have brought significant changes in the way users consume, interact, and engage with content. In this context, this study examines the role of gamification and social values in influencing user attitudes and increasing consumer engagement on the TokTok platform, especially in the Southeast Asian context. Using a quantitative approach and the Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) method, this study analyzed 100 valid respondents of TokTok users in Indonesia who had participated in TokTok's official livestream sessions or prize events. The results showed that social network exposure significantly influenced social motivation, recognition, and reciprocal benefits perceived by users. In addition, social recognition and motivation were shown to contribute strongly to positive attitudes, which then became the main predictors of user engagement. These findings indicate that community-based gamification mechanisms are able to encourage deeper and more sustainable interactions with digital platforms. This research is in line with Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) number 9: Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure, by encouraging inclusive digital innovation and strengthening participatory engagement. In addition, this study also supports SDG number 11: Sustainable Cities and Communities, by building inclusive and participatory digital communities. The social impact of this research includes encouraging active digital citizen action, as well as strengthening social cohesion in virtual interaction spaces.

title: High-Precision Measurement of Load Position and Speed in Harmonic Servo Drives with Dual-Side Resolvers
authors: Peng Liu, Zhong Wu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: To achieve accurate motor commutation and highprecision load-side control, resolvers are typically mounted on both the motor side and the load side in harmonic servo drives. However, the measurement accuracy of load-side motion information is limited by the intrinsic accuracy of resolver. Meanwhile, the motor-side resolver can not provide effective auxiliary information for load-side measurement due to inherent kinematic errors of harmonic reducer. In this paper, a highprecision measurement scheme for load-side motion information using dual-side resolvers is proposed. Firstly, a kinematic error estimator is designed using both motor-side and load-side measurements. Then, the estimated kinematic errors are used to compensate the indirect measurement values based on motorside resolver to suppress the effect of kinematic errors. Finally, the proposed strategy can fully exploit dual-side resolver data to enhance the detection accuracy of load-side motion information and overcome the precision limits of resolver. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Graphene-Based Compact Ultrawideband Terahertz Plasmon Antenna With Characteristic Mode Analysis
authors: Ramakrishna Mahapatra, Tej Raj, Dilip Kumar, Surajit Kundu
abstract: An ultrawideband planar patch antenna based on graphene, operating in the terahertz (THz) frequency range, is presented in this work. The radiator is planned on a high-permittivity silicon (Si) dielectric material as substrate with a relative permittivity of 11.9, and its structure comprises three layers: a ground plane, a dielectric substrate, and a graphene patch. The silicon substrate and ground plane share identical dimensions of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$30\times 35$</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, while the radiating patch measures <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$25\times 25$</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, offering a compact design suitable for THz integration. The proposed antenna exhibits ultrawideband (UWB) presentation, spreading an inclusive frequency range from 3.50 to 10 THz. This corresponds to a total bandwidth (BW) of 6.5 THz and a fractional BW of 96.29%. A deep reflection coefficient of −33.35 dB is observed at 7.831 THz, confirming excellent impedance matching. The radiator also exhibits the highest gain of 10.80 dBi and maintains high radiation efficiency across the band. To further validate the UWB, characteristic mode analysis (CMA) is conducted, providing deeper insight into the modal behavior of the structure. Given its high performance and compact size, the antenna is a suitable candidate for a wide range of THz applications.

title: Video-Seer: A Hierarchical Scene Selection and Multi-View Analysis Approach for Zero-Shot Video Question Answering
authors: Chenglei Huang, Jia Zhu, Zilong Li, Hanghui Guo, Jiajun Liu, Pasquale De Meo, Weijie Shi
abstract: Video Question Answering (VideoQA) is the task of answering a question in natural language by analyzing and understanding a video sequence. VideoQA is a typical manifestation of human intelligence, but it is one of the most complex activities for an artificial intelligence system. While vision-language large models excel in multi-modal understanding, they often struggle to capture causal and temporal dependencies in VideoQA. In this paper, we introduce Video-Seer, a novel training-free model inspired by human cognition for zero-shot video question answering. Video-Seer employs hierarchical scene selection through dynamic segmentation and multi-modal relevance scoring to identify question-relevant scenes. It dynamically allocates keyframes based on scene importance, prioritizing high-relevance regions, and integrates fine-grained keyframe grids with global sparse sampling for comprehensive multi-view modeling. Evaluated on challenging multiple-choice (NExT-QA, IntentQA, EgoSchema) and open-ended (MSVD-QA, MSRVTT-QA) VideoQA datasets, Video-Seer achieves accuracies of 71.4%, 65.5%, and 58.6% on multiple-choice tasks, surpassing comparable trained and zero-shot models using a lightweight 7B-parameter language model. These results demonstrate Video-Seer's robust video understanding, reasoning capabilities, and strong generalization without requiring extensive video-question pair training.

title: VRU Trajectory Prediction based on SA-TF-LSTM Considering Traffic-Actor Interaction for Automated Vehicles
authors: Tianshu Pang, Bolin Gao, Guoqiang Li, Hao Chen, Xi Zhang
abstract: This paper systematically investigates the prediction of Vulnerable Road Users’ (VRU, including pedestrian, cyclist, and electric cyclist) trajectories by leveraging the Action Intention Model (AIM), Revised Social Force Model (RSFM) and Social Attention-Transformer-Long Short Term Memory Network (SA-TF-LSTM) for automated vehicles. Firstly, an AIM based on the Transformer is developed for predicting the VRUs’ crossing/waiting intention. VRU type and heterogeneity (age and gender), distance between vehicle and VRU, speed of vehicle and VRU are considered. Secondly, a micro-dynamic RSFM is used to model the trajectories of VRUs for generating initially hypothetical future trajectories, which are then merged the historical trajectories with observed time as a new feature input. Furthermore, traffic data gathered by an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) is acquired and examined, and the Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) is utilized to adjust the parameters of the RSFM. Finally, a data driven SA-TF-LSTM is proposed for VRU trajectory prediction, and VRU crossing intention, traffic-actor interaction, VRU type and heterogeneity are considered. Social Attention is employed to ascertain the attention coefficients of the aforementioned factors. The results demonstrate that the data driven SA-TF-LSTM surpasses the existing methods, with a prediction accuracy enhancement of over 9% utilizing the collected traffic data. This significant improvement grants us substantial confidence in employing the SA-TF-LSTM within automated vehicles to bolster the safety of VRUs.

title: Ultrawideband, Beam-Steerable Transmitarray Antennas Using 3D Printing and Polarization Rotating Phase Shifters
authors: Shiva Hajitabarmarznaki, Joseph P. Berg, Tianqi Shen, John H. Booske, Nader Behdad
abstract: We present the design and experimental characterization of a three-dimensional (3D) printed, ultra-wideband transmitarray (TA) antenna with beam scanning capability. The TA incorporates a polarization-rotating unit cell (PRUC) that performs linear polarization rotation and functions as a wideband 1-bit spatial phase shifter impedance-matched to free space. Each PRUC comprises two orthogonal double-ridge horn antenna (DRHA) sections sandwiching a cross-dipole structure. The double-ridge horn antenna sections enable the wideband impedance matching. The cross-dipole layers in the center of the unit cell are hard-wired to be either connected or disconnected. Manually changing the orientation of the crossdipole structure reconfigures the unit cell between two operating states, enabling polarization rotation of +90° and −90°. Simulation results predicted that the PRUC operates effectively over 6–12 GHz (one-octave bandwidth), with a transmission coefficient of |Txy| > −0.5 dB, |Ryy| < −10 dB (corresponding to VSWR < 1.93), and a relative phase difference of 180° between the two operating states. A 400-element TA antenna with a size of 9λ0 × 9λ0 at 9 GHz was fabricated and experimentally characterized. The main body of the TAantennawas fabricated using stereolithography (SLA) 3D printing and metallized with copper using a commercial electroplating process.We experimentally evaluated the beam scanning potential of this TA antenna between 6-12 GHz by manually reconfiguring the spatial phase shifters. The measurements between 6-10 GHz demonstrate wide-angle two-dimensional (2-D) beam scanning capability ranging from −60° to +60°, corresponding to a fractional bandwidth of 50%.

title: LiBre: Towards Motion-Resilient Contactless Respiration Monitoring using Mobile LiDAR
authors: Junying Hu, Yongjian Fu, Xinyi Li, Lili Chen, Jinrui Zhang, Yaoxue Zhang, Ju Ren
abstract: In this paper, we present LiBre, a LiDAR-based system for real-time respiration monitoring that remains accurate and continuous even under device motion. LiBre addresses the core challenge of disentangling large-scale device movement from subtle thoracoabdominal motions by integrating three key components: (i) an Object-Centric Feature Extraction module that produces clean, geometrically consistent human point clouds and enables multi-target sensing with minimal environmental interference; (ii) a Contrastive Registration framework that combines standard Iterative Closest Point (ICP) and masked Iterative Closest Point (MICP) to decouple device motion from respiration-induced displacements; and (iii) a Directional Residual Projection strategy that automatically estimates the RoI and projects residual motion along the dominant respiratory axis, eliminating the need for manual annotation. Following a brief autonomous stationary initialization phase to establish the respiratory RoI and optimized weights, we implement a complete prototype and validate its real-time performance. Experiments with 10 participants demonstrate that LiBre achieves respiration monitoring with < 1 BPM error at sensing distances up to 4 m, under device motion speeds up to 30 cm/s, and at orientation angles up to 60°, while supporting multi-person scenarios. The system processes each frame within 120 ms, meeting the requirements for real-time mobile health monitoring in practical applications.

title: Innovating the Next Discontinuity Tight Coupling for AI and HPC
authors: Robert W. Wisniewski, Samantika Sury
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: For more than thirty years, the fastest supercomputer in the world doubled in performance every 1.2 years exponentially outpacing Moore’s Law of a performance doubling every two years. By some measures, AI is growing even faster. That performance increase was achieved in part by a series of technical discontinuities, from vector computing to massively scaled computation to GPUs. Each innovation brought a performance level almost unattainable with previous technologies. Using holistic system design and leveraging 3D integration opens the door for the next discontinuity of tight coupling. This paper describes the key challenges facing AI and HPC computing including the memory wall, productively and effectively leveraging accelerators, and the communication wall. It describes an architecture using holistic system design that demonstrates how the tightly coupled technologies enabled via 3D integration helps address those challenges.

title: Deep Understanding of Sign Language for Sign to Subtitle Alignment
authors: Youngjoon Jang, Jeongsoo Choi, Junseok Ahn, Joon Son Chung
abstract: The objective of this work is to align asynchronous subtitles in sign language videos with limited labelled data. To achieve this goal, we propose a novel framework with the following contributions: (1) we leverage fundamental grammatical rules of British Sign Language (BSL) to pre-process the input subtitles, (2) we design a selective alignment (SA) loss to optimise the model for predicting the temporal location of signs only when the queried sign actually occurs in a scene, and (3) we conduct self-training with refined pseudo-labels which are more accurate than the heuristic audio-aligned labels. From this, our model not only better understands the correlation between the text and the signs, but also holds potential for application in the translation of sign languages, particularly in scenarios where manual labelling of large-scale sign data is impractical or challenging. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art results, surpassing previous baselines by substantial margins in terms of both frame-level accuracy and F1-score. This highlights the effectiveness and practicality of our framework in advancing the field of sign language video alignment and translation.

title: Timeslot-Adaptive and Traffic Load-Aware Routing Computation in Two-layer LEO Satellite Networks
authors: Yonghan Wu, Jin Li, Yi Huang, Weixuan Fan, Qi Zhang, Danshi Wang, Min Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite networks, as a fundamental component of 6G networks, are designed to provide full coverage, low latency, and high quality of service (QoS) for satellite-terrestrial integrated networks (STIN). Topology representations and routing computation in dynamic LEO satellite networks have become key research focuses. However, balancing network dynamics with traffic load remains challenging due to inaccurate topology representation and inefficient routing in existing studies. To address this, we propose a timeslot-adaptive and traffic load-aware routing computation (TA-TLARC) scheme for two-layer LEO satellite networks. The two-layer LEO satellite networks consist of communication layer satellites (CLS) and relay and sensing layer satellites (RSLS). TA-TLARC adaptively adjusts timeslots based on traffic variations and utilizes distributed adjacency matrices for routing computation. Simulation results show that TA-TLARC achieves better performance than existing routing schemes in key QoS metrics such as routing success rate, delay, throughput, and packet loss rate. Although routing hops and power consumption increase within acceptable limits, the routing success rate of TA-TLARC remains 99.6% to 100%. The QoS performance, including delay, throughput, and packet loss rate, is improved by 10% to 40% compared to those of the comparative schemes under different traffic scenarios. The robustness of TA-TLARC is further analyzed and demonstrated to be acceptable under various failure conditions. The results demonstrate that the proposed TA-TLARC effectively addresses routing computation challenges and significantly improves QoS performance in two-layer LEO satellite networks.

title: Passive Intelligent Reflecting Surfaces for Efficient 6G mmWave Networks: Design, Validation, and Field Trials
authors: Simon Häger, João Ferreira, Stefan Böcker, Dominique Schreurs, Christian Wietfeld
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: 6G millimeter-wave (mmWave) communication systems aim to provide high-capacity networks that span larger areas than before. Particularly, obstacle-induced blockages leading to underconnected non-line-of-sight (NLOS) regions shall be mitigated with cost- and energy-efficient intelligent reflecting surfaces (IRSs) mounted at strategic positions. Static IRSs are particularly promising because they may be realized entirely passively, scaled more easily, incur less implementation loss, and integrated without control signaling. However, they must be deployed with scenario-tailored reflection characteristics. Against this background, this article first presents a holistic beam shaping approach for HELIOS IRS geometries, allowing for directed reflection patterns with pre-determined beamwidths and in-beam gains. The analytic model-driven investigation reveals that the changes of module tilt parameters, surface dimensions, and module arrangement successfully deliver quality of service (QoS)-compliant IRSs with little loss against the theoretical performance bound. We then conduct a cross-validation through laboratory measurements and electromagnetic (EM) simulations, which confirm the targeted far-field (FF) performance. The findings also attest good signal integrity and specular suppression characteristics that are better than that of external IRSs. An urban outdoor trial with commercial mmWave devices shows that NLOS user equipment (UE) connectivity stabilizes, with more than 10 dB improvements in both received and transmit power levels as beam management adopts the introduced reflection path. Consequently, throughput enhances by up to 275 Mbit/s.

title: Service Satisfaction Based User Selection and Resource Allocation for NOMA-Based Multi-Cell MEC Networks
authors: Ningzhe Shi, Yiqing Zhou, Ling Liu, Yihao Wu, Hanxiao Yu, Jinglin Shi
abstract: Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) is promising to enable low delay services with which users can offload computing intensive and delay sensitive tasks to the edge. Considering a multi-cell MEC (MC-MEC) network without sufficient resources to serve all users, user selection and non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) should be introduced. Then, to maximize the delay-aware average user service satisfaction degree (DA-AveUSD), user selection and resource allocation are jointly optimized (DA-JUSRA), which is modeled as a mixed integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) problem and proven to be NP-hard. To solve this problem, it is decomposed into two independent subproblems, i.e., the power allocation (PA) problem and the user selection, subchannel scheduling and computing resource allocation (USC) problem. Next, a convex evolutionary alternating optimization (CEAO) algorithm is proposed, which alternately applies the convex optimization method and the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT)-embedding enhanced elite genetic algorithm KKT-embedding E2GA to solve the PA and the USC problem, respectively. Simulations show that compared to the optimal exhaustive search algorithm, the proposed CEAO algorithm converges rapidly within a few iterations, with a gap in DA-AveUSD of less than 1% to the optimum performance. Next, compared to existing user selection schemes, DA-JUSRA with CEAO can enhance DA-AveUSD by more than 50% and yield a higher optimal load.

title: PolSAR-Tokenized Foundation Model for Robust Target Recognition
authors: Fan Gao, Qingjun Zhang, Dawei Ren, Xunhao Lin, Junjun Yin, Jian Yang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) is critical for all-weather monitoring and has been widely used. Polarimetric information offers rich electromagnetic scattering signatures that are vital for characterizing complex target structures. However, current methods predominantly rely on single-polarization data due to the scarcity of paired polarimetric datasets for ATR. Although the recently released ATRNet-STAR fills this gap and existing studies have achieved promising results, we identify a critical data leakage risk in the benchmark and establish a stricter evaluation protocol. To capitalize on large-scale single-polarization datasets while exploiting the polarimetric richness, we pre-train a DINOv2 backbone on SARDet-186K and adapt this foundation model on ATRNet-STAR into a ChannelViT architecture, which processes polarimetric channels as distinct token sequences. Furthermore, we introduce a sensor-mode-aware masking strategy, yielding a unified model adaptable to single, dual, and full-polarization inputs. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of- the-art accuracy while maintaining robustness across varying sensor modes.

title: Beamforming design for minimizing the signal power estimation error
authors: Esa Ollila, Xavier Mestre, Elias Raninen
abstract: We study the properties of beamformers in their ability to either maintain or estimate the true signal power of the signal of interest (SOI). Our focus is particularly on the Capon beamformer and the minimum mean squared error (MMSE) beamformer. The Capon beamformer, also known as the minimum power distortionless response (MPDR) or the minimum variance distortionless response (MVDR) beamformer, is a widely used method in array signal processing. A curious feature of both the Capon and the MMSE beamformers is their tendency to either overestimate or underestimate the signal power. That is, the power estimates of their output signals are not asymptotically unbiased (as the sample size approaches infinity). To address this issue, we propose to shrink the Capon beamformer by finding a scaling factor that minimizes the mean squared error (MSE) of the signal power estimate. The new beamformer, referred to as the Capon<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> beamformer, is evaluated against the Capon and MMSE beamformers in terms of signal power bias and MSE, and signal waveform MSE. The Capon<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> beamformer strikes a better balance between signal power and waveform estimation while also exhibiting minimal bias, which approaches zero as the sample size increases.

title: Fractional Programming for Stochastic Precoding over Generalized Fading Channels
authors: Wenyu Wang, Kaiming Shen
abstract: This paper seeks an efficient algorithm for stochastic precoding to maximize the long-term average weighted sum rates throughout a multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) network. Unlike many existing works that assume a particular probability distribution model for fading channels (which is typically Gaussian), our approach merely relies on the first and second moments of fading channels. For the stochastic precoding problem, a naive idea is to directly apply the fractional programming (FP) method to the data rate inside the expectation; it does not work well because the auxiliary variables introduced by FP are then difficult to decide. To address the above issue, we propose using a lower bound to approximate the expectation of data rate. This lower bound stems from a nontrivial use of the matrix FP, and outperforms the existing lower bounds in that it accounts for generalized fading channels whose first and second moments are known. The resulting approximate problem can be efficiently solved in closed form in an iterative fashion. Furthermore, for large-scale MIMO, we improve the efficiency of the proposed algorithm by eliminating the large matrix inverse. Simulations show that the proposed stochastic precoding method outperforms the benchmark methods in both Gaussian and non-Gaussian fading channel cases.

title: Radiation Efficiency and In-Vivo Link Model of Implantable Antennas in Lossy Media
authors: Xiao Fang, Zhengji Li, Dirk Plettemeier
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This work investigates the radiation efficiency and in-vivo link modeling of implantable antennas operating in lossy media such as human tissues. A comprehensive near-field analytical framework is developed to evaluate antenna radiation efficiency, which incorporates intrinsic structural losses (ohmic and dielectric), near-field losses, and compensated far-field attenuation in dispersive lossy media. Through theoretical derivation and full-wave simulations, it is demonstrated that the radiation efficiency advantage of magnetic antennas over electric antennas reaches a maximum when the antenna’s physical size is approximately 0.05 effective wavelength. By examining wave-impedance matching and the distribution of radiated power, this study proposes utilizing one effective wavelength serves as the optimal boundary between near-field and far-field regions for deeply implanted antennas in homogeneous tissues, with the wave impedance difference less than 2% and compensated radiation power difference less than 0.5dB beyond this boundary. Based on this boundary, a modified Friis transmission formulation is introduced for in-body link-budget prediction, providing theoretical basis and directional guidance for the optimized design of implanted antennas.

title: Mask-Supervised Dual-Branch Detection Network for Oriented SAR Ship Detection
authors: Zhengheng Liu, Yong Wang, Mengyu Li, Yiqun Zou
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Oriented ship detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images is widely used for maritime surveillance. However, existing methods suffer from two issues: 1) the feature enhancement process lacks explicit supervision, making it difficult to reliably enhance object features; and 2) only a prediction is generated for each proposal, which may cause missed detections. To address these two issues, we propose a mask-supervised dual-branch detection network (MSDD-Net), which integrates a mask-supervised feature enhancement (MSFE) module and a dual-branch detection head. In the MSFE module, multi-level features are first fused to generate a saliency map, and then this saliency map is explicitly guided to focus on the object regions by joint supervision of an object-aware mask and a center decay mask, with the aim of obtaining enhanced object features (i.e., addressing the first issue). Afterward, the enhanced object features are processed by a region proposal network to output multiple proposals. In addition, the dual-branch detection head generates two predictions for each proposal and matches each proposal with two ground-truth (GT) bounding boxes. Finally, a triplet loss is designed to improve the consistency between two predictions and their corresponding GT bounding boxes and the discrimination between two predictions, with the aim of reducing missed detections (i.e., addressing the second issue). Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of MSDD-Net over state-of-the-art methods.

title: Highly Reliable RRAM-Based Physical Unclonable Function With Auto-Write Technique
authors: Li Ni, Siyuan Wu, Jinwei Pu, Shuwen Xin, Aolin Wang, Xinhui Chen, Zhengxun Lai, You Meng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Physical unclonable functions (PUFs) have attracted significant attention for high-security applications, particularly in the Internet of Things (IoT). While resistive random access memory (RRAM)-based PUF technology offers excellent energy efficiency and integration density, its reliability remains a major challenge. This work proposes a highly reliable RRAM-based PUF that utilizes the 4T2R cross-coupled PUF cell to extract the response. Additionally, an automatic write-back technique is introduced to enhance the initial resistance mismatch in RRAM, thereby improving the PUF reliability. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed PUF achieves outstanding performance, with near-ideal uniqueness (50.02%), uniformity (50.11%), and robust randomness (passing NIST 800-22 tests). The design exhibits robust reliability across a wide operating range (from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$- 40~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$120~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C and 0.7 to 1.4 V), achieving a bit error rate (BER) of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lt 4.89\times 10^{-7}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Furthermore, the energy consumption per bit is optimized to 4.8 fJ at 1.1 V, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$25~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, showing its suitability for energy-constrained applications.

title: Joint Direct Positioning Determination Via Spatial and Spectral Fusion of Signals of Opportunity from LEO Satellites by using Sensor Array
authors: Meng Sun, Maoxiang Quan, Dongqi Shi, Jingjing Pan, Xiaofei Zhang, Yide Wang, Guangxia Li, H. C. So, Qihui Wu
abstract: Recently, passive sensing exploiting low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites as non-cooperative emitters has attracted considerable attention in the field of positioning, navigation, and timing (PNT). However, as LEO satellites are not dedicated for PNT, we cannot obtain the crucial information, such as signals structure and clock synchronization from the LEO signals of opportunity (SOPs), bringing challenges to LEO-PNT based techniques, such as Doppler tracking and code pseudo-range. Moreover, Doppler positioning requires ≥ 3 SOPs and the process of signal recording is time-consuming, often requiring several minutes to complete. In this paper, we address the LEO-PNT problem from the perspectives of sensor data processing and passive source localization.
