However, most existing UIE methods mainly focus on enhancing images for human visual perception, frequently failing to reconstruct high-frequency details that are critical for task-specific recognition. To address this issue, we propose a Downstream Task-Inspired Underwater Image Enhancement (DTI-UIE) framework, which leverages human visual perception model to enhance images effectively for underwater vision tasks. Specifically, we design an efficient two-branch network with task-aware attention module for feature mixing. The network benefits from a multi-stage training framework and a task-driven perceptual loss. Additionally, inspired by human perception, we automatically construct a Task-Inspired UIE Dataset (TI-UIED) using various task-specific networks. Experimental results demonstrate that DTI-UIE significantly improves task performance by generating preprocessed images that are beneficial for downstream tasks such as semantic segmentation, object detection, and instance segmentation. The code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/oucailab/DTIUIE.

title: Noise-Resilient Semantic Communication Via Frequency-Decoupled Quantization
authors: Zhiyuan Xi, Kun Zhu, Yuanyuan Xu, Dusit Niyato
abstract: Semantic communication has emerged as a promising paradigm in next-generation communication systems, leveraging advanced artificial intelligence (AI) models to extract and transmit semantic representations for efficient information exchange. However, the reliability of received information is often compromised by unpredictable semantic noise, such as corruptions or distortions in the transmitted representations. Traditional methods typically rely on adversarial training with artificially injected noise to improve robustness. Yet, these approaches suffer from limited adaptability to varying noise conditions and incur considerable computational overhead during training. To address these challenges, this paper introduces Semantic communication with High-and-Low Frequency Decomposition (Se-HiLo), a novel noise-resilient scheme designed for image transmission. Se-HiLo integrates a Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ) module that enhances robustness by constraining encoded representations within predefined discrete spaces, thereby eliminating the need for adversarial training. While FSQ strengthens resistance to noise, it inherently limits representational expressiveness. To mitigate this trade-off, Se-HiLo further incorporates a transformer-based high-and-low frequency decomposition module that separates image representations into distinct frequency components and encodes them into independent FSQ spaces, thus preserving semantic diversity and expressiveness. Extensive experiments validate that Se-HiLo significantly improves noise robustness and maintains accurate semantic communication across a wide range of noise environments.

title: On Synchronous Motor-Driven Mechanical Systems: Analysis and Linear Parameter-Varying Control
authors: Zoltán Téczely, Csaba Fazekas, Bálint Kiss
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: The actuator dynamics problem is a challenging aspect of controller design considering mechatronic systems. While it is tempting to neglect such effects, industrial applications tend to bear the burden of nonlinear motor characteristics. Ideally, the motor time constant is negligible compared with the system dynamics, but when the system enters marginal operating regions, it may increase abruptly, preventing fast and smooth actuation and may lead to oscillations, reduced performance, or even instability. In its simplest form, the permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) is equivalent to a linear system under coordinate transformation coupled with linearizing feedback. However, to account for the imperfections of field-oriented control (FOC), the motor model is redefined. This transformation leads to a less favorable flat output selection scheme, causing exact linearization to be a less viable option. Therefore, an alternative approach is proposed, in which the nonlinear motor character is mapped into a linear parameter-varying (LPV) control framework. The goal of this article is to design a robust control architecture boasting comparable performance to a baseline industrial solution while guaranteeing stability and improved robustness to parameter variations via a smoother actuation character. The presented method is validated on an electric power steering system equipped with a PMSM, where the high-level controller runs on AutoBox and all other elements are identical to a real-life system.

title: A Conditional Gaussian Mixture Model-Guided Transformer for Soft Sensing in Multicondition Industrial Processes
authors: Xu Li, Weiming Shao, Xindong Wang, Chihang Wei, Ben Wang, Junghui Chen
abstract: Soft sensing technology plays a crucial role in the real-time monitoring and optimization of key industrial variables. Recently, Transformers have emerged as a promising tool for soft sensor development, owing to natural advantages in dealing with long-range temporal correlations and complex nonlinearities. However, the conventional Transformer-based soft sensors are developed in a global learning framework, suffering from performance degradation in processes with multiple working conditions. To address this limitation, a conditional Gaussian mixture model (CGMM)-guided Transformer is developed in this article. Specifically, a CGMM, differentiating the physical properties and distributional discrepancies of manipulated and process variables, is first designed for high-accuracy recognition of the working conditions. Then, a condition-adaptive Transformer is proposed to accommodate variations in working conditions by capturing localized spatial-temporal characteristics based on the CGMM. Experimental results on both a numerical example and an industrial case demonstrate the superiority of the CGMM-Transformer over baseline models.

title: Customer Segmentation in Banking with RFM, CLV and K-Means: Insights from the BIAT Trading Room
authors: Sondes Hammami, Manar Haddad, Alaeddine Touzri
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: In a rapidly evolving financial ecosystem, customer behavioral segmentation has become an essential lever for personalizing banking services and enhancing strategic decision-making. This study presents a data-driven project conducted at the Arab International Bank of Tunisia (BIAT). The analysis is based on a real dataset provided by the trading floor, containing detailed information on client transactions.In this paper, we developed and compared different segmentation methodologies, including traditional approaches based on the RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) model and Customer Lifetime Value (CLV), as well as a machine learning-based method using k-means clustering.The k-means algorithm was applied using two approaches: first, based on a set of features defined by bank decision-makers; second, by combining RFM and CLV variables to integrate both past transactional behavior and future customer value. The comparison of these segmentation methods was conducted using two performance metrics. Results show that clustering-based segmentation with k-means outperforms traditional methods, providing more granular and actionable client profiles to guide targeted commercial strategies.

title: Applying LLMs to the TOGAF Preliminary Phase in Primary Education
authors: Agustinus Sembiring, Alfa Yohannis, Ariya Panna
abstract: This study explores the application of the TOGAF Preliminary Phase in elementary schools, supported by Large Language Models (LLMs). Adoption of enterprise architecture (EA) in schools is often hindered by limited resources and the absence of standardized documentation. Using a mixed-methods approach with 40 respondents, the research evaluates three LLM-generated artifacts: Architecture Vision, Stakeholder Analysis, and Architecture Principles. Results demonstrate that TOGAF inputs and outputs can be effectively adapted to primary education contexts. Quantitative findings show high evaluation scores across all dimensions (M ≥ 4.3), with practicality rated highest and clarity relatively lower. Qualitative insights highlight the relevance, consistency, and professionalism of the documents, though technical terminology reduces readability. Overall, the study underscores the potential of LLMs as co-creators that accelerate EA documentation and ensure consistency, while human validation remains essential for contextual alignment. This hybrid AI–human model offers an efficient, replicable approach to strengthening technology governance in resource-constrained schools.

title: EmFIA: A Novel Emulation-based Fault Injection Vulnerability Assessment Framework at RTL Level
authors: Tanvir Rahman, Shuvagata Saha, Sujan Kumar Saha, Farimah Farahmandi, Mark Tehranipoor
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: Fault-injection attacks (FIA) intentionally disrupt circuit behavior allowing adversaries to bypass safety mechanisms, disrupt system functionality, or extract sensitive information, thereby posing severe risks to the security and reliability of modern System-on-Chips (SoCs). However, pre-silicon security assessments targeting FIA predominantly rely on gate-level simulation or late-stage layout analysis, which are slow, limited in coverage, and often fail to capture realistic operating conditions—leaving critical vulnerabilities undetected until post-silicon stages. To address these limitations, we propose EmFIA, an emulation-driven register-transfer level (RTL) fault injection assessment framework designed to analyze security-critical vulnerabilities against FIA. EmFIA systematically analyzes securitycritical signals in a design by modeling the faults in hardware emulation platform, inserting SystemVerilog assertions, and monitoring security property violations. Demonstrated on a RISC-V SoC and standalone AES-128 and RSA-128 cores, EmFIA enables rapid exploration of fault scenarios, achieving speedups of several orders of magnitude compared to exhaustive gate-level simulation. EmFIA provides designers with fast, property-aware security insight early in the design cycle, significantly strengthening hardware resilience prior to fabrication.

title: Multi-Head Architecture for Open-Set Anomaly Detection of Fast Radio Bursts
authors: Benjamin Amblard, Sara El Bouch, Mauro Dalla Mura, André Ferrari, Cédric Richard
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs) are brief, highly energetic astrophysical phenomena. Detecting FRBs is particularly challenging due to the prevalence of radio frequency interference (RFI), which often obscures or mimics genuine FRB signals, leading to a high rate of false positives. To address this issue, we propose a dual-headed architecture that leverages two learning paradigms: contrastive learning and a selective reconstruction head designed for open-set anomaly detection. Our architecture incorporates a modified contrastive loss to account for both the variability and the class imbalance inherent in transient detection. Furthermore, by confining the reconstruction process to normal data (consisting of RFI and noise), the proposed approach enables robust discrimination between FRBs and interference signals.

title: LCDTwin: LLM-Driven Contextual Digital Twin for Actuator-Sensor Correlation in ICS
authors: Yooshin Kim, Wonje Heo, Eungyu Woo, Donghoon Shin
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Industrial Control Systems (ICS) suffer from a lack of explainability in anomaly detection due to reliance on scarce domain expertise and opaque models. To address this, we propose LCDTwin, an LLM-Driven Contextual Digital Twin, which is a reasoning framework for interpretable anomaly detection. LCDTwin uses a frozen LLM to infer and maintain humanreadable rules that describe normal sensor-actuator logic, which are continuously updated through a similarity-based mechanism without retraining. During inference, the LLM performs contextual reasoning to detect logical deviations from these learned rules. Evaluated on the SWaT dataset's P1 process, LCDTwin achieved robust performance, with GPT-5-mini showing the best balance (F1-score 0.67, Recall 1.00). Crucially, the lightweight models like GPT-5-nano offer cost-efficient operation (0.24 USD per 6h) with low latency. LCDTwin's approach grounds every detection in an explicit rule violation, providing human-readable explanations that significantly enhance operational transparency and trustworthiness.

title: A User Aware Indian Music Recommendation System
authors: Mayur Parvatikar, Manoj Mukunda, Jayanth Chikkarangaiah, Mamatha G S, Shobha G
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: The Hindustani music repertoire of the Indian subcontinent is a rich cultural legacy, renowned for its therapeutic properties and emotional depth. This work introduces an intelligent User-Aware Music Recommendation System that leverages the emotional and therapeutic richness of Hindustani classical music. In Hindustani music, a raga is a melodic framework of specific notes and rules for their arrangement, designed to evoke a particular mood or emotion, and is associated with therapeutic benefits. Building on this principle, the system is designed in three stages to provide culturally grounded, personalized recommendations. First, a specialized dataset is curated, combining attributes such as therapeutic benefits, emotional effects, and metadata derived from extensive studies on Hindustani ragas. Second, a Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) model, based on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks trained on the RAVDESS dataset, is employed to identify user emotions, which are mapped to the emotional essence of ragas through a mood mapper. Finally, a content-based filtering algorithm integrates mood mappings and health conditions to recommend music that aligns with the user's current state. The SER model achieves high accuracy (up to 93.6%) across multiple emotion categories. By providing different combination of user queries, recommendations are evaluated using cosine similarity, a customized Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) algorithm, Precision@K, and Diversity metrics. Results demonstrate that the system effectively suggests ragas aligned with health conditions such as stress, insomnia, and headache, outperforming baseline Spotify recommendations in precision and complementing them in diversity. By blending cultural heritage with computational intelligence, this work highlights the potential of AI driven Hindustani music recommendation as an example of AI for Social Good.

title: Autonomous Cleaning of Solar Panels in Floating Photovoltaic Systems
authors: Keerati Ubolmart, Boonyaporn Preechasuth, Natthapatch Lapsittiwong, Pawaris Tangtrakul, Khunanon Sawetkhotchakul, Phuriwat Kasamesookphaisal, Saifa Meethong, Ponwalai Chalermwattanatrai, Eakkachai Pengwang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This research presents an autonomous robot for cleaning solar panels in floating photovoltaic systems. The robot uses a dual-track mechanism to maintain stability on flexible platforms, while two RPLiDAR S3 sensors generate point clouds for real-time edge detection. A segmentation algorithm extracts panel boundaries, and a feedback controller adjusts track speeds to maintain alignment with the detected edge. Cleaning is performed by a single cylindrical brush with height adjustment regulated by dual load cells to ensure consistent contact pressure. A water-spray system is also implemented to enhance debris removal. Laboratory experiments demonstrate reliable autonomous motion with 89.1 % tracking efficiency. The dust removal performance is within 1 % in the restoring condition, while the dirty panel performance is reduced by 5-9%. These results confirm the system's capability to provide stable navigation and high cleaning performance for floating photovoltaic installations.

title: Exploring the Use of Blockchain in Digital Wallet Systems: Analysis of Impact on Security and Data Privacy of Users in Indonesia
authors: Oliver Hasan Padmanegara, Ratna Komala Putri, Oscar Karnalim
abstract: This research aims to explore the implementation of blockchain technology in the digital wallet (e-wallet) sector in the Republic of Indonesia. The issue of data privacy in Indonesia's digital wallet industry has not yet become a primary concern for many people. However, there are currently several phenomena that associate Indonesian citizens' personal data with digital wallet services. In this study, researchers use descriptive and thematic analysis methods to analyze secondary data obtained from trusted sources. This research proposes six research propositions and suggests a blockchain-based architectural model for managing consumer personal data in digital wallets. Research findings indicate that if digital wallets implement blockchain technology, this can effectively reduce privacy and security risks associated with consumer data management.

title: Ornamental Fish Counting based on YOLOv8
authors: Yuh-Chung Lin, Mengling Xiao, Ruochen Du, Shun Nian Luo, Ta-Wen Kuan, Shih-Pang Tseng
abstract: This research addresses the labor-intensive and error-prone nature of traditional ornamental fish counting, necessitated by challenges such as fish body non-rigidity and occlusion. A dataset comprising twelve fish species (e.g., Blue Tang, Yellow Tang) was established using data augmentation. The YOLOv8 framework was selected as the baseline model. To enhance feature extraction in complex scenarios, the SE (Squeeze and Excitation) attention module was embedded into the network, creating the YOLOv8-se model. Experiments demonstrated significant accuracy improvements: Blue Tang recognition increased from 89.07% to 92.21%, and Yellow Tang increased from 92.37% to 95.24%. The final system, based on the improved YOLOv8-se, was designed to support multi-source input for practical applications like inventory management.

title: About Evolution of Physics and Mathematics Courses in the Quantum Era
authors: Huber Nieto-Chaupis
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The soon massive usage of quantum devices, would push engineering programs to accelerate dramatic changes in the content of their courses, as well as to contemplate the insertion of new courses based entirely in quantum mechanics. Thus, one can wonder if such changes might be applied in an anticipated manner, some years before? What are the latent risks? There are solid impediments to carry out these changes in the short term? This paper tries to answer such questions through an exposition of some scenarios that would prepare the territory to an easy adaptation to quantum world. Thus, more than to speak about preparation, it is discussed the potential adaptability as to advance topics and courses without eliminate classical neither traditional approaches that are still essential and basic in the formation of engineers. Therefore, it is presented some educational schemes targeting the inclusion of quantum mechanics from the very beginning of some engineering programs. More than to allocate topics in an arbitrary manner, it is done by following sequential methodologies without to affect the quality of education, severely. Special attention has been paid on the synergy of mathematics and physics courses playing a noteworthy role at the beginning of programs.

title: SCC 2025 Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)

title: Adaptive Path Tracking Using a Dynamic PID Controller Enhanced by Proximal Policy Optimization
authors: Zhixuan Han, Peng Chen, Ying Guo, Bin Zhou, Guizhen Yu
abstract: This paper introduces a novel adaptive path tracking controller that integrates the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm with a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) control framework. Designed for challenging autonomous driving scenarios, this hybrid approach addresses the limitations of traditional controllers, which lack adaptability, and typical Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods, which often face training inefficiency and stability issues. Key innovations include real-time PID gain optimization via PPO, an adaptive error fusion mechanism with dynamic weighting of lateral and heading deviations, and a speed-adaptive preview mechanism. Notably, this framework does not require an accurate vehicle dynamics model. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms both conventional and learning-based methods across various high-curvature driving conditions, including S-curves and right-angle turns, achieving superior tracking accuracy and robust stability, thus validating its practical effectiveness.

title: Communication Efficient Multi-Vehicle Private Information Retrieval for VANETs
authors: Aiswarya S., Nujoom Sageer Karat, Deepthi P. P., B. Sundar Rajan
abstract: Internet of Vehicles (IoV) and Vehicular Micro Clouds (VMC) advance the services of Vehicular Ad-hoc Net works (VANETs) by offering flawless data sharing, enhancing the travel experience and convenience. However, this seamless data access also raises serious privacy concerns, as querying data may inadvertently reveal vehicle locations and behavior patterns. Solutions to protect the privacy of vehicles while accessing Location-Based Services (LBS) in VANETs are mostly based on cryptographic primitives, which become inefficient when the volume of accessed data is huge. Private Information Retrieval (PIR) techniques help to improve information retrieval rate. But PIR problem becomes very challenging when data is stored in single-server or colluding servers of the same service provider. However, the VANET scenario allows enhancement in the utilization of costly cellular spectrum for Network-to-Vehicle (N2V) communication, leveraging the spectrum available for Vehicle to-Vehicle (V2V) communication. In this work, we address the problem of single-server multi-vehicle private information retrieval (MVPIR) for VANETs with multi-server PIR solutions. The system consists of U vehicles, each requesting one or more of the K available messages from a centralized database, without revealing their data demands. We consider two privacy models: (i) full vehicle privacy, where the data demands of each vehicle remain hidden from all others (other vehicles and server), and (ii) group-private PIR, where the collective demand set of a group of vehicles is revealed within the group; but not individual data demands. We present capacity expressions for both these models with the corresponding designs of achievable schemes. Since the capacity-achieving solution for privacy model (ii) comes with a huge subpacketization cost, this work proceeds to analyze the trade-off between subpacketization level and achievable rate for various sub-optimal PIR schemes. Based on this analysis, a sub optimal scheme with a very low subpacketization level is proposed as a promising solution for highly dynamic VANET application scenarios. Despite its lower subpacketization, this scheme maintains information-theoretic privacy guarantees and demonstrates strong performance under realistic VANET parameters. Overall, our results extend classical multi-server PIR frameworks to single-server multi-vehicle domain, offering scalable, efficient, and deployable solutions that strike a balance between privacy, communication cost, and implementation feasibility.

title: Digital Twin-Assisted Task Offloading and Resource Allocation in ISAC-Enabled Internet of Vehicles
authors: Shanhao Zhan, Zhang Liu, Lianfen Huang, Shaowei Shen, Ziyang Bai, Zhibin Gao, Dusit Niyato
abstract: The convergence of the Internet of vehicles (IoV) and 6G networks is driving the evolution of next-generation intelligent transportation systems. However, IoV networks face persistent challenges, including low spectral efficiency in vehicular communications, difficulty in achieving dynamic and adaptive resource optimization, and the need for long-term stability under highly dynamic environments. In this paper, we study the problem of digital twin (DT)-assisted task offloading and resource allocation in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC)-enabled IoV networks. The objective is to minimize the long-term average system cost, defined as a weighted combination of delay and energy consumption, while ensuring queue stability over time. To address this, we employ an ISAC-enabled design and introduce two transmission modes (i.e., raw data transmission (DataT) and instruction transmission (InstrT)). The InstrT mode enables instruction-level transmission, thereby reducing data volume and improving spectral efficiency. We then employ Lyapunov optimization to decompose the long-term stochastic problem into per-slot deterministic problems, ensuring long-term queue stability. Building upon this, we propose a Lyapunov driven DT-enhanced multi-agent proximal policy optimization (Ly-DTMPPO) algorithm, which leverages DT for global state awareness and intelligent decision-making within a centralized training and decentralized execution (CTDE) architecture. Extensive simulations verify that Ly-DTMPPO achieves superior performance compared with existing benchmarks.

title: TextFuse: Text Information Guided Infrared and Visible Image Fusion
authors: Guohua Lv, Chaoqun Sima, Juncan Wang, Yongbiao Gao, Zhonghe Wei, Jinyong Cheng
abstract: Image fusion involves integrating the information of multiple source images into one fused image, maximizing each source image's advantages. Existing image fusion methods combining text information mainly focus on enhancing the fusion effect, ignoring the requirements of subsequent tasks. High level task oriented fusion methods are usually limited to image information processing, ignoring the potential of text information at the high-level semantic, such as scene understanding and target recognition. We propose an image fusion method called TextFuse, which combines text information and subsequent task requirements, to guide image fusion through the text description generated by the source image and subsequent task requirements. Specifically, we use the text generation model to generate text descriptions for the source image, and design strategies to generate additional text information for key areas to comprehensively express the content of the source image. In the fusion process, we use text information and detection network to guide the model, so as to enhance the model's understanding of the image, generate a fusion image containing rich semantic information and visual details, and improve the performance in subsequent tasks. The text information provides additional semantic context for the image fusion process, enhances the details of key areas, and significantly improves the quality of the fused image. At the same time, the guidance of subsequent tasks further optimized the performance of fused images in follow-up tasks. Experimental results show that the proposed method is significantly better than the state-of-the-art image fusion methods in terms of visual effect and follow-up task performance. The source code is available at: https://github.com/poison-pig/TextFuse.

title: IEEE Computer Society Benefits
authors: N/A

title: HyperSynergyX: Synergistic Drug Combination Prediction via Hypergraph Modeling and Knowledge Graph-Enhanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Qi Wang, Bingzheng Wu, Minglang Xu, Xiya Liu, Yiming Mao, Zhiheng Zhou, Guiying Yan
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Drug combination therapy is pivotal for complex diseases, but identifying synergistic three-drug regimens remains challenging due to both combinatorial explosion and the opacity of existing computational models. To address this, we introduce HyperSynergyX, an explainable framework that integrates synergy prediction with mechanistic explanation. Its core predictive component, a Dual-Biased Random Walk on Hypergraphs (DBRWH), models higher-order interactions among drugs on a three drug hypergraph and identifies latent combination patterns via tensor decomposition. To enhance interpretability, we couple DBRWH with a knowledge-graph–enhanced retrieval augmented generation (KG-RAG) module that retrieves mechanistically relevant subgraphs and uses them to generate biologically grounded hypotheses for predicted synergies. On breast-cancer data, DBRWH achieves AUROC/AUPRC of 0.9593/0.9453 under 5-fold cross-validation, and on lung cancer data it achieves 0.9262/0.9481, outperforming strong deep learning and hypergraph baselines. By linking predictive performance with mechanistic interpretability, HyperSynergyX provides a robust and transparent tool to accelerate multi-drug discovery and support rational regimen design in precision oncology. The code is available at: https://github.com/wangqi27/HyperSynergyX.

title: Boundary-Reinforced Reciprocal Point Learning with Correlation-Aware Multiclassification for Open-Set Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Tuo Xu, Yuefan Du, Xi Yang, Yingzeng Yin
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Existing open-set classification (OSC) methods for hyperspectral images (HSI) often treat unknown categories as a homogeneous cluster, failing to exploit spectral-spatial heterogeneity for fine-grained discrimination. To address this gap, a boundary-reinforced reciprocal point learning multi-classification network (RPLMN-BR) is proposed for robust HSI classification in open-set environments (OSE). First, a SeSaMLP feature extraction framework is designed to enhance discriminative spectral-spatial feature representation by integrating fully connected layers with spectral and spatial attention modules. Second, a weighted reciprocal point learning (RPL) module is fused with generative adversarial networks (GAN) to explicitly strengthen decision boundaries between known and unknown classes; a learnable dynamic threshold is introduced to adaptively construct a separable feature space, improving unknown instance detection. Finally, a correlation-aware multiclassification strategy is developed: global sample correlations (via Jaccard index) are used to build prototype relevance weights, and Pearson correlation coefficients between open-set instances and closed-set prototypes enable fine-grained discrimination of unknown categories. Extensive experiments on four public HSI datasets covering open-set domain adaptation (OSDA) and open-set multiclassification tasks demonstrate that RPLMN-BR achieves superior performance in both closed-set and open-set scenarios, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in harmonic open-set score, overall accuracy, average accuracy, and Kappa coefficient. The proposed framework provides a reliable solution for handling emerging unknown land cover types in remote sensing applications.

title: Surrogate-Assisted Neural Architecture Search with Performance Clustering_supp1-3672588.pdf
authors: Yu Xue
abstract: Evolutionary neural architecture search (ENAS) automates the design of neural network architectures but incurs significant computational costs during the evaluation phase of candidate neural networks in the evolutionary algorithm. To address this issue, surrogate models are widely adopted to estimate the performance of candidate architectures, thereby reducing resource consumption. However, existing surrogate models are primarily based on regression or classification. They are often hindered by interference from low-quality architectures, which limits search efficiency and final performance. To overcome these challenges, we propose surrogate-assisted neural architecture search with performance clustering (PCNAS). PCNAS introduces a contrastive learning-based performance clustering encoder (PCE). The PCE maps candidate architectures into an embedding space to form high-performance clusters while maximizing separation from low-performance architectures. This design mitigates the negative influence of poor-quality candidates. To enhance the discriminative power of the PCE, we incorporate a staged sample generation strategy to strengthen the hierarchical representation capacity of the PCE. Additionally, we introduce a training-free simple classifier to efficiently select high-performance architectures from the embedding space. PCNAS achieves high accuracy with low computational cost on NAS-Bench-101 and NAS-Bench- 201, outperforming previous approaches. Furthermore, PCNAS exhibits strong efficiency in the DARTS search space, discovering a high-performing architecture with a 2.53% error rate using only 0.5 GPU days of search cost, and on MobileNetV3 with ImageNet, achieving 78.12% top-1 accuracy using only 0.3 GPU days.

title: Seam-Guided Unsupervised Image Stitching with Parallax-Aware Mask Generation_supp1-3671350.pdf
authors: Shikui Wei
abstract: Image stitching under large parallax remains a challenging task due to the conflict of content alignment and shape preservation. Most methods focus on precisely aligning overlapping regions via spatially varying transformations, often causing unexpected distortions in large-parallax areas. Differently, we aim to produce stitched images that are both visually natural and free of artifacts. To this end, we present a parallax-aware unsupervised warping model for seam-guided image stitching. To preserve natural content, we first design an edge-enhanced mask generation module to distinguish large-parallax regions and suppress excessive deformation around these areas. It is constrained by a comprehensive objective function that integrates masked photometric difference, nontrivial mask learning, and adaptive regularization, simultaneously ensuring mask reliability and alignment robustness. Besides, to eliminate parallax artifacts, we incorporate a seam-guided alignment strategy into our warping network, which iteratively registers local regions with the assistance of optimal seam estimation. Through adaptively finetuning the warping model, we progressively improve the stitching quality with improved seam quality. To facilitate the learning process of perceiving parallax, we construct a new image stitching dataset with larger parallax than that of UDISD, which could benefit the model’s generalization in challenging scenarios. Experiments show our solution not only removes misaligned regions but also maintains shape consistency especially in challenging parallax scenarios.

title: Automated Design and Selection of Metaheuristics for PID Control in Power Electronics
authors: Daniel F. Zambrano-Gutierrez, Jose Carlos Ortiz-Bayliss, Ivan Amaya, Maricela Rodriguez-Nieto, Juan Gabriel Avina-Cervantes, Jorge M. Cruz-Duarte
abstract: This work introduces an alternative approach that utilizes a Hyper-Heuristic framework under the umbrella of Automated Algorithm Design to dynamically tailor Metaheuristics (MHs) for fine-tuning PID controller parameters. Our findings emphasize the robust consistency, high precision, and adaptability of the method in creating custom, metaphor-free MHs. We employ this methodology in a case study of real-world engineering problems to develop a control-heuristic-based tuner that significantly improves the performance of DC-DC Buck converters across diverse operational conditions. The analysis reveals dominant trends in search operator preferences, highlighting the reliance on genetic crossover and swarm dynamics. These insights provide actionable directions for similar applications in power electronics. Through rigorous statistical comparisons, we verify the superior efficacy and reliability of our approach relative to conventional methods, remarking on its potential for broad application in the field.

title: STDFL: A Spatio-Temporal-Aware Dynamic Federated Learning Framework for Spatial Crowdsourcing
authors: Mingyue Li, Zhihao Wang, Caixia Ma, Chunfu Jia, Liuling Qi, Ruizhong Du
abstract: Spatial Mobile Crowdsourcing (SMC) faces the dual challenge of ensuring privacy while managing dynamic, Non-IID spatio-temporal data. While Federated Learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving solution, traditional aggregation suffers from severe model drift due to evolving spatio-temporal contexts. Furthermore, existing approaches often decouple prediction from scheduling, failing to translate predictive insights into tangible task allocation efficiency. To address these challenges, we propose STDFL, a prediction-driven dynamic framework tailored for SMC. It features a hierarchical architecture combining client-side lightweight models for micro-patterns and a server-side Transformer for global dependencies. To mitigate drift, our Dynamic Spatio-Temporal Perceiving Aggregation adaptively weights updates based on spatial similarity and temporal freshness. For privacy, we integrate client-level <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(\epsilon , \delta )$</tex-math></inline-formula>-Differential Privacy to ensure formal protection. Finally, we introduce a Prediction-Driven Scheduler (PDS) that leverages predictive potentials for bipartite matching, theoretical analysis and stress tests confirm PDS achieves linear scalability and zero policy training cost, offering a superior real-time deployment trade-off compared to RL approaches. Experiments on Chengdu and Nanjing datasets show STDFL significantly outperforms SOTA baselines in efficiency and fairness,while achieving near-centralized prediction accuracy under rigorous privacy guarantees and effectively bridging the utility–privacy trade-off in spatial crowdsourcing.

title: NRSeg: Noise-Resilient Learning for BEV Semantic Segmentation via Driving World Models
authors: Siyu Li, Fei Teng, Yihong Cao, Kailun Yang, Zhiyong Li, Yaonan Wang
abstract: Birds’ Eye View (BEV) semantic segmentation is an indispensable perception task in end-to-end autonomous driving systems. Unsupervised and semi-supervised learning for BEV tasks, as pivotal for real-world applications, underperform due to the homogeneous distribution of the labeled data. In this work, we explore the potential of synthetic data from driving world models to enhance the diversity of labeled data for robustifying BEV segmentation. Yet, our preliminary findings reveal that generation noise in synthetic data compromises efficient BEV model learning. To fully harness the potential of synthetic data from world models, this paper proposes NRSeg, a noise-resilient learning framework for BEV semantic segmentation. Specifically, a Perspective-Geometry Consistency Metric (PGCM) is proposed to quantitatively evaluate the guidance capability of generated data for model learning. This metric originates from the alignment measure between the perspective road mask of generated data and the mask projected from the BEV labels. Moreover, a Bi-Distribution Parallel Prediction (BiDPP) is designed to enhance the inherent robustness of the model, where the learning process is constrained through parallel prediction of multinomial and Dirichlet distributions. The former efficiently predicts semantic probabilities, whereas the latter adopts evidential deep learning to realize uncertainty quantification. Furthermore, a Hierarchical Local Semantic Exclusion (HLSE) module is designed to address the non-mutual exclusivity inherent in BEV semantic segmentation tasks. The proposed framework is evaluated on BEV semantic segmentation using data generated by multiple world models, with comprehensive testing conducted on the public nuScenes dataset under unsupervised and semi-supervised settings. Experimental results demonstrate that NRSeg achieves state-of-the-art performance, yielding the highest improvements in mIoU of 13.8% and 11.4% in unsupervised and semi-supervised BEV segmentation tasks, respectively. The source code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/lynn-yu/NRSeg.

title: Enhancing Reliability of High-Performance Power Module with Advanced Silicone Encapsulation
authors: Zhengdong Wang, Ran Wang, Wang Zhang, Meng Luo, Xiaolong Cao
abstract: As a critical component of power modules, silicone gel (SG) insulating encapsulants play a vital role in determining the operational reliability and stability through their long-term electrothermal performance. In this study, we propose a novel encapsulation scheme for power modules that combines high electrical insulation with excellent thermal conductivity. We investigated its failure mechanisms under multi-stress aging conditions and systematically examined the evolution of its electrical properties. Additionally, the electric field distribution was analyzed through numerical simulations. Key results indicate that after thermal-oxidative aging, AO@NDA/SG achieves a breakdown strength of 20.54 kV, representing a 36.4% improvement over conventional SG, while the corresponding power module exhibits a partial discharge inception voltage (PDIV) of 7.73 kV, 26% higher than that of SG-encapsulated modules (6.13 kV). Simulation results reveal that the maximum electric field intensity in the AO@NDA/SG module is reduced by 26.4% compared to the SG module, indicating significantly enhanced field grading capability. Moreover, the AO@NDA/SG composite demonstrates superior thermal performance. This study provides valuable insights for the development of advanced insulating encapsulation solutions for power electronics packaging applications.

title: Reduced-Complexity Belief Propagation Decoding for LDPC Codes
authors: Heping Wan, Joonyoung Cho, Min Jang, Charlie Jianzhong Zhang
abstract: Two methods are proposed to reduce the complexity of the belief propagation (BP) decoding for low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes, with the focus on the layered decoding for quasi-cyclic LDPC (QC-LDPC) codes. The two methods can be separately incorporated into the layered decoding procedures and combined seamlessly to provide the two-fold complexity reduction. In the first method, the message updates regarding a subset of variable nodes are omitted. To construct the subset, the most reliable bit-level channels, derived from M-ary input channels, are identified. Then, we select the variable nodes with initial log-likelihood ratios (LLRs) exceeding a threshold among the ones assigned to the most reliable bit-level channels to form the subset. The threshold is designed and adjusted based on characteristics of the most reliable bit-level channels. Our numerical results demonstrate that the first method significantly reduces the complexity with negligibly impacting the error-correcting performance. In the second method, each layer of base graphs of QC-LDPC codes uses either sum-product algorithm (SPA) or min-sum algorithm (MSA) to update LLRs. A degree-based algorithm is presented to determine the layers employing either SPA or MSA. We compare with two intuitive methods: 1) applying SPA to randomly selected layers and MSA to the remaining layers, and 2) applying SPA to the core layers of 5G NR LDPC codes and MSA to the remaining layers. According to the numerical results, the degree-based algorithm maximally reduces the complexity while maintaining the same performance. To evaluate the joint method, the first method is applied on top of the second method. Further complexity reduction is achieved without degrading the performance of the second method.

title: EA-IPP2n: Energy-Aware Informative Path Planning Algorithm for UAVs
authors: Ahmad Merei, Hamid Mcheick, Alia Ghaddar, Giovanni Beltrame
abstract: Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have become increasingly vital in various missions due to their ability to access remote and hazardous environments. Effective deployment of UAVs in time-sensitive and energy-constrained missions requires effective path planning. These strategies must maximize mission utility while operating within strict energy and time budgets. This paper introduces an Energy-Aware Informative Path Planning algorithm (EA-IPP2n) that functions as a pre-mission planner, generating the UAV’s flight path prior to deployment. The algorithm simultaneously accounts for both distance and turning angle costs. This enables the UAVs to plan efficient paths in terms of energy consumption and completion time in reward-driven environments. The method models the environment as a graph of nodes that represent locations of interest with associated rewards. EA-IPP2n employs a two-hop look-ahead mechanism, in which the planner evaluates pairs of consecutive moves before committing to the next step, to prioritize high-reward while accounting for both budgets. Simulations show improved performance over baseline methods, including Hierarchical Informative Path Planning (HIPP), Multiple-UAV Informative Path Planning and Mapping (MIPP), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), Genetic Algorithm (GA), Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), and Greedy Search. EA-IPP2n achieved the highest total rewards (24.9 ± 3.6), exceeding the baseline algorithms by relative margins between 11% and 66%, while fully satisfying both time and energy constraints across all missions. It also achieves the highest mission performance score (0.380 ± 0.041), with an improvement of approximately 11% compared to the best-performing baseline, and relative gains between 11% and 57% across all evaluated algorithms. Furthermore, EA-IPP2n strictly respected both budgets in 100% of missions. While the time budget was enforced for all algorithms, only the enforced dual-budget formulation enabled HIPP and MIPP to satisfy both constraints in all cases. Greedy and GA violated the energy budget in all missions, and MCTS exceeded its limit in 40% of missions. In contrast, ACO, despite being heuristic and less informed, maintained full energy compliance. Thus, EA-IPP2n was the only method that achieved complete dual-budget compliance while maintaining the highest total reward performance.

title: Multi-temporal feature integration: learning complementary motion and semantic representations for gait recognition
authors: Md. Rokon Mia, Md. Zasim Uddin, Fady Alnajjar, Muhammed Swavaf, Md Atiqur Rahman Ahad
abstract: Gait recognition is a promising biometric modality for the non-invasive, long-distance identification of individuals based on their unique walking patterns. Despite its potential advantages, real-world gait recognition is hindered by the unconstrained nature of gait acquisition, where factors such as camera viewpoint, clothing, and carried objects can distort body shape and gait dynamics. While most existing methods rely on 3D convolutions to extract spatiotemporal features, they typically operate at a fixed temporal resolution (TR), which limits their ability to jointly model fine-grained motion details and high-level spatial semantics. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel method comprising two modules with a dual TR gait recognition strategy that integrates complementary features extracted from high TR (HTR) and low TR (LTR) video streams. The HTR feature extraction module captures subtle motion dynamics, while the LTR feature extraction module preserves rich spatial and semantic shape details. Additionally, an effective lateral fusion mechanism is employed to exploit multi-scale temporal information, enabling more discriminative feature representation for gait recognition. By fusing both features, the proposed method demonstrates strong robustness across diverse covariate conditions, including camera-view variations, carried objects, clothing variations, and real-world scenarios. Extensive experiments on four publicly available benchmark datasets (CASIA-B, CCPG, OULP, and Gait3D) validate the effectiveness of our proposed method, which achieves superior accuracy. On the CASIA-B dataset, the proposed method achieves average rank-1 accuracies of 98.9%, 96.2%, and 86.7% for the normal walking, carried object, and clothing variation covariate conditions, respectively. Our method also achieves average rank-1 accuracy on each dataset of 81.2% for CCPG, 99.1% for OULP, and 54.2% for Gait3D. The source code will be available at https://github.com/MDROKONMIA/HTR_LTR/.

title: AdapSNE: Adaptive Fireworks-Optimized and Entropy-Guided Dataset Sampling for Edge DNN Training
authors: Boran Zhao, Hetian Liu, Zihang Yuan, Li Zhu, Fan Yang, Lina Xie, Tian Xia, Wenzhe Zhao, Pengju Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: Training deep neural networks (DNNs) on edge devices faces challenges due to the large-scale datasets required, which are costly for edge devices, especially in large language model (LLM) tasks. To address this, a DNN-free method called Near-Memory Sampling (NMS) has been introduced. NMS reduces dimensionality and performs exemplar sampling in the reduced space, avoiding architectural bias and improving generalization. However, NMS has two limitations: 1) The mismatch between the search method and the non-monotonic property of the perplexity error function leads to the emergence of outliers; 2) Key parameter (i.e., target perplexity) is selected empirically, introducing arbitrariness and leading to uneven sampling. These two issues lead to representative bias of exemplars, resulting in degraded accuracy. To overcome these, we propose AdapSNE, which integrates the Fireworks Algorithm (FWA) for efficient non-monotonic search to avoid outliers and uses entropy-guided optimization for uniform sampling, ensuring representative training samples. To reduce the cost of iterative computations, we design an accelerator with custom dataflow and time-multiplexing mechanisms. Experimental results show that AdapSNE outperforms state-of-the-art methods, including both DNN-based (DQAS) and DNN-free (NMS) approaches, across small-scale image datasets, large-scale datasets, and the MMLU benchmark for LLM tasks.

title: Characteristics of Positive Streamer Discharge versus Atmospheric Humidity in a Three-Electrode Configuration
authors: Xiaobo Meng, Weisheng Zhuang, Lin Lin, Hongwei Mei, Liming Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: With large-scale offshore wind power connected to the main power grid, the high humidity environment of offshore wind power equipment has attracted more and more attention from researchers. It is very important for the safe and reliable operation of offshore wind power system to study the air gap discharge in high humidity environment. In the paper, the influence of the humidity on the streamer discharge in the air was conducted by experimental research and simulation research. At the different humidity, the streamer stable propagation field and streamer propagation velocity were measured under quasi-uniform background electric field. A fluid simulation model for streamer discharge was established, the influence of the humidity on various parameters during the development of streamer discharge was studied, and the mechanism of streamer discharge varying with humidity was analysed. With the increase of the humidity, the streamer stable propagation field increased, and the streamer propagation velocity decreased under the same electric field. The simulation results of streamer stable propagation field and streamer propagation velocity were in good agreement with the experimental results by choosing an appropriate streamer channel diameter. Therefore, an empirical formula for the variation of the streamer channel diameter with the electric field under different humidity conditions was obtained by fitting the simulation data. It should be noted that the results and the extracted effective-diameter correlation are specific to the present three-electrode configuration and the investigated humidity and electric-field ranges.

title: Monolithic Integration of BST Varactors and GeTe Switches Realizing a Millimeter-Wave Analog–Digital True-Time-Delay Phase Shifter
authors: Mehran Golcheshmeh, Raafat R. Mansour
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a hybrid analog–digital true-time-delay (TTD) phase shifter fabricated through a novel monolithic integration process that combines barium strontium titanate (BST) varactors with germanium telluride (GeTe) phase-change material (PCM) switches. The integrated process enables low-loss and low-cost TTD architectures by leveraging the fine-resolution tunability of BST and the low-loss, nonvolatile switching of GeTe. The proposed phase shifter achieves continuous group delay tuning equivalent to 360° phase shift over the 37–40 GHz band, with broadband operation from 10 MHz to 67 GHz. The analog–digital architecture achieves a low insertion loss of 7.8 dB and a wide delay range of 26.7 ps with analog coverage.
