title: Formation and Elimination of Leakage in the Fabrication of Bi-2223/Ag Tape
authors: X B Ma, S N Zhang, L H Jin, X Y Xu, X Q Liu, B T Shao, J F Li
abstract: 37-filamentary Bi2Sr2Ca2Cu3O10+δ (Bi-2223) superconducting tapes were prepared by the powder-in-tube (PIT) technique. Leakage occurring during high-temperature processing is a major issue that degrades both the engineering critical current density (Jₑ) and mechanical properties of Bi-2223 conductors. In this work, the critical silver sheath thickness required to eliminate leakage was systematically investigated by correlating leakage behavior with local sheath thickness in different tapes. The results demonstrate that the leakage can be effectively eliminated when the silver sheath thickness exceeds 38 μm, which is identified as the critical threshold. Furthermore, increasing the wall thickness of the outer Ag alloy tube is shown to be an efficient approach for producing optimized Bi-2223 superconducting tapes. When the wall thickness of the outer Ag-alloy tube is increased from 0.6 mm to 0.8 mm or greater, the silver sheath thickness in the tapes exceeds 42 μm, which was above the critical value required for leakage suppression. These findings provide a practical guideline for reducing leakage in Bi-2223 tapes and offered a viable strategy applicable to other multifilamentary superconducting tapes and wires.

title: Anti-Forensic Diffusion Via Dnf-Guided Image Generation
authors: Kevin Arias, Edwin Vargas, Henry Arguello
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Recent advances in forensic detection techniques, such as the Diffusion Noise Feature (DNF), have enabled highly accurate identification of AI -generated images by exploiting subtle statistical patterns introduced during the denoising process of diffusion models. In this work, we propose an antiforensic diffusion framework that regularizes the image generation process to produce images with DNF representations that closely resemble those of real images. Our approach integrates a DNF-guided regularization term into the reverse diffusion process, effectively minimizing the statistical distance between the generated and real image DNF distributions. Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly reduces the detection accuracy of state-of-the-art forensic detectors, demonstrating a more realistic image generation.

title: TinyCAN-IDS: Lightweight Teacher-Student Framework for Real-Time In-Vehicle Attack Detection
authors: Ngo Dang Thanh Ngan, Phan Minh Nhat Truong, Nguyen Thi Dinh Thuy
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Controller Area Network (CAN) is one of the most important and widely used communication networks in vehicles; however, CAN still faces risks of attack. Additionally, with the rapid development of AI, models have been deployed for security systems on the CAN bus. This paper proposes a dual-teacher knowledge distillation framework for CAN bus intrusion detection. Two complementary teachers are employed: a temporal teacher (GRU and MLM) capturing sequential dependencies and a frequency-domain teacher (Goertzel and MLP-MAE) learning spectral anomalies. A compact student model is trained using a hybrid loss combining classification, regression, and distillation objectives. Experiments on the Car-Hacking 2018 dataset [1] show that the student achieves 99.82% accuracy, 100% precision, 97.0% recall and 98.48% F1-score with a size of $\sim 37.5 \mathrm{~KB}$ (8-bit quant, excluding runtime buffers) and 7.95 ms inference latency (batch = 1), enabling efficient MCU deployment.

title: Adaptive Rule Based Energy Management System (EMS) for Battery-Supercapacitor Electric Vehicles Minimizing Battery Stress
authors: Ashruti Upadhyaya, Chitralekha Mahanta, Arghya Chakravarty
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: In this work, an adaptive rule based Energy Management System (EMS) is proposed for Electric Vehicles (EVs) using Battery/Supercapacitor (SC) based Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS). Rules for the EMS are designed considering SC State Of Charge (SOC), vehicle acceleration and battery threshold power. A continuous power distribution factor which depends upon battery SOC is added to improve the performance of the rule based framework. The impact of practical uncertainties such as vehicle mass variation, road slope variation and variation in both mass and slope are also analysed to test the efficiency and practicality of the proposed controller. A battery State Of Health <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{SOH})$</tex> model is applied to test the ageing stress over battery under different uncertain conditions. A real time simulator OPAL-RT is used to implement the proposed EMS. Simulation results show reduction of battery current by the proposed EMS as compared to Filter based strategy and Battery only EV undergoing conditions of varying vehicle mass, varying road slope, varying vehicle mass and road slope both for US06 and SC03 drive cycle. Hence robustness of the proposed rule based EMS is established ensuring enhanced participation of the SC in HESS leading to improvement in battery life and vehicle performance.

title: Trajectory Tracking Control of Differential Tracked Robots Based on Three-Dimensional Interpolation and Model Predictive Control
authors: Pu Zhang, Jian Sun, Yongling Fu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: The trajectory tracking of differential tracked robots faces challenges such as system uncertainties and low tracking accuracy. To address this issue, a differential tracked robot kinematic model was established based on the instantaneous center of rotation. A three-dimensional equivalent interpolation method for two-dimensional planar paths was proposed. A model predictive controller(MPC) incorporating the three-dimensional equivalent distance was designed. This controller calculated the error model, linearized it through Taylor expansion, and transformed it into a standard quadratic programming problem, making it more suitable for mobile robots with limited computational resources. Finally, the control performance of the proposed method was verified in both simulation environments and robot prototypes. The results indicate that the designed controller achieves high tracking accuracy and can adapt to complex polygonal paths.

title: Storm-Time Ionospheric Corrections for HF and GNSS During the 10 to 11 May 2024 Superstorm over Southern Africa
authors: Bernard Tonderayi Mangara
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: A rapid data-based system generates storm-time ionospheric corrections for HF links and GNSS positioning throughout the 10 to 11 May 2024 superstorm over southern Africa. The system accepts IGS GIM (IONEX) VTEC data together with GIRO ionosonde measurements from Hermanus and 1-min OMNI drivers including SYM-H and Bz while using GOES X-ray as a D-region proxy. The 3-D Ensemble Kalman Filter generates time-dependent VTEC and Ne (z approximately 300 km) fields from regional electron density estimates to produce delta MUF and a basic absorption metric. The system trains a ridge-regression emulator to generate lightweight nowcasts and short-lead predictions by using drivers and GIRO / VTEC features to forecast delta MUF and delta Absorption and a GNSS PPP uplift indicator across 0-to-6-hour time lags. The analysis shows the fast depletion and recovery of the main phase and the VTEC reached about 80 TECU and the 300 km layer had pronounced structure. The delta MUF emulator reproduces the analysis-derived target with high fidelity (correlation r approximately 0.98, RMSE approximately 0.04), indicating practical skill for frequency management and link planning. The PPP uplift time series shows storm-time degradation consistent with enhanced gradients and irregularities. The combined EnKF plus emulator approach yields operationally tractable corrections that can support HF frequency selection and GNSS integrity monitoring across southern Africa during severe geomagnetic storms.

title: Application and Detection of Hardware Trojans Applied to Valid Data States of NCL Combinational Circuits
authors: João Pedro Pereira Magalhães, Tales Cleber Pimenta, Diogo Leonardo Ferreira Silva
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: The globalization of digital circuit manufacturing has reduced costs and enabled large-scale production. However, reduced visibility into the supply chain can lead to supply issues and the insertion of malicious modifications into the original design. Hardware Trojans are alterations introduced into circuits to degrade performance, change functionality, or leak information. This paper proposes the application of the detection method for Null Convention Logic (NCL) combinational circuits, targeting the valid data states (’01’ and ‘10’). Furthermore, it reviews existing methods for detecting Hardware Trojans in NCL architectures. The propose method investigated is based on probabilistic transition calculations. Within this framework, the output probabilities of each logic element-specifically, the likelihood of transitioning to a ‘ 10 ‘ or ‘ 01 ‘ state-are computed based on the corresponding input probabilities. Consequently, should unauthorized modifications be present, the algorithm identifies them by detecting statistical divergences between the probabilistic values calculated for the golden (original) circuit and the circuit under test.

title: Mapping Knowledge Landscapes and Emerging Trends of Generative AI in Medical Education: A Bibliometric and Visualization Analysis from 2019 to 2025
authors: XinYu Zhang, Sihua Cao, Xule Zhu, Yuyang Jia, Shiying Zhou
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Background: With the rapid advancement of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) technologies, particularly large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, their integration into the field of medical education is deepening. Medical education, as the core domain for cultivating clinical reasoning, professional skills, and humanistic care, is facing opportunities and challenges related to paradigm restructuring. Objective: This study aims to systematically review the application status of GenAI in medical education, delineate its knowledge structure, identify core themes, and map evolutionary trends, thereby providing evidence-based references for theoretical development and educational practice in this field.Methods: Using the Web of Science Core Collection as the data source, a targeted search strategy was constructed. Literature published between January 1, 2019, and October 11, 2025, was retrieved, resulting in the final inclusion of 1,014 relevant publications. Bibliometric analysis and visualization were conducted utilizing tools including VOSviewer and CiteSpace, focusing on publication trends, national collaboration, author co-authorship, keyword co-occurrence, clustering, and burst detection.Results: The study reveals a “burgeoning growth” in GenAI research within medical education, with the year 2023 marking a critical inflection point. The United States (367 publications) and China (152 publications) emerged as the dominant contributors globally. Research hotspots are concentrated on themes such as “clinical reasoning,” “ChatGPT,” “formative feedback,” and “curriculum development,” forming a three-tiered knowledge structure encompassing “technological foundations educational applications - specialized integration.” Keyword evolution analysis indicates a shift in research focus from initial technical validation towards educational integration and ethical reflection.Conclusion: Generative artificial intelligence is reshaping medical education in terms of resource generation, skill training, and assessment, demonstrating significant application potential and developmental prospects. Future efforts should strengthen interdisciplinary collaboration, evidence-based empirical research, and construct trustworthy AI-enhanced educational systems to facilitate the scientific and responsible integration of GenAI into the entire process of medical talent cultivation.

title: Comparison between Altman Z-Score and Machine Learning in Predicting Financial Distress of Energy Companies in Indonesia
authors: Ewaldo Dwi Yulesta, Rifat Januar Purwanto, Synthia Atas Sari
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Company financial health is a crucial aspect that affects business continuity, capital market stability, and investor confidence. Financial distress poses a serious threat to companies, especially in developing countries like Indonesia, as it can lead to defaults and bankruptcy with broad impacts. This situation worsened due to multidimensional crises, including the COVID-19 pandemic (2020–2022), which caused revenue declines and rising debt burdens across sectors, prompting the need for reliable early detection systems. This study aims to build a financial distress prediction model for Indonesian companies, particularly in the energy sector, during 2020–2023, using machine learning methods such as Logistic Regression, XGBoost, and Random Forest. Additionally, the study compares the performance of these machine learning models with traditional approaches like Altman Z-Score (1968) and Zmijewski X-Score (1984) to assess prediction accuracy. Findings in this study prove that machine learning methods, especially XGBoost, provide more accurate prediction in identifying financial distress. This predictive model functions as an early warning system beneficial for investors’ decision-making, corporate management financial strategy improvement, and regulators to maintain national capital market stability. Thus, financial distress prediction not only mitigates bankruptcy risk but also serves as a strategic tool for fostering healthier and more sustainable corporate governance in Indonesia’s energy sector.

title: Advancing Vision-Aided Beam Prediction: Knowledge Distillation Meets Active Learning
authors: Gouranga Charan, Nhan Thanh Nguyen, Ahmed Alkhateeb
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: The growing demand for high-throughput and lowlatency wireless applications in 5 G and beyond, such as autonomous driving and extended reality, has increased the need for accurate beam prediction in millimeter-wave (mmWave) and subterahertz (sub-THz) systems. Machine learning offers a promising solution, but practical deployment is often limited by model complexity and data labeling requirements. This paper investigates two complementary techniques to address these challenges: knowledge distillation (KD) and active learning (AL). KD enables compact models to approximate the performance of larger models, while AL reduces labeling effort by selecting informative training samples. We evaluate both techniques using a vehicle-toinfrastructure scenario from the DeepSense 6G dataset. Results show that KD improves Top-1 beam prediction accuracy by up to 12%, while AL reduces labeled data requirements by up to 30%. These findings highlight the potential of KD and AL for enabling efficient, scalable machine learning solutions in next-generation wireless systems.

title: Deus Ex LLMs: AI vs Humans in Post-Quantum Cryptographic Hardware Code Generation
authors: Ethan Cornett, Rahul Magesh, Sharath Pendyala, Elif Bilge Kavun, Aydin Aysu
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: Emerging Post-Quantum Cryptographic (PQC) schemes such as FALCON demand highly optimized hardware implementations to meet strict area and execution time constraints on embedded devices. Traditional hardware designs rely heavily on expert-crafted Register Transfer Level (RTL) or High Level Synthesis (HLS) code, which is time-consuming and error-prone. In this work, we explore the use of large language models (LLMs) for accelerating the development of cryptographic hardware, focusing on FALCON’s performance-critical Samplerz subroutine. We propose a design flow that iteratively leverages LLMs to generate, refine, and evaluate synthesizable C code using HLS tools. We analyze generated designs across a range of models (e.g., GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Grok), compare them with prior hand-crafted RTL designs, and report implementation metrics including Area-Delay Product (ADP) and synthesis convergence. Alongside achieving implementations within 4% execution time and 30% area of expert-tuned code, our results demonstrate that LLMs can discover novel hardware optimizations. We finally identify key challenges in prompt engineering, numerical stability, and testbench overfitting, and provide actionable recommendations for future AI-assisted hardware design frameworks.

title: XCAD: eXplainable Collusion and Adversary Detection Framework for Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Hariprasauth Ramamoorthy, Unnikrishnan Nair, Suresh Sundaram
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: In dynamic multi-agent environments such as e-commerce and healthcare, identifying collusive behavior among agents is critical to maintaining trust and reputation systems. While adaptive graph clustering has made it possible to detect collusive agents, these methods often function as black-boxes, offering little insight into the ‘how’ and ‘why’ behind the detection. This paper introduces XCAD (eXplainable Collusion and Adversary Detection), a novel framework designed to enhance collusion detection with an interpretable explanation layer. By combining adaptive, online, and lifelong learning with graph clustering, XCAD not only uncovers suspicious agent clusters but also explains their internal trust dynamics and adversarial patterns. Using simulations on the real-world dataset like Cora network dataset, X CAD demonstrates its ability to detect and diagnose collusive behaviors such as Echo Chambers and Reciprocal trust loops. Outputs include both justifications and actionable counterfactual explanations, transforming opaque detections into transparent, strategic insights that are vital for high-stakes multi-agent systems.

title: Marker-Based Recognition for Autonomous Micro-Drone Flight: An FPGA-Optimized Feasibility Study
authors: Diego Marcelo Ramirez Jove, Keisuke Sugiura, Yoshiki Yamaguchi
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: Drones are nowadays essential in construction and structural maintenance, providing high-resolution images for structural assessments and precise interventions. However, complex maneuvering in confined spaces and reduced communication with external positioning systems, such as GPS, pose challenges for conventional drones. Micro-drones, with their small size that minimizes failure impact and enhances adaptability in restricted or hazardous environments, have gained increasing research interest for expanding drone applications across various fields. This study presents an energy-efficient design that incorporates an optimized combination of basic color separation and a custom downsized version of the Sobel operator to detect markers placed along the trajectory of the drone in real time. The flight instructions stem from the marker identification to fine-tune altitude, horizontal position, and orientation of the micro-drone before moving forward to the next marker. The proposed strategy demonstrated accurate marker detection under favorable lighting conditions 97% of the time, while significantly reducing BlockRAM usage.

title: MRC-CNN: A Lightweight Multi-scale CNN with Residual Connections for HRV-based Emotion Recognition
authors: Yuxi Liu, Xinyi Liu, Bo Zheng, Sheng Li
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Mental health monitoring is crucial for early detection and intervention. Heart rate variability (HRV) derived from electrocardiogram (ECG) signals provides a reliable physiological basis for objective emotion assessment. This paper proposes a lightweight Multi-scale Residual Connections Convolutional Neural Network (MRC-CNN) designed to capture both short-term and long-term discriminative information under limited computational resources while maintaining training stability. Within each module, parallel convolutions with different kernel sizes are employed to obtain multi-scale receptive fields, which are fused through channel projection. Residual connections are introduced to alleviate gradient vanishing and improve convergence, while Batch Normalization, L2 regularization, and Dropout are applied to control model capacity. Experiments on the WESAD dataset demonstrate that MRC-CNN achieves 0.76 accuracy, 0.70 precision, 0.71 recall, and 0.71 F 1 -score on the test set, outperforming both single-scale and non-residual baselines. Ablation studies further confirm the complementary contribution of multi-scale branches and the stabilizing effect of residual connections. To assess cross-subject generalization we ran leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV), which reveals substantial inter-subject variability, indicating baseline shifts and uneven data quality. For interpretability we applied SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) and found IBI (inter-beat interval), BPM (heart rate) and PNN20 to be the top features. The result aligns with LOOCV findings and suggests that variable data quality and inter-subject baseline shifts undermine cross-subject generalization. Overall, MRC-CNN achieves a balance between accuracy and efficiency, showing strong potential for deployment in edge-computing scenarios.

title: The Role of Wireless Communication Networks in Supporting IoT in Smart Cities
authors: Mohamed Farag Abughalia, Sana Ahmed Abdaljlil, Amer R. Zerek, Alaadeen Ashtwei Tantoush
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: The rapid expansion of smart cities has significantly increased the demand for efficient, scalable, and reliable Internet of Things (IoT) infrastructures. Wireless communication networks play a central role in enabling ubiquitous connectivity, real-time data exchange, and remote control within urban IoT ecosystems. This paper investigates the role of several wireless technologies—namely Wi-Fi 6, 5G, LoRaWAN, and Zigbee—in supporting IoT applications in smart-city environments. A comparative performance analysis is conducted using Python-based simulations implemented through the NS-3 and SimPy frameworks, focusing on critical performance metrics such as latency, throughput, energy consumption, packet delivery ratio (PDR), and network scalability.The simulations model multiple smart-city use cases, including intelligent transportation systems, smart grids, and environmental monitoring. The results reveal clear trade-offs between technologies: 5G achieves ultra-low latency suitable for and Zigbee demonstrate superior energy efficiency for low-data-rate sensing and control networks. Wi-Fi 6 offers balanced performance for indoor and medium-range applications. The findings underscore the necessity of hybrid wireless architectures that integrate these complementary technologies to meet the diverse technical requirements of future urban IoT deployments.This study provides practical insights that contribute to the design and optimization of sustainable wireless communication frameworks for next-generation smart cities.

title: A Hybrid Quantum-Classical LSTM Framework for Short-Term Load Forecasting
authors: Hsiang-Yu Teng, Yu-Ming Chang, Long-Chen Hung
abstract: Short-Term Load Forecasting (STLF) plays a critical role in the efficient operation of modern smart grids, enabling real-time energy dispatching, demand response, and renewable energy integration. This study presents a Hybrid Quantum Long Short-Term Memory (HQLSTM) model designed to enhance forecasting accuracy for multivariate electrical load data. The HQLSTM architecture combines a classical preprocessing layer, an optional Quantum Neural Network (QNN) feature transformation layer, a recurrent LSTM core, and a fully connected prediction head. The model was trained and evaluated using real-world load and meteorological datasets, achieving a validation RMSE of 0.1817 and a test RMSE of 0.2308, with an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> value of 0.948 after denormalization. These results demonstrate superior predictive performance compared with CNN-LSTM baselines, effectively capturing both short-term fluctuations and long-term cyclical behaviors of load demand. The analysis confirms stable convergence, robust generalization, and practical interpretability across varying operational conditions.

title: Beyond Price: How User Capability and Software Flexibility Shape the Competition Between Open Source and Proprietary Software?
authors: Ying Liu, Wei Li, Man Shen
abstract: The rise of Open source software (OSS) has significantly transformed the software industry landscape. This study develops a duopoly competition model based on the Hotelling framework to analyze how user capability and demand for software flexibility affect the competition between OSS and proprietary software (PS) vendors. Results demonstrate that OSS serves as more than just a low-cost alternative. When users highly value software flexibility, open- source vendors achieve greater profitability than proprietary vendors. Conversely, when demand for flexibility is low, open- source vendors face competitive disadvantages. Furthermore, increased diversity in user capability diminishes the competitive advantage of open-source vendors. This research provides valuable insights for software vendors in formulating competitive strategies and business model decisions based on specific market characteristics.

title: A Multi-Agent Collaborative Decision-Making Approach for Adaptive Mesh Refinement in Numerical Weather Prediction and Its Application Value in the Digital Economy
authors: Donghua Liu, Yi Ding, Jiali Feng, Jiadan Hong, Guoying Yu, Ruihong Xu
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: High-precision and efficient Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) serves as critical infrastructure for the digital economy, enabling data-driven decision-making across various sectors. However, the computational cost of achieving higher resolution remains a fundamental constraint. This paper proposes an innovative Multi-Agent System-based Adaptive Mesh Refinement (MAS-AMR) framework coupled with an NWP model. By enabling intelligent, dynamic allocation of computational resources based on real-time atmospheric states, our method significantly enhances computational efficiency. Numerical experiments using an idealized tropical cyclone case demonstrate a 15% reduction in forecast error within key weather regions compared to traditional static methods, under an equivalent computational budget. Beyond meteorological advancements, this research analyzes the application value of this technology in the digital economy. We discuss how efficient NWP can reduce operational costs for weather-sensitive industries (e.g., logistics, energy), mitigate financial risks through more accurate forecasting, and ultimately empower the development of data-intensive, intelligent services in smart cities, forming a solid foundation for a weather-ready digital economy.

title: A Targeted ROI Scanning Strategy for Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography with Uniform Spatial and Temporal Sampling
authors: Duo-Hao Zhao, Hao-Zhe Zhong, Shi-Ming Li, Jun-Xiu Zhang, Sai-Nan Wang, Wen-Xin Zhang, Liang-Qi Cao, Jian-Feng Huang, Jia-Cheng Zhang, Qin Li, Xiao Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) is a non-invasive imaging technique that provides depth resolved visualization of microvascular networks without exogenous contrast agents. However, traditional scanning strategies suffer from limitations including non-uniform sampling, incompatible with the BM-scan mode, inflexible field of view (FOV) shapes and data redundancy due to unnecessary coverage of non-target areas. To address these issues, this study proposes a novel targeted ROI scanning strategy for OCTA that enables customizable FOVs while ensuring uniform spatial and temporal sampling. As a proof of concept, we use a circular FOV to simulate that of optical systems and the eye, showcasing its efficiency. The proposed strategy integrates uniform spatial and temporal sampling mechanisms. It discretizes the ROI into evenly distributed sampling points, groups them into equivalent B-scans, and employs a dynamic timing mechanism with flyback compensation to maintain consistent inter-frame intervals. This mechanism supports the widely used BM-scan protocol and avoids the need for post-acquisition resampling, thereby reducing interpolation artifacts and improving computational efficiency. Simulations and in vivo experiments on animal anterior segments and retinas demonstrate that the proposed strategy enables circular FOVs and achieves imaging quality comparable to the traditional raster scanning strategy, while reducing the scanning area to 81.1%, achieving an 18.9% reduction in acquisition time and a 23.3% improvement in scanning efficiency. In summary, the proposed strategy provides a framework with the potential for customizable FOVs and achieves high efficiency without compromising imaging quality, making it highly suitable for OCTA scientific research and clinical diagnosis.

title: Multicontrolled Multiresonance Generation in THz Metamaterial Absorber
authors: Pankaj Kumar Singh, Gaurav Varshney
abstract: A multitunable, ultrathin terahertz (THz) metamaterial absorber is implemented with the hybrid resonator of graphene and phase change material, vanadium dioxide (VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>). The dimensions and geometry of resonator are selected to operate with multiple resonances, which can be controlled electrically, thermally, and by angle of incidence on electromagnetic (EM) wave. The resonances can also be separated and merged to obtain the broad or narrow spectrum for appropriate values of Fermi energy of graphene and thermal conductivity of VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>. The absorber can offer a high thermal sensitivity. The hybrid resonator utilizes the interface of conductive-dispersive boundaries, and hence, the quality factor of the generated resonances can be controlled using the incidence angle. Few generated resonances can be independently tuned using all the tuning factors. Moreover, Brewster’s effect generates few resonance peaks with the specific polarization of the incident wave. The operation of absorber is validated using multiple interference and reflection theory.

title: Hallucination in Generative AI-Enabled Wireless Communications: Concepts, Issues, and Solutions
authors: Xudong Wang, Jiacheng Wang, Lei Feng, Dusit Niyato, Ruichen Zhang, Jiawen Kang, Zehui Xiong, Hongyang Du, Shiwen Mao
abstract: Generative AI (GenAI) is driving the intelligence of wireless communications. Due to data limitations, random generation, and dynamic environments, GenAI may generate channel information or optimization strategies that violate physical laws or deviate from actual real-world requirements. We refer to this phenomenon as hallucination in GenAI-enabled wireless communications, which results in invalid channel information, spectrum wastage, and low communication reliability but remains underexplored. To address this gap, this article provides a comprehensive concept of wireless hallucinations in GenAI-driven communications, focusing on hallucination mitigation. Specifically, we first introduce the fundamental, analyze its causes based on the GenAI workflow, and propose mitigation solutions at the data, model, and post-generation levels. Then, we systematically examine representative hallucination scenarios in GenAI-enabled communications and their corresponding solutions. Finally, we propose a novel integrated mitigation solution for GenAIbased channel estimation. At the data level, we establish a channel estimation hallucination dataset and employ generative adversarial networks (GANs)-based data augmentation. Additionally, we incorporate attention mechanisms and large language models (LLMs) to enhance both training and inference performance. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed hybrid solutions reduce the normalized mean square error (NMSE) by 0.19, effectively reducing hallucinations in wireless communications.

title: Guest Editorial Energy-Efficient Optical Communications, Optical Integrated Circuits, and Signal Processing
authors: Yun Chen, Aseema Mohanty, Keshab K. Parhi
publish: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems

title: Adaptive Text Prompting: A Training-Free Framework for Zero-Shot Action Recognition
authors: Shadab Zahra, Nhan Thi Cao, Taejoon Kim
abstract: Zero-shot action recognition (ZSAR) has emerged as a key frontier in video understanding, which leverages pre-trained visual–semantic knowledge in recognizing unseen actions. Although recent approaches have improved performance by introducing temporal modeling, multimodal prompting, or fine-tuning of vision language model (VLMs), these strategies increase model complexity and often underexploit the inherent capacity of pre-trained models. In this work, the solution to this problem is derived from a text-centric and training-free perspective. Building upon contrastive language-image pre-training (CLIP) pre-trained on Kinetics-400, a novel dual-branch text alignment framework is proposed, which emphasizes simplicity and semantic precision. For each action class, we construct two textual prompts: (1) the raw class name and (2) a large language model (LLM) generated descriptive keywords list highlighting the discriminative visual cues for semantically similar actions. At inference, the model computes frame-level video–text similarities, followed by temporal averaging, and selects the maximum score across the two text branches. This adaptive strategy enables the model to determine when detailed semantic context is beneficial. For actions with high visual or semantic overlaps, enriched textual descriptions offer essential discriminative cues, whereas simple, unambiguous actions are better matched using concise class labels that avoid unnecessary noise. Despite its lightweight design, the approach yields substantial gains, improving top-1 accuracy on UCF101 and HMDB51 by +4.0% and +6.1%, respectively, over the prior training-free method, demonstrating the effectiveness of LLM-generated prompts and dual-branch alignment. The source code can be accessed at https://github.com/IIPNet/Adaptive-Text-Prompting.

title: Truth Discovery From Multiple Dependent Sources
authors: Shuang Wang, He Zhang, Xiaoping Li, Taotao Cai, Michael Sheng, Jixiang Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Recently, the widespread use of smart devices for Internet of Things has led to a massive growth in data and information on the World Wide Web. However, multiple sources from the Web often provide conflicting descriptions for the same objects, thereby complicating the task of truth discovery, especially when sources may copy information from others. Existing approaches typically neglect the dependence and accuracy of these sources, resulting in low accuracy and efficiency in truth discovery. To solve the problem, we propose DepenBaye, a source-dependent truth discovery framework, which incorporates Bayesian probability, the Simulated Annealing method, and the expectation maximization (EM) method. By evaluating the copy probability of sources based on false claims, reliable sources are identified with a simulated annealing method to improve efficiency. According to the EM method, source reliability and claim confidence are iteratively calculated to discover the latent truth. DepenBaye’s performance has been validated through extensive experiments, which outperforms existing approaches in efficiency and effectiveness.

title: Hybrid Score-Driven Multivariate Probabilistic Forecasting of Correlated PV Generation Using Deep Mixture Density Network
authors: Zhiqiang He, Can Wan, Kaiming Zhang, Ping Ju
abstract: With massive integration of photovoltaic (PV) generation in power systems, multivariate probabilistic forecasting (MPF) is essential for analysis and decision-making of power systems, which provides comprehensive information about uncertainty of future PV generation. Traditional MPF methods separate probabilistic forecasting and dependency structure estimation, limiting their ability to generate multivariate distributions in an analytical form. This paper innovatively proposes a hybrid score-driven MPF method to directly generate semi-parametric joint predictive density of correlated PV generation using deep mixture density network. Firstly, an integrated framework of MPF based on multivariate Gaussian mixture models is established to unify the training of probabilistic forecasting and the optimization of conditional correlation modeling. Then, a novel hybrid score-based network loss function with the closed-form expression is meticulously devised for the robust training of MPF. In addition, graph structure learning is incorporated to adaptively capture spatial dependency among correlated PV generation. To address overwhelming parameters of joint distributions, a parameter-reduced structure of covariance matrices using incomplete Cholesky decomposition is formulated, which enhances the tractability of MPF in high-dimensional settings. Comprehensive case studies on three realistic PV power datasets are conducted to validate the superior performance of the proposed method for day-ahead multivariate probabilistic forecasting.

title: Masthead
authors: N/A

title: A Nanoscale-Resolution and Low-Coupling Two-DOF Piezoelectric Microgripper for Micromanipulation
authors: Wenhui Yu, Anqi Huang, Jie Deng, Jinghan Guan, Junkao Liu, Yingxiang Liu
abstract: Micromanipulation technology has played a pivotal role in advancing fields such as biomedicine, micro/nano manufacturing, and materials science, while the continuous progress in these domains imposes increasingly stringent requirements on the precision and multifunctionality of micromanipulation systems. Most existing microgrippers are limited to single degree-of-freedom (DOF) operation and commonly suffer from coupling errors, which restrict their applicability. This work presents a two-DOF piezoelectric (PZT) microgripper, in which PZT ceramic plates are bonded to the compliant mechanisms for actuation, and two-stage lever mechanisms are employed for displacement amplification, with one side generating gripping displacement and the other producing rubbing displacement. To address the coupling issue, three types of flexure hinges and their geometric parameters are theoretically analyzed and compared. The active compensation modules are incorporated into the structure, where compensation PZT plates combined with closed-loop control dynamically adjust the coupled displacement, thereby effectively suppressing coupling errors. Experimental results confirm that the proposed microgripper achieves two-DOF operation at nanoscale resolution, with coupling ratio minimized to only 0.09%. Moreover, its performance is further validated through experimental demonstrations, highlighting its effectiveness in complex micromanipulation operations.

title: Bridging the Educational Divide: A Delay-Tolerant Networking Approach for Equitable Digital Learning in Rural Areas
authors: Salah Abdeljabar, Mohamed-Slim Alouini
abstract: Access to quality education remains unequal, particularly in rural areas where internet connectivity is limited or nonexistent. This article introduces a framework for a digital learning platform that uses delay-tolerant networking (DTN) to extend educational opportunities to underserved communities. Unlike conventional models that rely on continuous internet access, DTN offers an affordable and sustainable solution by leveraging existing transportation infrastructure. Beyond its technical contributions, the framework addresses ethical imperatives by promoting educational equity and digital inclusion. We present a prototype tested on a university campus, demonstrating the feasibility of DTN for educational delivery. By addressing the digital divide, this framework aligns with global goals of inclusive education and sustainable development.

title: Multi-Scale Signal-Image Fusion Model Based On ECoGfor Automatic Detection of Early-stage Traumatic Brain Injury
authors: Chenyu Zhang, Yinzhe Wu, Jeanne Boyer-Chammard, Sharon Jewell, Anthony J. Strong, Guang Yang, Martyn G. Boutelle
abstract: Spreading depolarizations (SDs) are key drivers of secondary brain injury, yet existing bedside monitoring methods that use electrocorticography (ECoG) analyze electrodes and frequency bands separately, thereby obscuring the joint spatiotemporal patterns of SDs. Therefore, this paper introduces a multi-scale signal–image fusion framework that for the first time enables SDmonitoring as a joint multi-modal multi-band spectral image-based analysis. The ECoG signal is converted into a persistent spectral de-weighted spectrogram (PSd-Spec) and joined with multi-band features, through Transformer-CNN jointly empowered blocks: Multi-Channel and Band Transformer Block (MCBTB) and Multi-Scale Adaptive Fusion (MSAF). The network extracts short- and long-range dynamics in a multi-scale time window, while an attention-driven channel weighting module adaptively models the spatial propagation of the electrode strips. On 500h of neuro-ICU recordings, the proposed approach achieved 92.6% accuracy, 84.9% sensitivity. Relative to the best single-modality base line, performance improved by at least 18%, and SD onset was identified on average of 8 min before expert observation. The results suggest that multi-scale fusion of spectral images with ECoG signals yields a clinically actionable early-warning approach and extends quantitative imaging methods to intracranial electrophysiology.

title: Coarse-to-fine Global Localization for Autonomous Vehicles in GNSS-denied Environments
authors: Tongzhou Zhang, Yu Chen, Gang Wang, Ying Zhang, Choon Ki Ahn, Hai Zhang, Jue Hu
abstract: Map-based global localization is crucial for intelligent transportation systems, acting as the foundation for path planning and autonomous navigation, particularly in GNSS-denied environments. However, LiDAR-based localization systems frequently encounter “unbalanced matching” caused by misalignment between query scans and the pre-built map, leading to degraded precision. To address this issue, a coarse-to-fine hierarchical framework is presented, which decomposes the large-scale localization problem into two sequential stages. Firstly, coarse retrieval is decoupled into an efficient 1D descriptor search followed by a robust 2D verification, ensuring global scalability and viewpoint invariance. Secondly, fine positioning is recast from a multi-constellation perspective, transforming pose estimation into solving a system of multi-sphere joint equations. This strategy mitigates the limitations of single registration, thus yielding a system with enhanced stability and accuracy. Finally, the proposed framework is validated on both the KITTI dataset and a dataset collected during an actual trajectory in a university campus. It achieved an average F1-score of 0.868 on the former dataset, and an average F1-score of 0.838 on the latter dataset. Additionally, the framework achieved decimeter-level localization accuracy in a real campus environment, with an error including the z-axis of 0.55 meters.
