publish: Companion Proceedings of the 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction
abstract: None

title: A Magneto-Electric Dipole Antenna Array with Modified Aperture for Gain Enhancement
authors: Shozab Shafiq, Yijing He, Qasim Ali, Wenhao Tan, Syed Muzahir Abbas, Houjun Sun
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents a wideband, high-efficiency, all-metallic 8×8 Magneto-Electric Dipole (MED) antenna array for fixed-beam Ka-band applications. The design integrates three key features: 1) a novel MED element using mirrored U-shaped structures to achieve a 65.7% element bandwidth; 2) a low-profile, horn-shaped aperture that boosts gain by approximately 1 dB; and 3) a compact H-plane waveguide feeding network with miniaturized T-junctions. A fabricated prototype demonstrates a 36.1% impedance bandwidth, a peak gain of 28.8 dBi, and cross-polarization below -40 dB. The all-metal construction ensures high efficiency, making the array a scalable solution for next-generation Ka-band systems.

title: Vision and Causal Learning Based Channel Estimation for THz Communications
authors: Kitae Kim, Yan Kyaw Tun, Md. Shirajum Munir, Christo Kurisummoottil Thomas, Walid Saad, Choong Seon Hong
abstract: The use of terahertz (THz) communications with massive multiple input multiple output (MIMO) systems in 6G can potentially provide high data rates and low latency communications. However, accurate channel estimation in THz frequencies presents significant challenges due to factors such as high propagation losses, sensitivity to environmental obstructions, and strong atmospheric absorption. These challenges are particularly pronounced in urban environments, where traditional channel estimation methods often fail to deliver reliable results, particularly in complex non-line-of-sight (NLoS) scenarios. This paper introduces a novel vision-based channel estimation technique that integrates causal reasoning into urban THz communication systems. The proposed method combines computer vision algorithms with variational causal dynamics (VCD) to analyze real-time images of the urban environment, allowing for a deeper understanding of the physical factors that influence THz signal propagation. By capturing the complex, dynamic interactions between physical objects (such as buildings, trees, and vehicles) and the transmitted signals, the model can predict the channel with up to twice the accuracy of conventional methods. This model improves estimation accuracy and demonstrates superior generalization performance. Hence, it can provide reliable predictions even in previously unseen urban environments. The effectiveness of the proposed method is particularly evident in NLoS conditions, where it significantly outperforms traditional methods such as by accounting for indirect signal paths, such as reflections and diffractions. Simulation results confirm that the proposed vision-based approach surpasses conventional artificial intelligence (AI)-based estimation techniques in accuracy and robustness, showing a substantial improvement across various dynamic urban scenarios. This framework provides a promising solution for enabling resilient THz communication, offering scalability and practicality for future 6G deployments in diverse urban landscapes.

title: A Distributed Storage Routing Model and Algorithm for Resource Scarce and Abundant Network Slicing
authors: Wei Guo, Xiaoli Wang, Yuping Wang
abstract: In both resource-scarce and resource-abundant scenarios (i.e., in the scenarios where service demands exceed/do not exceed available resources), it is a critical challenge to design efficient task allocation model and algorithm in network slicing in 5G/Beyond 5G (B5G)/6G networks, especially in resource-scarce scenario. This challenge faces the following three difficult tasks: 1) fully utilize network resources, 2) satisfy all user requests, and 3) make the best trade-offs among service latency, service failure rate, and Mobile Network Operator (MNO) cost. To address this challenge, we propose a three objective optimization model, the Versatile Storage Routing (VSR) model. It facilitates collaborative multi-device sharing for a single Virtual Network Function (VNF) and parallel multi-path data transmission, thus enabling full utilization of the resources. Also, it can provide efficient resource allocation schemes in both resource-abundant and resource-scarce scenarios to satisfy all user requests. Furthermore, we design a novel evolutionary algorithm for VSR model called VSR-EA based on Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA II) framework. It features customized encoding/decoding and evolutionary operators to handle multi-device storage allocation, multi-path routing selection and distributed transmission resource allocation. As a result, the best trade-offs among service latency, service failure rate, and MNO cost can be obtained. Simulation results demonstrate that VSR-EA exhibits broader applicability and yields significantly superior solutions compared to benchmark algorithms.

title: A Low-Cost Bidirectional Dual-Circularly Polarized Beam-Scanning Reflect-Transmit Antenna Based on Multifunctional Metasurface
authors: Xin-Zhi Bo, Zhen-Guo Liu, Nuno Borges Carvalho, Ming Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: In this communication, a low-profile and low-cost bidirectional dual-circularly polarized (dual-CP) beam-scanning reflect-transmit antenna based on the multifunctional metasurface (MFM) is proposed and demonstrated. The proposed bidirectional dual-CP beam-scanning antenna consists of only two MFMs and two feeds integrated at the center of each metasurface, offering advantages of simple structure and low cost. The two MFMs are constructed with subwavelength single-arm spiral microstrip lines in a receiver-transmitter (Rx-Tx) configuration, which can independently and simultaneously modulate the phase of the reflection and transmission coefficients of the dual-CP polarized electromagnetic waves just using a simple rotation technique. For validation, a prototype with a center frequency of 15 GHz is designed, fabricated, and measured. When operating in LHCP mode, the proposed antenna provides ±40° beam scanning for the front direction with a scan loss of 1.40 dB. When operating in RHCP mode, it can also provide ±40° beam scanning for the back direction with a scan loss of 1.45 dB. The proposed antenna achieves peak gains of 19.4 and 20.0 dBic in the +z/-z directions, respectively. With its compact design, low-cost fabrication, and bidirectional beam-steering capability, this antenna is highly promising for low-cost wireless communication systems.

title: Hybrid Framework for Efficient Design of Square-Loop Frequency-Selective Surfaces
authors: Michael Thompson, Christophe Fumeaux, Amin Abbosh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: An exploratory data analysis framework is introduced to develop an equivalent circuit model for square-loop frequency-selective surfaces. The approach provides insights into the behaviour of square-loop arrays and the capability of predicting electromagnetic behaviour for a range of geometries beyond purely analytical methods. The model provides a statistical framework that can be used to design a hybrid model, still incorporating elements of electromagnetic theory as a basis, but not bound by many assumptions that traditionally limit the model’s applicability. As an application example, the model is used to design a band-stop spatial filter for the K band. The filter is designed for a <-10 dB stop-band from 18.2 GHz to 25.1 GHz and performs exceptionally well in the out-of-band region, maintaining a measured transmission higher than -1.2 dB below the stop band from 0-15.2 GHz and higher than -1.4 dB from 26.3-40 GHz.

title: Trajectory Similarity Hash Learning with Spatio-Temporal GRU
authors: Yuan Cao, Chenxu Wang, Zifan Liu, Lei Li, Bin Wang, Yanwei Yu
abstract: Trajectory similarity computation plays a critical role in a wide range of trajectory-related applications, including transportation optimization and behavior study. Most studies aim at learning discriminative real-valued trajectory representations. However, these methods struggle to scale to large datasets due to their linear time complexity. To address this problem, only one hypergraph hash learning approach (HHL-Traj) has been proposed to realize efficient trajectory similarity computation by calculating Hamming distances among the trajectory hash codes. Nevertheless, it fails to effectively integrate the spatial and temporal information of trajectory data with semantic relevance. In this paper, we present a novel Trajectory Similarity Hash Learning method with Spatio-Temporal GRU (TrajH-ST), which fuses the spatial and temporal features through reset and update gates within the network architecture. Additionally, we design an alternating sampling strategy to generate two sub-trajectories for contrastive learning, which enhances both generalization and robustness compared to nonuniform sampling techniques. To optimize the proposed end-to-end model, we develop an objective function that incorporates InfoNCE loss, alignment loss, and quantization loss. Extensive experiments on two widely-used trajectory datasets demonstrate that the proposed model consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving accuracy improvements of up to 5.58% and 4.33% with real-valued and binary features, respectively. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/caoyuan618/Traj-ST</uri>

title: Dual-target molecule generation model based on multi-task pre-training method
authors: Niu Ziru, Ye Ziyu, Dong Hao, Chen Hao, Bai Zhongshan
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Designing molecules with activity against dual targets is a critical challenge in drug discovery, as it requires balancing molecular validity, diversity, and dual-target specificity. We propose a Transformer-GNN based generative framework that integrates multi-task pretraining with reinforcement learning. In the pretraining stage, the model is trained on the largescale ChEMBL24 dataset through a combination of molecular property prediction (QED, SA), atom-level masking, and fragment-level masking tasks, enabling it to capture both global structural semantics and local chemical contexts. In the finetuning stage, we incorporate docking scores as reinforcement rewards to adapt the generator toward dual-target specificity, progressively biasing generation toward molecules that exhibit favorable binding profiles for both targets. Extensive experiments on the GSK3 $\beta \mid$ JNK3 dual-target inhibitor generation task demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple evaluation metrics. Specifically, the generated molecules reach the highest scores in validity, uniqueness, novelty, and diversity, while also achieving the largest proportion of dual-target active compounds among all generated samples. These results show that combining structured multi-task pretraining with reinforcement fine-tuning provides an effective and generalizable strategy for controllable dualtarget molecule design, paving the way toward more robust and practical computational drug discovery pipelines.

title: Probabilistic Multilayer Perceptron with Applications to Data Fitting
authors: Huber Nieto-Chaupis
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Among the most important objectives inside of territory of the statistical schemes becomes the prediction of nonlinear system from a little knowledge of past. However, such systems might not be well understood so that would not have space for reliable predictions. In this paper, more than to go through routes that apparently try to model nonlinear systems, it is chosen a fitting mechanism based entirely in the theory of artificial neural networks. Thus for example, when system behaves fully nonlinear, one can employ mathematical methodologies based in perceptron. Under this view, input and weight of perceptron can be adjustable in order to seek the mathematical function more representable or that adjusts well to system. Applications to test the proposed approach of this paper are presented.

title: Enhancing Explainability for Exchange Rate Forecasting via Self-Reflective Reinforcement Learning
authors: Yuchang Zou, Di Han, Zikun Guo, Jianlin Feng, Yan Hu, Yuke Wang
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Exchange rate forecasting faces the challenge of balancing accuracy and explainability. Meanwhile, large language models (LLMs) are mainly used for feature extraction and have few applications in generating explainable forecast results, and there is a lack of in-depth analysis of the reasoning process. In order to address this issue, we propose the Self-Reflective Reinforcement Learning (SRRL) framework, which integrates machine learning, LLMs and reinforcement learning. The framework’s methodology is as follows: firstly, it utilises the forecast results of the Space-Time and Times-Net fusion model (ST-fusion model) to automatically construct a dataset that includes “explanation-reflection”. Secondly, it uses this dataset to fine-tune an LLM via reinforcement learning. Finally, the fine-tuned LLM generates forecast signals and reasonable explanations. The experimental results demonstrate that the explanations generated by the fine-tuned LLM show significant improvements in logical coherence and relevance to input evidence, thereby effectively enhancing the quality of model explainability.

title: Advanced High-Order Graph Convolutional Networks with Assorted Time-Frequency Transforms
authors: Ling Wang, Ye Yuan, Xin Luo
abstract: A dynamic graph (DG) is adopted to portray the evolving interplay between nodes in real-world scenarios prevalently. A high-order graph convolutional network (HGCN) is equipped with the ability to represent a DG by the spatial-temporal message passing mechanism built on tensor product. Concretely, an HGCN utilizes the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) to implement temporal message passing and then employs face-wise product to realize spatial message passing. However, DFT is only a special case of assorted time-frequency transforms, which considers the complex temporal patterns partially, thereby resulting in an inaccurate temporal message passing possibly. To address this issue, this study proposes six advanced time-frequency transform-incorporated HGCNs (TF-HGCNs) with discrete Fourier, discrete Hartley, discrete cosine, Haar wavelet, Walsh Hadamard, and slant transforms. In addition, a potent ensemble is built regarding the proposed six TF-HGCNs as the bases. Finally, the corresponding theoretical proof is presented. Empirical studies on six DG datasets demonstrate that owing to diverse time-frequency transforms, the proposed six TF-HGCNs significantly outperform state-of-the-art models in addressing the task of link weight estimation. Moreover, their ensemble outstrips each base's performance.

title: A Survey of Universal Rays and Effectiveness on the Ionosphere layer and GNSS Signals
authors: Abdussalam Masoud Ammar, Marai Abusetta
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: The ionosphere, a layered region of Earth’s upper atmosphere, strongly affects electromagnetic signal propagation, particularly for satellite navigation. This study surveys ionospheric behavior under solar and cosmic influences and its impact on GNSS performance. We analyze phenomena such as solar flares, cosmic ray ionization, and space weather anomalies, linking them to signal degradation mechanisms including Faraday rotation, scintillation, and total electron content (TEC) variability. A comparative review of Klobuchar and NeQuick-G correction models examines structural differences, predictive accuracy, and adaptability to dynamic geophysical conditions. Integrating classical resonance theory with modern space weather monitoring, findings show Klobuchar provides stable delay offsets, while NeQuick-G better accommodates temporal and spatial TEC changes, especially near the equator. These insights support improved GNSS reliability, next-generation correction algorithms, and resilient satellite communication in disturbed space environments.

title: Detection of Abnormal Behaviors in the Elderly Based on Multimodal Data
authors: Jiamin Liu, Chang Yu, Xinrong Zhu
abstract: As China gradually steps into an aging society, the proportion of the elderly in the total population is increasing day by day. At the same time, the security of older groups is becoming increasingly prominent. With age, the physical function of the elderly group deteriorates, the ability to control and coordinate the body is far less than that of the younger period, and the risk of accidental injury cannot be ignored. Therefore, the research on the detection methods of abnormal behavior of the elderly will help to reduce the phenomenon of serious disability and even death caused by accidental injuries. In this paper, we propose a model for detecting abnormal behavior of the elderly based on GoToV and Le2i multimodal data. The hybrid model established by SVM+ Random Forest (RF) algorithm and the model established based on XGBoost are used to detect abnormal behaviors such as acceleration outliers and falls in elderly activities as abnormal situations in elderly activities. By comparing the results of the two models, we can find out a more suitable model for detecting abnormal behavior of the elderly, which can help reduce the possibility of serious injury caused by accidents.

title: Digital Marketing Strategy of Fresh Food Based on Consumer Purchasing Behavior
authors: Siyu Wan, Mengyuan Li, Lebing Wang
abstract: In the context of China’s evolving consumer market, the fresh food sector presents significant opportunities. At the same time, consumers are not only concerned about the price of fruit, but are paying even greater attention to its quality, freshness, and purchasing convenience. Taking a Chinese brand, Goodfarmer, as an example, this paper examines the factors of consumers’ purchase intention of fresh food. Based on 289 valid samples, this research uses SPSS for empirical analysis, identifying four key marketing strategy factors: place, product, price, and promotion. This study further proposes targeted digital marketing strategies for the fresh food industry in the digital era.

title: Deep Graph Reinforcement Learning for UAV-to-Ground Secure Communication with Height-Dependent Line-of-Sight Estimation in Urban Scenarios
authors: Farman Ali, Irfan Khan, Mardeni Roslee, Yasir Ullah, Azmi Ismail, Idris O.A
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: This paper proposes a deep graph reinforcement learning (DGRL) framework for U2G secure communication, incorporating height-dependent LoS estimation into trajectory and security optimization. The proposed system models the UAV’s six-dimensional (6D) mobility while capturing spatial interactions between users, obstacles, and unauthorized receivers (UR) using a graph neural network (GNN). A soft actor–critic (SAC) RL algorithm is applied to optimize the UAV’s trajectory with maximizing the secrecy rate. The LoS probability is mathematically expressed as a function of UAV height and elevation angle, calibrated using ITU-R and 3GPP TR 38.901 specifications. To validate the framework, a ray tracing (RT) simulation environment is developed, modeling realistic urban scenario and multipath propagation based on ITU and 3GPP guidelines. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method enhances secure coverage, LoS reliability, and trajectory efficiency compared to baselines.

title: Manager-Executor: Collaboration Model Based on Hierarchy Multi-LLMs to Enhance the Efficiency of LLM Tasks
authors: Lichen Zhang, Wenke Li, Xinyue Huang, Zhenbo Zhang
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: With the additional growth in natural comprehension, rationality, and generation for large language models (llMs), the inefficiencies, non-scaling, and non-coordination issues for the case with multiple agents grow more severe. In this work, we use an organizational structure-inspired collaboration model between Manager and Executor for our system. Our system offloads global planning and the sub-task decomposition for Manager agents with large capacities and reserve the rest for Executor agents with lightweights for taskexecution. A Model Context Protocol (MCP), a format-based communication protocol, is specified for standardized inter-agent coordination. To assess our model, we experiment with our model under three normal NLP tasks: long document summarization, multi-turn question answering, and structured table generation. In assist, we use a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) module as an auxiliary accelerator. Experimental results demonstrate significant gains for our model for the measures of task-execution efficiency, memory consumption, and coordination quality under the single-agent baseline measure. Comparison with the recent systems like HALO and Agent Orchestra reveals the superiority for our system with static task decomposition, structure-based communication, and role partitioning.

title: Radar Odometry Using 3D Feature-Assisted ICP
authors: Yerang Mok, Hanyeol Lee, Chan Gook Park
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: In this study, we propose a radar odometry method based on iterative closest point (ICP) that incorporates 3D feature information. Radar-based localization has been actively studied due to the robust perception capabilities of radar, even under adverse environments. However, radar point clouds are typically sparse, noisy, and contain numerous outliers, which can degrade the accuracy of data association. To address this limitation, our registration method leverages local geometric structure in ICP through 3D feature descriptors. The method utilizes fast point feature histograms to establish initial correspondences between point clouds and integrates feature-based association into the registration process. This enables ICP registration to benefit from geometric structure, even in sparse radar point clouds. Experimental results on public radar datasets demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves improved localization accuracy compared to standard ICP approaches.

title: A Probability-Based Labeling Method Considering Interim Risk for Financial Time Series
authors: Zhiqi Wang, Feng Gu
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: The labeling strategies are well-explored in domains such as computer vision and language processing, but in the financial domain, they are underexplored. Unlike labeling in other domains, where the expert might already be involved, or a commonly accepted standard field discipline can be applied, financial labeling requires expertise and may still exhibit subjectivity biases. Thus, the automated labeling methods are necessary to promote improvement in the financial prediction field. Several studies have examined different labeling schemes. However, most work primarily focuses on improving the classification accuracy, often overlooking the true motivation behind the labeling. The labeling should generate actionable trading signals that help improve returns or manage risk, rather than just boosting the prediction accuracy. Failing to align labeling strategies with profitability objectives hinders the practical value of models for real-world trading applications. In addition, the existing labeling methods take into account point-to-point differences, overlooking the interim risk. This study introduces an automated, probability-based labeling method that considers the interim risk to address this gap. Based on the backtesting results, the proposed method demonstrates its effectiveness and applicability for practical investment.

title: Hyperchaos and Genetic Algorithm-Based Optimized S-Box for Secure Image Encryption
authors: Rim Amdouni, Mohamed Ali Hajjaji
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Substitution boxes (S-boxes) play a crucial role in the security of modern cryptographic systems, particularly in block ciphers and image encryption schemes. Traditional S-box design methods rely on algebraic and heuristic techniques, but these approaches often struggle to achieve an optimal balance between security and efficiency. In this paper, we propose a novel method for generating highly secure S-boxes by leveraging chaotic Boolean functions (CBFs) and genetic algorithms (GAs). The chaotic properties of Boolean functions provide a strong source of nonlinearity, while genetic algorithms optimize the cryptographic properties of the S-box by iteratively enhancing nonlinearity, differential uniformity, and avalanche characteristics. The generated S-box is evaluated using key security metrics and is integrated into an image encryption scheme to demonstrate its effectiveness. Experimental results confirm that the proposed method enhances security properties and improves resistance against common cryptographic attacks.

title: Development of Gamified Learning Website for Academic Integrity
authors: Oscar Karnalim, Cristianto Tri Arthurito, Swat Lie Liliawati
abstract: With the prevalence of breaches of academic integrity, several tools are dedicated to informing students about academic integrity. However, the tools either have limited ways to assess students’ knowledge or do not focus on user engagement. We develop a gamified learning website that covers five breaches of academic integrity: plagiarism, collusion, contract cheating, exam cheating, and research fraud. Unique to our website, it features gamification where students can get more game points by completing tasks or achieving high quiz scores. According to our evaluation, which involved 52 students and lecturers through three experiments, our website shows a promising impact on improving students’ awareness of academic integrity.

title: Uncovering Key Drivers of Absenteeism in Manufacturing: A Machine Learning-Based Feature Selection Study
authors: Myriam Gharbi, Rawaa Mhimdi, Sondes Hammami, Rim Ben Amara
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Absenteeism management is a critical issue for manufacturing companies that rely heavily on direct human labor for production. High absenteeism rates can significantly hinder operational productivity. This paper aims to identify the most influential features affecting absenteeism in a textile production plant. The study begins with thorough data preprocessing, including handling missing values and outliers. Subsequently, various analytical techniques, particularly from the field of machine learning, are applied to determine the most relevant predictors of absenteeism. Specifically, we apply four complementary feature selection techniques: Mutual Information, Correlation Analysis, Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE), and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). The results reveal that 9 out of 36 features play a significant role in explaining absenteeism patterns. Identifying these key variables provides valuable insights for decision-makers to better target their efforts and enhance absenteeism management strategies.

title: Data Driven Evasion Policy for Repeated Pursuit Evasion Problem with Unknown Imperfect Pursuer
authors: Linkun He, Ran Zhang, Huifeng Li
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: A novel repeated pursuit-evasion (PE) problem with unknown imperfect pursuer is proposed and addressed using model based reinforcement learning. Grounded in aerospace applications, the problem adopts a repeated game formulation, where the evader engages repeatedly with the pursuer with unknown imperfect policy for multiple rounds, and the objective for the evader is to optimize the evasion policy based on data of preceding rounds so that evasion is achieved with minimum number of rounds. Unlike previous works that rely on prior knowledge of the pursuer's policy, our approach focus on efficient data exploitation in each round. Specifically, we adopt the probabilistic inference for learning control (PILCO) method, where a Gaussian process is adopted to model the unknown imperfect pursuer. This allows for efficient data exploitation and transforms the problem into the deterministic pay-off optimization with analytical gradients, enabling successive policy optimization in each round. Numerical results on a typical aerospace application demonstrate that the proposed method achieves successful evasion in remarkably few rounds, with the optimized policy aligning closely with the analytical optimal solution assuming complete knowledge of the pursuer.

title: Weighted Spatial Encoding Grids for Digital Twin City Applications Using Implicit Neural Representations of Environmental Factors and Geometric Complexity
authors: Pengfei Xu, Bin Zhang, Xuren Deng, Shi Zhou, Ranyu Chen, Huiming Luo
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: With the rapid development of digital twin city platforms, urban multimodal data have grown explosively, making efficient spatial querying and computation a critical challenge. Conventional grid-based encoding methods hierarchically partition 3D space but struggle to capture nonlinear geometric features and exhibit low query efficiency in large-scale scenarios. This paper proposes a weighted spatial grid partitioning method that integrates geometric complexity with environmental factors. A neural network is used to learn a continuous mapping from spatial coordinates to environmental factor intensities, enabling interpolation at arbitrary positions. These outputs, combined with geometric complexity, drive a weighting model that adaptively subdivides high-importance grids. Considered factors include airflow, illumination, magnetic field, and GPS signals, with support for incremental extension to additional factors. A case study on low-altitude economy applications incorporates wind speed as a key factor for aerial vehicle flight. Experimental results show that the proposed method significantly improves spatial query efficiency compared with traditional approaches.

title: Energy-Saving Control Method for Fully Electro-Hydraulic Positive Flow Excavators Based on Optimal Flow Distribution
authors: Yifan Sun, Yi Xi, Guoqiang Gao, Huzhao Ling, Chaofu Liu, Yuchao Liu, Shuyi Yang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: To enhance the operational efficiency of fully electronically controlled constant flow control excavator electrohydraulic systems, this study investigates dual-pump flow distribution strategies for energy conservation, targeting maximum pump efficiency. Firstly, a high-precision pump efficiency model for plunger pumps was developed using machine learning methods. Experimental validation demonstrated that the model's predicted efficiency values exhibited a relative error of merely 3.98% compared to experimental measurements, significantly outperforming the computational accuracy of traditional theoretical models. Subsequently, an optimisation function targeting minimal system input power was formulated based on this pump efficiency model. The influence of different flow distribution patterns on system input power under varying pressure, rotational speed, and flow conditions was analysed, yielding the most energy-efficient dual-pump flow distribution strategy for each operating scenario. Finally, following validation of the experimental simulation model's accuracy, dynamic response characteristics of the hydraulic system under different flow distribution schemes were simulated. The research findings indicate that: when the system's total flow demand does not exceed the maximum flow capacity of a single pump, independent oil supply from a single pump is the most energy-efficient approach; when the system's total flow demand exceeds the supply capacity of a single pump, equal flow distribution between both pumps is more energy-efficient; the flow distribution strategy has negligible impact on the system's response characteristics. Consequently, the proposed flow distribution strategy is applicable to the control systems of existing excavators.

title: Series-Arc Analysis Using a Normalized FFT Harmonic Energy Ratio in Power Distribution Systems with SFCL
authors: Min-Ho Yoon, Seung-Su Choi, Sung-Hun Lim
abstract: Detecting series arc faults in motor-connected distribution systems is challenging due to the dynamic impedance of induction motors. The inclusion of a Resistive Superconducting Fault Current Limiter (SFCL) further complicates detection by potentially dampening the high-frequency arc signatures required for identification. This paper experimentally investigates whether a harmonic-based detection metric remains robust under the combined influence of motor inrush dynamics and SFCL resistive operation. We utilize a three-cycle sliding Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to compute a band-limited harmonic energy ratio, H(k) over the 200–2000 Hz band. Tests compare wye and delta starting methods and two arc locations: the main feeder (Case 1) and the motor branch (Case 2). Experimental results show that under the same line voltage, the delta connection produced a larger natural inrush(≈65A) compared to the wye connection (≈26A). The SFCL effectively functioned as a soft-starter, limiting the delta inrush to 35 A via a resistive quench(≈80V). Although this resistive damping attenuated the absolute arc current spectra, the normalized metric H(k) successfully distinguished arc faults. In Case 1, H(k) consistently exceeded the baseline by a factor of 3 to 8. In Case 2, despite a masking effect caused by the parallel resistive load under wye starting, the metric remained reliable, showing a distinct 5-fold increase in the delta configuration and a gradual rise at the motor terminals. This study validates that the proposed harmonic-energy metric provides a practical, connection-aware indicator for series arc detection, capable of overcoming the signal suppression introduced by the SFCL.

title: Prospects of kinetic inductance current sensors for multiplexed microcalorimeter readout
authors: Galen C. O'Neil, Simon R. Bandler, Ian Fogarty Florang, John A. Mates, Daniel S. Swetz, Thomas R. Stevenson, Leaf A. Swordy, Joel N. Ullom, Michael R. Vissers, Jordan D. Wheeler, Wonsik Yoon, Paul Szypryt
abstract: Superconducting microcalorimeter arrays with hundreds to thousands of pixels are enabling new measurements and capabilities in exotic atom spectroscopy, x-ray astronomy, nuclear materials analysis, and many other fields. We consider the prospects of multiplexing microcalorimeters with the kinetic inductance current sensor (KICS), an alternative to the superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) historically used to read out cryogenic microcalorimeters. We show that the amplifier limited noise of the KICS is determined by its dynamic range, which is a free design parameter and tuneable with a DC current bias. We predict that KICS can meet the requirements to read out prototype metallic magnetic calorimeter (MMC) pixels designed for the Lynx x-ray satellite mission concept with the use of low-latency tone tracking. We propose a KICS design that has both high coupling efficiency and high isolation between the RF and DC components of the circuit. We argue that the KICS provides engineering trade-offs that are better suited to the readout of microcalorimeters when compared to the analogous microwave SQUID multiplexer.

title: How Virtual Presence Co-creation of Online Service Recovery Affect Observers?
authors: Wei Li, Ying Liu
abstract: Social media virtual presence contribution co-creation of the recovery response by interacting or communicating with the complainant and the company in webcare. This is important for the company, but the effect on observers are not receiving sufficient attention. This study focuses on the background of social media, to explore the effects of interactive virtual presence after different levels of online service recovery on observers' brand attitudes, as well as the influence mechanism mediated by the attribution of locus and attribution of stability. The scenario-based experiment shows that when service recovery is successful or unsuccessful, positive interactive virtual presence can increase the observers' brand attitudes; when service recovery is very successful or successful, negative interactive virtual presence significantly decrease the observers' brand attitudes; the attribution of stability mediates the effect of interactive virtual presence on observers' brand attitudes.

title: Superior Electromagnetic Shielding for Non-Bearing Masonry Walls Using Innovative Conductive Infills and FRCC Coatings
authors: Nassreddien Hussien, Mostafa Ahmed Nasr, Mark Farag, Hend Malhat, Nasser Qaddoumi, Sherif Yehia
abstract: This article presents two innovative methods to enhance electromagnetic (EM) shielding in nonbearing masonry walls constructed from normal-weight concrete (NC) hollow blocks. Two different block sizes are investigated, 400 × 200 × 200 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> and 400 × 200 × 150 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. The first method involves filling hollow NC blocks with either NC or two conductive concrete mixes (M1 and M2), incorporating carbon and fiber additives. It achieves shielding effectiveness of 20–36 dB across 1–10 GHz, significantly exceeding that of NC-filled blocks (1–25 dB). The second method applies a 2 cm fiber-reinforced conductive composite (FRCC) coating, in planar or corrugated configurations, to one or both sides of masonry walls. Double-sided FRCC coatings, particularly with corrugated surfaces, deliver superior SE up to 50 dB, compared to 10–25 dB for uncoated walls, with scattering reducing reflections. Simulations using ANSYS high-frequency structure simulator confirm these findings, indicating power transmission of less than 1% for FRCC-coated walls and up to 96% absorption for conical corrugated designs. Both methods demonstrate robust EM-shielding across diverse frequencies and illumination directions, offering practical solutions for nonbearing masonry walls in civil engineering applications requiring enhanced EM interference protection.

title: Spatio-Temporal Hypergraph Attention Networks for Brain Disease Analysis
authors: Chaojun Li, Peiliang Gong, Shengrong Li, Chunwei Tian, Yinbo Yu, Ran Wang, Daoqiang Zhang, Qi Zhu
abstract: Functional brain connectivity networks capture complex relationships and temporal evolution between brain regions, which have become increasingly important for diagnosing neurological disorders. However, existing methods, which are primarily based on vector or graph representations, struggle to adequately characterize the intricate spatio-temporal topological architecture of functional brain networks. Additionally, they predominantly rely on data-driven paradigms and lack priors pertaining to cross-windows network interactions. To address these issues, we propose a spatio-temporal hypergraph attention network framework for brain network analysis. Specifically, we first propose a temporal attention network architecture embedded with temporal similarity-driven prior knowledge, which effectively extracts long-range dependency information from fMRI by combining multi-head self-attention mechanisms and cross-window temporal prior knowledge. Second, we design a hierarchical hypergraph generation module that fuses local and global brain topological information to achieve multi-scale modeling of high-order spatio-temporal structures. Additionally, the spatial attention network, developed based on transformer architecture, employs hypergraph message passing mechanisms to effectively construct multi-level spatial interaction relationships between brain regions. Finally, a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) is adopted for classification. Experiments on the ADNI and PD datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms several state-of-the-art approaches in diagnostic performance and provides discriminative graph features for relevant brain disease diagnosis.

title: ActPrompt: In-Domain Feature Adaptation via Action Cues for Video Temporal Grounding
authors: Yubin Wang, Xinyang Jiang, De Cheng, Dongsheng Li, Cairong Zhao
abstract: Video temporal grounding, including moment retrieval and highlight detection, is an emerging topic aiming to identify specific clips within videos. In addition to pre-trained video models, contemporary methods utilize pre-trained visionlanguage models (VLMs) to capture detailed characteristics of diverse scenes and objects from video frames. However, as pretrained on images, directly using pre-extracted VLM features neglects the domain gap between the pre-trained and temporal grounding datasets, thus inducing domain shifts due to the data-level distribution disparity. As a result, VLMs may struggle to distinguish action-sensitive patterns from static objects, making it necessary to adapt them to specific data domains for effective feature representation over temporal grounding. In this work, we address two primary challenges to achieve this goal. Specifically, to mitigate high adaptation costs, we propose an efficient preliminary in-domain fine-tuning paradigm for feature adaptation before standard downstream training, where downstream-adaptive features are learned through several well-designed pretext tasks that ensure improved performance. Furthermore, to integrate action-sensitive information into VLMs, we introduce Action-Cue-Injected Temporal Prompt Learning (ActPrompt), which injects action cues into the image encoder of VLMs to discover action-sensitive visual patterns better. This is followed by context-aware temporal prompt learning, which considers both action cues and temporal context to enhance the ability to recognize patterns associated with actions for downstream tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ActPrompt is an off-the-shelf training framework that can be applied effectively to various SOTA methods, resulting in notable improvements.

title: A dual attention based selective ensemble prediction method for remaining useful life
authors: Feng Luo, Guojin Si, Yijing Fan, Xiao Wu, Yiyang Wang, Tangbin Xia, Lifeng Xi
abstract: With the increasing complexity and scale of modern equipment systems, remaining useful life (RUL) prediction faces three major challenges: (1) feature redundancy in multi-source sensor data, (2) difficulty in modeling long-term temporal dependencies in degradation processes, and (3) multi-modal degradation patterns arising from multiple failure modes. To overcome these challenges, this study proposes a novel prediction framework that integrates dual-attention temporal convolutional networks with a selective ensemble strategy driven by particle swarm optimization (DATCN-PSOSEN). Specifically, the model incorporates channel attention at the input end of the TCN structure, enabling the model to adaptively evaluate the importance of sensor channels. Simultaneously, the temporal attention mechanism applies weighting to the time dimension of features extracted by the TCN, enhancing the model’s ability to dynamically capture temporal dependencies, thus forming the DATCN base learner. Multiple sliding window lengths are used to diversify base learners. Additionally, the PSOSEN employs the PSO algorithm to effectively enhance the model’s robustness by automatically pruning and weighting base learners. Extensive experiments on the C-MAPSS dataset show that the proposed DATCN-PSOSEN achieves superior overall performance compared to state-of-the-art RUL prediction models. Notably, it reduces RMSE by up to 14.2% and Score by 24.7% relative to a typical CNN-based baseline (DCNN).

title:
Aluminum Nanoparticle-Enhanced a-Ga
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Solar-Blind UV Photodetectors with High-Performance via Localized Surface Plasmon Resonance
authors: Jiao Fu, Meiqi Wang, Fei Wang, Zuoxi Zhao, Shaoqing Wang, Xiangtai Liu, Zhan Wang, Haifeng Chen, Yunhe Guan, Daming Chen, Qin Lu, Lingfei Li, Yachao Zhang, Jincheng Zhang
abstract: Gallium oxide (Ga2O3), as an emerging ultra-wide-bandgap semiconductor materials, has demonstrated significant potential in solar-blind ultraviolet photodetection. This study prepared aluminum nanoparticles (NPs) to significantly improve the performance of amorphous Ga2O3 (a-Ga2O3) metal-semiconductor-metal (MSM) solar-blind ultraviolet photodetectors. Under a 10 V bias voltage, the Al-NPs/a-Ga2O3 device achieve a peak responsivity (R) of 2.24 × 103 A W-1, an external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 1.1 × 106 %, and a specific detectivity (D*) of 1.4 × 1012 Jones, corresponding to improvements of 597 %, 1867 %, and 611 %, respectively, compared to pristine a-Ga2O3 photodetectors without Al-NPs. These enhancements are mainly ascribed to the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect generated by the Al-NPs, which effectively enhances the light absorption and carrier excitation, thereby improving the ultraviolet signal detection capability of the device. This work provides a simple, low-cost and easily reproducible method that offers new ways and possibilities to optimize the performance of optoelectronic devices.

title: Control Profile Derivation for Collision Imminent Steering via Solutions of Large-Scale Optimization Problem
authors: Chanwoo Heo, Gayoen Ahn, Aldo Sorniotti, Seunghoon Woo
abstract: In collision-imminent situations where braking alone is insufficient, integrated steering control is an effective avoidance measure. However, a generalized strategy that minimizes the required avoidance distance by fully leveraging a vehicle’s physical limits has not been established, as prior studies have focused on computational challenges or proposing new architectures. This paper proposes a framework to derive such a strategy by defining a large-scale optimization problem that integrates steering, traction, and braking. The most effective control profiles are identified from numerous solutions using a clustering algorithm. This data-driven approach overcomes the initial value dependency of non-convex optimization, enabling the discovery of globally optimal strategies. The derived optimal strategy exhibits novel control methods including the use of traction force near an obstacle and enables collision avoidance at shorter distances than conventional real-time controllers. These results suggest that a control structure with separated path generation and tracking is effective for implementing the proposed strategy. This study provides a foundational reference for designing advanced controllers for collision-imminent situations and a base framework for various derivative studies.

title: Structure-Aware Conditional Diffusion Generation for Incomplete Multi-View Clustering
authors: Yuanyang Zhang, Yijie Lin, Xinhang Wan, Jie Xu, Li Yao, Weiqing Yan, Chang Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Incomplete multi-view clustering (IMVC) has attracted increasing attention in recent years, owing to the prevalence of missing data in real-world multi-view scenarios. Existing imputation-based IMVC methods partially mitigate the impact of missing information but still face three key limitations: (i) overlooking latent structural relationships among samples, which leads to imputed representations deviating from the true distribution; (ii) decoupling imputation from clustering, which reduces the discriminability of the recovered representations; and (iii) exhibiting low efficiency, which makes it difficult to balance recovery quality and inference speed under complex missing scenarios. To address these issues, we propose a Structure-Aware Conditional Diffusion Generation (SACDG) framework. During training, SACDG first models local structural relationships via adaptive neighborhood graphs and injects them as conditional priors into the diffusion model, where a cross-attention mechanism integrates these priors into the noise prediction process to learn structure-aware generative capability. Meanwhile, a semantic distribution alignment module is introduced to leverage pseudo-labels for enforcing cross-view consistency, thereby enhancing semantic discriminability. During inference, SACDG integrates cross-view structural information through cross-view adjacency fusion to guide the reverse denoising trajectory, and employs deterministic DDIM sampling to efficiently and stably recover the representations of missing views. Extensive comparative experiments and ablation studies on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that SACDG achieves superior clustering performance and improved efficiency over state-of-the-art methods. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/zhangyuanyang21/SACDG</uri>.

title: SS-RAM: Spatial-Spectral Attribution Interpretability for HyperSpectral Imagery
authors: K. Venkataraman, P. Viswanath, P.V. Arun, Satish Balantrapu
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: This work proposes a novel unified explainability framework “Spatial–Spectral Relevance Activation Mapping” (SS-RAM), designed for Hyperspectral Images (HSI) super-resolution models. SS-RAM integrates three attribution mechanisms: relevance conservation via layer-wise relevance propagation, spatial localization through higher-order gradient-based activation mapping, and spectral fidelity via band-wise contribution analysis. These attributions are adaptively fused using a band-specific learnable coefficient, optimized through a multi-objective loss that jointly enforces fidelity, conservation, and faithfulness without manual tuning of weighting parameters. This framework is implemented as a post-hoc analysis on a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)-based HSI super-resolution backbone, and SS-RAM produces interpretable heatmaps that simultaneously capture where the model attends spatially and which spectral bands drive the reconstruction. The experiments on four benchmarked HSI datasets (Indian Pines, Salinas, Pavia University, and WHU-Hi-LongKou) demonstrate near-perfect relevance conservation (≈0.999), high faithfulness (≈0.99), and balanced attribution sparsity, consistently outperforming single-source explainability methods such as LRP, Grad-CAM++, and Shapley values. The proposed framework provides robust and physically meaningful insights into deep HSI generative models there by enhancing trust and interpretability in remote sensing applications.

title: GMTNet: Global Feature Integration Mamba-Transformer Hybrid Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
authors: Zhengqian Feng, Xiaofeng Li, Yuan Gao, Wang Li, Gang Li, Mingle Zhou
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Infrared Small Target detection (IRSTD) is crucial for surveillance, rescue and early warning applications, but it still faces many challenges due to weak signal, limited texture and clutteredbackground. Existing methods based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) lack global context information, while methods based on Transformers and state-space models may be too complex or too smooth, resulting in missed detections. We propose a Mamba-Transformer hybrid network called GMTNet, which is able to selectively fuse global semantics and local details. GMTNet contains three key components: (1) a directional state-space Transformer module for combining direction-aware long-range modeling with fine local cues; (2) a wavelet-based multidimensional lossless downsampling module to preserve high-frequency information during resolution reduction; (3) DualAug module for combining geometric and photometric perturbations for robustness. Experiments on IRSTD-1K, NUAA-SIRST and NUDT-SIRST show that GMTNet consistently outperforms the state of the art methods and can provide robust and efficient infrared small target detection.
