title: AMPD: Adaptive Motion Planning Diffusion for Robot Motion Planning in Complex Environments
authors: Xiangxi Chen, Yuzhi Jiang, Renzhi Lu
abstract: Robot motion planning is a core technology for complex manipulation tasks, widely applied in industrial automation, human-robot collaboration, and service robotics. Traditional planning methods face a dilemma between efficiency and solution quality under complex constraints: sampling-based methods can be inefficient, optimization-based methods may get trapped in local optima, and learning-based methods often generalize poorly. Recently, diffusion-model-based planners have achieved high-quality trajectory generation and can incorporate constraints via gradient guidance. However, most existing approaches apply step-wise guidance at every reverse step, causing excessive inference time; moreover, guidance in early high-noise stages is often low-fidelity and yields limited benefit, wasting computation. We propose AMPD (Adaptive Motion Planning Diffusion), which leverages diffusion noise dynamics to improve real-time efficiency while preserving feasibility. AMPD introduces two complementary designs: (1) a smoothness-aware training objective (SmoothLoss) that shapes the learned prior toward physically plausible trajectories, reducing the need for corrective guidance; and (2) a time-adaptive guidance schedule that allocates guidance computation to denoising stages where gradients are more informative. Experiments on the densely constrained EnvNarrowPassageDense2D benchmark demonstrate that AMPD improves both efficiency and safety over the diffusion baseline MPD, reducing inference time from 1.184s to 0.812s (~1.46× speedup) while increasing the feasible-sample fraction from 73.10% to 91.40%, and decreasing collision intensity from 0.74% to 0.08%. Furthermore, extended evaluations on an extreme maze environment validate the planner’s scalability. AMPD maintains robust performance (89.2% success rate, sub-second inference) where baselines fail due to local optima or timeouts.

title: A Six-Phase Soft-Switching Motor Driving Topology and Its Modulation Strategy for Low Common-Mode Voltage
authors: Hui Zou, Zisen Lin, Shangzhi Pan, Wenqiang Lin, Ruixuan Wang, Jinwu Gong, Xiaoming Zha
abstract: This article proposes a six phase synchronous auxiliary resonant commutated pole soft switching inverter (6P-SARCP) for dual three-phase permanent magnet synchronous motor (DT-PMSM). By introducing two resonant inductors, the proposed new topology realizes the zero voltage turn-on condition of all the main circuit switches, which significantly reduces the switching loss of the main switching elements in the working process. On the other hand, this topology uses edge aligned PWM (EAPWM) scheme, which not only simplifies the control process of the auxiliary branch, but also further reduces the demand for controller resources. Further analysis shows that the modulation method can keep the system running efficiently and suppress the common mode voltage of the double three-phase system to 0.167·V DC. To verify the effectiveness and practicability of the above theoretical analysis results, a detailed simulation study and experimental tests are carried out. The final data show that the proposed 6P-SARCP topology combined with EAPWM modulation strategy performs well in improving the performance of DT-PMSM, and has high engineering application value.

title: Empathy: The Missing Ingredient in Software Engineering
authors: Sue Black

title: A General and Non-Destructive P-Type Doping Strategy towards Two-Dimensional Complementary Technology
authors: Guang-Hua Xu, Shiyou Chen, Yue-Yang Liu
abstract: One stumbling block for two-dimensional (2D) materials moving towards logic circuits is the lack of controllable and high-quality p-type 2D semiconductors. Here, by performing ab initio calculations on a series of atomistic interface models, we elucidate the mechanism behind an effective and universal strategy to modulate 2D semiconductors to p-type without damaging the channel material itself. This is realized by stacking the 2D semiconductors on a 2D hole reservoir material, typically CrOCl, so that the semiconductors release electrons and absorb holes. The strategy is confirmed to be effective for MoS2, MoSe2, and WSe2 with single or multiple layers. An analytical formula is further derived to relate the defect concentration, material thickness, and the degree of p-type modulation, providing design guidelines for this non-destructive doping strategy. Hopefully, this general and non-destructive p-type doping strategy will promote the application of emerging 2D semiconductors in CMOS technology and logic circuits.

title: A Wild Terrain Segmentation Network for Navigable Areas via Entropy-guided Global Sparsification and Dynamic Attention Fusion
authors: Wei Li, Muxin Liao, Wenbin Zou
abstract: Global semantic and multi-scale semantic-spatial information have been shown to be critical for semantic segmentation. However, extracting these features often involves high computational costs and fails to fully leverage the complementary relationships between multi-scale features, presenting a challenge for terrain segmentation. In this paper, we propose the Wild Terrain Segmentation Network (WTSNet), which aggregates these two feature types while ensuring notable performance for autonomous navigation systems in distinguishing navigable areas within wild environments. WTSNet consists of two core modules: the Entropy-guided Global Sparsification Module (EGSM) and the Dynamic Attention Fusion Module (DAFM). The EGSM, comprising the Entropy-guided Block and Global Sparsification Block, utilizes information entropy and Top-K sparsification as thresholds to enable deep global semantic modeling with low computational cost. Meanwhile, the DAFM integrates the Dynamic Fusion Block and Attention Modulation Block, progressively aggregating multi-scale semantic-spatial features by exploiting the complementary channel-spatial relationships between neighboring features. Extensive experiments on various wild terrain datasets demonstrate that WTSNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods in identifying navigable areas, showcasing its robust ability to support the safety of autonomous navigation systems in wild environments. The code for WTSNet will be made available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/lv881314/WTSNet</uri>.

title: DeDiff-4DGS: Fusing Temporal Correlations and Diffusion Priors for Dynamic 3D Scenes
authors: Hoang Nguyen Nguyen, Wei Xiang, Kang Han, Lu Yu, Phu Lai, Tianyu Chen, Yi-Ping Phoebe Chen
abstract: Reconstructing dynamic 3D (4D) scenes is challenging due to complex temporal dynamics and viewpoint sparsity in monocular videos. Existing extensions of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D-GS) with its temporal modeling often fail to capture temporal correlations across frames, leading to redundant 3D Gaussians and reduced efficiency. To address this limitation, we propose DeDiff-4DGS, a framework that integrates temporal correlations and diffusion priors through two novel modules. The Temporal 3D Gaussian Latent Fusion (T3DLF) module fuses temporal information from sparse reference frames to promote spatio-temporal coherence and reduce the number of required 3D Gaussians. The Latent Diffusion Converter for 3D Gaussians (LDC3D) module enriches reference frames with semantic priors, complementing T3DLF under sparse-view conditions. Experimental results on standard benchmarks demonstrate that DeDiff-4DGS delivers higher reconstruction quality and improved efficiency over current state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Real-Time Prediction of Player Engagement from Multimodal Data
authors: Ammar Rashed, Shervin Shirmohammadi, Ihab Amer, Mohamed Hefeeda
abstract: Predicting player engagement in video games offers significant advantages for game design and quality assessment. Current approaches for predicting engagement, however, are costly, invasive, and/or suffer from recall bias, which limits their employability in practice. This paper presents a new multimodal neural network model for engagement prediction and conducts systematic analyses to identify practical solutions for real-world applications. We develop a hybrid architecture combining convolutional downsampling with transformer-based sequence modeling that effectively processes temporal data from multiple sensing channels, including EEG, eye tracking, and webcam footage. Through comprehensive comparative analysis, we determine which modalities and specific features most strongly contribute to engagement detection. Our evaluation demonstrates that webcam-based approaches utilizing geometric facial features and deep learning-based emotional embeddings achieve performance comparable to specialized biometric equipment while requiring only standard hardware. Feature attribution analysis reveals that head pose dynamics and facial embeddings are the most influential engagement signals, with neurophysiological features showing limited contribution. Cross-modal analysis confirms that facial emotion-based combinations achieve the strongest performance. Statistical power analysis reveals participant variability, highlighting generalization challenges. These findings demonstrate that effective engagement measurement is achievable using accessible webcam technology, offering an optimal balance between accuracy and accessibility for widespread commercial implementation in gaming contexts.

title: Pneumatic Multi-mode Silicone Actuator with Pressure, Vibration, and Cold Thermal Feedback
authors: Mohammad Shadman Hashem, Ahsan Raza, Sama E Shan, Seokhee Jeon
abstract: A wide range of haptic feedback is crucial for achieving high realism and immersion in virtual environments. Therefore, a multi-modal haptic interface that provides various haptic signals simultaneously is highly beneficial. This paper introduces a novel silicone fingertip actuator that is pneumatically actuated, delivering a realistic and effective haptic experience by simultaneously providing pressure, vibrotactile, and cold thermal feedback. The actuator features a design with multiple air chambers, each with controllable volume achieved through pneumatic valves connected to compressed air tanks. The lower air chamber generates pressure feedback, while the upper chamber produces vibrotactile feedback. In addition, two lateral air nozzles utilize a vortex tube to generate a cold thermal sensation. To showcase the system's capabilities, we designed two unique 3D surfaces in the virtual environment: a frozen meat surface and an abrasive icy surface. These surfaces simulate tactile perceptions of coldness, pressure, and texture. Comprehensive performance assessments and user studies were conducted to validate the actuator's effectiveness, highlighting its diverse feedback capabilities compared to traditional actuators that offer only single feedback modalities.

title: Optimizing High-Level Synthesis Sparse Matrix-Vector Kernel for Alveo FPGAs
authors: Federico Favaro, Ernesto Dufrechou, Juan P Oliver, Pablo Ezzatti
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Sparse matrix-vector multiplication (SpMV) is a critical kernel in scientific computing and high-performance applications, yet it remains challenging to optimize due to irregular memory access patterns and low computational intensity. This work targets the AMD Vitis SpMV kernel on HBM-equipped Alveo FPGAs and introduces hardware-aware optimizations that significantly improve performance, especially in reduced precision scenarios. Building on prior evaluations of the Vitis kernel, we propose two key enhancements: coefficient grouping and index packet coalescing. These reduce padding overhead and improve memory bandwidth efficiency without requiring a complete redesign of the vendor-supplied kernel. Experimental results over 33 matrices from the SuiteSparse collection show up to 4× speedup and an average improvement of 1.6× compared to the original Vitis kernel. Our method demonstrates how practical architectural adaptations can yield substantial performance gains, supporting the growing trend toward lower-precision and energy-efficient computing in FPGA-based accelerators.

title: Customized Interior-Point Methods Solver for Embedded Real-Time Convex Optimization
authors: Jae-Il Jang, Chang-Hun Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper presents a customized second-order cone programming (SOCP) solver tailored for embedded real-time optimization, which frequently arises in modern guidance and control (G&C) applications. The solver employs a practically efficient predictor-corrector type primal-dual interior-point method (PDIPM) combined with a homogeneous embedding framework for infeasibility detection. Unlike conventional homogeneous self dual embedding formulations, the adopted approach can directly handle quadratic cost functions without requiring problem re formulation. This capability allows the solver to directly address quadratic objective SOCP problems, while avoiding unnecessary performance degradation caused by the loss of sparsity due to problem reformulation. To support a systematic workflow, we also develop a code generation tool that analyzes the sparsity pattern of the problem to be solved and generates customized solver code using a predefined code template. The generated solver code is written in C with no external dependencies other than the standard library math.h, and it supports complete static allocation of all data. Additionally, it provides parsing information to facilitate the use of the solver by end users. Finally, benchmark and numerical experiments on an embedded platform demonstrate that the developed solver outperforms the existing solvers on problem scales typical of G&C applications.

title: Dynamic Regret for Byzantine-Robust Online Federated Learning
authors: Haibao Tian, Qiyong He, Zhihai Qu, Xiuxian Li
abstract: Federated learning enables decentralized model training across multiple clients without exchanging raw data, making it a crucial paradigm for privacy-preserving machine learning. However, its deployment in adversarial and dynamic environments remains fundamentally challenging, particularly under Byzantine attacks. Existing online Byzantine-robust methods face two critical limitations: (i) a strong reliance on the Independent and Identically Distributed (IID) assumption to achieve sublinear regret, and (ii) an inability to adapt to non-stationary environments due to their reliance on static regret metrics. These challenges substantially limit their applicability in practical federated learning scenarios characterized by heterogeneity and non-stationarity. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Byzantine-robust online algorithm, BR-OMGD, which performs multiple local gradient descent updates and incorporates a robust aggregation mechanism to mitigate adversarial effects under the weak growth condition. The algorithm achieves a nearoptimal dynamic regret bound of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(S* T )$</tex-math></inline-formula> without requiring IID assumptions, where <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$S* T$</tex-math></inline-formula> denotes the squared path-length. When the online problem reduces to the offline case, BR-OMGD further guarantees exact linear convergence under strong convexity, smoothness, and the weak growth condition, even in the presence of data heterogeneity and Byzantine adversaries. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first result establishing such convergence guarantees under these conditions. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-100) demonstrate the proposed algorithms practical effectiveness, showing improved robustness and lower dynamic regret under Byzantine attacks.

title: UTOPY: Unrolling Algorithm Learning via Fidelity Homotopy for Inverse Problems
authors: Roman Jacome, Romario Gualdrón-Hurtado, Leon Suarez-Rodriguez, Henry Arguello
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Imaging Inverse problems aim to reconstruct an underlying image from undersampled, coded, and noisy observations. Within the wide range of reconstruction frameworks, the unrolling algorithm is one of the most popular due to the synergistic integration of traditional model-based reconstruction methods and modern neural networks, providing an interpretable and highly accurate reconstruction. However, when the sensing operator is highly ill-posed, gradient steps on the data-fidelity term can hinder convergence and degrade reconstruction quality. To address this issue, we propose UTOPY, a homotopy continuation formulation for training the unrolling algorithm. Mainly, this method involves using a well-posed (synthetic) sensing matrix at the beginning of the unrolling network optimization. We define a continuation path strategy to transition smoothly from the synthetic fidelity to the desired ill-posed problem. This strategy enables the network to progressively transition from a simpler, well-posed inverse problem to the more challenging target scenario. We theoretically show that, for projected gradient descent-like unrolling models, the proposed continuation strategy generates a smooth path of unrolling solutions. Experiments on compressive sensing and image deblurring demonstrate that our method consistently surpasses conventional unrolled training, achieving up to 2.5 dB PSNR improvement in reconstruction performance. Source code at https://github.com/romanjacome99/utopy

title: Design and Implementation of an Interactive Robotics Training Platform Using BlockMicc Programming
authors: Kridsada Wangkaew, Artit Suanpraserth, Sathaporn Suprom, Sethakarn Prongnuch
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of an interactive robotics training platform using the BlockMicc visual programming environment. The system integrates blockbased programming with real-time hardware control through an Arduino UNO R4 Wi-Fi microcontroller, enabling users to create and execute robot programs via a web-based drag-and-drop interface. The hardware platform incorporates LM35 temperature, MQ-2 gas, and HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensors, along with servo motors, RGB LEDs, and DC motors mounted on a Wall-E LEGO robot. Two experiments were conducted to validate the software functionality and hardware responses. All 22 software and 6 hardware tests achieved a 100% success rate, confirming stable communication and reliable system performance. The results demonstrate that BlockMicc provides an intuitive and effective tool for robotics education, simplifying programming and enhancing hands-on learning.

title: Deep Temporal Causal Discovery: A Framework for Uncovering Nonlinear and Time-Lagged Dynamics
authors: Jun Xiang Jordan Chan, Jun Yu Chai, Hong Liang Ong, Immanuel Jun Kai Loh, Zhong Hao Benson Tan, Xiaorong Li, Elisa Ang Yun Mei, Victor Wang Peng Cheng
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Understanding causal mechanisms underlying sealevel variability is essential for environmental forecasting and coastal resilience planning. Traditional statistical and machine learning approaches often capture associations but fail to model nonlinear, time-lagged dependencies or provide interpretability. This paper investigates the Temporal Causal Discovery Framework (TCDF), a deep learning-based method for uncovering nonlinear and lagged causal relationships in multivariate climate time series. The framework is enhanced with configurable hyperparameters, attention-based visualizations, and efficient caching mechanisms, all integrated within a modular, web-based causal discovery platform. Comparative experiments with baseline methods, such as Granger Causality, PCMCI, ICALiNGAM, and GES, demonstrate TCDF's unique capability to reveal hidden nonlinear dependencies and adaptive lag structures in real-world datasets. Results show that TCDF consistently identifies robust causal drivers of Sea Level Anomalies (SLA) and Water Level (WL), validated through noise, permutation, and placebo-based refutation tests. Overall, TCDF provides a practical, interpretable, and scalable framework for causal-aware environmental forecasting.

title: A Machine Learning Approach for Intervention Recommendation and Production Forecasting in Mature Ecuadorian Oil Fields
authors: Estefi Batallas-Riofrío, Ricardo Flores-Moyano, Maria Baldeon-Calisto, Felipe Grijalva
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: A machine learning (ML) approach is presented to optimize decision-making in well intervention planning for mature oil fields in Ecuador. Supervised classification and regression are used to automate two critical tasks: (1) recommending the most appropriate intervention type, and (2) forecasting the expected fluid production increment ($\triangle \mathrm{BFPD}$) postintervention. Drawing on four integrated historical databases encompassing over 76,000 records of production, pressure, and reservoir attributes, a structured pipeline was implemented, including pre-processing, feature selection, and class balancing using SMOTE + TomekLinks. For activity classification, Random Forest paired with SelectKBest yielded the best performance, with an F1-macro score of $\mathbf{0. 7 9}$. For production forecasting, XGBoost achieved an $R^{2}$ of 0.728 and MAE of 219.11 for REPERF interventions. Because of the poor predictive performance of MATRIX STIMULATION ($R^{2}\lt0$), clustering (K-means + PCA) was explored, revealing internal heterogeneity, but insufficient to improve accuracy, highlighting the need for enhanced data granularity. This dual-model approach reduces the manual candidate screening time by over 2,000 hours annually, introduces data-driven prioritization in campaign planning, and lays the foundation for scalable, real-time recommendation systems in high-density oil field operations.

title: Multilingual Speech-Driven Talking-Face Generation with Precise Lip Synchronization
authors: Che-Wen Chen, Jhing-Fa Wang, Din Yuen Chan, James Chao, Shu-Yan Liou, Hsin-Chun Tsai
abstract: We propose a modular pipeline that converts Chinese text into Taiwanese Hokkien speech and synthesizes a photorealistic talking-face video. The system couples a Taiwanese-aware TTS front end (hybrid word segmentation, iTaigi Tâi-lô romanization with dynamic tone annotation, tone-aware Tacotron2) with a Seed-VC–style timbre converter and a two-stage speech-driven talking-face synthesizer using cross-modal fusion, temporal modeling, and diffusion rendering. On an internal set, the speech reaches MOS 4.602 and CER 2.01%. On talking-face benchmarks, we obtain FID 29.18, CPBD 0.498, and LSE-D 7.23, surpassing representative baselines in realism, sharpness, and lip-sync. The design is lightweight and modular, runs on a single RTX 3080, and is transferable to other low-resource tonal languages.

title: Design and Implementation of an AI Agent for Product Selection and Intelligent Marketing in Cross-Border E-Commerce
authors: Guohua Xiao, Wenjun You, Chenyu Fan, Yuxi Hu, Shih-Pang Tseng
abstract: Cross-border e-commerce presents two fundamental challenges for merchants: identifying suitable products for specific marketing scenarios, and generating persuasive multilingual promotional content across different channels. Traditional workflows rely heavily on manual judgment and content creation, resulting in variation, inefficiency and limited scalability. This paper proposes a lightweight but complete AI agent system for product selection and intelligent marketing, built upon a small transparent demo dataset, a heuristic evaluation mechanism and prompt-driven generation using the DeepSeek large language model (LLM). The system strictly follows the Perception-Memory-Knowledge-Policy-Action (PMKPA) framework to ensure interpretability and extensibility.Two functional modules are implemented: (1) a product selection agent that ranks items for a given campaign description and provides human-readable reasoning, and (2) a marketing generation agent that produces multilingual, channel-specific promotional content A Gradio web interface enables deployment local environments without additional configuration. Experimental demonstrations show that even with minimal data, the system produces meaningful product rankings and fluent marketing copy adaptable to several languages and channels. Although simplified, the system reflects modern LLM-agent design principles and serves as a reproducible blueprint for teaching, research and prototyping in AI-driven e-commerce.

title: Utilization of Google Gemini for Automated Assessment and Feedback to Enhance Programming Education at the Senior High School Level
authors: Jarot S. Suroso, Grace Amin, Liswandi, Achmad Cahyadi
abstract: The assessment of programming assignments in high school faces challenges with efficiency and feedback quality. Manual grading is slow and subjective, while conventional autograders typically only evaluate functional correctness, neglecting qualitative aspects like code readability and good programming practices. This research investigates the use of Large Language Models (LLMs), specifically Google Gemini, to develop a comprehensive automated system that provides both objective scores and in-depth, human-like qualitative feedback. A prototype was developed to analyze C++ tasks and evaluated through a direct comparative study, benchmarking Gemini's results against an expert teacher's assessment. Findings indicate Gemini has significant potential as a reliable tool, accurately identifying functional errors while also providing relevant insights into code quality. The novelty lies in its holistic approach beyond correctness and its pedagogical validation against a human expert. This Gemini-based system can bridge the gap between machine objectivity and human feedback, significantly enhancing programming education.

title: Investigating the Classification of Autism Quotient-50 Quesrtionnaire Using Large Language Models
authors: Sarwat Qureshi, Mohsen Farid
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) screening relies heavily on standardized questionnaires like the Autism Quotient (AQ-50), among others, possibly followed by an open-ended question-answer session to accurately assess the individual. Still, the manual scoring and interpretation methods currently in use create significant barriers to fast, accurate, accessible, and scalable assessment.This study validates the AQ-50 questionnaire (adult version) using a two-step analytical approach: First, it compares the effectiveness of three large language models in categorizing the 50 items according to Baron-Cohen’s established framework, which groups questions into five main areas of ASD symptoms: Social Skills, Attention Switching, Communication, Imagination, and Attention to Details, each comprising 10 items. Second: the study examines how these questions relate to the core symptoms, considering that about half of the AQ-50 questions are negatively phrased.In this context, this paper investigates the performance of GPT-4 in zero-shot and few-shot classification with contextual examples, as well as HuggingFace’s BART-large-mnli in zero-shot classification.The study highlights issues in the categorical structure of the questionnaire, demonstrated by the inconsistent behaviour of large language models across various ASD symptom domains. The study also shows the potential of automated ASD symptom classification using GPT-4, which can achieve near clinically acceptable classification while helping to address the limitations of questionnaires in assessment accessibility.

title: Fault Diagnosis Model-Based Methods Overview of Lithium-Ion Battery in Electric Vehicles
authors: Houda Sta, Sabeur Jemmali, Bilal Manai
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Lithium-ion batteries, widely used for their high energy density and long lifespan, remain susceptible to failures such as short circuits and thermal runaway. To ensure safety, reliability, and optimal performance, effective diagnosis techniques are essential. This paper focuses on model-based diagnosis approaches, including the parity space, the state estimation, the parameter estimation and the structural analysis method. These approaches rely on battery models such as electrochemical models and equivalent circuit models (ECMs).Each technique offers distinct advantages in terms of implementation complexity, diagnosis accuracy, and suitability for real-time applications.

title: Personalized EEG-Based Passthought Authentication Using Custom-Fit Ear Devices: A Secure, Multi-Factor Approach
authors: Ezekiel Nwokolo, Tahmina Akter Anondi
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This study investigates the feasibility of utilizing a single custom-fit earpiece equipped with embedded electroencephalogram (EEG) electrodes for authentication based on userspecific mental tasks, termed passthoughts. Passthoughts represent a novel biometric solution that enhances security while ensuring user-friendliness by leveraging unique brainwave patterns associated with individual cognitive activities. In our experiments, We achieved an impressive authentication accuracy of 99.82% using three small electrodes integrated into a custom earpiece, which significantly surpasses the performance of existing EEG-based authentication methods. Our system effectively combines both inherence factors, represented by the individual's unique brain activity, and knowledge factors, derived from the cognitive tasks that users voluntarily perform. Furthermore, our authentication method has demonstrated robustness against various spoofing attempts, as attackers faced substantial challenges in attempting to replicate the specific passthoughts associated with individuals. This highlights the inherent security advantages of our approach. Although the study's sample size is relatively small, the results strongly suggest the potential of ear-EEG systems for practical everyday authentication applications, showcasing favorable outcomes in terms of task memorability and user satisfaction. We also critically examine several limitations of our study, including the need for greater sample diversity and the constraints imposed by controlled experimental settings. To address these limitations, we propose future research directions that include testing our authentication system in a wider range of real-world environments, expanding the sample size to enhance generalizability, and improving usability through the development of dry electrode technology. Ultimately, our work lays the groundwork for a robust and discreet authentication method, particularly wellsuited for high-security applications as well as those requiring enhanced accessibility for users. The integration of personalized EEG readings with a practical form factor presents an innovative avenue for advancing secure authentication in an increasingly digital world.

title: AI-Driven Peripheral Neural Interfaces for Decoding Movement Intent from EMG Signals
authors: Samuel George-White, Anil Bharath, Dario Farina
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Non-invasive decoding of human motor intention remains a central challenge for neural interfacing and hu-man-robot integration. This paper presents an artificial intelligence framework for interpreting latent motor states from high-density surface electromyography (sEMG) recorded during a Go/No-Go paradigm, with emphasis placed on No-Go trials, in which movement is prepared but voluntarily cancelled. By analysing tibialis anterior muscle activity, we demonstrate that peripheral signals can reveal cortical processes beyond overt force generation, demonstrating the potential feasibility of wearable thought-controlled interfaces. A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained on preprocessed beta-band (13 – 30 Hz) EMG data achieved a mean accuracy of 76.8% across three states-Baseline, Preparation, and Cancellation-significantly exceeding Support Vector Machine, Recurrent Neural Network, and Long Short-Term Memory baselines. The analysis of class-activation mappings and temporal prediction windows indicated that motor intent could be inferred up to 300 ms before explicit movement onset. These findings extend peripheral brain-interfacing concepts and open routes toward real-time, muscle-driven control of robotic and prosthetic systems.

title: A Novel CMOS Time Register
authors: Johnatan Felipe Silva Garcia, Dalton Martini Colombo, Kamal El-Sankary, Mahsa Zareie
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: This work presents a time register designed for time-domain signal processing, employing a delay line in a ring configuration topology combined with a digital counter to achieve a high dynamic range. The proposed architecture supports both addition and subtraction operations, offering a practical framework for understanding the implementation of such functionalities in the time domain. The register is capable of efficiently processing signals with durations ranging from picoseconds to milliseconds and demonstrating feasibility for physical implementation.

title: A Survey of Data-Driven Traffic Simulation: Developments and Research Trends in Modeling Approaches
authors: Xiaoyong Zhang, Zhenwu Chen, Sen Wang, Qiyue Peng, Kechen Zhu, Jianrong Xu, Yong Zhou, Chenliang Wo, Xiaochun Zhang
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Traffic simulation is essential for research in intelligent transportation and autonomous driving. Traditional rulebased and statistical approaches offer interpretability but fail to capture the realism, diversity, and complexity of human driving behavior. With the increasing availability of large-scale trajectory datasets, research has gradually shifted toward datadriven simulation based on machine learning. Prior surveys have summarized tasks, evaluation metrics, and applications. However, a large number of novel methods have recently emerged at the modeling level, requiring systematic review and analysis. Building on earlier work, this paper conducts a comprehensive investigation of recent advances, with a particular focus on methodological updates. According to their learning paradigms and methodological characteristics, existing approaches are categorized into several major classes, including imitation learning, reinforcement learning, discriminative learning, generative learning, and hybrid learning. Representative methods are analyzed in terms of their underlying ideas, design principles, and technical foundations, and their strengths and limitations are systematically compared. On this basis, the survey synthesizes the distinctive features and inherent challenges of different paradigms, providing an integrated perspective on data-driven traffic simulation. Finally, it outlines future research directions toward large-scale, realistic, and interpretable simulation systems.

title: Intelligent Detection and Localization of Simultaneous Faults in a Wind Energy Conversion System Using Multiclass SVM and LSTM methods
authors: Laila Bouaziz, Mehdi Dhaoui, Mouna Ben Hamed
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: This paper presents an intelligent approach for fault detection and localization in a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) based on a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (PMSG). Two artificial intelligence methods are investigated and compared: the multiclass Support Vector Machine (SVM) and the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network. The proposed methodology consists of data preprocessing, feature extraction, labeling of different fault classes, and the training and testing of both models under several operating conditions. Ten sensor and actuator faults (F1–F10) are introduced, including simultaneous fault scenarios, to evaluate the robustness and accuracy of the diagnostic algorithms. Simulation results demonstrate that the LSTM model significantly outperforms the multiclass SVM, achieving higher detection and localization accuracy, even under complex fault combinations. The study highlights the efficiency of LSTM-based learning in capturing temporal dependencies in WECS dynamics and provides a promising solution for reliable and automated fault diagnosis in wind turbine systems.

title: Analysis of Product Line Architecture Framework: A Case Study on Automated Soybean Peeling Systems
authors: Ezra Peranginangin, Christoper Eduard, Suryadiputra Liawatimena, Mahmud Iwan Solihin
abstract: The application of agile product development approach in modern manufacturing enables the delivery of diverse product solutions while maintaining operation efficiency. This paper is part of research in product architecture model focusing on product line architecture framework adapted for the design of smart product and automated machinery that bridging design strategic-level objectives with technical-level of product design. The framework resulted from this research was generated from principles and insights from mechatronics and computer vision, that able to contribute as the guidance for typical system designers through defining, conceptualizing, and validating product line designs. The conceptual framework is applied to develop the automated soybean peeling machine and describing how mechanical, electrical, and machine learning system is collaborated and optimized. The experimental results from the soybean peeling machine operation demonstrates efficiency and cleanliness using aeration cycles and computer vision. The experiment also justifies the framework’s utility in translating design options into tangible performance outputs. This research suggests methodological roadmaps for designing intelligent product design lines for ensuring robust performance, design flexibility, and efficient resource utilization in evolving automated system business landscape.

title: Fast and Interpretable Sparse Array Reconstruction Via Broad Neural Networks for Automotive Radar
authors: Lifan Xu, Yunqiao Hu, Shunqiao Sun, Ryan Wu, Binbin Shi, Jun Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Sparse antenna arrays offer high angular resolution for automotive radar with reduced hardware complexity and coupling effects. However, their inherent high sidelobe levels can degrade angle estimation performance. While deep neural networks (DNNs) have demonstrated strong reconstruction capabilities, their high computational cost, complexity, and limited interpretability pose challenges for real-world deployment. This paper proposes a broad neural network (BNN) framework for efficient and interpretable sparse signal interpolation. By employing parallel structures and a custom lightweight activation function, BNNs achieve competitive accuracy with reduced complexity and improved hardware efficiency. Experimental results on both simulated and real-world datasets show that the proposed approach outperforms conventional methods in sparse data interpolation, offering a practical and scalable solution for automotive radar applications.

title: Design Of A Low-Cost Interactive Braille Learning Device For Visually Impaired Children
authors: Erica Ayeyi Amoa Atta, Kofi Adu-Labi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Education is a fundamental right, yet many children in Ghana, especially those with visual impairments, face significant barriers to literacy. According to the Ghana Statistical Service, only 6.5% of school-aged individuals with visual impairments have attended second-cycle education, while over 40% have never received any formal education. While braille remains the primary medium for literacy among the visually impaired, the tools required for learning braille, such as Perkins braille machines are expensive, costing over $1,000 and often limited in many schools. This project aims to address these challenges through the design of a low-cost, interactive braille learning device for young visually impaired children in Ghana. The device will feature tactile braille cells, audio feedback, and a simple, child-friendly interface. The methodology combines human-centered design with very affordable prototyping techniques to ensure affordability, accessibility and relevance. The system will support users in learning the English alphabet and constructing two- and three-letter words through a multi-sensory approach.

title: Digital Twin System for Urban Building Energy Efficiency: A Case Study in Shenzhen
authors: Hang Su, Songbo Zhang, Tianyu Li, Biao Yang, Sheng Li
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Digital twin technology is increasingly recognized as a cornerstone for sustainable digital cities, enabling real-time integration between physical environments and their virtual counterparts. This study develops a Unity-based digital twin system that integrates Internet of Things (IoT) devices with occupancy-centric control (OCC) to enhance building energy performance and provide insights at the urban scale. The system incorporates temperature sensors and millimeter-wave radar for accurate occupancy detection, enabling automatic regulation of air conditioning and lighting. A digital twin platform was implemented to mirror the office environment, visualize realtime operations, and quantify energy performance. A four-month field experiment in Shenzhen showed that the OCC strategy reduced total electricity consumption by 11.9%. In terms of specific end-uses, the system shortened HVAC runtime by $\mathbf{2 5. 9 6 \%}$ and lighting runtime by $\mathbf{1 3. 3 6 \%}$; these runtime reductions translated into the overall electricity savings observed, without compromising occupant comfort. When extrapolated to Shenzhen’s office building stock, the findings suggest annual savings of over 12 GWh per million square meters of office area. This study demonstrates that digital twin platforms, when combined with IoT-driven occupancy sensing, can effectively bridge building-level innovations and city-scale sustainability goals, offering a scalable pathway for digital urban energy management.

title: Noise and Quantization Parameterization of Photonic Convolution Accelerator
authors: Mateus Vidaletti Costa, Mauricio Gomes de Queiroz, Raphael Cardoso, Ian O’Connor, Arnan Mitchell
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: Large-scale convolutional neural networks (CNNs) often rely on dedicated digital hardware, constrained by latency, throughput, and energy efficiency. Photonic hardware offers a promising alternative, but its analog nature and optical complexity pose challenges for electronic design automation (EDA) and design space exploration (DSE), limiting large-scale analysis. This work presents a novel parameterization methodology that quantifies the impact of noise, quantization, and kernel choice on a photonic convolution accelerator (CA), leveraging high-speed simulation tool. Using the MNIST dataset with $\mathbf{1 0}$ kernels, our results reveal up to a $3.5 \times$ difference in the root mean squared error (RMSE) between Blur and Laplacian kernels, demonstrating the critical role of kernel choice. The proposed simulation approach is also over $100 \times$ faster than conventional methods, making the analysis feasible, whereas performing it with traditional techniques would be impractical, if not impossible.

title: 3D Facial Landmarks Video Analytics for Student Engagement
authors: Jie Zhao, Omar Ashour, Michael Mattern, Conner Norbeck
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: The rapid transition to online and hybrid classrooms has increased the need for automated systems that are capable of interpreting student engagement through non-verbal cues. Traditional techniques rely on two-dimensional (2D) video analytics or the use of specialized hardware such as eye-tracking glasses. Both of these approaches limit the scalability and accuracy of the results. In this work, we introduce a 3D facial landmarkbased video analytics framework that quantifies student engagement without the need for specialized equipment. Using MediaPipe for real-time 3D landmark extraction and the Close Eye Aspect Ratio (CEAR) model for engagement classification, the proposed work distinguishes attentive from drowsy states in students during recorded sessions. Experimental results showed that 3D CEAR measurements provide more stable and accurate engagement indicators compared to 2D baselines. These findings validate the potential of 3D vision analytics to enable a more accessible and hardware-independent engagement assessment method.

title: Methodology for End-to-End Automation of Computer Vision Model Pipeline for Industrial Applications
authors: Sobolevskii Vladislav, Gaponyako Fedor, Gromov Ilya
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: This paper presents a methodology for the comprehensive automation of developing deep learning models for computer vision tasks in industry. The proposed solution automates key stages of the model lifecycle: automation of the training data labeling process, automatic image augmentation based on the analysis of dataset monotonicity metrics, model generation and training using a modified genetic algorithm, and automation of subsequent integration into third-party systems through automatic generation of software wrappers. The system features a web interface and a thin mobile client, simplifying its use in industrial applications. The greatest emphasis is placed on a universal and comprehensive methodology for automated model generation and optimization. Experimental testing on diverse applied tasks, including safety control, construction material identification, and wildlife monitoring, confirmed the system’s effectiveness. The results demonstrate high model performance the achievement of an 88% automation degree for the model lifecycle, enabling the creation of accurate models without the need for machine learning specialists.

title: Correlation Between Process Variability and Radiation Hardness in Digital Circuits
authors: Elias Ramos, Augusto Weber, Wilian Padilha, Renan Carlos Gomes de Farias, João Baptista Martins, Ricardo Reis
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: The objective of this work is to unify, with mathematical rigor, the analyses to estimate the effects of process variability and the effects caused by radiation in digital circuits using a single analysis. First, we proved that to estimate the effects of process variability on delay and power, it is enough to estimate at least one of the two. Later, a relationship between the effects of radiation and the effects of variability was obtained through a common element between the two: the entropy of the circuit. We applied the presented methods to compare circuits with the operations: matrix multiplication, graph searches, encryption algorithms, and neural networks. All of the circuits are designed using a 180 nm technology. We were able to verify which circuits are more sensitive to the effects of variability and radiation simultaneously through the proposed method.

title: How to Choose Evidence in Qualitative Research — —Based on Bayesian Theory
authors: Cuiping Zhou, Yi Zhu
abstract: Qualitative research evidence selection crucially impacts conclusions' reliability and theoretical value, yet traditional methods depend heavily on subjective judgment and lack systematic evaluation tools. This study introduces Bayesian theory to develop an evidence screening and evaluation framework. Through collaborative work involving qualitative experts and AI tools, and analyzing 32 qualitative studies, the research applies Bayesian analysis to assess hypothesis-evidence alignment and validity. The findings demonstrate how Bayesian methods effectively quantify evidence weight and enhance case evaluation. Based on these results, practical recommendations are provided regarding application guidelines, methodological suitability, standardization, and research models for Bayesian approaches in qualitative research.

title: Why You Should Care About Green Clean Sustainable Software
authors: Sigrid Eldh

title: A Study of Visual-Language Model for Defect Detection in Manufacturing Industries
authors: Nik Azimah Zainab Nik Mohd Rahimi, Teo Yu Xuan, Muhammad Syukri Mohd Talib, Wong Chin Wee, Muhammad Hafiz Samsuri, Chan Yan Bin, Zulaikha Kadim
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Deep learning models such as YOLO and Faster R-CNN have improved defect detection but remain limited by data requirements and generalization challenges. Recent advances in vision-language models (VLMs) integrate visual perception with natural language reasoning, offering interpretable, context-aware, and scalable solutions. It provides interpretability by generating natural-language outputs that can explain predictions to support decision-making. Fine-tuning also enables the VLMs to learn subtle distinctions between defective and non-defective products. These characteristics make fine-tuned VLMs a practical and efficient solution for intelligent and sustainable manufacturing systems. The model demonstrated a mean Average Precision (mAP@0.5:0.95) of 0.69 indicating strong detection accuracy and precise localization. These capabilities make VLMs promising tools for achieving sustainable, intelligent, and zero-defect manufacturing.

title: High Detection Rate Self-Powered Ultraviolet Photodetector Based on the Mixed-dimensional BP/n-GaN Heterojunction_supp1-3672974.docx
authors: SHUFANG MA
abstract: In recent years, mixed-dimensional p-n heterojunctions have exhibited promising optoelectronic properties. With the advancement of optoelectronic devices, unbiased (self-powered) photodetectors can better meet the requirements of specific scenarios. However, under zero bias operation, the photocurrent is typically small, resulting in limited detectivity. To address this issue, this paper constructed a BP/n-GaN heterojunction photodetector through mechanical exfoliation, successfully increasing the performance of the GaN photodetector by 4-6 orders of magnitude. First, black phosphorus (BP) was synthesized via chemical vapor transport (CVT) and subsequently transferred onto an /n-GaN film to construct a BP/n-GaN heterojunction. Then, the I-V characteristics, light response and other key performances of the self-powered ultraviolet photoelectric detector based on the BP/n-GaN heterostructure were investigated. It was found that under no external bias and 365 nm illumination, the heterostructure device exhibits good rectification behavior, with a responsivity of 0.023 A/W and a detectivity is 2.9 × 1011 Jones. Compared to the GaN photoelectric detector fabricated in this paper, the performance of the heterostructure device has improved by 4 orders of magnitude in terms of detection rate and response rate. Moreover, the UPS, ultraviolet-visible absorption spectroscopy tests and theoretical calculations determined the type II band alignment of the BP/n-GaN heterojunction, which can effectively separate photogenerated carriers. This study indicates that this BP/n-GaN heterojunction photodetector leverages the high carrier mobility of black phosphorus to enhance carrier transport and collection under zero bias, providing an emerging low-power UV photodetection platform relevant to nanophotonics oriented UV sensing and communication.

title: SHC: Deeply Activating Human-like Cognitive Ability for Visual Question Answering
authors: Fengjuan Wang, Zhenxue Wang, Gaoyun An, Congyan Lang, Dapeng Oliver Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Human cognitive mechanism depends on a sophisticated information processing framework, including perception, attention, memory, language, reasoning, problem solving and decision-making. However, current research only focuses on isolated process rather than systematically simulating human cognitive mechanism. Meanwhile, with the rapid development of large language models, related works have predominantly centered on language-level exploration, while in-depth mining of visual information remains insufficient. Here, to deeply activate the multi-modal understanding ability, a Systematic Human-like Cognitive (SHC) method is proposed for visual question answering, where the above mentioned sophisticated seven processes are systematically modeled as three core modules: hierarchical perception, semantic refinement and dynamic reasoning. The Hierarchical Perception Module (HPM) extracts hierarchical features from different levels to simulate the incremental integration mode of biological neural system. Based on the selective attention theory, a Semantic Refinement Module (SRM) is designed as a key-value accumulation optimization mechanism that enhances high-level semantics from low-level features via a multi-level cascaded attention structure. Finally, the Dynamic Reasoning Module (DRM), following the utility maximization decision theory, employs a dual weighting mechanism to dynamically fuse high-level semantic features and low-level fine-grained features, forming a unified high-quality visual representation that is then fed into the large language model for reasoning together with the text input. Experimental results demonstrate that SHC achieves competitive performance on multiple visual question answering benchmarks, including VQA-v2, Text-VQA, GQA, and ScienceQA, as well as multimodal evaluation benchmarks such as POPE, MMB, MME, and MM-Vet. Comparative experiments with multiple models of the same-scale validate the latent capacity of SHC to prompt the performance of multi-modal understanding tasks and its superiority in fine-grained visual information perception, and even surpasses multimodal models with larger-scale on certain tasks. Our code will be released on GitHub: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/fjwang3/SHC</uri>.

title: Understanding Data Influence With Differential Approximation_supp1-3670471.pdf
authors: XIAOJUAN QI
abstract: Data plays a pivotal role in the groundbreaking advancements in artificial intelligence. The quantitative analysis of data significantly contributes to model training, enhancing both the efficiency and quality of data utilization. However, existing data analysis tools often lag in accuracy. For instance, many of these tools even assume that the loss function of neural networks is convex. These limitations make it challenging to implement current methods effectively. In this paper, we introduce a new formulation to approximate a sample's influence by accumulating the differences in influence between consecutive learning steps, which we term Diff-In. Specifically, we formulate the sample-wise influence as the cumulative sum of its changes/differences across successive training iterations. By employing second-order approximations, we approximate these difference terms with high accuracy while eliminating the need for model convexity required by existing methods. Despite being a second-order method, Diff-In maintains computational complexity comparable to that of first-order methods and remains scalable. This efficiency is achieved by computing the product of the Hessian and gradient, which can be efficiently approximated using finite differences of first-order gradients. We assess the approximation accuracy of Diff-In both theoretically and empirically. Our theoretical analysis demonstrates that Diff-In achieves significantly lower approximation error compared to existing influence estimators. Extensive experiments further confirm its superior performance across multiple benchmark datasets in three data-centric tasks: data cleaning, data deletion, and coreset selection. Notably, our experiments on data pruning for large-scale vision-language pre-training show that Diff-In can scale to millions of data points and outperforms strong baselines.

title: Memory Semantics over UCIe: A Scalable Architecture for High-Bandwidth and Power-Efficient On-Package Memory
authors: Debendra Das Sharma, Swadesh Choudhary, Onufryk Peter, Rob Pelt
abstract:
Emerging computing workloads such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) and data-intensive applications face increasing pressure from the memory wall, as existing on-package memory solutions are unable to deliver the power-efficient bandwidth demands. We propose extending the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe)TM standard with optimized memory semantics to meet the bandwidth demands. Our approaches enable cost-effective, power-efficient, modular, and scalable on-package memory solutions across the entire compute continuum.

Our architecture leverages existing DRAM technologies (LPDDR and HBM) through a logic die. We also propose a novel approach for the long-term where the DRAM dies natively implement UCIe instead of traditional parallel memory buses. Compared to conventional LPDDR and HBM-based solutions, our UCIe-based designs achieve up to 10x higher bandwidth density, 3x lower latency, and 3x power reduction.

title: An Energy-Per-Bit Scaling Law for Frame-Synchronous Massive IoT Uplinks
authors: Mikael Gidlund
abstract: Massive Internet-of-Things (IoT) is a key sixth-generation (6G) use case characterized by sporadic short-packet uplink traffic and a very low activity factor ρ. In fifth-generation New Radio (NR)-like uplinks, periodic broadcast and reference signaling introduce a load-insensitive structural power term. For a fixed cell configuration, we show that if this term decays more slowly than linearly in ρ, the energy-per-bit (EPB) necessarily diverges as ρ → 0. We then study a burst-driven asynchronous uplink in which devices transmit event-driven pilot–data bursts and the base station (BS) performs joint delay and carrier-frequency offset (CFO) estimation and multi-user detection. Within the BS-side uplink EPB definition used here, removing the frame-maintenance structural term decouples the base station’s readiness power from the rigid frame structure, yielding bounded EPB in the ultra-sparse regime. The associated spectral-efficiency penalty relative to an ideally synchronized reference is not fundamental but decays on the order of L−1 p with pilot length Lp, providing a tunable trade-off between energy efficiency and area spectral efficiency. Cyclic-prefix orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (CP-OFDM) simulations confirm the predicted scaling and demonstrate up to 103× EPB reduction at ρ = 10−2 with near-synchronous throughput for moderate pilot overhead.

title: Hybrid Codebook Design for Localization Using Electromagnetically Reconfigurable Fluid Antenna System
authors: Alireza Fadakar, Yuchen Zhang, Hui Chen, Musa Furkan Keskin, Henk Wymeersch, Andreas F. Molisch
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing
abstract: Electromagnetically reconfigurable fluid antenna system (ER-FAS) introduces additional degrees of freedom in the electromagnetic (EM) domain by dynamically steering per-antenna radiation patterns, thereby enhancing power efficiency in wireless links. Unlike prior works on spatially reconfigurable FAS, which adjust element positions, ER-FAS provides direct control over each element's EM characteristics to realize on-demand beam-pattern shaping. While existing studies have exploited ER-FAS to boost spectral efficiency, this paper explores its application for downlink localization. We consider a multiple-input single-output (MISO) system in which a multi-antenna ER-FAS at the base station serves a single-antenna user equipment (UE). We consider two reconfigurability paradigms: (i) a synthesis model where each antenna generates desired beampatterns from a finite set of EM basis functions, and (ii) a finite-state selection model in which each antenna selects a pattern from a predefined set of patterns. For both paradigms, we formulate the joint baseband (BB) and EM precoder design to minimize the UE position error bound. In the synthesis case we derive low-dimensional closed-form expressions for both the BB and EM precoders. For the finite-state model we obtain closed-form BB structures and propose a low-complexity block-coordinate-descent algorithm for EM pattern selection. Analytical bounds and extensive simulations show that the proposed hybrid designs for ER-FAS substantially improve UE positioning accuracy over traditional non-reconfigurable arrays.

title: A Multitask Framework with Cross-task Selective Feature Sharing for Remote Sensing Image Time Series Analysis
authors: Milena Atanasova, Luca Bergamasco, Francesca Bovolo
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Satellite image time series are used to solve dynamic tasks in Earth Observation due to their rich information content, like change detection, land cover classification, etc. The amount of information in image time series and the interconnection among tasks may benefit from solving the tasks simultaneously in a unified framework taking advantage of a shared representation. However, in the literature, most of the methods perform a single task only. While methods for multiple tasks deal with single-date or bitemporal images, thus they fail to: i) model temporal dynamics in time series and ii) model temporal correlations across tasks. In this paper, we propose a novel multitask deep learning framework to analyze remote sensing image time series (more than two images) in terms of various temporal relationships, handling both short-term and long-term temporal information. The framework aims to learn both task-common and task-specific representations to capture spatio-temporal task interdependencies. To this end, we use feature sharing in a multitask setting with a single encoder and a decoder for each task. We design a novel cross-task selective feature sharing mechanism in order to inter-exchange spatio-temporal task information. We flow down the framework into a model that deals with two tasks: frame-by-frame abrupt change detection and multitemporal semantic segmentation. The model is tested on three datasets of multispectral image time series, acquired by Landsat-8 (annual series of 12 images), Sentinel-2 (annual series of 12 images), and Gaofen-2 (3 images across years) sensors. Results show the effectiveness of the proposed framework in various settings and demonstrate the validity of the multitask model.
