title: Model Predictive Suspension Force Control of Bearingless Switched Reluctance Motor
authors: Che Li, Xilian Wang, Zhuliang Huang, Ruikang Yang, Junfeng Gui
abstract: The article proposes a model predictive suspension force control (MPSFC) to suppress the rotor’s radial displacement of bearingless switched reluctance motor (BSRM) during suspension. It can handle the complex coupling between the torque and suspension systems and between suspension forces in multiple directions which exhibit time-varying characteristics. Within each control step, model predictive control (MPC) will be first implemented for the torque system, where the predicted torque winding current can be obtained to achieve decoupling between the torque winding and the suspension winding with the mutual inductance model. Then, the suspension winding current and suspension force will be predicted sequentially through the prediction model considering one-step delay compensation. The concept of suspension force vector (SFV) is also proposed in this article to present the combined effect of mutually coupled suspension forces. The optimal control voltage vector will be selected by cost function to minimize the deviation between the predictive SFV and the desired one. The experiment results on a three-phase 12/8 BSRM demonstrate the proposed method can significantly reduce the energy consumption and radial displacement of the rotor and achieve robust suspension under low speed and external interference.

title: A Novel Time-Window Scheduling Algorithm With Network Calculus Model in Time-Sensitive Networking
authors: Wenxue Hu, Lei Sun, Zhangchao Ma, Rong Huang, Yushan Pei, Jianquan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Traffic scheduling plays a critical role in Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) for ensuring high reliability and deterministic latency. In this paper, we propose a novel window-based scheduling approach for the Time-Aware Shaper (TAS). By allowing packets to wait in egress queues before forwarding, our approach relaxes the strict timing constraints imposed by existing packet-based schedulers. We employ a generalized Network Calculus (NC) framework built on an End-to-End (E2E) network model, to analyze the upper-bound latency, which is then used to assess the schedulability of Time-Critical (TC) traffic. Inspired by the Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID) closed-loop control architecture, we introduce an Incremental PID-based Search (IPS) algorithm to optimize schedulability, where the P, I, and D terms are leveraged to scale update steps, maintain search momentum, and dampen the oscillations, respectively. To accommodate various traffic classes, throughput constraints for non-TC traffic are incorporated as bounds on window lengths. Simulation experiments were performed on a multi-node network topology carrying large traffic volumes. Under optimal PID settings, the proposed IPS algorithm was evaluated against the well-validated Simulated Annealing (SA) method under a unified scheduling framework with identical decision variables and constraints to ensure a fair comparison. Results show that IPS consistently achieves higher schedulability and requires fewer iterations for flow counts ranging from 100 to 600. Furthermore, a real-time simulation platform based on OMNeT++ was developed, and the effectiveness of the proposed wait-allowed scheduling model was validated through optimized GCL configurations.

title: FFAFormer: A Feature Fusion Attention Former of Point-Image for 3D Object Detection
authors: Yufeng Jin, Chongben Tao, Chen Wang, Feng Cao, Xizhao Luo, Zhen Gao, Zufeng Zhang, Sifa Zheng, Yuan Zhu
abstract: In current multimodal feature fusion algorithms of 3D object detection for autonomous driving, there is a lack of accurate allocation of weights between features from different fields, which leads to mismatch and subsequent data loss. Ultimately, the accuracy of 3D object detection is reduced. Therefore, a Feature Fusion Attention Former of Point-Image (FFAFormer) is proposed for 3D object detection. Specifically, the features are firstly sampled and encoded through a Feature Encoding Mechanism of Dual Domain Space (FEM-DDS). Meanwhile, precise weight allocation is performed between different domain features, which maximizes the utilization of feature information from different modalities by the Transformer attention. Then, the interaction depth of features at different scale points is enhanced by Multi-Scale Depth Prediction (MSDP), which further refines features and reduces the loss of information. Finally, more accurate fusion of features from different domains are achieved by Dynamic Attention Feature Aggregation (DAFA). Cross-modal features are produced by aligning different features across modalities at a fine-grained level, which are achieved by a learnable dynamic offset and cross-attention mechanism. The effectiveness of FFAFormer is demonstrated by extensive experiments on the KITTI, NuScenes, and Waymo datasets. A real autonomous vehicle platform is also used to verify the proposed algorithm, which shows that the proposed method has enough generalization in complex real-world environments.

title: Deterministic Learning-Based Fault Identification for Nonlinear Sampled-Data Systems: Learning Accuracy Analysis
authors: Tianrui Chen, Jiajue He, Jingtao Hu, Cong Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: In this article, a sampled-data fault identification (SDFI) scheme for nonlinear uncertain systems is proposed based on the deterministic learning approach, and the learning performance of the SDFI algorithm is analyzed. First, a learning-based estimator is designed, and a sampled-data (SD) linear time-varying (LTV) system is employed to describe the learning systems. Second, by constructing a special time-varying symmetric positive definite matrix, the exponential convergence property of the SD LTV system is derived. Third, explicit formulas for learning accuracy are established, which illuminate the relation between the learning performance, persistent excitation (PE) level of neural networks (NNs), and the parameters of the learning system. The main advantage of the theoretical results given in this article is that the parameters in learning accuracy formulas can be derived from measurable signals, allowing for effective evaluation of the learning performance of the SDFI scheme in practical applications. Simulation studies of a robot manipulator and a compressor system are included to show the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Dual-Granularity Collaborative Progressive Reasoning Network for Referring Remote Sensing Image Segmentation
authors: Hongtai Yao, Haifeng Wang, Qian Wei, Le Zhao, Meng Tian, Yong Jin, Lin Zhou, Chunrong Wu, Haiyang Qin
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Referring Remote Sensing Image Segmentation (RRSIS) aims to recognize specific targets in remote sensing images based on natural language expression. However, synergy between coarse-granularity global alignment and fine-granularity local matching of image-text bimodal features is insufficient in existing methods. Furthermore, the cognitive reasoning logic of human visual perception is ignored during the processing of fine-granularity features. Consequently, the model's semantic understanding and target recognition capabilities in complex scenes are limited. To address these issues, we proposed the Dual-Granularity Collaborative Progressive Reasoning Network (DCPRNet) conforming to cognitive logic. First, dynamic extraction and alignment of multimodal features between the remote sensing image and the referring expression were performed. Multimodal representations containing global semantic information were established by the Global Context Perception Module (GCPM). Secondly, in the Fine-granularity Target Reasoning Module (FTRM), deep collaborative alignment of dual-granularity multimodal features was achieved with the injection of remote sensing priors. And the progressive reasoning on remote sensing targets was performed. Then, in the Asymptotic Feature Aggregation Module (AFAM), semantic depth conflation between features of different scales was balanced, and the feature-semantic consistency constraints were formed. Finally, specific targets were effectively recognized. We evaluated the performance of our proposed method on the public RRSIS-D and RefSegRS datasets. The performance of our method was superior to that of state-of-the-art methods in overall performance.

title: WE-YOLO: Wavelet Enhanced YOLO for Small Object Detection in Optical Aerial Remote Sensing Images
authors: Junding Sun, Yunzhi Guo, Xinke Zhi, Xiaosheng Wu, Jianlong Wang, Zhaozhao Xu, Bin Pu, Zhan Gao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Small object detection in optical aerial remote sensing imagery presents significant challenges due to limited pixel coverage and weak feature representation. This paper introduces Wavelet Enhanced YOLO (WE-YOLO), an improved detector incorporating two novel modules: Mixed Haar Wavelet Downsampling (MixHWD) and Spatial Frequency Enhanced Mamba (SFEM). The MixHWD module minimizes information loss during downsampling through Haar wavelet transform, while its hybrid architecture enhances feature extraction. The SFEM module employs an optimized Mamba block for efficient global context modeling with linear complexity, complemented by wavelet-based spatial frequency domain feature enrichment. WEYOLO achieves AP scores of 22.1%, 59.9%, and 18.8% on AITOD- v2, DIOR-H, and VisDrone-DET2019 datasets, respectively, surpassing the YOLO11n baseline by 2.5%, 1.4%, and 1.0%, respectively. The experimental results demonstrate superior detection accuracy while maintaining comparable parameters and computational efficiency to YOLO11n.

title: Training Sequence and Amplitude Modulation Pilot Tone Aided Nonlinear Interference Monitoring for Coherent Optical Communication Systems
authors: Hao Zhou, Wen Zuo, Fan Yang, Du Tang, Yingjie Jiang, Fei Xie, Yan Zhao, Bing Ye, Hengying Xu, Chenglin Bai, Yaojun Qiao
abstract: A nonlinear interference (NLI) monitoring method using a training sequence (TS) and amplitude modulation pilot tone (AMPT) is proposed for coherent optical transmission systems. To estimate the total noise, including NLI noise and amplifier spontaneous emission (ASE) noise, the TS is used to obtain prior information and subtract it from the received signal. Then, to separate the NLI noise, including self-channel interference (SCI) and cross-channel interference (XCI) noise, from the total noise, the AMPT induces power oscillations in the NLI noise so that the NLI and ASE powers can be extracted using different frequency characteristics. Finally, the NLI-induced optical SNR (OSNR) penalty can be estimated using these powers. Verified by a 5-channel 64-GBaud 1500-km simulation system, the proposed method can accurately estimate the SNR of total, SCI, XCI, and ASE noise, achieving NLI-induced OSNR penalty estimation errors of less than 0.39 dB. We also conducted an experiment in a 64-GBaud 500-km coherent transmission system with dual-polarization 16 quadrature amplitude modulation (DP 16QAM). The OSNR penalty estimated by the proposed method achieves a 0.41-dB monitoring error with 50-μs time-window data. The proposed method offers a better solution for achieving low-cost, real-time, and high-accuracy fiber-optical performance monitoring in coherent transmission systems.

title: iSecure: AI-Enhanced Blockchain-Based Network Intrusion Detection for Academic Institutions in the Philippines
authors: Warren Panizales, Dan Michael Cortez, Herminiño Lagunzad, Carolyn M. Samonteza
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Academic institutions in the Philippines are rapidly adopting digital platforms for learning, research, and administration, but this shift has also made them more vulnerable to cyberattacks. Traditional intrusion detection systems (IDS) often face challenges in handling large traffic, detecting advanced threats, and providing timely responses. To address these issues, emerging approaches combine Artificial Intelligence (AI) and blockchain to build more secure and reliable security frameworks. AI-based IDS models, such as deep learning, federated learning, and anomaly detection, have shown improved accuracy in identifying cyber threats and reducing false alarms. However, AI alone may lack transparency and trust in multi-user environments. Blockchain technology offers immutability, decentralized trust, and tamper-proof logging, making it a strong complement to IDS. This paper proposes iSecure, an AI-enhanced blockchain-based IDS framework designed for Philippine academic institutions. By combining the smart detection capabilities of AI with the trusted record-keeping of blockchain, iSecure aims to strengthen cybersecurity resilience, protect sensitive academic data, and ensure safe digital learning and research.

title: FASA-PM: An Explainable Fuzzy-Adaptive System for Autonomous Portfolio Management
authors: Navya Sharma, Pandey Shourya Prasad, Chinmay Sultania, Madhav Rao
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: The growing integration of artificial intelligence into financial markets has intensified the need for models that balance adaptability and interpretability. Traditional econometric and heuristic-based trading frameworks offer transparency but struggle under non-stationary market conditions, whereas deep reinforcement learning (DRL) models adapt effectively, albeit as opaque “black boxes.” To address this limitation, we propose FASA-PM-a Fuzzy-Adaptive Hybrid Intelligent System for Portfolio Management that unifies fuzzy logic, neural learning, and reinforcement-based adaptation within a transparent framework. The core Fuzzy-Adaptive Signal Aggregation (FASA) module converts heterogeneous technical and sentiment indicators, including RSI, Bollinger Bands, moving averages, and news polarity, into fuzzy linguistic variables. These variables are then fused through a lightweight neural network to capture nonlinear dependencies while maintaining interpretability. Reinforcement learning dynamically adjusts signal weights in response to market regimes, and a convex optimisation layer ensures financially feasible and risk-aware allocations. A SHAP-based explainability module attributes portfolio decisions across the fuzzy, neural, and reinforcement layers, ensuring end-to-end transparency. Empirical evaluation on 50 S&P 500 equities (2010-2024) and real-time deployment in 2025 show that FASA-PM outperforms heuristic-only, mean-variance, and DRL-only baselines, achieving a Sharpe ratio of 0.96 and reduced drawdowns, establishing a “glass-box” paradigm for explainable financial intelligence.

title: Transforming Religious and Cultural Heritage through Digital Tourism: A Competitive Strategy Canvas Approach in Kupang, Indonesia
authors: Yolla Margaretha, Elizabeth Wianto, Cindrawaty Lesmana, Maya Malinda
abstract: Religious and cultural heritage tourism is a crucial driver of sustainable growth, especially in developing regions. Kupang, East Nusa Tenggara, boasts unique assets such as historic churches, traditional houses, weaving traditions, and colonial sites, yet its potential remains underutilized due to weak marketing, limited strategic positioning, and fragmented collaboration. This study applies the Competitive Strategy Canvas (CSC)-BIDES FORMAT framework—consisting of Focus, Operational Efficiency, Research, Monitoring, Agility, Talent, Customer Relationships, Innovation, Differentiation, Effective Marketing, and Strategic Partnerships—to assess tourism competitiveness in Kupang. A qualitative case study was conducted through stakeholder interviews, field observations, and secondary data analysis. Findings demonstrate strong differentiation through cultural and religious narratives, but challenges remain in operational efficiency, digital promotion, and talent development. The study also demonstrates that strategic competitiveness in Kupang's religious and cultural tourism relies heavily on digital adoption, stakeholder collaboration, and capacity building. Policymakers and practitioners should focus on strengthening digital infrastructure, enhancing human resources, and institutionalizing data-driven decision-making.

title: Peltier-Based Fuzzy Logic Temperature Control in Magnet Power Supply
authors: Nattawat Yachum, Surapong Kokkrathoke, Wiwek Phacheerak, Jakri Nadeedanklang, Danai Khampiranon
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: This paper presents the research and development of a temperature control system for future deployment in control units of prototype magnet DC power supply at Synchrotron Light Research Institute (SLRI) in Thailand. The electronic devices in control units are sensitive to thermal fluctuations, which reduce performance in high-precision DC power supply. To address this, a compact thermal management solution was developed using a Peltier thermoelectric module integrated with a multi-input single-output (MISO) fuzzy logic control system executed on a programmable logic controller. A comprehensive test bench was established incorporating aluminum heat load, thermocouple sensors, current controllers for bidirectional Peltier operation, and heating elements. The system continuously monitors the aluminum heat load temperature using a high-accuracy sensor for acquisition and processing of the temperature signal and dynamically adjusts the Peltier current. The dual-input fuzzy logic controller utilizes temperature error and accumulated temperature error as inputs, processed through 15 linguistic rules with superior handling of nonlinear thermal dynamics. The controller generates bidirectional current control signals ranging from -2A to +2A. Experimental results demonstrate that the system maintains the heat load temperature within ±0.1°C of the setpoint with settling times of approximately 30 seconds, with no overshoot and effective disturbance rejection. Testing from 62.5°C initial temperature successfully controlled setpoints of 46-48°C at 31°C ambient conditions. The solution provides reliable precision thermal management for critical electronic systems.

title: Imagining the Rare: Diffusion-Augmented and Few-Shot Learning Approaches for Severely Imbalanced Mushroom Species Classification
authors: Varun Khurana, Cheng Siong Chin, Huizhi Liang, Boon Siong Wee
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Fine-grained species recognition faces critical challenges from extreme class imbalance, where rare species essential for biodiversity monitoring often have fewer than 10 training samples. We present a comprehensive study comparing two complementary approaches for automated mushroom classification: diffusion-based synthetic augmentation with multi-stage quality control, and prototypical few-shot learning with balanced sampling. Using the Danish Fungi 2020 Mini dataset (182 species, 38.5:1 imbalance ratio), we demonstrate that diffusion augmentation excels at moderately rare classes (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10-50$</tex> samples), improving balanced accuracy to 53.01% and macro F1 to 52.88%, while few-shot learning achieves 72.68% accuracy on extremely rare species (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<10$</tex> samples) through rapid adaptation. Our novel hybrid framework combining both approaches reaches 76.34% episode accuracy. Critical to success is our two-stage quality control pipeline using CLIP semantic verification (threshold 0.75) and FID distributional filtering (threshold 30), which proves essential, gated augmentation outperforms ungated by 5.78 percentage points while maintaining 74.8% sample acceptance. For the 46 rarest species, our methods achieve substantial F1-score improvements: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 7. 3 {\%}}$</tex> (diffusion), <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 2. 6 {\%}}$</tex> (few-shot), and 26.8% (hybrid). All implementations, models, and synthetic datasets are publicly available to advance ecological computer vision research.

title: Enhancing Knowledge Graph-based Credit Default Prediction via Double-Filter Negative Sampling
authors: Qixian Li, Zikun Guo, Bocheng Wang, Xicheng Du, Siyuan Chen, Wenyu Jiao
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Knowledge graphs (KGs) are increasingly used in credit default prediction to model complex data relationships. However, the performance of KG embedding (KGE) is often hindered by low-quality negative samples generated by traditional random negative sampling (RNS) strategies. These low-quality samples limit the model’s ability to learn effective features of data relations. To address this, we propose a Double Filter based Similarity Negative Sampling (DNS) strategy. DNS first employs Bisecting K-means to cluster semantically similar entities, then performs a secondary filtering step to remove overly similar entities from each cluster, thus preventing pseudo-negative examples. We integrate DNS with the TransR model to create the TransR-DNS algorithm, which generates high-quality negative samples to optimise the KGE process. Experiments on a public credit dataset demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms baselines. By replacing traditional sampling with DNS, we improve the prediction performance across various models, validating the effectiveness of our strategy.

title: Optimizing Adversarial Patch Placement to Evade Pedestrian Detectors via Genetic Algorithm
authors: Yutong Wang, Taihao Shi, Senchun Chai
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Adversarial attacks against pedestrian detection systems face challenges due to high intra-class variety. Existing research primarily focuses on low intra-class variety targets or optimizes patch content while neglecting spatial placement. This paper proposes a genetic algorithm-based method for optimizing adversarial patch placement to enhance attack success rates against the YOLOv2 and Faster R-CNN detectors. First, base adversarial patches are generated via multi-loss joint optimization. Then, a genetic algorithm framework searches for optimal positions within detection bounding boxes, utilizing the sum of pedestrian confidence scores as the fitness function and incorporating tournament selection, linear crossover, and adaptive mutation. Experiments demonstrate significant improvements in attack success rates on both the Inria and PennFudan datasets. For YOLOv2, the improvements over fixed-position attacks are 10.38% on Inria and 9.25% on PennFudan. For Faster R-CNN, the corresponding improvements are 2.48% and 8.68% respectively. This work highlights the critical role of spatial optimization, providing valuable insights and guidance for future research on robustness, particularly in the context of security-critical systems potentially vulnerable to physical-world attacks.

title: Leveraging Large Language Models and Machine Learning for Cyber Threat Intelligence: Detecting Advanced Persistent Threats in Saudi Arabia
authors: Ahmed A. Alghamdi
abstract: Saudi Arabia's digital transformation under Vision 2030 has positioned the nation as a prime target for Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)—sophisticated, sustained cyberattacks that target critical infrastructure including energy, finance, and government sectors. Unlike conventional cybercrime, APTs employ advanced evasion techniques, supply chain compromises, and prolonged dwell times, making detection exceptionally challenging. Current Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) practices rely heavily on manual analysis of unstructured text data from threat reports, security blogs, and alert feeds—a process that is slow, error-prone, and unable to keep pace with the volume and velocity of emerging threats. This paper proposes an automated CTI framework that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks for intelligent threat campaign detection. Our approach leverages Named Entity Recognition (NER) to extract critical threat indicators from unstructured CTI sources, followed by LSTM-based temporal pattern analysis to identify coordinated APT campaigns. We evaluate our framework on a curated NER dataset comprising real-world threat intelligence reports, achieving 92% accuracy in threat campaign detection—a significant improvement over traditional rule-based approaches. The framework demonstrates particular effectiveness in identifying entity relationships and temporal attack patterns characteristic of APT operations. This preliminary study contributes: (1) a novel integration of LLMs and LSTM for automated CTI analysis, (2) empirical validation on real-world threat data, and (3) a contextual analysis of APT threats specific to Saudi Arabia's digital landscape. Our findings suggest that automated, AI-driven CTI analysis can substantially reduce threat detection time while improving accuracy, offering a scalable solution for organizations facing sophisticated cyber adversaries.

title: Baseera: An AI-Based Sound Recognition and Visual Alert System for the Deaf
authors: Rand Khalid Abdulrahman Alhaqbani, Hateef Hisham Saud Almady, Zuhaira Muhammad Zain
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Individuals who are deaf or hard of hearing face significant challenges in perceiving environmental sounds that are critical for safety and situational awareness. This paper presents Baseera, an intelligent sound classification system designed to detect and alert users to five high-priority auditory events: fire alarms, sirens, gunshots, car horns, and dog bark. The system follows a structured audio processing pipeline involving data preprocessing, Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC) feature extraction, and model evaluation using both classical machine learning algorithms—Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Random Forest (RF)—and a deep learning model based on a one-dimensional Convolutional Neural Network (1D-CNN). Experimental results evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and area under the curve (AUC), showed that while SVM achieved comparable overall test accuracy, it failed to produce valid AUC scores under a one-vs-rest setup, indicating limited reliability for multi-class sound classification. In contrast, the 1D-CNN consistently provided high accuracy (96%), balanced per-class performance, and valid AUC values across all classes (ranging from 0.73 to 0.97), demonstrating superior confidence calibration and robustness. Finally, the trained 1D-CNN was successfully deployed into a mobile application that delivers real-time visual and vibration alerts alongside a history log, demonstrating the practicality of the system in real-world scenarios and its potential to enhance safety and independence for individuals with hearing impairments.

title: A Scalable 4 Kelvin 24×24 LED Matrix for KIDs Focal Plane Mapping
authors: F. Cacciotti, P. de Bernardis, A. Coppolecchia, I. Di Nitto, L. Lamagna, S. Masi, A. Paiella, G. Pettinari
abstract: Large arrays of cryogenic detectors are essential for enhancing the sensitivity of Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) telescopes. Lumped Element Kinetic Inductance Detectors (LEKIDs) are widely used in modern CMB experiments due to their intrinsic frequency-domain multiplexing, low noise, and fast response time. Depending on the operating wavelength, waferscale arrays can host up to several tens of thousands of LEKIDs densely packed within a readout bandwidth of a few hundred MHz, enabling the readout of up to a thousand detectors on a single feedline. However, fabrication imperfections and nonideal effects introduce small, random fluctuations in the expected resonant frequencies, sometimes causing resonance swaps and disrupting the one-to-one correspondence between each detector's resonant frequency and its position by design in the focal plane. Precise knowledge of this mapping is crucial for identifying inactive pixels, troubleshooting, optimizing detector yield, and accurately determining the sky region each detector observes. In this work, we present the development and testing of a 24 × 24 LED matrix, with a 2.5 mm pitch, operating between 4 K and 1 K with negligible dissipation, emitting at 660nm, and designed to spatially map densely packed LEKID arrays. The measurement principle relies in sequentially switching on the LEDs of the matrix and measuring the effect of ballistic phonons produced by LED light absorption in the substrate, which locally perturb the superconducting state of the detectors. We tested the system, including custom analysis pipeline, with an array of 313 LEKIDs on a 2 inches wafer and a 2.44 mm pitch in the horncoupled configuration. Possible extensions of the method based on localized infrared illumination at 1550nm are briefly outlined as a perspective for future work.

title: A 3 K Testbed to Screen Superconducting Circuits Based on a Commercial Probe Card with 140 Contacts
authors: Erin E. Maloney, Carl D. Reintsema, Johnathon D. Gard, Malcolm S. Durkin, W. Bertrand Doriese, John P. Biesecker, Richard A. Lew, Tammy J. Lucas, Nathan J. Ortiz, Daniel R. Schmidt, Michael R. Vissers, Joel N. Ullom, Douglas A. Bennett
abstract: The NIST Quantum Sensors Division has developed a novel cryogenic testbed to qualify the two-dimensional superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) time division multiplexer (TDM) devices that will provide front-end readout of the transition-edge-sensor detector array in the Xray Integral Field Unit (X-IFU) imaging spectrometer onboard NewAthena. This screening system uses a custom probe card with 140 tungsten-cantilever probe tips to connect to the device under test. Probing instead of wirebonding is required to enable subsequent bump bonding of TDM chips to X-IFU carrier wafers. It also allows testing of wafers before dicing, which allows some fabrication steps to occur after testing, including potential defect repair. The system is able to accommodate up to 150 mm wafers but is currently being used to test circuits on 76.5mm (‘3 inch’) wafers. The probe card is aligned and lowered to contact the DUT while at room temperature, so the system does not need or allow cold mechanical motion or cold optical access. The system is cooled by a commercial, two-stage, Gifford-McMahon cryomechanical cooler. Cooling to 3 K is achieved in less than 12 hours and the acquisition of a full set of test vectors takes less than an hour. Including re-warming to room temperature, the full test cycle can be run on a 48-hour cadence from one device to the next. With tens of test cycles successfully completed, this system has proven to be robust, reliable, and efficient.

title: Clustering and Analysis of Customer Segmentation for Indonesian Fashion Brands
authors: Quezvanya Chloe Milano Hadisantoso, Abba Suganda Girsang, Melva Hermayanty Saragih, Shih-Hsiung Lee
abstract: The modest fashion retail industry faces challenges in understanding the diverse preferences and shopping patterns of customers. To address this, this study proposes a customer segmentation and product association analysis approach by integrating the RFM-T Model (Recency, Frequency, Monetary, and Time) with the Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) and Apriori algorithms. Fuzzy C-Means is employed to cluster customers based on their purchasing behavior, while Apriori is applied to identify product associations frequently bought together. The results indicate that FCM, with four optimal clusters achieves a Silhouette Score of 0.4419, demonstrating that the clustering effectively groups customers with similar shopping patterns. Additionally, Apriori-based association analysis successfully identifies various product combinations frequently purchased together, such as the "Hijab Cover" category, which has a strong relationship with "Prayer Sets", as well as the combination of "Bottoms" and "Knitwear" with "Outerwears". With this approach, modest fashion retail companies can gain a deeper understanding of customer behavior, develop more effective marketing strategies, and optimize data-driven product recommendations. These findings can be utilized to enhance customer loyalty through personalized marketing strategies, product bundling, and more efficient inventory management.

title: Robust power regulation of wind turbines using sliding mode and rise-based pitch control
authors: Bechir Fatnassi, Borhen Torchani, Anis Sellami, Germain Garcia
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: This research introduces a first-order Sliding Mode Control (SMC) approach applied to two-mass, variable-speed wind turbine systems, with particular attention to the regulation of the pitch angle β. Moreover, a Robust Integral of the Sign of the Error (RISE) control framework is employed to enhance the system’s robustness and ensure improved dynamic performance. The proposed approach leverages robust control techniques to ensure that power is maintained at a desired and stable level. Maintaining power stability is crucial in industrial and energy applications, as it guarantees operational stability, efficiency, and compliance with established standards. In electrical grids, delivering consistent power helps balance supply and demand, preventing disruptions such as frequency variations. Additionally, maintaining steady power minimizes mechanical and thermal stress on critical components, such as wind turbines and generators, thereby extending their lifespan and reducing wear caused by sudden fluctuations. The proposed SMC and RISE model leverages the robustness of control this ensures optimal performance even in uncertain conditions. Simulation results carried out in Matlab/Simulink confirm the model's effectiveness in achieving the desired dynamic performance objectives.

title: Comparative Machine Learning Approaches for Sentiment Classification of Fiscal Policy Discourse on Social Media
authors: Siti Maesaroh, Emil R. Kaburuan, Ratri Sariningtias, Prastika Indriyanti
abstract: The decision to raise the Value Added Tax (VAT) rate to 12% in 2025 has elicited diverse public responses, especially on the social media platform X (previously Twitter). This study seeks to categorize public attitudes regarding the policy utilizing two text classification algorithms: Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Multinomial Naïve Bayes. The Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) was applied to the training data to address class imbalance in the sentiment data (positive, negative, neutral). Data were gathered via web crawling using terms relevant to the VAT rise issue, then underwent preprocessing, TF-IDF transformation, and division into two scenarios (80:20 and 70:30). The models' performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics. The findings indicate that the SVM method without SMOTE achieved optimal performance, with an accuracy of 90.52% and an AUC of 0.860. Simultaneously, the Naïve Bayes algorithm showed improved performance after SMOTE, especially in recall and F1-score, while its overall performance remained inferior to that of SVM. Consequently, SVM is considered more reliable for sentiment classification tasks, although SMOTE has demonstrated efficacy in improving Naïve Bayes performance, particularly for addressing imbalanced datasets.

title: Design of a Compact Dual-Band Antenna for Sub-6 GHz 5G and IoMT Applications
authors: Amira Bensidhom, Rania Rabhi, Hamza Ben Hamadi, Said Ghnimi, Mohamed Salah Karoui, Ali Gharsallah
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: This paper presents a compact dual-band coplanar waveguide (CPW)-fed monopole antenna designed for sub-6 GHz 5G and Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) applications. The antenna is designed on a Rogers RT5880 substrate and incorporates a defected ground structure (DGS) featuring hexagonal ring slots to enhance radiation performance and improve gain. Dual-band operation is achieved through the integration of dual folded U-shaped microstrip arms into the radiating element, producing two distinct resonances at 3.5 GHz and 4.7 GHz. Full-wave electromagnetic simulations show that the antenna achieves a bandwidth of 198.5 MHz centered at 3.5 GHz and 238.5 MHz centered at 4.7 GHz, ensuring robust performance across both 5G sub-6 GHz bands. The antenna also exhibits peak realized gains of 4.1 dBi and 4.5 dBi, respectively, with radiation efficiency exceeding 90% in both bands. Owing to its compact size, high gain, and wide operational bandwidth, the proposed antenna is a strong candidate for integration into 5G-enabled biomedical devices, connected ambulance platforms, and low-power IoMT systems.

title: EEG-Based Multi-Level Stress Screening Using Machine Learning
authors: Nazmus Sakib, Md. Kafiul Islam, Tasnuva Faruk
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Stress is a common mental health condition affecting millions, often manifesting in both emotional and physical symptoms. Severe stress can further contribute to depression and, in extreme cases, may lead to sudden death. Accurate assessment of stress levels is crucial for enabling timely interventions and preventing serious outcomes. This study examines a machine learning-based approach for classifying multi-level stress among university students using EEG signals. Participants were initially assessed and categorized into different stress severity groups using the PSS-10 screening tool. EEG data were then recorded, filtered into several frequency bands, and segmented into non-overlapping intervals of 1,2,3,4, and 5 seconds to examine the effect of segment duration on classification performance. Feature extraction encompassed eight time and frequency domain features, and the Bagged Trees classifier was employed to classify stress levels. The results demonstrate the potential of EEG-based systems as non-invasive tools for stress monitoring, offering valuable support for mental health diagnosis.

title: A Telemanipulation System with Constraint-Aware Shared-Control for Contact-Rich Robot Tasks
authors: Qilong Yuan, Keng Peng Tee
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This work introduces a robot telemanipulation with constraint-satisfying shared-control for robot tasks with both precision and contact force requirements. To enable a human operator to intuitively teleoperate a manipulator to perform contact-rich tasks, we introduce two novel features, namely i) a pose registration method to determine the object grasp pose with respect to the target environment, and ii) motion mapping to re-target input device motion to robot motion that satisfies geometric task constraints. Additionally, force-feedback compensation relative to the constraint geometries is employed to ease contact-rich teleoperation for the human. Experiments of human-robot co-assembly of IKEA furniture and Lego bricks have been conducted, and the results show that the system can quickly register the poses and allow the human operator to work with the robot to perform the assembly tasks with greater speed and success rate, and with reduced contact forces. Our approach provides means to telemanipulate the robot over different motion ranges, register key poses, and intuitively share the control of the task motion with the robot, which can be useful for many human-centered robotic applications.

title: Indirect Neural Network-Based Adaptive Control For a Thermal Process
authors: Bentaher Amel, Zribi Ali
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Thermal processes are challenging to regulate using classical control techniques due to their high intricacy and dynamic nonlinearity. In order to regulate thermal processes, this research suggests an indirect adaptive neural control approach. Using a feedforward neural network emulator and control, the technique entails adjusting the connection weights online. The controller modifies input actions applied to the system, while the emulator simulates the dynamics of the process.

title: Robotics: Machine Learning for Data-Driven Grasp Synthesis
authors: Tahmina Akter Anondi, Ezekiel Nwokolo
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This paper explores advanced machine learning methodologies for synthesizing and evaluating robotic grasps, particularly for underactuated and adaptive robotic hands. It details techniques for acquiring target grasps from diverse data sources, including human demonstrations and simulations. The core contribution lies in utilizing statistical machine learning approaches to generate novel object grasps and plan complex manipulation sequences. This paper highlights how learning-based frameworks can significantly enhance the autonomy and adaptability of robotic systems in grasping tasks.

title: Coordinated Optimization of Electric Vehicle Charging in Urban Hydrogen-Electric Distributed Digital Twin Environment: A Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Yunchong Zhang, Nianru Chen, Ge Song, Bin Zhang, Hongbin Xie, Haoran Zhang, Guoying Yu
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: With the rapid growth of electric vehicles (EV) in cities, there is an increasingly urgent need for efficient and sustainable charging solutions. This paper focuses on the complex challenge of optimizing EV charging in emerging hydrogenelectric distribution networks and proposes a novel framework named COHEDN. This framework not only constructs a digital twin simulation system environment for EV charging optimization in urban hydrogen-electric distribution networks but also adopts a Transformer-based multi-agent reinforcement learning approach. COHEDN is dedicated to addressing the unique complexities of such urban integrated multi-energy systems, including multi-energy coordination, long-term temporal dependencies, massive state and action spaces, and decentralized decision-making with communication challenges. Our core innovations are as follows:(1) Utilizing Transformer models to extract long-term temporal features;(2) Processing highdimensional action spaces through Q-value decomposition;(3) Enabling efficient communication between agents via attention mechanisms;(4) Employing state prediction to accelerate the training process. Experimental results based on the urban multistation EV charging simulation environment show that, compared with existing benchmark methods, COHEDN exhibits superior performance in cost reduction, enhanced robustness, and improved generalization performance. These results verify its effectiveness in achieving coordinated optimization in complex urban multi-energy environments.

title: A New Instability And Stability Criterion For Discrete Time-Delay Systems
authors: Dhia Eddine Mnasri, Ahmed Jaballi, Anis Sakly, Ahmed El-Hajjaji
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: This paper addresses a new instability and stability analysis study for linear discrete-time delay systems for single and multiple-delays scenarios. the proposed approach relies on the Jaballi-Sakly-Hajjaji (JSH) transformation, which reformulates the delayed system into an equivalent delay-free representation with reduced dimension. Furthermore, we designed two new adequate conditions for stability and instability for delay-dependent and delay-independent systems in the case of single and multiple delays, respectively. Subsequently, to enhance the practical relevance of the results presented in this work, a unified matrix polynomial technique and spectral radius are used to check the asymptotic stability for the delay-dependent criterion for the two-case scenario. The effectiveness of the proposed stability and instability theories is improved through five numerical simulation examples.

title: Research on Intelligent Prediction Method of Drinking Water Quality based on Machine Learning
authors: Jinhua Liu, Haoran Dong, Jiatong Yu
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Drinking water quality significantly impacts public health. Researching monitoring and prediction technologies for drinking water is crucial to ensure water quality and align with the national plan of “Green Water and Green Mountains.” We developed a deep neural network model using Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) to forecast the water quality of Hangzhou’s drinking water in our research. Water quality data, including metrics like COD, pH, NH3-N, and turbidity, were gathered from the primary urban region of Hangzhou between the first quarter of 2018 and the first quarter of 2023 for preprocessing. The LSTM model showed strong performance in forecasting water quality time series. We used LSTM to estimate drinking water quality and then compared these predictions with the actual data. The research results suggest that the precision of the water quality metrics needs enhancement with a limited sample size. The LSTM-based deep neural network model demonstrates significant promise with satisfactory learning performance for predicting water quality. The model’s predictions can be used by agencies to create policies and initiatives to enhance water quality, ensuring the safety of inhabitants’ drinking water.

title:
787-V GaN-on-Si Quasi-Vertical Trench MOSFET With Thick Bottom SiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
authors: Maoqing Ling, Jingang Li, Ang Li, Zheyuan Hu, Jie Zhang, Zhiwei Sun, Zhenghao Chen, Fuqiang Guo, Ping Zhang, Harm van Zalinge, Ivona Z. Mitrovic, Kain Lu Low, Sen Huang, Xuelin Yang, Bo Shen, Wen Liu
abstract: Owing to the lack of high-quality thick oxides formed by in situ oxidation, gallium nitride (GaN) trench MOSFETs are subject to pronounced electric field crowding at the trench bottom corners, which limits their <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small>-state blocking capability. In this work, based on a GaN-on-silicon (Si) epitaxy, a thick SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> deposition process was applied to the trench bottom. This approach effectively redistributes the electric field, shifting the field concentration from the dielectric/GaN corners into a 300-nm SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> layer. This terminal structure makes the device breakdown more dependent on the intrinsic blocking capability of GaN rather than on premature electric-field crowding at the trench bottom corners. As a result, a state-of-the-art breakdown voltage of 787 V was achieved for the quasi-vertical structure on a heterogeneous substrate. Furthermore, the device with the thick bottom oxide also exhibits a high threshold voltage (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sub>) of 7.6 V, an <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>/<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small> ratio exceeding <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{10}^{\text{7}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, a specific <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>-resistance (R<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{ \text {on},\text {sp}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) of 3.9 m<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Omega \cdot \text {cm}^{\text{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, and a Baliga’s figure of merit (BFOM) of 159 MW/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text {cm}^{\text{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, demonstrating the promise of this process for GaN-on-Si trench MOSFETs in future high-power electronics.

title: Cascade-Driven Opinion Dynamics on Social Networks
authors: Elisabetta Biondi, Chiara Boldrini, Andrea Passarella, Marco Conti
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Online social networks (OSNs) have transformed the way individuals fulfill their social needs and consume information. As OSNs become increasingly prominent sources for news dissemination, individuals often encounter content that influences their opinions through both direct interactions and broader network dynamics. In this article, we propose the Friedkin–Johnsen on cascade (FJC) model, which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first attempt to integrate information cascades and opinion dynamics, specifically using the very popular Friedkin–Johnsen model. Our model, validated over real social cascades, highlights how the convergence of socialization and sharing news on these platforms can disrupt opinion evolution dynamics typically observed in offline settings. Our findings demonstrate that these cascades can amplify the influence of central opinion leaders, making them more resistant to divergent viewpoints, even when challenged by a critical mass of dissenting opinions. This research underscores the importance of understanding the interplay between social dynamics and information flow in shaping public discourse in the digital age.

title: Finite-Time Optimal Containment Control for Nonlinear Fractional-Order Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Xiru Wu, Yibo Zhao, Yi Lu
abstract: This paper investigates the finite-time optimal containment control problem for a class of fractional-order multi-agent systems with unknown nonlinear dynamics. In a departure from the conventional stability-focused approaches, an optimal control strategy is proposed, whereby finite-time containment is guaranteed while control resource consumption is significantly reduced. To realize this optimal strategy online, a simplified reinforcement learning algorithm within an identifier-critic-actor architecture is adopted. Specifically, the neural network update laws are derived from fractional-order Lyapunov theory, which ensures stable and convergent learning. A composite controller is further designed by integrating these reinforcement learning components into a fractional-order optimized backstepping control framework. The stability of the closed-loop system is rigorously proven via fractional-order Lyapunov analysis, which guarantees that all signals in the closed-loop system are uniformly bounded. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed strategy is verified via numerical simulations.

title: HASFL: Heterogeneity-Aware Split Federated Learning Over Edge Computing Systems
authors: Zheng Lin, Zhe Chen, Xianhao Chen, Wei Ni, Yue Gao
abstract: Split federated learning (SFL) has emerged as a promising paradigm to democratize machine learning (ML) on edge devices by enabling layer- wise model partitioning. However, existing SFL approaches suffer significantly from the straggler effect due to the heterogeneous capabilities of edge devices. To address the fundamental challenge, we propose adaptively controlling batch sizes (BSs) and model splitting (MS) for edge devices to overcome resource heterogeneity. We first derive a tight convergence bound of SFL that quantifies the impact of varied BSs and MS on learning performance. Based on the convergence bound, we propose HASFL, a heterogeneity-aware SFL framework capable of adaptively controlling BS and MS to balance communication-computing latency and training convergence in heterogeneous edge networks. Extensive experiments with various datasets validate the effectiveness of HASFL and demonstrate its superiority over state-of-the-art benchmarks.

title: A Particle-based Collision Probability Estimation Framework for Uncertainty-aware Risk Evaluation in Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Abdallah Hossam, Víctor Jiménez-Bermejo, Jorge Villagra, Francisco Navas, Vicente Milanés
abstract: Risk evaluation is a critical task for the safety of autonomous driving systems. A significant factor for evaluating risk is estimating collision probability, as it indicates the rate of exposure to collision events. To obtain an accurate representation of collision probability, possible situational variations and uncertainty in the autonomous driving system must be considered. However, most existing approaches attempt to find simplified metrics that fail to represent uncertainties effectively. In this paper, a particle-based collision probability estimator is developed. The proposed approach employs a specialized particle system to estimate collision probability while accurately capturing uncertainties in the state space of traffic participants. Its performance, evaluated against a state-of-the-art method across multiple scenarios, demonstrates its ability to effectively capture diverse collision events and represent the underlying uncertainty distribution.

title: TCKD: Task-Oriented Contrastive Knowledge Distillation for Object Detection
authors: Zeyuan Liu, He Huang, Siwei Sun, Kaibing Zhang, Lan Yang, Tao Gao
abstract: Most existing prediction-based distillation methods for object detection suffer from misalignment between distillation losses and optimization objectives, which hinders the effective transfer of critical knowledge and severely limits the student's improvement. To address this challenge, we propose a novel task-oriented knowledge distillation (TCKD) method that transfers both classification and regression knowledge through two efficient distillation losses: class-wise contrastive distillation loss (CCDL) for classification and point-wise contrastive distillation loss (PCDL) for regression. Specifically, CCDL constructs positive and negative samples based on class predictions and establishes intra-class consistency and inter-class discrepancy between samples. This guides the model to effectively focus on regions where the objects align with their corresponding classes. PCDL generates positive and negative samples by leveraging sample points at different spatial positions and captures finer-grained localization differences through similarity computation. Moreover, the proposed foreground-focused strategy used for PCDL effectively reduces background noise and alleviates the distribution imbalance between the foreground and background. Extensive experiments on the MS COCO2017, visDrone2019, and TinyPerson benchmarks demonstrate that TCKD not only achieves superior performance on large-scale datasets and challenging small object datasets but is also adaptable to various detectors.

title: Posterior Augmented CVAE for Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction with Momentary Observation
authors: Yuxuan Wu, Le Wang, Sanping Zhou, Ning Ding, Gang Hua
abstract: Pedestrian trajectory prediction aims to forecast potential movements of pedestrians given their historical observations, which plays an important role in ensuring safety of autonomous driving and robot navigation in social scenes. Plenty of works have achieved great advances when sufficient historical information is available. However, in many real-world cases in which observations are incomplete or limited due to tracking losses or sudden emergence of agents. Although recent works attempt to address this issue by designing momentary observation feature extractors or performing bi-directional prediction using diffusion to assist momentary feature learning, they are sub-optimal in prediction accuracy or computationally inefficient. In this paper, we propose a posterior augmented conditional variational autoencoder to perform momentary trajectory prediction. Specifically, we introduce unobserved historical features in the posterior encoder in CVAE, which enforces the model to utilize useful unobserved features to facilitate momentary feature learning and prediction. Moreover, reverse-enhanced multi-head attention is leveraged to make the model enhance the focus of the model on different but supplementary parts of unobserved features concerning momentary observation. This enables the model to learn more comprehensive representations, ultimately improving its ability to assist in accurate momentary predictions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on ETH-UCY and Stanford Drone Datasets and our proposed module does help pedestrian trajectory prediction with momentary observations.
