title: DNN Partitioning and Offloading in Vehicular Networks Based on RSMA
authors: Zhijian Lin, Shaowei Yang, Celimuge Wu, Wenhao Wu, Zhizhao Lu, Jiguang He, Linhuang Wu
abstract: The proliferation of intelligent connected vehicles and related applications with deep neural network (DNN) inference tasks has boosted a pivotal requirement for the unprecedented low latency and low energy consumption of the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) networks. However, it is difficult to deploy large-scale and computation-intensive DNN on resource-constrained vehicles. Inspired by the idea of distributed computing, a DNN partitioning and offloading strategy based on RSMA is designed, where various computational auxiliary nodes are introduced in a stochastic geometry approach to alleviate the computation dilemma of the task vehicle. This strategy selects the optimal DNN model partition points based on the computing capability of the auxiliary nodes, and then develops the optimal task offloading strategy to realize the effective distribution and execution of tasks between the edge server and the service vehicle. To minimize the average cost problem, we propose a joint optimization algorithm combining heterogeneous-earliest-finish-time algorithm and successive convex approximation algorithm (HEFT-SCA) to achieve the optimal offloading strategy. Experimental results show that compared to the baselines, the HEFT-SCA can reduce the average cost.

title: Compact and Lightweight PRPM Focusing and Field-Tuning Method for High-Power High-Perveance Klystrons
authors: Z. X. Su, J. C. Cai, X. C. Lin, X. K. Zhang, J. Zhang, Z. Zhang, G. Y. Pan, Z. X. Liang, P. C. Yin, L. N. Yue, J. Xu, H. R. Yin, Y. Xu, G. Q. Zhao, W. X. Wang, Y. Y. Wei
abstract: A compact and lightweight permanent magnet (PM) focusing system for high-power, high-beam-perveance klystrons is presented. Conventional uniform PM (UPM) focusing is bulky, and periodic permanent magnet (PPM) focusing cannot supply adequate axial field when large pole piece apertures and RF waveguide cutouts are required, making it unsuitable for perveance above <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.8~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>P. To overcome these limitations, the periodic reverse permanent magnet (PRPM) concept, originally developed for multibeam klystrons (MBKs), is adapted to high perveance single-beam klystrons (SBKs) and structurally improved through pole piece shims that equalize the periodic height and symmetric iron plates that enhance the axial field without introducing a transverse magnetic field. The modified PRPM structure increases all axial field peaks except the first by more than 30% and enables strong confinement using smaller magnet rings. For an X-band 20-MW, 2.2-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>P klystron, the design provides about 35% higher axial field peaks within a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10.6\times 22$</tex-math> </inline-formula> cm assembly weighing approximately 5 kg. Compared with conventional UPM focusing, the transverse dimension is reduced by nearly four times, and the overall weight is reduced by eight times. In addition, a practical tuning method employing symmetric paired iron blocks for local field reduction and bridging iron blocks for controlled enhancement offers up to 20% local reduction and about 2% enhancement. This approach provides fine correction for fabrication tolerances, offering limited but practical adjustability without requiring structural redesign.

title: Tunable Triple-Mode Terahertz Metamaterial Absorber Enabled by VO₂–Graphene Integration
authors: Bylapudi Rama Devi, Manpuran Mahto
abstract: A multifunctional terahertz (THz) metasurface has been proposed employing vanadium dioxide (VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>) and graphene featuring a novel configuration comprising gold and a polyimide dielectric spacer to support triple operational modes. VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> undergoes a phase transition under external stimuli like temperature, while graphene offers controllable conductivity via chemical potential. Metasurface exhibits broadband absorption when VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> transitions to its metallic phase, achieving over 90% absorption between 1.91 and 3.66 THz, with maximum absorption reaching above 98% at three distinct frequencies 2.11, 2.99, and 3.55THz with a 62.9% relative bandwidth, and the absorption range can be dynamically controlled by modifying the Fermi level of graphene, which is tuned at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu _{c}= 0$</tex-math> </inline-formula> eV. In the insulating phase of VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>, graphene chemical potential is fixed at 0 eV and the metasurface facilitates polarization transformations. The device operates as a wideband linear polarization converter (LPC), achieving over 90% polarization conversion within 1.66–2.10 THz and 2.81–2.94 THz and 100% at 1.88 THz, supporting linear to circular polarization (LP-CP) and linear cross polarization conversion (LP-CPC) at 2.22–2.72 THz, 1.38–1.54THz, and 3.01–3.11 THz. These findings support the potential of combining VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> and graphene for versatile THz devices with enhanced adaptability and performance. The designed metasurface exhibits versatility that indicates potential for future integration into wireless communication infrastructures, radiation emission systems, THz sensors, spatial light modulation, and thermophotovoltaic devices subject to experimental validation.

title: PQMFA: Lightweight and Provably Secure Post-Quantum Multi-Factor Authentication Key Exchange Scheme for Smart Grid
authors: Weien Xiao, Qianqian Wu, Yubo Yuan, Taotao Gao, Xin Huang
abstract: The smart grid (SG) couples power infrastructure with pervasive communication and automated control, but this connectivity enlarges the attack surface of metering and communication links. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is therefore essential to prevent impersonation and unauthorized access. However, most MFA schemes rely on classical hardness assumptions and may not withstand quantum attacks. Moreover, SG terminals often operate under asymmetric resources and stringent latency constraints. Hence, it is urgent to develop post-quantum MFA schemes that are both secure and efficient, and practically deployable in SG environments. To ensure secure smart-grid communications in the post-quantum era, we propose a lattice-based lightweight and provably secure multi-factor authentication key exchange scheme (PQMFA). PQMFA integrates MFA into a lattice-based key exchange framework and leverages techniques such as fuzzy verifiers and honeywords to effectively resist password guessing attacks. Additionally, a novel lattice-based asymmetric computation method is introduced to reduce the computational overhead for smart meters. The security of PQMFA is formally proven under the Real-Or-Random (ROR) model. Comparative analysis with benchmark schemes demonstrates that PQMFA not only achieves strong resistance against various known attacks and ensures three-factor security, but also strikes a practical balance between security and efficiency.

title: Modeling of Eddy Current Soldering and Temperature Distributions in Solar Cell Tabbing Process Under Layer of Glass
authors: Janne Lauri, Dara Moazami Goodarzi, Tapio Fabritius
abstract: In photovoltaic modules, individual cells are interconnected with metal ribbons that are typically copper wires coated with soldering alloy. Due to different thermal expansion coefficients of copper and silicon as well as residual stress and cracks originating from a soldering process, thermal fatigue can appear after years of usage in the field and will lead to severe cracks and poor electrical connection between the cells. We have demonstrated eddy current soldering as a method to solder solar cell’s interconnections through a glass. The method was proposed to repair and refurbish bad electrical connections encapsulated underneath a glass layer. The glass encapsulant hampers the monitoring of actual temperatures in the soldering area, and therefore, only the glass surface temperature was used for monitoring process temperature. Soldering temperature and time will have an influence on soldering contact properties, morphology, and reliability. Thus, it is important to know absolute temperatures within the soldering zone. In this work, we simulate the eddy current soldering process under layer of glass to evaluate temperatures within the ribbon and the solar cell during the soldering process. The simulation results are verified with the data obtained from the experiments.

title: SAR-3DGS: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Synthetic Aperture Radar Novel View Synthesis and Target Reconstruction
authors: Zixuan Zhang, Yunkai Deng, Wei Xiang, Heng Zhang, Fengli Xue
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Novel view synthesis of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery is constrained by inherent properties such as view-dependent anisotropic scattering, occlusion, and layover. These constraints lead to slow convergence and limited physical interpretability in existing approaches. To overcome these bottlenecks, we propose the SAR-3DGS framework. SAR-3DGS is a differentiable rendering pipeline for novel view synthesis and 3D reconstruction from SAR data. The proposed SAR-3DGS framework establishes a forward rendering model from 3D scenes to SAR images by integrating two key modules: Scattering Splatting, driven by slant-range projection, and Shadow Splatting, which models radar beam occlusion effects. A geometry point cloud extraction algorithm based on local density analysis filters out outlier points to extract the target's three-dimensional geometry. Experiments on both simulated and real datasets comprehensively evaluate SAR-3DGS's capabilities in novel view synthesis and 3D reconstruction. Results demonstrate that, for novel view synthesis, SAR-3DGS surpasses state-of-the-art baselines and achieves markedly reduced training time. For 3D reconstruction, SAR-3DGS successfully recovers the target's 3D point cloud from multi-view SAR images. Future work will extend the method to efficient 3D reconstruction from spaceborne multi-view SAR imagery.

title: Power Flow-based Estimation of Short-Circuit Currents from Inverter-based Resources
authors: Ramon Abritta, Gabriel M. G. Guerreiro, Alexey Pavlov, Børre Tore Børresen, Elisabetta Tedeschi, Ranjan Sharma, Ivo C. Da Silva Junior
publish: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
abstract: The generator side of inverter-based resources (IBRs) interfaced with back-to-back converters is electrically decoupled from the grid. During electrical faults, grid-following IBRs behave as voltage-dependent current sources due to grid code requirements and converter control. This paper presents Newton-Raphson formulations for estimating short-circuit currents from IBRs using novel power flow modeling approaches to address voltage-dependent current sources. The work shows how to represent dq0 current injections, which are typically implemented in control architectures based on phase-locked loops. The power flow formulations are adapted to capture grid codes with different characteristics, such as the injection of negative-sequence currents during unbalanced faults. Comparisons against a field-validated electromagnetic transients (EMT) model reveal mean absolute errors of less than 0.5% for the proposed approach when estimating steady-state short-circuit currents from type IV wind turbine generators under grid-following control.

title: Recovering Pulse Waves from Video Using Deep Unrolling and Deep Equilibrium Models
authors: Vineet R. Shenoy, Suhas Lohit, Hassan Mansour, Rama Chellappa, Tim K. Marks
abstract: Camera-based contactless monitoring of vital signs, also known as imaging photoplethysmography (iPPG), has seen applications in driver-monitoring, perfusion assessment, affective computing, and more. iPPG involves sensing the underlying cardiac pulse from video of the skin and estimating vital signs such as the pulse rate or a full pulse waveform. Some previous iPPG methods impose model-based sparse priors on the pulse signals and use iterative optimization for pulse wave recovery, while others use end-to-end black-box deep learning methods. In contrast, we introduce methods that combine signal processing and deep learning methods in an inverse problem framework. Our methods estimate the underlying pulse signal, pulse rate, and pulse rate variability from facial video by learning deep-network-based denoising operators that leverage deep algorithm unfolding and deep equilibrium models. Experiments show that our methods can denoise an acquired signal from the face and infer the correct underlying pulse rate and pulse rate variability, achieving pulse rate estimation performance consistent with the state-of-the-art on well-known benchmarks, all with less than one-fifth the number of learnable parameters as the closest competing method.

title: En-route Charging Coordination for Electric Trucks
authors: Joas Kahlert, Ruiting Wang, Jonas Mårtensson
publish: 2026 IEEE Forum for Innovative Sustainable Transportation Systems (FISTS)
abstract: The electrification of long-haul freight transport introduces several new challenges, such as the limited capacity and congestion at en-route charging infrastructure. To reduce waiting times during peak periods, this paper proposes a framework for coordinated charging scheduling. The approach employs a mixed-integer formulation to optimize charging-related costs across charging, operation, battery degradation, and congestion delay, considering a range of scenarios. The results demonstrate that coordinated scheduling yields substantial cost savings up to 36% compared to uncoordinated scheduling, particularly by reducing battery degradation and delay costs.

title: The CRM Data Quality Advantage - Driving Pharma Performance Through Data Engineering and AI
authors: Sivasai Nadella, Supraja Chinthala, Vasudeva Chemuru
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Pharma organizations are increasingly using Customer Relationship Management (CRM) solutions to manage physician relationships. However, high volumes of intricate data produced in such systems are often low-quality, which hinders strategic decision-making. This paper examines the key intersection of CRM data engineering, data quality, and machine learning within the pharma sector. We propose an end-to-end approach to transform raw CRM data into actionable insights for commercial drivers of performance. The study relies on a simulated dataset, "PharmaCRM-360," constructed from representative customer behavior and sales trends. While the reliance on a synthetic dataset provides a controlled test environment, it limits real-world generalizability; therefore, we highlight the need for validation on actual industry datasets. The pipeline is built with Python, Pandas, and Scikit-learn to clean, transform, and data feature engineer data. A proprietary data quality score mechanism is suggested to assess CRM data validity. XGBoost and a custom neural network are trained using cleaned data to predict prescribing behavior and segment healthcare professionals. Results demonstrate a strong positive relationship between higher quality data and predictive accuracy, underscoring the necessity of converged data handling techniques in pharma. The implications for sales and marketing strategy are discussed, with emphasis on future scalability, compliance, and ethical deployment considerations. Keywords: Pharmaceutical CRM, Data Engineering, Data Quality, Machine Learning, Sales Forecasting.

title: An Experimental Study on the Performance of a Small-Scale Smart Air Conditioning System for Residential Use: A Case Study in Auckland, New Zealand
authors: Mustafa I. Fadhel
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The studies on Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) and living conditions have increased in recent years and have become a global priority, because of growing awareness of its impact on human comfort, health and productivity. Maintaining good air quality has become essential as people spend more time indoors to reduce health risks, improve well-being, and create more sustainable and energy-efficient buildings. This paper aims to design and perform a performance evaluation of a small-scale, low-cost air-conditioning control system for a typical bedroom in a New Zealand household (case study: Auckland). A small-scale, low-cost air-conditioning control system was studied and evaluated using an evaporative air conditioner as the ACU (Air Conditioning Units), a stepper motor for air control, temperature, humidity and proximity sensors, and an Arduino microcontroller. Mechanical analysis was carried out using the HAP (Hourly Analysis Program) software to maintain comfortable indoor conditions, showing that the room requires a 1-ton ceiling air conditioning unit. The developed hardware was tested and evaluated. A proximity sensor is incorporated, allowing us to monitor the presence of a person inside the room, enabling the system to automatically open and adjust depending on the number of individuals present in the room, and temperature and humidity parameters were monitored effectively. The results showed that the average temperature and relative humidity (RH) differential between the outlet air and inlet air of the evaporator were 5 <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">°</sup>C and 10% respectively. The hardware effectively controlled the indoor cooling system, and the results demonstrate that the developed prototype can enhance comfort in dry climate conditions.

title: AquaSave: Event-Driven Wake-Up Protocol for Low-Power Field Sensors in Water Monitoring Applications
authors: Rashmi Gupta, Saurabh Sarathe
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The demand for real time, long term environmental monitoring has led to the deployment of wireless sensor networks in remote aquatic settings. However, energy constraints remain a critical barrier, as conventional duty cycled protocols either waste power during idle listening or miss short lived critical events. This paper introduces AquaSave, a novel event driven wake up protocol designed for low power field sensors used in water quality monitoring applications. AquaSave integrates an ultra low power wake up receiver (WuRx) with an embedded event detection engine capable of filtering out irrelevant signals and selectively activating the sensor node only upon detecting environmentally significant anomalies, such as abrupt pH shifts or pollutant surges. Unlike traditional MAC based or externally synchronized wake up systems, AquaSave operates autonomously and continuously, relying on local analog signal features to trigger activation. Extensive simulation results using EPA water datasets show that AquaSave achieves over 67% energy savings compared to S MAC and nearly 30% over WuRx MAC, while maintaining 96.5% event detection accuracy and a median latency of just 95 milliseconds. The proposed protocol offers a scalable, context aware, and energy efficient solution for next generation aquatic sensor deployments. AquaSave is also extensible to other domains where event driven, ultra low power sensing is required, including smart agriculture and pollution monitoring.

title: Energy Prediction Based on Monitoring of Environmental Parameters for a Solar Panel
authors: Cristian Fosalau, Cristian Zet, Anamaria Hariton
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: IoT is a common paradigm nowadays when continuous monitoring of various quantities is needed. An important requirement when deploying an IoT system in the field is the energetic autonomy of the measurement nodes. A usual solution for supplying the nodes with energy when power grids are not available is the solar panel. The sun provides free energy everywhere on the Earth, but some difficulties are encountered when employing this kind of harvester. One of them is the discontinuity of the available sun energy, sometimes of large periods of time, when the nodes are prevented to be supplied with energy. In such a situation, optimization of nodes consumption is a must. In this paper, a study regarding the influence of external factors such as solar irradiation, temperature and humidity upon the electric power delivered by a solar panel is presented. These dependences are modeled using machine learning (ML) algorithms fed for training and testing with real data acquired with a special designed monitoring system, also detailed in the paper. The conclusion of the study is drawn upon the algorithm providing the best performance and a discussion regarding possibilities of running the algorithms on the CPU unit of the measurement node for power consumption optimization and control.

title: Enhancing Masked Face Recognition Robustness via Two-Stage ArcFace Pretraining and AdaFace Fine-Tuning
authors: Hameed Ur Rehman, Zahid Mahmood, Athar Waseem, Muhammad Mohsin Jadoon, Waheed Ur Rehman
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Face recognition systems have been severely impacted by the widespread use of face masks, which obscure key facial regions and significantly degrade recognition performance. To address this challenge, we propose a novel two-stage AdaFace model that achieves state-of-the-art accuracy under masked conditions. Our approach strategically leverages the strengths of both ArcFace and AdaFace techniques, combining the discriminative power of ArcFace with the adaptability of AdaFace through a sequential fine-tuning process. In the first stage, a model is pretrained using ArcFace to establish robust identity embeddings, followed by fine-tuning with AdaFace to adapt to masked inputs. The proposed two-stage model consistently achieves remarkable accuracy, reaching up to 100% even in the presence of synthetic facial occlusions. This breakthrough offers significant potential for real-world applications such as secure authentication, public safety, and surveillance systems where reliable face recognition is critical. Our findings highlight the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed method in challenging, occlusion-prone environments.

title: Real-time Signal Processing on Transition Edge Sensor for Confocal Spectral Microscopy
authors: Koki Shirota, Takahiro Kikuchi, Kazuki Niwa, Tetsuya Tsuruta, Nobuhiko Nomura, Daiji Fukuda
abstract: Broadband confocal spectral imaging in life-science microscopy often requires high excitation power to secure sufficient signal-to-noise ratio, which can induce phototoxicity and photobleaching. A transition-edge sensor (TES) offers high-efficiency, low-dark-count, single-photon energy-resolving detection; however, under continuous-wave excitation, the microsecond-scale TES pulse decay makes the readout susceptible to pulse pile-up, degrading both energy resolution and throughput. Here we developed a practical TES-based confocal spectral imaging platform by implementing a trapezoidal filtering algorithm as a linear time-invariant convolution on a field-programmable gate array (FPGA) for real-time pulse-height analysis. The FPGA synchronizes TES pulse-height information with scan metadata and streams the data to a host PC to assemble a spectral image cube. Using an 848 nm pulsed laser, we calibrated the pulse-height bin to photon energy with a third-order polynomial and converted energy histograms to wavelength via a Jacobian transformation. The proposed TES laser-scanning microscope (TES-LSM) successfully acquired spectroscopic images of fixed bovine pulmonary artery endothelial cells stained with Alexa Fluor 488 at an excitation power of 112 nW, achieving an image signal-to-noise ratio exceeding 30. In repetition-rate tests, the trapezoidal filter maintains nearly constant performance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E/\Delta E \approx 5$</tex-math></inline-formula>) up to 100 kHz and enables photon-number discrimination up to 120 kHz, outperforming an optimal filter at high count rates. These results demonstrate real-time, pile-up-tolerant TES readout for low-power confocal spectral imaging.

title: AI in 6G-Enabled Sustainable and Green Networking
authors: Sümeye Nur Karahan, Sedat Çimen, Deniz Karhan, Merve Güllü, Mustafa Serdar Osmanca
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The evolution toward sixth-generation (6G) networks promises unprecedented performance but introduces significant sustainability challenges due to escalating energy consumption and network complexity. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of Artificial Intelligence (AI) as a foundational technology for enabling sustainable and green 6G networking. We investigate how AI-driven mechanisms can address the critical trade-offs between Quality of Service (QoS) and energy efficiency. The study explores the application of advanced AI techniques, including Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL), Federated Learning (FL), and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), to key operational domains such as dynamic resource allocation, intelligent power control via base station sleep scheduling, energy-aware load balancing, and efficient spectrum management. Furthermore, the paper examines practical use cases in smart cities, green connectivity for rural areas, and renewable energy integration to demonstrate the tangible impact of AI on reducing the network’s carbon footprint. Finally, we discuss the prevailing challenges, including data limitations and performance trade-offs, and identify key future research directions. The analysis concludes that embedding AI at the core of the 6G architecture is indispensable for realizing a technologically advanced and environmentally responsible wireless future.

title: The Influence of Natural Language Processing-Based Chatbots and Customer Satisfaction on Customer Retention in Marketplaces
authors: Daniel Cahyadi, Nelson Duta Tan Surya, Ivan Pranoto, Lasmy
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: This research investigates the impact of Natural Language Processing (NLP)-based chatbots and customer satisfaction on customer retention in Shopee, the leading online marketplace in Indonesia. Leveraging IBM SPSS 29 for quantitative analysis, data was gathered from 417 respondents via structured online questionnaires. Findings reveal that interactions with NLP-based chatbots are significantly associated with increased customer satisfaction (regression coefficient = 0.049, t = 6.229, p < 0.001). In addition, higher satisfaction levels are shown to positively influence customer retention (β = 0.186 , t = 7.409, p < 0.001). The analysis also indicates that customer satisfaction partially mediates the relationship between chatbot usage and customer retention (β = 0.055, p < 0.001). These results underscore the strategic importance of AI-powered tools—such as Shopee’s "Choki" chatbot—in delivering personalized, efficient customer service that fosters user loyalty and repeat usage. While this study is limited by a brief data collection period, the findings provide meaningful implications for e-commerce platforms aiming to enhance customer retention through AI-driven innovation.

title: A Source-Weighting Mechanism to Enhance Evidence Quality for Fake News Detection
authors: Hsin-Chun Tsai, Jhing-Fa Wang, Bo-Xuan Fang
abstract: The proliferation of online misinformation requires robust automated fact-checking systems. Many of these systems rely on evidence retrieved from search engines, making their ac-curacy highly dependent on the quality of the search results. This dependency creates a significant vulnerability, as low-quality, biased, or false information retrieved from the web can compromise the entire verification process. To address this "garbage in, garbage out" problem, we propose a Source-Weighting Mechanism designed to enhance the quality of evidence before it is used for final analysis. Our approach systematically triages and prioritizes information by implementing a multi-tiered, heuristic-based weighting system that assigns credibility scores to different source types. Highly credible sources, such as official fact-checking organizations and international news agencies, are assigned the highest weight. Reputable media outlets receive medium weight, while user-generated content from forums and unverified websites is given the lowest weight. By ranking and weighting evidence snippets according to source credibility, our mechanism mitigates the impact of informational bias and provides a more reliable foundation for downstream reasoning models. This work demonstrates a practical and effective method for improving the trustworthiness and accuracy of evidence-based fake news detection systems.

title: Leveraging AIS Data based on a heterogenous stacking ensemble technique for Enhanced Vessel type Classification
authors: Ines Abdellaziz, Kais Brira
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Illicit maritime activities and the increasing presence of the "dark fleet," in which vessels deliberately disable or falsify Automatic Identification System (AIS) signals, underscore the need for reliable vessel identification methods. This paper presents a heterogeneous stacking ensemble framework for vessel type classification using AIS data collected over seven days from the U.S. Marine Cadastre website. The dataset was rigorously filtered to remove noise and inconsistencies, and a comprehensive set of static, dynamic, and operational features was extracted to capture vessel characteristics and movement behaviors. To address data imbalance, an adaptive oversampling strategy was applied prior to model training. The proposed framework integrates deep learning models (CNN and Bi-LSTM) trained on AIS trajectories with traditional machine learning models (Random Forest and LightGBM) trained on aggregated features, while a meta-learner combines their predictions. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the ensemble achieves superior performance, with an accuracy of 89.57%, weighted precision of 91.41%, weighted F1-score of 89.91%, and near-perfect class separability (AUC > 0.98), significantly outperforming individual models and ML-only or DL-only ensembles. These findings highlight the effectiveness of hybrid ensemble learning for vessel classification, offering improved robustness for maritime domain awareness and operational surveillance.

title: Guaranteed Cost Consensus for Multi-Agent Systems Under Event/Self-Triggered Mechanism
authors: Tu Zhang, Guobao Zhang, Amr Alanwar, Yongming Huang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This paper studies the event/self-triggered consensus problem for multi-agent systems in terms of saving control costs and communication resources. Based on the dynamic eventtriggered mechanism, a designed weighting matrix is integrated into the triggering condition for better consensus performance and fewer triggering times such that a Zeno-free event-triggered control strategy is established, as well as the corresponding selftriggered mechanism is also given. By setting a control cost index composed of the relative neighboring errors and control signals, the consensus is reached with a guaranteed overall cost. Numerical simulation validates the given method.

title: Fmplug: Plug-In Foundation Flow-Matching Priors for Inverse Problems
authors: Yuxiang Wan, Ryan Devera, Wenjie Zhang, Ju Sun
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: We present FMPlug, a novel plug-in framework that enhances foundation flow-matching (FM) priors for solving ill-posed inverse problems. Unlike traditional approaches that rely on domain-specific or untrained priors, FMPlug smartly leverages two simple but powerful insights: the similarity between observed and desired objects and the Gaussianity of generative flows. By introducing a time-adaptive warm-up strategy and sharp Gaussianity regularization, FMPlug unlocks the true potential of domain-agnostic foundation models. Our method beats state-of-the-art methods that use foundation FM priors by significant margins, on image super-resolution and Gaussian deblurring.

title: Empowering Women Globally through Information Technology to Bridge the Digital Gender Divide for Inclusive Development
authors: Sweety Rani Dhar, Niaz Morshed, Ishraf Rahman, Mohammad Abu Sufian, Zenith Matin Guria
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The accelerating evolution of information technology has the capacity to reshape societal structures worldwide; nevertheless, numerous women across diverse regions continue to experience limited access to, and low proficiency with, these digital resources. This article investigates the capacity of IT to function as an enabler for women, concentrating on the elimination of the digital gender divide as a catalyst for equitable development. Through an appraisal of contemporary patterns, enduring obstacles, and exemplary interventions, the analysis substantiates the assertion that expanded access to technology generates tangible gains for women in the domains of education, entrepreneurial activity, health, and civic engagement. The discussion further evaluates the effects of targeted digital literacy instruction, gender-sensitive policy frameworks, and the cultivation of transnational coalitions directed toward promoting systemic digital inclusion. The article concludes that a co-ordinated, multi-dimensional strategy, one that interlinks technological, social, and economic dimensions, is indispensable for the effective empowerment of women by information technology and, consequently, for the fulfillment of the Sustainable Development Goals and the establishment of more just and equitable societies.

title: A Dimension Reduction Approach for AI-Driven Human Presence Sensing via Hyperspectral Imaging
authors: W.A.D.U Wijesinghe, Chinthaka Premachandra, Lasith Gunawardena
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) provides rich spectral information that is well-suited for human detection in complex environments such as disaster sites. However, the high dimensionality of HSI data imposes computational challenges. In this study, we propose a 3D convolutional neural network (3D CNN)-based approach for binary human presence classification using hyper-spectral data. To reduce the computational load while retaining key spectral features, we apply a band reduction technique based on spectral pooling within the biologically significant 500–700 nm wavelength range. Our model achieves 94% accuracy in controlled test environments, demonstrating strong potential for real-world applications in victim detection.

title: From Secure Storage to Compute-in-Memory: A Versatile Memory System using 1T-nC FeRAM
authors: Rakesh Acharya, Rudra Biswas, Jiahui Duan, Prapti Panigrahi, Kai Ni, Vijaykrishnan Narayanan
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: This study explores the versatility of 1T-nC ferro-electric RAM (FeRAM) memory capable of operating in both secure storage and highly energy-efficient Compute-in-Memory (CiM) modes without incurring any extra design overhead. In contrast to conventional, volatile 1T-1C Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM), having significant energy cost due to the continuous refresh cycles, FeRAM presents a robust non-volatile memory solution, enabling efficient data retention with minimal power consumption. Bitwise logic operations (AND, OR) are efficiently implemented using a single, unmodified 1T-nC FeRAM cell and single-row activation (SRA), while NOT operation is realized by a modified sense amplifier. These facilitate bulk bitwise operations with significant energy benefits compared to multi-row based DRAM solutions. Furthermore, we demonstrate key-based (K) cryptographic operations which help in storing Plain Text (PT) in Cipher Text (CT) format tightly coupled with the memory write/read cycles in secure-mode operation with minimal overload while maintaining robust security. In addition, the advantages of vertical 3D integration of $1 \mathrm{~T}-\mathrm{nC}$ FeRAM are evaluated, and thermal profiling of the remnant polarization ($\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{R}}$) is done. Furthermore, we demonstrate that 1T-nC FeRAM memory reduces energy consumption by 31% compared to DRAM by assessing 8 real-world data-intensive workloads utilizing bulk bitwise operations.

title: GsBuilding: Extracting Proxy Building Models from Multi-View Aerial Imagery via Gaussian Splatting
authors: Luyang Wang, Yunlong Li, Qichen Zhang, Guancheng Li, Yunshan Lv
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Recent advances in Gaussian Splatting have demonstrated impressive reconstruction speed and rendering quality, making it highly suitable for large-scale urban scene reconstruction. This paper proposes a novel reconstruction framework that generates building proxy models using only multi-view aerial imagery. Unlike traditional urban reconstruction pipelines that rely on LiDAR or dense point clouds, our approach leverages the geometric properties and novel-view rendering capabilities of Gaussian splatting. It reconstructs urban scenes in two stages, progressing from a coarse urban layout to detailed building-level models, and produces high-quality proxy models for individual buildings in complex urban scenes.

title: Frequency-Domain Augmented Multi-View Co-Training for Robust Face Clustering
authors: Zexiao Liang, Dong Xi, Xinqing Zeng, Xuhang Chen, Zhen Wang
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: This paper presents a co-training framework for multi-view spectral clustering that leverages complementary information across views. Effectively integrating heterogeneous multi-view data, such as spatial and frequency face representations, remains challenging. Existing fusion methods often underutilize inter-view consistency and complementarity. Our approach iteratively refines similarity graphs with discriminative spectral embeddings from other views, incorporating highfrequency texture components to enhance robustness against illumination, expression, and occlusion. Extensive experiments demonstrate superior performance over methods in accuracy and normalized mutual information.

title: End-to-End Implementation of Efficient YOLO on NPU for Real-Time Plant Disease Detection
authors: Wenying Zhang, Shih-Pang Tseng, Lei Jiang
abstract: With the worsening global food crisis, ensuring food security and reducing food losses have become national strategies for many countries. Traditional methods for detecting plant diseases and pests heavily rely on manual inspection, which is time-consuming, labor-intensive, and prone to human errors. This has prompted scientific researchers to explore innovative and effective approaches to mitigate agricultural losses. Deep learning-based object detection offers a promising solution; however, deploying such models in real-world agricultural environments remains challenging because of the high computational demands and associated hardware costs, particularly when relying on GPU-based systems. This paper presents a lightweight, real-time plant disease detection system based on neural processing units (NPUs) and the YOLOX-Nano object detection model. By leveraging the high energy efficiency, low power consumption, and compact integration capabilities of NPUs, we address the limitations of GPU-based inference, including excessive power requirements, high cost, and limited mobility. The system allows deployment on embedded and mobile platforms such as smart agricultural robots, unmanned ground vehicles, and handheld inspection devices to enable real-time, on-site diagnosis. Furthermore, the framework supports multi-domain and multi-functional applications through algorithmic adaptation, paving the way for scalable and sustainable smart agriculture solutions.

title: RailDet: An End-to-End 3D Perception Network in Railway Environments
authors: Wentao Liu, Zhangyu Wang, Songyue Yang, Zhicheng Zhao
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Autonomous trains have become a key development direction in the railway, offering the potential to significantly enhance operational efficiency while ensuring safety. In autonomous train perception systems, accurate 3D rail-track detection and 3D object detection are of paramount importance. However, existing methods typically address only one of these tasks, lacking an effective integration of both. To address these challenges, this paper proposes RailDet, an end-to-end 3D perception network for railway environments including 3D railtrack line detection and 3D object detection. RailDet consists of two components: a topology projection-based 3D Rail-track Detection (3DRD) module and a BEV-based 3D Object Detection (3DOD) module. First, image features are extracted in a unified manner. Then, the 3DRD module designs 3D anchor points and projects them onto the feature maps to obtain anchor features, which are subsequently used to predict the 3D positions of the railtrack. On the other hand, the 3DOD module transforms the image into the bird's-eye view (BEV), leveraging the inherent distance information to estimate the 3D positions of obstacles. Through this design, RailDet enables simultaneous 3D rail-track detection and object detection within a single network. Experimental results show that the proposed RailDet achieves a 3D object detection accuracy of 32.5% and a 3D rail-track detection accuracy of 82.5%. The results underscore the robustness and potential of our approach for accurate 3D rail-track and 3D object detection in complex railway environments.

title: Discrete Event triggered Sliding Mode Control for a discrete fractional order Hammerstein system
authors: Aicha Znidi, Ahmed Said Nouri
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: This paper presents two proposed methods for controlling fractional-order Hammerstein systems. Firstly, a discrete sliding mode control method is developed, which combines the principles of fractional calculus and sliding mode control to achieve robust and precise control performance. The proposed method aims to address the challenges associated with controlling fractional-order Hammerstein systems by combining the benefits of both fractional calculus and sliding mode control techniques. Secondly, the control signal is enhanced by integrating an event-triggered mechanism. This mechanism reduces the frequency of control signal updates, thereby alleviating the computational burden and enhancing resource utilization. The combination of these two methods offers a promising approach for controlling fractional-order Hammerstein systems, providing improved performance and efficiency. The performance of the developed approach is validated through a simulation example.

title: Knowledge-Guided Generative Re-Stylization of CIM Models with Feature Continuance
authors: Yu Zhang, Yufeng Du, Wenbo Chen, Zhouying Song, Qin Ping, Bin Zhang, Yunchong Zhang, Yunshan Lv, Xieqin Huang
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: City Information Modeling (CIM) serves as the digital bedrock for smart cities but faces a critical dilemma: high-fidelity 3D models inherently expose sensitive information, limiting multi-party data sharing. Existing desensitization methods, such as visual obfuscation or procedural replacement, sacrifice visual realism and semantic integrity for security. To resolve this, we propose a novel paradigm: “Knowledge-Guided Feature-Continuous Regeneration.” Rather than modifying original data, our approach abstracts a building into a structured “visual knowledge fingerprint,” which serves as a genetic blueprint to generate entirely new digital assets. We introduce a four-stage hybrid AI pipeline—Deconstruct-Generate-Reconstruct-Render—leveraging a purpose-built architectural layout generation model (Arch-Layout LoRA) and a knowledge-driven procedural engine. Experiments demonstrate that this method generates photorealistic, diverse, and architecturally valid models that sever the link to sensitive source data while controllably inheriting macro-style and semantic features. This work provides a “usable-but-unseen” security solution, transforming single data sources into diverse digital assets for the CIM ecosystem.

title: Real-Time Faulty Wiring Detection in Aircraft Using YOLO Framework with IoT-Enabled Thermal Infrared Imaging and Graph Algorithm for Shortest-Path Analysis
authors: Bishal Kumar Rauniyar, Bijay Prasad Shah Rauniyar, Ashish Gupta, Anurag Jaiswal, Santos Kumar Baliarsingh
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Faulty wiring remains a critical source of electrical failure and fire hazards in aircraft systems. This study presents a real-time dual risk faulty wiring detection that combines IoT-enabled thermal infrared (IR) imaging, a YOLO based architecture for fault-classification model, and a graph-based shortest-path algorithm for spatial risk mapping. Initially, IoT thermal sensors embedded in sensitive aircraft zones capture baseline infrared signatures of healthy wiring. The collected dataset is used to train a YOLO model capable of differentiating normal and faulty heat patterns. During operation, thermal frames are streamed continuously to the onboard system, which detects anomalies and quantifies wiring health on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0-100 {\%}$</tex> scale. Detected faults are then analyzed through a graph algorithm that traces the shortest-path endpoints to identify the most probable propagation routes of thermal or electrical risk. The integrated framework delivers autonomous early warning, fault localization, and health visualization through a cloud dashboard. Experimental evaluation demonstrates high precision in anomaly detection and accurate localization of affected nodes, establishing a reliable foundation for predictive maintenance in aviation systems.

title: Energy-Efficient Computation of TensorFloat32 Numbers on an FP32 Multiplier
authors: Per Larsson-Edefors
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: Several new shorter floating-point formats have been proposed to match requirements of emerging application workloads. To simplify hardware development in the presence of an increasing number of formats, one practical design option is to use as much as possible preexisting hardware, such as standard 32-bit IEEE-754 (FP32) floating-point units, to handle emerging, less complex formats. We evaluate the case where we use an FP32 multiplier to run Nvidia TensorFloat32 data. While the FP32 multiplier area is not as small as a dedicated TensorFloat32 multiplier, we show that energy per operation scales well with the mantissa width reduction and that smart pin assignment can leverage uneven input vector switching activities to significantly decrease energy for reduced precisions.

title: Balancing Current and Historical State Information in Remote Tracking Systems: A Randomized Update Approach
authors: Sunjung Kang, Chengzhang Li, Christopher G. Brinton, Atilla Eryilmaz, Ness B. Shroff
abstract: The traditional goal in remote tracking of a dynamic source is to keep the current estimate at the destination as close as possible to the true state. However, in domains such as surveillance applications, the destination is also interested in reconstructing the past trajectory of states for further processing. This requires striking a balance between providing current versus past state information so that the destination can optimize the trade-off between the metrics of freshness and reconstruction queue length. In this work, we propose a randomized update policy that decides between head-of-line versus tail-of-line packets in the update queue. As such, our policy combines the strength of Last-Come-First-Serve (LCFS) service discipline (which aims at reducing the age) with the strength of First-Come-First-Serve (FCFS) service discipline (which aims at reducing the reconstruction delay). We evaluate the performance of our proposed policy in terms of its randomization parameter, which can be optimized given the system parameters to achieve a better trade-off.

title: FedRA: A Fully Decentralized Federated Learning Framework for Robust Intelligence Sharing across O-RAN dApps
authors: Sotirios T. Spantideas, Anastasios E. Giannopoulos, George A. Levis, Panagiotis Trakadas
abstract: In this article, we propose a resilient and fully decentralized federated learning framework specifically adapted for Open Radio Access Networks (O-RAN) named FedRA that enables collaborative intelligence between dApps. At first, we present how FedRA can be deployed in the O-RAN architecture by using already existing interfaces between the well-defined RAN components and how real-time ML applications (dApps) can interact to share their distilled intelligence. Furthermore, the software components of FedRA framework are briefly described, along with the step-by-step deployment workflow, outlining practical guidelines for its implementation. Finally, the FedRA framework is validated in a simulated environment by using both real and simulated datasets that report the time series of the throughput provided by multiple radio units. Two different Machine Learning models that are hosted in FedRA nodes are used, aiming to either detect anomalies in the upcoming network traffic or forecast the data-rate values. In both cases, the resulting accuracy confirms the validity of the FedRA framework in the training process of the cell-specific ML models, as well as the decentralized collaborative intelligence sharing among them. FedRA is also compared to typical client/server approach in terms of achieved model accuracy and network communication cost.

title: OS-MambaDiff: An Optical-SAR Fusion Diffusion Framework With Mamba for Robust Cloud Removal in Remote Sensing Imagery
authors: Peizhen Yang, Bo Huang, Yuanxin Ye, Jiacheng Zhang, Min Zhao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Although optical remote sensing imagery has been extensively used in various Earth observation tasks, its practical utility is frequently compromised by cloud cover and adverse weather, which lead to missing data and disrupt the temporal continuity of observations. In contrast, Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) is an active remote sensing technology that functions independently of solar illumination and atmospheric conditions. Its ability to penetrate clouds and fog ensures the provision of reliable all-weather imagery. Motivated by the complementary characteristics of optical and SAR observations, we present OS-MambaDiff, a Mamba-based diffusion framework to fuse SAR information and restore cloud-contaminated optical images. This method can effectively fuse information from SAR and optical images without the need for a cloud mask, achieving high-precision cloud-free reconstruction. To this end, we design a Mamba-based Cross Modal Fusion Module (MCMF), which introduces Mamba state space modeling and its 2D extension SS2D to enable efficient feature fusion between heterogeneous modalities. Furthermore, a Cloud Loss based on differentiated constraint is designed. This loss function guides the model to learn cloud-free features while suppressing overfitting to cloud-contaminated condition images, thereby preventing the model from simply copying the conditional image output. Additionally, a Sparsity-Aware Gated Fusion (SAGF) mechanism is constructed to adaptively inject fused SAR and optical features during the diffusion denoising process, while sparse regularization is employed to achieve stability and selectivity in feature modulation. Extensive experiments on the newly released cloud removal benchmark dataset SMILE-CR and the large-scale benchmark cloud removal dataset SEN12MS-CR show that OS-MambaDiff outperforms existing optical synthetic aperture radar (Optical–SAR) cloud-removal techniques.

title: Development of a Digital Twin Model for a Hydrogen Production Plant based on Artificial Intelligence Techniques
authors: Ruben Aquize Palacios, Juan M. Mauricio Villanueva, Aurelio Morales-Villanueva, Cesar Briceño Aranda, Oswaldo A. Waters Torres, James Erick Vílchez García, Roberto M. Dioses Soriano, Eduardo Roberto Rodriguez
abstract: The global energy transition has driven the adoption of green hydrogen as a key energy vector for industrial decarbonization and the integration of renewable energy sources. This work presents the development of a Digital Twin model applied to a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) hydrogen production plant, integrating artificial intelligence techniques for simulation, prediction, and adaptive updating. The proposed model combines electrical, thermal, and hydraulic variables from the real system—acquired at the experimental plant of the National University of Engineering, with artificial neural network algorithms to estimate hydrogen production and energy efficiency. The methodology includes data acquisition, preprocessing, normalization, and incremental learning stages, allowing the digital twin to adapt to new operating conditions and account for data drift. The results demonstrate a high correlation between real and estimated values, confirming the accuracy and robustness of the model under different operating scenarios. Therefore, the results confirm that the proposed digital twin represents a reliable tool for monitoring, prediction, and performance analysis of hydrogen production plants.

title: FLAIR: Federated Learning via Adaptive Influence and Resource Coordination
authors: Koffka Khan, Wayne Goodridge
abstract: Adaptive video streaming (also known as adaptive bitrate or ABR streaming) must intelligently balance competing Quality of Experience (QoE) factors-video quality, rebuffering, and smooth playback-under widely varying network conditions. Traditional learning-based ABR algorithms struggle to generalize across users' diverse network environments and QoE preferences [21] [22]. Generic federated learning (FL) approaches, on the other hand, often suffer in this setting due to client heterogeneity (network speeds, device capabilities) and multi-faceted QoE objectives that standard FL algorithms do not explicitly optimize. In this work, we propose FLAIR, a Federated Learning-based framework that employs Adaptive Influence and Resource Coordination for video streaming. FLAIR holistically addresses the challenges of training a global ABR policy model across many users without collecting their data. It introduces: (1) QoE-weighted model aggregation to prioritize updates from clients in critical streaming conditions, (2) influence-guided client selection via an influence matrix that identifies clients most impactful to improving global QoE, and (3) entropy-based scheduling of workload to dynamically include clients with novel or changing network conditions. We integrate a new Influence-Entropy-Diversity (IED) metric directly into FLAIR's optimization objective and convergence analysis, proving it can accelerate convergence by reducing gradient variance. Extensive experiments on both real- world streaming traces and large-scale synthetic scenarios demonstrate that FLAIR significantly improves average QoE (e.g., higher video quality and fewer stalls) compared to conventional federated training and state-of-the-art methods. Notably, FLAIR's global model adapts swiftly to network shifts (such as WiFi-to-cellular handoffs) and scales to thousands of clients, indicating its practical deployability for internet-wide adaptive streaming.

title: Scalable Modular MIMO Radar with Redundant Virtual Arrays for Robust Near-Field Sensing in 6G Edge Systems
authors: Hiroki Mori
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Near-field radar sensing is poised to become a key enabler in future 6G networks, especially for edge applications requiring high spatial resolution under stringent computational and hardware constraints. However, conventional MIMO radar architectures suffer from major limitations: single-chip systems lack aperture scalability, while cascaded multi-chip designs introduce significant complexity and are not inherently robust to near-field phenomena such as spherical wavefront distortion and range-angle coupling. This paper proposes a scalable modular MIMO radar architecture that achieves aperture extension and near-field robustness by intentionally introducing overlapping virtual antennas across physically connected modules. By leveraging structural redundancy, the proposed design enables accurate angular estimation and high-quality imaging, even when using computationally efficient far-field beamforming techniques. Simulation results show that the architecture maintains reliable performance across both sparse and dense target environments, offering a favorable trade-off between resolution, robustness, and implementation complexity. The proposed system presents a practical and energy-efficient solution for near-field sensing in resource-constrained edge and IoT devices, making it highly suitable for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) in 6G networks.

title: Interrupted Sampling Repeater Jamming Suppression Based on Ridge Line Filtering Guided by Discrete Points in Near-Field Distributed Coherent Aperture Radar
authors: Bowen Zhang, Xiaochuan Wu, Yu Song, Di Yao, Xin Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Traditional monostatic radar significantly enhances signal gain and detection performance through coherent integration of multi-channel echo signals. However, the complex electromagnetic environment created by active deception jamming, which is coherent with the target, consistently prevents monostatic radar from achieving precise target detection and jamming localization, severely degrading the effectiveness of target detection. To address the challenge of separating and locating jamming sources that are spatiotemporally proximate to the target under Interrupted Sampling Repeater Jamming (ISRJ), this paper proposes a jamming suppression method based on Ridge Line Filtering (RLF) guided by discrete points. This method not only achieves jamming suppression but also leverages the spatially distributed architecture of Distributed Coherent Aperture Radar (DCAR) to enable near-field cross-localization of jamming sources. First, RLF guided by high resolution time–frequency (TF) discrete points is constructed at each radar station to separate the target and jamming components. Simultaneously, a jamming amplitude control coefficient is introduced to suppress transient residuals at the segment boundaries of the repeater jamming signals, thereby achieving effective joint jamming suppression. Subsequently, a cross localization method based on the Relative Time Difference of Arrival (RTDOA) between target and jamming signals, combined with the bistatic elliptical range sum, is proposed to calculate the true position of jamming sources relative to DCAR system, achieving near-field jamming cross localization. Finally, simulation analysis and comparison were conducted between the proposed method and current mainstream, highly reliable novel algorithms. The applicability of the proposed method was evaluated in terms of target gain loss and signal to-jamming-plus-noise ratio (SJNR). The results demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed method in jamming suppression, jamming source localization, and target detection.

title: Utility-Aware Resource Allocation for Hybrid NOMA in MEC: A Matching-Coalition Game Approach
authors: Haolin Liu, Hao Yin, Haibo Zhou, Zhiquan Liu, Shujuan Tian, Yong Xie
abstract: The massive influx of uplink task offloading in Multi-access Edge Computing (MEC) systems poses a significant challenge to the capacity of wireless networks. This challenge highlights a fundamental trade-off between Orthogonal Multiple Access (OMA), which provides interference-free but spectrally inefficient communication, and Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA), which enhances capacity at the cost of significant inter-user interference. To navigate this trade-off, we introduce a novel Hybrid NOMA (H-NOMA) framework that offers differentiated communication services. The framework allows users to choose between premium OMA channels for latency-sensitive tasks and shared NOMA channels for others, creating an economy where performance can be traded for cost. Within this framework, we formulate the resource allocation problem with the objective of maximizing the total system utility, defined as the sum of all individual user utilities, under budget, computation, and communication constraints. To solve this NP-hard problem, we devise a novel multi-stage game-theoretic algorithm, the Matching-Coalition Game with Coordinate Descent (MCGCD). Our approach synergistically combines matching theory for a fast and initial channel assignment, a cooperative coalition game to refine allocations by explicitly managing NOMA externalities, and a coordinate-descent-based algorithm for optimal power control. Extensive simulations demonstrate that our proposed algorithm significantly outperforms benchmark methods in improving system utility, reducing average task completion latency, and increasing the number of admitted tasks.

title: Label-free Electrochemical Immunosensor Toward IGFBP-7 Biomarker for Early Monitoring of Acute Kidney Injury
authors: Sukanya Unson, Waralee Ruankham, Kamonrat Phopin, Tanawut Tantimongcolwat
abstract: Insulin-like growth factor-binding protein 7 (IGFBP-7) is a potential biomarker for early diagnosis of acute kidney injury (AKI). Herein, we have developed a novel label-free electrochemical immunosensor based on the self-assembly of 6-(ferrocenyl)hexanethiol (Fc) and antibody-coupled L-cysteine on the gold nanoparticle-modified electrode (Ab/Fc-Au/SPCE) for sensitive detection of IGFBP-7 in urine. Fc was directly embedded on the electrode surface to serve as an intrinsic redox signal for the IGFBP-7 immunosensor. The binding of IGFBP-7 target to the immobilized antibody resulted in a decrease in the peak current of the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurement. The Ab/Fc-Au/SPCE exhibited a good linear DPV response to IGFBP-7 concentrations ranging from 0.0001 to 100.0 pg mL<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.07 pg mL<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>. Validation using spiked artificial urines showed recovery rates in a range of 79.8 to 109.3%. The proposed immunosensor demonstrated good specificity and long-term stability, which are crucial properties for biosensor implementation. Importantly, the electrochemical IGFBP-7 immunosensor can function without the need for external redox agents, requiring only simple steps of sample incubation and subsequent PBS buffer rinsing. Our findings demonstrate a promising potential of Ab/Fc-Au/SPCE immunosensor as a Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) device for rapid detection of IGFBP-7 in early AKI diagnosis.

title: Correction to “Local Information Privacy and Its Applications to Data Aggregation”
authors: Bo Jiang, Ming Li, Ravi Tandon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: In our previous works [1, 2, 3], we defined <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(\epsilon ,\delta )$</tex-math></inline-formula>-Local Information Privacy (LIP) as a context-aware privacy notion and presented the corresponding privacy-preserving mechanism. Then we claim that the mechanism satisfies <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(\epsilon ,0)$</tex-math></inline-formula>-LIP for any <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\epsilon \gt 0$</tex-math></inline-formula> for arbitrary <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$P_{X}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. However, this claim is not completely correct. In this document, we provide a correction to the valid range of privacy parameters of our previously proposed LIP mechanism. Further, we propose efficient algorithms to expand the range of valid privacy parameters. Finally, we discuss the impact on our experimental results, the rationale of the proposed correction and corrected results. Proofs of the main results in this paper are provided in our full version [6].
