title: ML-Guided Branch Predictor Optimization for Embedded RISC-V SoCs
authors: Umer Shahid, Muhammad Tahir, Bilal Zafar
abstract: This paper explores branch predictor design for the CVA6 RISC-V core using gem5 simulation and machine learning-driven parameter optimization with comprehensive validation and cross-architecture analysis. Seven predictors are systematically evaluated using a multi-dimensional framework capturing performance, power, and embedded-specific metrics. Optuna-based tuning with Tree-structured Parzen Estimator explores 2,847 configurations to optimize energy-delay and area-performance trade-offs. Results show ML-optimized tournament predictors achieve 23.7% better performance than static predictors and 8.4% improvement over default configurations, while consuming 31% more power. The optimized bi-modal predictor emerges as optimal for power-constrained IoT applications, offering 94% of tournament predictor performance at 42% lower power consumption. Cross-architecture comparison with ARM Cortex-A53 reveals 9.3% better prediction accuracy for RISC-V with equivalent complexity. The framework provides validated workload-specific recommendations for embedded RISC-V systems with statistical confidence intervals and failure case analysis.

title: A Multi-Semantic Multi-Partition Dual-Layer Alternating Joint Update Markov Random Field Method for Remote Sensing Image Semantic Segmentation
authors: Hongtai Yao, Longxiang Tan, Le Zhao, Chen Zheng, Meng Tian, Yong Jin, Zhentao Hu, Qinglan Peng, Jiayuan Yan
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Semantic segmentation of remote sensing images is an important research direction in remote sensing science. With the increasing resolution of remote sensing imagery, precise semantic segmentation faces the critical challenge of balancing the segmentation of small targets within large-scale remote sensing images. While Markov Random Field (MRF) models have shown promise in spatial modeling, existing multi-layer approaches often suffer from unidirectional information transfer and insufficient feature interaction. Consequently, they struggle to preserve small-scale objects against strong global statistical constraints, leading to smoothed-out boundaries and incomplete information transmission. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Multi-Semantic Multi-Partition Dual-Layer Alternating Joint Update Markov Random Field method (MSMP-DLMRF). We construct a heterogeneous dual-layer framework. The first layer is a coarse-grained high-semantic original layer for global consistency, while the second layer is a fine-grained low-semantic partition layer designed to increase the proportion of small-object areas. Crucially, an alternating joint iterative update mechanism is designed, and a dynamic bidirectional feedback loop is established, so that the exploitation of spatial–feature–semantic relationships across layers is maximized. The proposed method was tested on texture images, GeoEye, LoveDA, Gaofen Image Dataset (GID) and ISPRS Potsdam datasets. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior segmentation performance compared to selected methods. Specifically, MSMP-DLMRF exhibits significant advantages in recognizing small-scale targets and maintaining boundary integrity in complex scenes.

title: Demonstration of a 270-W G-Band Prototype TWT With −3 dB Bandwidth Over 18 GHz
authors: Jian Zhang, Jin Xu, Jinchi Cai, Pengcheng Yin, Xiancai Lin, Hairong Yin, Lingna Yue, Yong Xu, Guoqing Zhao, Wenxiang Wang, Yanyu Wei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: To pursue the ultimate power-bandwidth product (PBP) and efficiency-bandwidth product (EBP) performance of G-band traveling wave tubes (TWTs), in-depth theoretical and simulation-based designs of both the high-frequency and electron optical systems were conducted utilizing a novel rhombic grating slotted sine waveguide (RGS-SWG) and a double-period magnetic focusing system. Theoretical analyses of high-frequency properties and electron beam transmission characteristics demonstrate that the RGS-SWG exhibits low insertion and reflection losses, a broad −3 dB power bandwidth exceeding 19 GHz, and a high maximum output power surpassing 276 W. Furthermore, the high-current electron beam (126 mA) can be stably transmitted over a distance of 73 mm within the high-frequency system without interception. Subsequently, a prototype RGS-SWG TWT was fabricated, and the experimental validation was completed. Hot test results indicate a peak output power of 276 W, a −3 dB power bandwidth greater than 18 GHz, and PBP and EBP values of 4968 W<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot $</tex-math> </inline-formula>GHz and 153.3%<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot $</tex-math> </inline-formula>GHz, respectively. The measured PBP and EBP of this broadband high-power TWT reach approximately <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$5.5\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> those of state-of-the-art existing devices.

title: Few-shot Strip Steel Surface Defect Segmentation via Pre-trained Variational Auto-Encoder based Latent Gaussian Process Regression
authors: Xiaofei Zhou, Xuan Wang, Gongyang Li, Deyang Liu, Qingshan She, Xiaobin Xu, Runmin Cong
abstract: Recently, few-shot strip steel surface defect segmentation has received more and more concerns. However, the existing few-shot segmentation methods usually adopt the frozen encoder, which is pre-trained on the classification task and can only provide class-related knowledge. Therefore, we propose a novel method, namely pre-trained variational auto-encoder based latent gaussian process regression (LGPR), to conduct few-shot strip steel surface defect segmentation. Firstly, different from previous methods, the frozen Variational Auto-Encoder (VAE) based encoder and decoder, which are pretrained by using the pixel-level self-supervised task (i.e., image reconstruction), can provide rich image-related knowledge. This ensures the effective characterization of defect regions. Secondly, by deploying a gaussian process regression in the latent feature space generated by the VAE-based encoder, pixel-level correlation between support features and query features can be efficiently built. This operation is non-parametric and doesn’t bring any training overhead. Besides, we deploy transformer-based projectors to dig long-range contextual cues of support and query features. Extensive experiments are performed on two public datasets, and the experimental results clearly show that our model consistently outperforms the state-of-the-art models with a large margin. Both the codes and results are publicly available at https://github.com/Hlao-hub/LGPR.

title: Enhancing PLS of Indoor IRS-VLC Systems for Colluding and Non-Colluding Eavesdroppers
authors: Rashid Iqbal, Ahmed Zoha, Salama Ikki, Muhammad Ali Imran, Hanaa Abumarshoud
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Most intelligent reflecting surface (IRS)-aided indoor visible light communication (VLC) studies ignore the time delays introduced by reflected paths, even though these delays are inherent in practical wideband systems. In this work, we adopt a realistic assumption of IRS-induced time delay for physical layer security (PLS) enhancement. We consider an indoor VLC system where an IRS is used to shape the channel so that the reflected signals add constructively at the legitimate user and create intersymbol interference at eavesdroppers located inside the coverage area. The resulting secrecy capacity maximisation over the IRS element allocation is formulated as a complex combinatorial optimisation problem and is solved using deep reinforcement learning with proximal policy optimisation (PPO). The approach is evaluated for both colluding eavesdroppers, which combine their received signals, and non-colluding eavesdroppers, which act independently. Simulation results are presented for a range of indoor layouts and user placements, demonstrating significant secrecy capacity gains. In a worst-case scenario, where the eavesdroppers have stronger channels than the legitimate user, the proposed PPO-based IRS allocation improves secrecy capacity by 107% and 235% in the colluding and non-colluding cases, respectively, compared with allocating all IRS elements to the legitimate user. These results demonstrate that time-delay-based IRS control can provide a strong secrecy advantage in practical indoor VLC scenarios.

title: Predicting Cognitive Performance from Three-Electrode Auditory Brainstem Responses Using Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Mobina Malekifar, Yasmeen Hamza, Ye Yang, Hung Cao, Fan-Gang Zeng
abstract: Age-related cognitive decline is a growing global concern, motivating the search for non-invasive, accessible biomarkers to support early detection and monitoring. Click-evoked auditory brainstem responses (ABRs), collected in routine clinical settings, offer a promising signal source. Building on prior evidence that ABRs relate to cognitive function, this study investigates whether raw human ABR waveforms can predict cognitive performance using deep learning without manual peak measurements. We used a dataset from 118 adults spanning a broad range of cognitive abilities, pairing each click-evoked ABR with a cognitive score. A one-dimensional convolutional neural network (CNN) was trained to learn time-series patterns directly from the raw signal. Model performance was evaluated using two, five, and ten-fold cross-validation and compared against traditional wave V metrics and a randomized-input baseline. After adjusting scores for age, the CNN achieved a mean area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.77 ± 0.06, outperforming all benchmarks. To interpret model decisions, Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) was applied. Three key latency windows were identified: 1.8 to 2.3 ms, 3.2 to 4.0 ms, and 4.9 to 6.5 ms. These correspond to canonical ABR waves I, III, and V, supporting the physiological relevance of the learned features and highlighting Wave III as a previously underutilized marker. Although limited to click stimuli from a single recording system, this work demonstrates that a CNN can extract meaningful features from raw ABRs collected using only three electrodes and predict cognitive status more accurately than traditional methods using the same dataset.

title: Crosstalk-Resistant Multifunctional Sensor Based on APTES-Treated Polyaniline/rGO Composite for the Independent Detection of Swelling, Temperature, and CO_supp1-3670850.docx
authors: Hyun-Seok Kim
abstract: Thermal runaway in battery systems involves simultaneous swelling, temperature rise, and CO2 release, necessitating multifunctional sensors capable of detecting these stimuli without signal interference. In this study, we present a crosstalk-resistant multifunctional sensor designed to detect multiple physical stimuli without signal interference. The device incorporates a tempered-glass interlayer that physically and electrically isolates two sensing units. The lower layer, composed of Au and two polyaniline/reduced graphene oxide (PANI/rGO) capacitors, detects both the magnitude and direction of swelling through differential capacitance analysis. The upper sensing layer, fabricated from an APTES-treated PANI/rGO composite, enhances CO2 adsorption and is encapsulated with a selective PDMS layer to decouple temperature and gas responses. The sensor exhibited capacitance variations of 16.58% and 25.04% for square and rectangular swelling packs, respectively, with differential responses of 2.13% and 4.54%, enabling directional discrimination. For temperature sensing, a current variation of 23.8% was observed at 354 K, while CO2 detection at 100 ppm produced a current variation of 12.65%. Temperature effects were compensated using a fitting model, yielding a corrected CO2 response (ygas) of 8.8% at 100 ppm. Coefficients of determination of 0.9520 and 0.9426 were obtained under combined temperature-gas and temperature-only conditions, respectively, confirming the reliability of the model. Overall, the proposed sensor demonstrates a crosstalk-resistant design that enables independent detection of each stimulus while maintaining high selectivity and mechanical stability, underscoring its potential as a multiphysics sensing platform for industrial safety monitoring.

title: Unifying Modality and Scale: Visual Mamba for Feature Fusion in RGB-X Crowd Counting
authors: Yaocong Hu, Mengbo Jia, Pindeng Wang, Ning Jiang, Ke Xu, Wenbo Zhu, Chao Xie, Huanjie Tao, Tianming Ni, Teng Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Crowd counting has long been a crucial topic in the domains of computer vision and video surveillance. In particular, with the widespread use of thermal or depth cameras, RGB-X crowd counting has emerged as a prominent research focus. Although depth or infrared images provide complementary information, the core challenge remains in effectively unifying heterogeneous cross-modality and cross-scale information to form a comprehensive representation of crowd distributions in complex scenes. To address this problem, we propose a novel Mamba-based framework, termed UMS-VMamba-CC for multi-modal (RGB-X) crowd counting. Specifically, we design the cross-modality disentanglement fusion visual Mamba (CMDF-VMamba) that uses self-supervised learning to decompose modality-invariant and modality-specific features in spatial-frequency domains, followed by multi-modal feature aggregation via the gating mechanism. For cross-scale fusion, we design the cross-scale pyramid fusion visual Mamba (CSPF-VMamba), which adopts the bi-directional pyramid structure to incorporate low-level features into high-level representations during downsampling, while upsampling high-level features and aggregating them with low-level features through state space contextual modeling. Comprehensive experiments on multiple mainstream datasets demonstrate that the UMS-VMamba-CC framework achieves competitive performance for RGB-X crowd counting.

title: Boosting Tool Calling Capabilities of Large Language Models via a Novel In-context Learning Approach
authors: Junhao Dong, Wei Zhu
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in natural language understanding, yet they struggle with tasks requiring real-time information retrieval, complex computations, or integration with external tools. Tool calling has emerged as a key paradigm to extend LLM functionalities, but existing methods often rely on fine-tuning or generic retrieval models that are computationally expensive or suboptimal for task-specific demonstration selection. In this paper, we propose DRanker, a lightweight and effective in-context learning framework that enhances tool calling through intelligent demonstration retrieval and reranking. DRanker employs a fine-tuned reranker model, optimized with a ranking-aware loss function, to select high-quality demonstrations from a candidate set retrieved via dense embeddings. Evaluated on the ToolACE and BFCL benchmarks, DRanker consistently outperforms competitive baselines across multiple LLM backbones. Our work highlights the importance of task-aligned demonstration retrieval and offers a scalable solution for improving LLM-based tool calling without significant computational overhead.

title: Analysis of Single Event Transient in SOI GaN-based FinFET due to Heavy-Ion Irradiation
authors: P S Rajakumar, S Satheesh Kumar
abstract: GaN devices offer unrivaled efficiency, high-frequency performance, and superior radiation tolerance over traditional silicon technologies, making them well-suited for space applications. In this work, the SET analysis of GaN-based FinFETs operated in both JL and INV modes under heavy-ion radiation was carried out using a 3-D TCAD simulator. The analysis explores the effect of ion strike location, charge collection, and also the SET impact under various ion incident angles and LET values. Simulation results demonstrate that the channel is the most sensitive region. The peak transient current occurs at a 45° incidence angle. In contrast to JL-mode, INV-mode SOI GaN FinFET improves SET tolerance by exhibiting smaller transient currents, reduced charge collection, and less sensitivity to ion impacts. Furthermore, temperature analysis was also performed to evaluate the influence of ambient temperature on the SET current. When compared to its silicon counterpart, SOI GaN FinFET shows significantly enhanced radiation hardness due to its wide band gap and lower intrinsic carrier concentration. The findings highlight how the SOI GaN FinFET influences SET mitigation, offering valuable direction for designing robust, radiation-hardened nanoelectronic devices.

title: Resilient Initial-Dependent Coordination via Transformed Consensus and Distributed Compensation
authors: Yu Feng, Fuyong Wang, Zhongxin Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of resilient initial-dependent coordination in multi-agent systems with abnormal nodes. Initial-dependent coordination refers to the process where each node's final value converges to the transformed average of the initial values, with inter-node relationships modeled using augmented transformation matrices. This formulation captures a broad class of coordination and information fusion tasks involving coordinate transformations. We propose a resilient transformed consensus protocol and define the conditions required to achieve initial-dependent coordination in the presence of abnormal nodes. To implement these conditions, we design a distributed accounting and compensation mechanism. Specifically, each node maintains a private ledger that records real-time interaction data with its neighbors. Abnormal behaviors are detected by reconciling accounts with neighboring nodes, leveraging historical interaction information. The accounting mechanism provides a more flexible and effective detection condition. To recover from the impact of abnormal behaviors, we design a distributed compensation scheme that guides normal nodes to adjust their states, mitigating the adverse effects caused by abnormal nodes. Finally, numerical simulations in a sensor network under various abnormal behaviors validate the effectiveness of our approach.

title: xDLG: Cross Domain Label-Guided Learning for Cross-Modal Unsupervised Domain Adaptation in 3D Semantic Segmentation_supp1-3668619.mp4
authors: Hui Yin
abstract: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) is critical for cross-modal 3D semantic segmentation in real scenarios, as it significantly reduces the annotation costs in new domains. Existing methods primarily focus on various alignment across domains or modalities, however, domain shift and modal differences are inbuilt, and learning under the guidance of source semantic knowledge instead of forced alignment may be more favorable for UDA tasks. Motivated by this insight, we propose cross Domain Label-Guided learning (xDLG) for cross-modal UDA 3D semantic segmentation, which utilizes multimodal pseudo-labels generated online as a bridge for cross-domain label knowledge transfer. Specifically, we propose two types of label-guided learning: Label-Guided Semantic Prototype Learning (LG-SPL) and Label-Guided Pseudo-Label Learning (LG-PLL). First, in LG-SPL, we utilize massive class instances from the source domain to correct the semantic prototypes in the target domain, embedding genuine semantic similarity to tackle the issue of class confusion. Next in LG-PLL, we conceptualize the refinement of pseudo-labels as a reverse diffusion process, leveraging label information from the source domain as a guiding signal to adjust the semantic distribution of pseudo-labels on a global scale. Furthermore, we incorporate domain-specific cues from target 2D images as supplementary conditions throughout the denoising stages, ensuring semantic consistency at finer details. We evaluate our method on 3D semantic segmentation tasks. The results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple cross-modal UDA scenarios.

title: UBATrack: Spatio-Temporal State Space Model for General Multi-Modal Tracking
authors: Qihua Liang, Liang Chen, Yaozong Zheng, Jian Nong, Zhiyi Mo, Bineng Zhong
abstract: Multi-modal object tracking has attracted consider able attention by integrating multiple complementary inputs (e.g., thermal, depth, and event data) to achieve outstanding performance. Although current general-purpose multi-modal trackers primarily unify various modal tracking tasks (i.e., RGB-Thermal infrared, RGB-Depth or RGB-Event tracking) through prompt learning, they still overlook the effective capture of spatio-temporal cues. In this work, we introduce a novel multi-modal tracking framework based on a mamba-style state space model, termed UBATrack. Our UBATrack comprises two simple yet effective modules: a Spatio-temporal Mamba Adapter (STMA) and a Dynamic Multi-modal Feature Mixer. The former leverages Mamba's long-sequence modeling capability to jointly model cross-modal dependencies and spatio-temporal visual cues in an adapter-tuning manner. The latter further enhances multi-modal representation capacity across multiple feature dimensions to improve tracking robustness. In this way, UBATrack eliminates the need for costly full-parameter fine-tuning, thereby improving the training efficiency of multi-modal tracking algorithms. Experiments show that UBATrack outperforms state-of-the-art methods on RGB-T, RGB-D, and RGB-E tracking benchmarks, achieving outstanding results on the LasHeR, RGBT234, RGBT210, DepthTrack, VOT-RGBD22, and VisEvent datasets.

title: Provable Filter for Real-World Graph Clustering
authors: Xuanting Xie, Erlin Pan, Zhao Kang, Wenyu Chen, Bingheng Li
abstract: Graph clustering, an important unsupervised problem, has been shown to be more resistant to advances in graph neural networks (GNNs). Most existing methods focus on homophilic graphs and ignore heterophily. This significantly limits their applicability, since real-world graphs exhibit a structural disparity and cannot simply be classified as homophily and heterophily. To fill this gap, we provide a principled way to handle practical graphs. Interestingly, we find that most homophilic and heterophilic edges can be correctly identified on the basis of neighbor information. Motivated by it, we construct two graphs that are highly homophilic and heterophilic, respectively. They are used to build low-pass and high-pass filters to capture holistic information. We make the first attempt to provide a theoretical analysis connecting the relationship between filters and clustering performance. Important features are further enhanced by the squeeze-and-excitation (SE) block. We validate our approach through extensive experiments on both homophilic and heterophilic graphs, as well as a co-saliency detection application. Empirical results demonstrate the superiority of our method compared to state-of-the-art clustering methods. In particular, our method achieves an average accuracy improvement of 1.82% on heterophilic graphs and 0.83% on homophilic graphs compared to the best baselines, including RGSL and DGCN.

title: Contrastive Learning and Adversarial Disentanglement for Privacy-Aware Task-Oriented Semantic Communication
authors: Omar Erak, Omar Alhussein, Wen Tong
abstract: Task-oriented semantic communication systems have emerged as a promising approach to achieving efficient and intelligent data transmission in next-generation networks, where only information relevant to a specific task is communicated. This is particularly important in 6G-enabled Internet of Things (6G-IoT) scenarios, where bandwidth constraints, latency requirements, and data privacy are critical. However, existing methods struggle to fully disentangle task-relevant and task-irrelevant information, leading to privacy concerns and suboptimal performance. To address this, we propose an information-bottleneck inspired method, named CLAD (contrastive learning and adversarial disentanglement). CLAD utilizes contrastive learning to effectively capture task-relevant features while employing adversarial disentanglement to discard task-irrelevant information. Additionally, due to the absence of reliable and reproducible methods to quantify the minimality of encoded feature vectors, we introduce the Information Retention Index (IRI), a comparative metric used as a proxy for the mutual information between the encoded features and the input. The IRI reflects how minimal and informative the representation is, making it highly relevant for privacy-preserving and bandwidth-efficient 6G-IoT systems. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CLAD outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of semantic extraction, task performance, privacy preservation, and IRI, making it a promising building block for responsible, efficient and trustworthy 6G-IoT services.

title: Broadband Near-Infrared Compressive Spectral Imaging System with Reflective Structure
authors: Yutong Li, Zhenming Yu, Zhang Wei, Liming Cheng, Jiayu Di, Liang Lin, Jingyue Ma, Tongshuo Zhang, Yue Zhou, Zhao Haiying, Kun Xu
abstract: Near-infrared (NIR) hyperspectral imaging has become a critical tool in modern analytical science. However, conventional NIR hyperspectral imaging systems face challenges such as high cost, bulky instrumentation, and inefficient data collection. In this work, we demonstrate a broadband NIR compressive spectral imaging system that is capable of capturing hyperspectral data that cover a broad spectral bandwidth ranging from 700 to 1600 nm. By segmenting wavelengths and designing specialized optical components, our design overcomes hardware spectral limitations to capture broadband data, while the reflective optical structure makes the system compact. This approach provides a novel technical solution for NIR hyperspectral imaging.

title: Toward Climate-Adaptive Low-Carbon Power System Planning: A Multistage Stochastic Framework Considering Climate Uncertainties
authors: Chenjia Gu, Jiaqi Ruan, Yiwei Qiu, Tianlei Zang, Shi Chen, Zhao Xu, Fushuan Wen, Pei Zhang, Zhao Yang Dong, Peng Wang
abstract: Climate change is progressively reshaping the spatiotemporal dynamics of renewable energy sources such as wind and solar, intensifying the complexity and uncertainty of long-term power system planning. Existing planning frameworks are largely focused on climate mitigation strategies but often overlook the critical dimension of climate adaptation, limiting their efficacy in managing evolving climatic risks. In response, this article proposes a multistage stochastic low-carbon planning framework that incorporates climate-related uncertainties into system planning decision-making. By embedding climate evolution trajectories into the planning horizon, the proposed approach determines optimal stage-wise planning pathways that jointly accommodate mitigation goals and adaptation imperatives under long-term climate uncertainties. First, a systematic climate uncertainty modeling approach is developed to capture both scenario uncertainty and climate response uncertainty through the construction of a representative scenario tree. Second, to reconcile the temporal mismatch between coarse-resolution climate projections and the finegrained requirements of power system planning, a climate-consistent temporal downscaling method is proposed to transform long-term climate projections into high-resolution, hourly level data. Third, to address the computational complexity inherent in the multistage planning problem, a tailored decomposition-based stochastic dual dynamic programming algorithm is developed, which operates on a stage-wise clustered scenario tree to leverage the tree’s structural compactness for accelerated convergence and scalable optimization under climate-related uncertainties. Numerical studies demonstrate that the proposed climate-adaptive planning framework enhances the power system’s ability to manage climate-induced risks while maintaining cost-effectiveness across a wide range of plausible climate futures.

title: Energy-Efficient Spiking Attention YOLO Network for SAR Ship Detection
authors: Zihan Wang, Yanxing Liu, Yizhe Fan, Qiang Li, Zhe Zhang, Shushan Qiao, Bingchen Zhang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) ship detection is critical to a wide range of maritime applications. Traditional artificial neural networks (ANNs) achieve high detection performance but incur substantial energy consumption, limiting their deployment on edge devices. In contrast, brain-inspired spiking neural networks (SNNs) offer energy-efficiency benefits. Moreover, attention mechanisms can strengthen ship feature representation and suppress background noise in SAR imagery, thereby improving detection accuracy. Motivated by these observations, we propose ESA-YOLO: an energy-efficient spiking attention–based YOLO network for SAR ship detection. First, ESA-YOLO adopts a spike-driven paradigm relying on sparse additive operations to effectively reduce energy consumption. Second, it integrates a spiking attention mechanism. This module captures content-dependent global interactions and long-range context, improving detection performance. Experiments on the HRSID dataset confirm ESA-YOLO’s energy efficiency for SAR ship detection. The results also show that SNNs can be deployed on neuromorphic hardware such as the Wentian chip to reduce energy consumption.

title: The Effect of the COVID-19 Pandemic on Stock Return in Technology Sector Companies on the Indonesian Stock Exchange
authors: Riki Martusa, Meythi Meythi, Ririn Suyanto, Sarah Novita Umbu Dongu, Cynthia Stephani Pardosi
abstract: This study looks into whether stock returns of technology businesses registered on the Indonesia Stock Exchange are affected by the Covid-19 pandemic. The growth in positive confirmed cases, deaths, and Covid-19 recoveries are among the data used. The Covid-19 pandemic has had an impact on several economic sectors, including the stock market, as seen by changes in the Jakarta Composite Index (JCI). Developing nations like Indonesia have been particularly hard hit [1]. Seven technological businesses that had varying overall stock returns during the pandemic make up the sample. Multiple linear regression with a partial test and coefficient of determination is part of the analysis technique. The findings indicate that while an increase in cases resulting in death has no discernible effect on stock returns, an increase in overall Covid-19 infections does. During the pandemic, digital transformation offers IT companies a chance to boost productivity and profitability. In light of the global crisis, the findings offer crucial information for stakeholders and investors in the technology sector.

title: Active–Subspace Particle Filtering for Efficient Inference in High-Dimensional State-Space Models
authors: Petar M. Djurić, Nahid Shirdel Abdolmaleki, Anand Ravishankar, Joaquín Míguez
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Particle filtering methods provide a powerful framework for state estimation in nonlinear and non-Gaussian systems. However, their performance deteriorates in high-dimensional settings due to the difficulty of proposing particles that align with the posterior distribution. In many models, the likelihood depends primarily on a low-dimensional projection of the latent state. We introduce the active-subspace particle filter (AS-PF) that exploits this structure to improve sample efficiency. At each time step, the AS-PF estimates a likelihood-informed subspace using particle-based score statistics and constructs structured proposals that concentrate particle mass along the informative directions. The method employs an optimal importance function (which minimizes the weight variance) in the active subspace and propagates the inactive components under the prior. This approach reduces the effects of dimensionality and enhances robustness in complex state-space models. Experimental results on synthesized data demonstrate that AS–PF consistently outperforms standard particle filters, particularly when the observation model has a low-dimensional structure.

title: Bat-Grid SLAM: Bio-Inspired Active SLAM Approach Using Neural Grid Cells for Autonomous Navigation
authors: Chun Yu Lim, Henrik Hesse, Chee Kiat Seow
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Active Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping (SLAM) systems face significant computational bottlenecks due to frontier-based exploration strategies requiring extensive environmental analysis for path planning. Drawing inspiration from the hexagonal firing patterns of grid cells in the mammalian entorhinal cortex, we present a bio-inspired active SLAM framework, Bat-Grid SLAM, that transforms spatial exploration into an efficient tessellated navigation problem. Our approach replaces computationally expensive frontier detection with a structured hexagonal grid representation that mimics the spatial encoding mechanisms observed in rodents. Grid cells and the Laplacian of Gaussian algorithm create an optimal scanning zone, with fewer exploration points, resulting in 16 percent faster mapping performance compared to conventional active SLAM techniques.

title: Performance Dynamics in Big Data Cloud Streaming Systems
authors: Tahmina Akter Anondi, Ezekiel Nwokolo
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This paper investigates the empirical performance characteristics of in-memory streaming database management systems within a Big Data Cloud context. We present a comparative analysis of a modern distributed streaming framework against a commercial counterpart, evaluating their throughput and latency under varying conditions. The methodology incorporates a continuous feedback approach for data generation and performance analysis, enabling precise measurement and identification of system saturation points. This work provides critical insights into the operational dynamics and scalability of streaming architectures in Big Data Cloud environments, informing future design and optimization efforts.

title: Modeling, Sizing and Simulation of a Water Pumping System Based on an Induction Motor and a Centrifugal Pump
authors: Ali Bouagga, Mohammed Mazen Alhato, Soufiene Bouallègue
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Modeling and simulation of water pumping systems are essential for optimizing energy efficiency and improving the performance of agricultural and industrial installations. This paper presents a numerical approach for modeling, sizing and simulating a water submersible pumping system, consisting of a centrifugal pump and a three-phase induction motor. The centrifugal pump is chosen for its ability to provide a constant flow rate and its simplicity in integrating into large-scale pumping systems. The three-phase induction motor, due to its robustness, reliability, and low maintenance cost, is a promising choice to drive the centrifugal pump. Dynamic modeling of the designed pumping system is performed and numerical simulations are carried out allowing the study of the transient behaviors of the plant, including variations in the flow rate, pressure, and performance characteristics of the motor. Demonstrative results show the effectiveness of the proposed induction motor-drive centrifugal pump in terms of ability to provide constant power and fast transient responses to load changes. The suggested water pumping solution is analyzed for its reliability, energy efficiency and performance.

title: Editorial Interview: Proliferating Free-Space Laser Communications for Space-Based Networks and Scientific Exploration

title: Retrospective motion correction in MRI using disentangled embeddings
authors: Qi Wang, Veronika Ecker, Marcel Früh, Sergios Gatidis, Thomas Küstner
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: Physiological motion can affect the diagnostic quality of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). While various retrospective motion correction methods exist, many struggle to generalize across different motion types and body regions. In particular, machine learning (ML)-based corrections are often tailored to specific applications and datasets. We hypothesize that motion artifacts, though diverse, share underlying patterns that can be disentangled and exploited. To address this, we propose a hierarchical vector-quantized (VQ) variational auto-encoder that learns a disentangled embedding of motion-to-clean image features. A codebook is deployed to capture finite collection of motion patterns at multiple resolutions, enabling coarse-to-fine correction. An auto-regressive model is trained to learn the prior distribution of motion-free images and is used at inference to guide the correction process. Unlike conventional approaches, our method does not require artifact-specific training and can generalize to unseen motion patterns. We demonstrate the approach on simulated whole-body motion artifacts and observe robust correction across varying motion severity. Our results suggest that the model effectively disentangled physical motion of the simulated motion-effective scans, therefore, improving the generalizability of the ML-based MRI motion correction. Our work of disentangling the motion features shed a light on its potential application across anatomical regions and motion types.

title: Coherent Signal DOA Estimation for Coprime EMVS Array via TNN Minimization
authors: Mingcheng Fu, Xianpeng Wang, Yuehao Guo, Xiang Lan, Guilu Wu, Dandan Meng
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: In this paper, we suggest a direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation algorithm for coherent sources with coprime electromagnetic vector sensor (EMVS) array. Firstly, the array interpolation is performed on the output of the coprime EMVS array, and a tensor nuclear norm (TNN) minimization problem is constructed to recover the corresponding third-order tensor. Based on the reconstructed tensor, the DOA estimates can be obtained by exploiting the MUISC searching. Compared with the existing methods, the proposed method can offer enhanced DOA estimation accuracy. Experimental results validate the performance superiority of the proposed algorithm over the state-of-the-art methods.

title: Digitalization of MSME Financial Records: The Foundation of Innovation and Sustainable Industrialization in Indonesia
authors: Wahyu Sardjono, Lisfi Dita Anggraini, Sarim, Ilham Radito, Widhilaga Gia Perdana, Maryani Maryani
abstract: Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) represent a foundational component of the Indonesian economy, contributing in excess of 60% to the national Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and employing nearly 97% of the labor force. However, the majority of MSMEs still face challenges in efficient financial management due to error-prone manual records. Digitalization of financial records through accounting information systems is an important solution to improve efficiency, transparency, and access to formal financial services. This article examines the role of digitizing MSME financial records in supporting innovation and sustainable industrialization in accordance with Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 9, which focuses on building resilient infrastructure, inclusive industrialization, and innovation. Using a literature review method, this article finds that digitization can increase MSME productivity by up to 35%, accelerate access to formal financing, and support data-driven decision-making. However, challenges such as low digital literacy and uneven internet infrastructure are still major obstacles. This article recommends digital literacy training, government support, and the development of affordable accounting applications as strategic steps to accelerate the digital transformation of MSMEs in Indonesia.

title: The Role of Word of Mouth and Social Media in Indonesian MSMEs Recruitment Through the Lens of Social Capital
authors: Christian Lim, Kevin Christian Yordan, Jasmin Callista Abubakar, Synthia Atas Sari
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Indonesian MSMEs role in job creation and economic growth is extremely significant. However, their recruitment practices are still embedded in their social capital through word of mouth (WOM) and social media. Despite the growing studies in WOM and social media recruitment, there is a lack of research that focuses on Indonesian MSMEs through the lens of Social Capital. Employing a qualitative approach, we conducted semi-structured interviews with 12 Indonesian MSMEs owners or managers to understand the dynamics of WOM and social media in MSMEs recruitment. It is revealed that referrals are still evaluated despite sourced from social ties, with cultural fit commonly preferred and professionalism outweighs pressure to hire from personal networks. Social media raises credibility and authenticity concerns despite its expansive reach, while WOM is widely used due to trust factor, credibility, and cultural alignment. The research contributes to the Indonesian MSMEs recruitment literature by integrating Social Capital with WOM and social media.

title: Design and Implementation of a Multi-Agent Testing Platform for Autonomous Driving Simulation
authors: Xiaoyong Zhang, Jianrong Xu, Sen Wang, Jiaxiang Zhu, Youlun Zheng, Xizheng Wang, Xiaochun Zhang
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: High-fidelity simulation is essential for validating autonomous driving systems in diverse and safety-critical conditions, and without the cost and risk of real-world testing. However, existing platforms often lack realistic multi-agent interaction, comprehensive scenario coverage, and standardized closed-loop evaluation. This paper presents a modular multi-agent testing platform for autonomous driving simulation supporting both software-in-the-loop (SIL) and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing. The platform integrates scenario generation, agent behavior modeling, multi-modal sensor simulation, and a four-dimensional evaluation framework covering safety, efficiency, comfort, and robustness. A transformer-based agent model with context-aware embedding, hierarchical temporal windows, and dual-query decoding captures complex inter-agent interactions in dense traffic. Experiments with up to 500 concurrent agents in realistic urban layouts with 0.78 RMM score demonstrate that the platform achieves high scalability and temporal fidelity, while enabling reproducible, statistically rigorous comparisons with rule-based and learningbased baselines.

title: Adaptive Underactuated Robotic Gripper with Gear-Multi-Link Mechanism for Composite Grasping*
authors: Yiwei Sheng, Wenzeng Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This paper introduces an adaptive finger mechanism suitable for environmental constraints, named the GML Gripper (Gear-Multi-Link Gripper). This mechanism is based on the coupling of a symmetric five-linkage mechanism and a two-stage parallelogram system and is actuated by a single actuator to achieve multiple composite grasping functions. Specifically, the GML Gripper utilizes the linear motion characteristics of the symmetric five-linkage to achieve compliance in the vertical direction and employs the two-stage parallelogram linkage system to ensure line contact at the fingertip. Parallel grasping achieves a firm hold on conventional objects through stable line contact, asymmetrical scooping can effectively grasp thin objects, and symmetrical scooping allows for the grasping of irregular or large-volume objects. After analyzing the working principle of the mechanism through kinematics and statics, a 3D-printed prototype was used for experimental validation of the gripper's grasping performance under environmental constraints. This research provides a new approach to the design of adaptive grippers and expands the potential applications of underactuated robotic grippers in complex environments.

title: Optimizing User Experience at Bfresh Dental Care Clinic: Usability Testing Analysis and Efficiency Improvement
authors: Annisa Ardillah, Medita Kurnia Atikasari, Vania Rahadyanti Yogi Iswari, Teguh Prasandy
abstract: The Bfresh Dental Care website, as an online denial health service platform, still presents several usability issues that hinder patients from accessing information and booking appointments efficiently. This study evaluates the user experience of the BFresh Dental Care Clinic website using usability testing based on five dimensions: learnability, efficiency, memorability, errors, and satisfaction. Data were collected through an online questionnaire from 100 users and analyzed using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM). The structural model explains 45.8% of the variance in efficiency, 49.2% in memorability, and 43.8% in satisfaction. Learnability shows a large effect on both efficiency and memorability, while errors have a moderate effect on satisfaction (F-square = 0.168); efficiency contributes only a small effect to satisfaction (F-square = 0.032). These findings indicate that error prevention and easy-to-learn interactions are more critical to user satisfaction than speed alone, particularly in the doctor’s schedule search feature, which users still perceive as inefficient. The study proposes a redesigned doctor’s schedule page with enhanced search filters and clearer navigation to improve information access and support more efficient appointment booking on the BFresh Dental Care website.

title: Real-Time Robot Perception and Response for Luggage Carrying Service
authors: Reem Moumen Mohammed Idres, Zati Hakim Azizul, Nor Aniq Haqym Nor Azman
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Service robots are increasingly being developed to assist humans in various environments. However, in public environments, identifying individuals who require luggage handling assistance remains an underexplored area in service robotics. While existing studies focus on surveillance or object tracking, few address perception-driven interaction for assisting people with their belongings. This paper presents the integration and implementation of vision and speech models on a ROS-based mobile robot to identify individuals who may require help with luggage carrying. The proposed system integrates a YOLOv8 object detection model trained on five classes: person, backpack, handbag, paper bag, and suitcase, with an RGB-D camera to obtain depth-based distance measurements. The robot applies specific proximity-based rules to identify a Person of Interest (POI). It initiates interaction with the user through a touchscreen interface. Experimental results demonstrate the system's ability to accurately detect both individuals and luggage, while enabling real-time interaction. Future work will aim to improve detection accuracy in crowded environments, integrate voice-based interaction and enable the robot to identify individuals who genuinely need assistance through behavioural cues.

title: Enhancing Student Well-being Through Generative AI: A Design Science Approach to Creative Learning
authors: Jarot S. Suroso, M. Fahri Hibatullah Suroso
abstract: Learning in the classroom can be stressful for students, leading to mental health issues. With numerous tasks and academic activities to complete. Information Technology offers a solution. Generative AI, in particular, can enhance the learning process. This research aims to provide a solution using Generative AI as a tool to develop creative learning that fosters a fun environment. The method employed was Design Science Research, which provides a framework for developing creative learning and innovative artifacts that promote student well- being. By utilizing DSR, the experiment demonstrated an increase in student motivation and interactive learning, resulting in a positive classroom atmosphere experience for teachers and students.

title: Diffusion Models Learn Low-Dimensional Distributions VIA Subspace Clustering
authors: Peng Wang, Huijie Zhang, Zekai Zhang, Siyi Chen, Yi Ma, Qing Qu
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: In this work, we develop a new mathematical framework to explain how diffusion models can effectively learn low-dimensional distributions from a finite number of training samples without succumbing to the curse of dimensionality. Motivated by the intrinsic low-dimensional structure of image data, we theoretically analyze a setting in which the data distribution is modeled as a mixture of low-rank Gaussians. Under a suitable network parameterization, we show that optimizing the training objective of diffusion models is equivalent to solving the canonical subspace clustering problem over the training samples. This equivalence allows us to demonstrate that the sample complexity for learning the underlying distribution scales linearly with the intrinsic dimension of the data, rather than exponentially with the ambient dimension. Our theoretical findings are further supported by empirical evidence that demonstrates phase transition phenomena in generalization on both synthetic and real-world image datasets.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Integrated Order Picking and Inventory Control
authors: Rajgopal Devabhaktuni
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Contemporary warehouse systems experience increased sophistication in striking a balance between operational efficiency, response time, and cost containment. This paper delineates a unified Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) framework for the simultaneous optimization of three dependent warehouse sub-functions—dynamic order picking, multi-echelon inventory control, and storage assignment with replenishment—in a single decision environment. A Deep Q-Network (DQN) absorbs a joint policy that jointly coordinates picker routing, echelon-level inventory allocations, and SKU storage dynamics under a sequential state–action formulation. The model has been trained and tested on 429 synthesized warehouse states developed using a SimPy discrete-event simulator interfaced with TensorFlow-based DRL agents. Results show that the DRL framework proposed here dominates existing traditional heuristic and rule-based schemes by decreasing average order cycle time by 22% and inventory holding cost by 15% and achieving increased fulfillment consistency. The research demonstrates the possibility of AI-based warehouse management systems that are competent to perform end-to-end optimization across operation levels.

title: Implementing Real-Time Retail Shelf Monitoring via YOLOv8 on a ROS-Based Mobile Robot
authors: Dao Jing Hoong, Zati Hakim Azizul, Jing Hui Chia
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Maintaining optimal shelf inventory and product availability is critical in the contemporary retail landscape to maximise sales and customer satisfaction. The demand for service robots is increasing, but few existing works have explored retail product detection, classification, and counting on a robotic platform. This paper presents an automated, real-time system for monitoring retail products. The primary goal is to detect, classify, and count retail products on shelves to streamline inventory management and minimise stockouts. This work utilised the YOLOv8 object detection model, selected for its superior balance of speed and accuracy, and trained it on the Freiburg Groceries Dataset. The system was implemented on a robot based on ROS. The model achieved a mean Average Precision (mAP50) of 88.3%, and completed detection within 3 seconds, showcasing a robust and scalable solution for automated retail product management.

title: Multi-turn Planar Magnetic Loop Antenna for Surface Partial Discharge Detection
authors: Mohamed Hadj Said, Mayssa Salem, Sana Chouabi, Mossaad Ben Ayed, Andrea Cavallini, Fares Tounsi
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: This paper presents the design, simulation, and experimental validation of a PCB-based multi-turn square loop antenna on an FR-4 substrate for detecting artificial surface partial discharges (SPD). SPD is a specific type of partial discharge (PD) that occurs along insulation surfaces in high-voltage equipment and is often caused by contamination, moisture, or poor installation. Early detection is critical to avoid insulation failure and equipment damage. The proposed antenna is optimized with a self-resonant frequency of 40 MHz, within the VHF range where SPD signals typically radiate. After simulation, the antenna was fabricated using standard PCB processes and tested in a controlled SPD environment. Measurements confirmed successful SPD detection, with a 30 mV output observed at a 1 MΩ oscilloscope input. Compared to conventional sensors, the developed solution offers key advantages: compact size, lightweight construction, and low manufacturing cost—making it a practical and scalable alternative for PD monitoring in high-voltage applications.

title: The Influence of Corporate Culture on Employees’ Organizational Identification in The St. Regis Shanghai Jingan
authors: Na-Na Li, Yi-Ran Qian
abstract: This study explores the influence of hospitality corporate culture on employees’ organizational identification using The St. Regis Shanghai Jingan as a case study. In terms of corporate culture, consistency, mission and adaptability score above average and outperforms involvement. Employee organizational identification scores moderately positive. Belonging and values identification score highly than emotional and development identification. Particularly, employees expressed low identification with career development opportunities. Corporate culture exhibits a strong positive correlation with organizational identification, and consistency within corporate culture exerts a significant positive influence on employees’ organizational identification. Based on the findings, a series of measures are proposed to improve employees’ organizational identification, including strengthening cultural alignment, refining career development mechanisms, and stimulating communication, team cooperation and employee participation in hotel operation.

title: Molecular-driven Multi-view Hypergraph Contrastive Learning for Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction
authors: Xinyu Li, Ruijie Li, Qiao Ning, Shikai Guo, Hui Li, Qian Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Recent concerns have arisen over adverse reactions caused by drug combinations, and drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction helps identify potential risks by forecasting interactions between drugs. Previous methods have primarily explored drug interactions from the superficial level of drug molecules, often overlooking the internal structural information of the molecules. To this end, we propose Mol-HCL, a multi-view hypergraph contrastive learning framework based on molecular view. In this framework, we construct the molecular view to learn the internal information of drug molecules and, based on this, develop structural view and semantic view. These three views collaboratively learn both intra-molecular and inter-molecular information. Subsequently, we incorporate hypernodes into the structural view and design a novel hyperchain, integrating it into the semantic view to capture latent neighbor drug node structural relationships and long-range DDI chain semantic information. After that, contrastive learning is performed between the structural hypergraph and the molecular view, as well as between the semantic hypergraph and the molecular view, to enhance the representations learned from the molecular view. Finally, we conduct experiments on two real-world scientific datasets. The experimental results demonstrate a significant improvement of Mol-HCL over existing methods, showcasing its effectiveness and advantages in DDI prediction. The data and source code are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ningq669/Mol-HCL</uri>.

title: Towards Generalizable Deepfake Detection via Forgery-aware Audio-Visual Adaptation: A Variational Bayesian Approach
authors: Fan Nie, Jiangqun Ni, Jian Zhang, Bin Zhang, Weizhe Zhang, Bin Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The widespread application of AIGC contents has brought not only unprecedented opportunities, but also potential security concerns, e.g., audio-visual deepfakes. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop an effective and generalizable method for multi-modal deepfake detection. Typically, the audio-visual correlation learning could expose subtle cross-modal inconsistencies, e.g., audio-visual misalignment, which serve as crucial clues in deepfake detection. In this paper, we reformulate the correlation learning with variational Bayesian estimation, where audio-visual correlation is approximated as a Gaussian distributed latent variable, and thus develop a novel framework for deepfake detection, i.e., Forgery-aware Audio-Visual Adaptation with Variational Bayes (FoVB). Specifically, given the prior knowledge of pre-trained backbones, we adopt two core designs to estimate audio-visual correlations effectively. First, we exploit various difference convolutions and a high-pass filter to discern local and global forgery traces from both modalities. Second, with the extracted forgery-aware features, we estimate the latent Gaussian variable of audio-visual correlation via variational Bayes. Then, we factorize the variable into modality-specific and correlation-specific ones with orthogonality constraint, allowing them to better learn intra-modal and cross-modal forgery traces with less entanglement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our FoVB outperforms other state-of-the-art methods in various benchmarks.

title:
Passivation-Free Ga-Polar AlGaN/GaN Recessed-Gate HEMTs on Sapphire With 2.8W/mm P
                    <sub>OUT</sub>
                    and 26.8% PAE at 94 GHz
authors: Ruixin Bai, Swarnav Mukhopadhyay, Michael Elliott, Ryan Gilbert, Jiahao Chen, Rafael A. Choudhury, Kyudong Kim, Yu-Chun Wang, Ahmad E. Islam, Andrew J. Green, Shubhra S. Pasayat, Chirag Gupta
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this work, we demonstrate a passivation-free Ga-polar recessed-gate AlGaN/GaN HEMT on a sapphire substrate for W-band operation, featuring a 5.5-nm Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.35</sub>Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.65</sub>N barrier under the gate and a 31nm Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.35</sub>Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.65</sub>N barrier in the gate access regions. The device achieves a drain current density of 1.7A/mm, a peak transconductance of 750mS/mm, and low gate leakage with a high <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>/<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small> ratio of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{7}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Small-signal characterization reveals a current-gain cutoff frequency of 127GHz and a maximum oscillation frequency of 203GHz. Continuous-wave (CW) load-pull measurements at 94GHz demonstrate an output power density of 2.8W/mm with 26.8% power-added efficiency (PAE), both of which are approximately twice the values previously reported for Ga-polar GaN HEMTs fabricated on sapphire substrates and are comparable to state-of-the-art Ga-polar GaN HEMTs on SiC substrates. Considering the low cost of sapphire, the simplicity of the epitaxial design, and the reduced fabrication complexity relative to N-polar devices, this work highlights the potential of recessed-gate Ga-polar AlGaN/GaN HEMTs on sapphire as a promising candidate for next-generation millimeter-wave power applications.

title: BD-YOLO: Boosting Small Bridge Detection via Edge-Aware Super-Resolution and Gabor-Laplacian Convolutions
authors: Chengyi Shi, Xin Li, Xin Lyu, Xuejie Zhang, Xinyu Li, Che Gong, Hao Yan, Jing Liu, Fan Liu, Hongmin Gao
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Bridge detection (BD) in remote sensing images (RSIs) is an essential task for applications such as transportation monitoring and emergency response. However, detecting small bridges remains challenging because they occupy limited pixels, exhibit weak semantic cues, and are dominated by high-frequency structural textures, which are often inadequately preserved by conventional detectors during feature extraction and multi-scale fusion. To address these challenges, we propose BridgeDetection-YOLO (BD-YOLO), a YOLO-based detection framework tailored for small bridge detection in RSIs. BD-YOLO explicitly targets two key limitations of existing YOLO-based detectors. First, a BridgeConvolution (BrConv) module is introduced to enhance discriminative high-frequency bridge structures while suppressing background interference by integrating Tanh-modulated Gabor and deformable Laplacian-of-Gaussian kernels. Second, an Edge-Aware Super-Resolution (EASR) module is incorporated into the feature fusion stage to recover fine-grained structural details through edge-guided PixelShuffle upsampling, enabling more effective texture-aware multi-scale aggregation. Experiments conducted on two benchmarks demonstrate that BD-YOLO consistently outperforms representative detection methods in small bridge detection. In particular, BD-YOLO improves mAP@50 from 68.4% to 72.1% on the GLH-Bridge dataset and from 49.0% to 50.1% on the MBDD dataset compared with the YOLOv8 baseline, while achieving up to a 6.2% mAP@50 gain over lightweight small-object detectors. Ablation studies further validate the individual effectiveness of BrConv and EASR, as well as their complementary contributions to enhancing high-frequency structural representation and fine-detail recovery. The source codes and datasets are available at https://github.com/chengyi11/BD-YOLO/tree/master.

title: An Underactuated Variable Stiffness Continuum Robot with Multi-Layer Jamming Spherical Joints
authors: Xinpei Ai, Can Li, Yadong Zhang, Yifei Chen, Chen Chen, Huijiang Wang, Rui Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Continuum robots(CRs) are featured with high compliance and adaptability. However, existing CRs commonly face issues of insufficient stiffness and actuation redundancy. This paper proposes a novel design of multi-layer jamming spherical joint (MLJSJ) and develops a pneumatically actuated variable-stiffness continuum robot. A segmented spherical expansion actuator airbag, resembling Tanghulu, is fabricated via the lost-wax method to meet the positive-pressure actuation requirements. The proposed robot consists of four serially connected continuum segments, each containing four spherical joints for smooth bending motion. By leveraging multi-layer frictional interactions within jamming structures, the robot achieves rapid and wide-range stiffness modulation under relatively low air pressure. Each continuum segment can be locked via a dedicated actuation airbag, with its pressure regulated by a proportional valve to enable rapid switching between rigid and flexible states. By switching the lock-unlock states of segments, the variable-stiffness mechanism decouples multi-segment motions into single-segment bending, enabling sequential actuation of all segments by using only four embedded alloy rods. A dedicated experimental setup was developed to characterize performance, demonstrating that a single segment reaches a maximum stiffness of 1403.03 N/m, with a stiffness modulation ratio of 35.85 and switching times ranging from 0.07 s to 0.43 s. Furthermore, by controlling the stiffness states and bending motions of different segments, the single segment of robot demonstrates a maximum payload capacity of 2 kg and versatile motion flexibility verified through obstacle avoidance and grasping tasks.
