title: Development of An Edge Computing System for Estimating Root Mean Square and Median Frequency of Surface Electromyogram with Mechanomyograms
authors: Shing-Hong Liu, Bo-Yan Wu, Wenxi Chen, Jia-Jung Wang, Peir Renn Wang
abstract: Following the development of smart wearable devices and edge computing technologies, a system capable of assessing the condition of the muscle activity may have high potential for application in sport’s fields. Usually, both the root mean square (RMS) and median frequency (MDF) of surface electromyogram (sEMG) have been used to evaluate the isotonic and isometric muscle conditions. However, there exists a major disadvantage in the measurement of sEMG, since it is easily interfered by skin and electrode’s motion during the isotonic contraction. Such interference may decrease the signal to noise ratio (S/N) and the precision of RMS and MDF in sEMG. Thus, the aim of this study is to apply mechanomyograms (MMGs) to estimate the RMS and MDF of sEMG with the machine learning (ML) models. The proposed method was implemented in a STM32 microcontroller (MCU) to achieve a real-time portable system for monitoring muscle activities. The experiment recruited 20 healthy subjects who were requested to walk 150 meters, then to climb seven floors and return, followed by walking another 150 meters. During this process, sensors of sEMG and MMG were attached to the vastus lateralis and gastrocnemius muscles for synchronous signal acquisition. To predict two indexes, RMS and MDF of sEMG, this study extracted five parameters from MMGs, including RMS, MDF, standard deviation (SD), skewness, and kurtosis. In the PC-based analysis, these five parameters were used to train and test the ML models. But, in the STM32-based analysis, the MDF of MMGs was not included due to computational resource limitations. This research compared the performance of several regression models, including Random Forest (RF), CatBoost, XGBoost, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and k-Nearest Neighbors (KNN). The hyperparameter tuning was made via the Optuna tool. The Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) was used as the primary evaluation metric. The PC-based system without any model limitation showed that the XGBoost model demonstrated the best results with PCCs of 0.911 and 0.902 for estimating RMS and MDF of sEMG, respectively. Its size was 1947 kB and 2085 kB, respectively. The STM32-based system simulated by the PC-based system only using four time-domain parameters and under 1MB of model limitation showed that the XGBoost model provided PCCs of 0.876 and 0.858 for RMS and MDF estimations, respectively. Its size was only 435 kB and 483 kB, respectively. Then, we embedded this XGBoost model in a STM32F756ZG NUCLEO development board for edging computing. Its performance showed PCCs of 0.856 and 0.864 for RMS and MDF estimations, respectively. This study successfully explored an efficient model for estimating RMS and MDF of sEMGs using five parameters from MMGs, and XGBoost with best performance were embedded in STM32F756ZG microcontroller to execute an edge computing for monitoring the muscle activities in real time. PCC and MAPE of XGBoost approached 0.856 and 21.69% for RMS estimation, and 0.864 and 20.04% for MDF estimation. The size of the STM32-based XGBoost for MDF estimation was 483 KB, and for RMS estimation was 435 KB. Therefore, the proposed edge computing models effectively balance the accuracy and resource limitations, which supports a new application in the future portable physiological monitoring system.

title: An Integrated Auxiliary Power System for High-Speed Electromagnetic Suspension Maglev Trains
authors: Haoyun Wang, Kun Zhang, Jiaxin Zhang, Xinmai Gao, Ruikun Mai
abstract: To achieve non-contact auxiliary power supply during low-speed operation of high-speed electromagnetic suspension maglev trains while minimizing the weight of the coupling mechanism, this paper proposes an integrated high-low speed auxiliary power supply system. During low-speed operation and station stops, two-phase transmitting coils are added to the stator slots to generate a high-frequency magnetic field, while the existing onboard collector coils are repurposed for harvesting high-frequency energy. To ensure efficient transmission of both low-frequency motional energy and high-frequency induced energy to the onboard electrical equipment, a hybrid high-low frequency energy coexistence circuit (HLC) is designed, which achieves full compensation of high-frequency energy while exerting minimal impact on low-frequency energy transmission. The proposed system has no impact on the train’s original systems and requires no additional coils to be installed on the vehicle. To validate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed solution, a full-scale (1:1) prototype was developed and experimentally validated. Results demonstrate that a single power generation branch can consistently deliver over 600 W of output power, regardless of whether the collector coil is aligned with the stator tooth or slot.

title: CNN-based vessel tracking in infrared images with an unmanned wing-in-ground vehicle
authors: Miguel Domínguez, Sarthak Mishra, Ramón A. Suárez Fernández, Zorana Milošević, Claudio Rossi, Sergio Domínguez
abstract: The urge for the development of unmanned autonomous vehicles that can satisfy the needs of the supply chains, covering distances in the shortest time possible while being environmentally sustainable, has prompted new lines of research that try to achieve all of these requirements into existing, yet not that popular vehicles, such as the wing-in-ground effect vehicle, whose proximity to the ground during its flights results in a reduction of drag and high lift, which translates into excellent performances in terms of energy consumption. Focusing on providing the vehicle with another tool towards autonomous operability, in this paper, we have developed a vessel detection and tracking system for the wing-in-ground craft using Convolutional Neural Networks trained to detect boats in infrared images. In order to test and validate the system, we have developed a dual simulator architecture using X-Plane 12 as the flight and physics simulator and Gazebo as the sensors system and the maritime environment simulator. The results of this article and the experiments presented here are a step forward toward the future operability of an unmanned wing-in-ground vehicle in defense, surveillance, and search and rescue missions performed at night or under low visibility conditions, in complex scenarios such as the maritime environment.

title: Learning Prompt Adapters for Forgetting-Free Continual Image Super-resolution
authors: Chaowei Fang, Bolin Fu, De Cheng, Chengpei Tang, Guanbin Li
abstract: Continual image super-resolution (CISR) aims to efficiently adapt a pre-trained model to a variety of tasks while retaining knowledge from previously learned tasks, minimizing the need for intensive independent training. The primary challenges include catastrophic forgetting due to varying data distributions and degradation types, along with the necessity for high adaptability. While prompt-based continual learning has proven effective in image classification, its direct application to super-resolution (SR) often fails to meet the demands for detailed pixel-level restoration and domain discrimination in low-level characteristics. To address these challenges, we propose Learning Prompt Adapters (LPA), which dynamically generates pixel-wise prompts through a combination of multi-granularity prompt bases and identities. By adaptively integrating these prompts into the Transformer architecture, we effectively improve the model’s performance on fine-grained details in super-resolution tasks, as well as enhancing the model’s adaptability to new tasks and preserving knowledge from previous ones. Through organizing the low-rank prompt bases with specific identities, we set up an effective solution to managing cross-task differences and enhancing prompt richness. Extensive experiments on benchmarks comprising the NYU, RealSR, DIV2K, REDS, and MANGA109 datasets with diverse degradation types demonstrate that LPA significantly outperforms existing continual learning methods. Codes of this paper are available at: https://github. com/dummerchen/LPA.

title: Observer-Based Adaptive Prescribed Performance Control for Vehicular Platoons With Actuator Faults and Mixed Dead Zone–Saturation Constraints
authors: Long Cheng, Xiangyong Chen, Tao Yang, Guanghui Wen, Feng Zhao, Jianlong Qiu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: In this article, a fixed-time prescribed performance control (PPC) scheme is proposed for vehicular platoons with uncertain dynamics and actuator faults, dead zones, and saturation. A novel sigmoid function is employed to smoothly approximate asymmetric actuator saturation and dead zones, and a unified framework is constructed that can simultaneously account for actuator faults. To handle model uncertainties and external disturbances, an observer is designed to estimate disturbances within a prescribed time. The proposed scheme uses adaptive sliding-mode control to ensure that tracking errors converge to prescribed steady-state regions within a fixed time to achieve the fixed-time individual vehicle stability and string stability without considering initial errors between vehicles. Simulation experiments are performed to verify the effectiveness of the proposed scheme.

title: Breaking the Bottleneck of Heterogeneity in Federated Learning Through GBG: Grouping, Block Training, and Global Distillation
authors: Zehu Zhang, Lu Dong, Jiangtao Guo, Junyong Jiang
abstract: Federated learning (FL) collaboratively trains a global model across multiple clients without sharing local data, effectively utilizing data while preserving privacy. However, real-world data are often non-independently and identically distributed ( non-IID) and heterogeneous across clients, making standard FL less suitable for practical deployment. To tackle these challenges, we propose a novel framework named GBG which includes grouping, block training and global distillation. First, it introduces a novel grouping mechanism that clusters heterogeneous clients into different groups. Clients in these groups perform different training tasks. Second, it employs Symmetric Balanced Incomplete Block Design (SBIBD) to construct intra-group blocks and establishes a new training paradigm called block training. Finally, by incorporating mutual learning, the GBG framework enables the effective development of both personalized block-level models and a global model. Moreover, we demonstrate the convergence of block training in combination with existing works. Additional experiments further demonstrate that the GBG framework achieves favorable results in both testing accuracy and training error.

title: Edge ML and IoT-Based Real-Time Detection of Unauthorized Connections in Low Voltage Power Distribution
authors: Arvind Kumar Gupta, Aurobinda Routray, VN Achutha Naikan, Prosenjit Das
abstract: Electricity theft in low-voltage distribution networks not only results in financial losses but also degrades power quality, posing significant risks to sensitive consumer electronics and smart home appliances due to voltage fluctuations. Connecting other appliances before the energy meter or short-circuiting the input and output terminals of an energy meter are two prevalent methods of compromising the reliability of the residential energy supply. To detect such tampering, this study presents a consumer-centric Edge ML solution for real-time theft detection that sends a small test signal through the customer’s power line. By adding the traps, this injected test signal is limited between the electric pole and the energy meter’s output terminal. The detection of power theft is greatly aided by this injected test signal. The consumer’s line current is measured using a current transformer (CT). The test signal is separated from the other line components using a bandpass filter, and its features are extracted using the fast Fourier transform (FFT), standard deviation (STD), and kurtosis. To enable low-latency inference at the consumer edge, the trained model is optimized and deployed on a microcontroller-based edge device. The proposed system demonstrated effective real-time detection capabilities and communicated power-theft alerts to service providers via an Internet of Things (IoT) interface. Both offline and real-time evaluations validate the system’s effectiveness in securing the smart residential energy environment.

title: A Subnanosecond Rising Edge High-Voltage Ultrashort Pulse Generator Based on Diode Opening Switch and Avalanche Transistor Module
authors: Chengxiang Li, Xinquan Huang, Sutao Liao, Shiyu Weng, Yunting Xu, Xin Cao, Shiyu Zhang, Leqiang Deng, Yan Zhou
abstract: High-voltage ultrashort pulses with fast-rising edges have great prospects in the fields of biomedical, food sterilization, military weapons, and so on. It is hard to balance high-voltage and fast-rising edge requirements in the development of ultrashort pulse generators. This article proposed a novel high-voltage ultrashort pulse generator with a subnanosecond rising edge based on the diode opening switch (DOS) and the avalanche transistor module (ATM). By analyzing the output characteristics of DOS through the 1-D Poisson equation, the factors affecting the rising edge and amplitude in the circuit were determined, thereby determining the parameters of the DOS circuit. A pulse rising edge sharpening circuit has been designed based on the characteristics of avalanche transistor collector–emitter fast voltage rise rate conduction. The microstrip transmission line is designed as a sharpening capacitor to increase the voltage rise rate of energy. Furthermore, a PSPICE circuit simulation model was built to study the sharpening effect of ATM and the influence factors. Compared to existing DOS circuits with a rising edge of 3.1 ns, the prototype pulse generator developed in this article generates ATM-based ultrashort pulses with a rising edge of 0.6 ns and an amplitude of 4.2 kV and realizes an increase in output amplitude and a shortening of the rising edge at the same time.

title: Spatiotemporal Dynamic Hypergraph Neural Network for Large-scale Long-term Online Precipitation Forecasting
authors: Zhenghong Wang, Wei Huang, Zhan Lin, Yi Wang, Tianqiang Huang, Fan Zhang, Zhou Huang, Yu Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: As a key component of meteorological disasters, precipitation underpins decision-making in economic and social sectors dependent on weather data. Mitigating its socio-economic impacts requires long-lead-time, high-resolution forecasting, with spatiotemporal feature learning being central to this task. Recently, Spatiotemporal Graph Neural Networks have emerged as proliferating models for capturing meteorological dynamic patterns. However, existing precipitation forecasting methods face two major challenges: (1) graph neural networks rely on pairwise relationships, limiting their ability to capture dynamic high-order spatiotemporal interactions critical to climate evolution; (2) precipitation’s non-stationary, highly fluctuating nature discerns long-term temporal patterns. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Spatiotemporal Dynamic Hypergraph Neural Network (STDHGNN) for large-scale, long-term online precipitation forecasting. It develops an adaptive graph and hypergraph generation method to simulate precipitation aggregation and multi-directional diffusion (driven by atmospheric circulation, urban agglomerations or topographic lifting, etc.), and a dynamic replay mechanism to adapt time and frequency domains for online learning of long-term precipitation’s key frequencies and periodicity. Extensive experiments on 26-year datasets covering 5 nations show STDHGNN outperforms state-of-the-arts for up to 30-day lead times. This work provides new insights for re-evaluating precipitation’s spatiotemporal patterns and innovates high-order interaction integration in deep learning.

title: OFDM-Based Underwater Visible Light Communication
authors: Martin Dratnal, Lukas Danys, Rene Jaros, Lukas Hlavaty, Radek Martinek
abstract: Underwater visible light communication (UVLC) offers a high-speed and low-latency alternative to traditional underwater data transmission methods such as acoustic and radio frequency systems, which face inherent limitations in aquatic environments. This letter presents the design and implementation of an orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM)-based UVLC system using multi-order quadrature amplitude modulation (M-QAM) with a focus on reliable performance in real-world conditions. Experiments were conducted in three water conditions—clear, murky, and dynamically turbid—using a 545 nm LED. The results show that 64-QAM provides stable performance across all conditions, while higher-order schemes, such as 128-QAM and 256-QAM, offer increased data rates but suffer from higher bit error rates under turbidity. The study demonstrates that the proposed system can maintain short-range high-throughput communication in variable underwater environments.

title: CALIPSO-Guided Dual-branch Network with Spectral-Spatial Attention for Himawari-8 Sea Fog Detection
authors: Zhe Zeng, Shuo Zhang, Ling Ke, Hui Sheng, Dan Liu, Shanwei Liu, Zhongheng Wu, Qing Wei
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Sea fog poses a critical challenge to maritime safety. Current sea fog detection methods based on geostationary satellite data predominantly rely on the visual interpretation of samples, yet conventional visual interpretation methods are prone to subjective biases in sample labeling. This study proposes a novel dual-branch Patch-CNN architecture using Himawari-8 satellite data, which addresses labeling uncertainty through CALIPSO-derived vertical aerosol profiles that unambiguously discriminate sea fog from low-level clouds. Training samples were exclusively constructed from CALIPSO vertical feature masks (VFM), ensuring spectral reliability by eliminating cloud-fog ambiguity. The framework integrates three distinctive features: (1) Spatial-context preservation via patch-based inputs to encapsulate neighborhood characteristics around sample points; (2) multispectral feature extraction through a dual-branch architecture that separately captures characteristics from visible-near infrared and infrared channels; and (3) adaptive feature fusion employing attention-weighted feature integration to enhance discriminative representation. Evaluated across the Yellow-Bohai Sea region, the model achieved 89.74% overall accuracy and 82.98% detection probability, outperforming conventional machine learning and deep learning benchmarks. In validation testing with meteorological station data, the proposed method exhibited stronger robustness than traditional machine learning and deep learning methods, with 87 true positives, while case studies using CALIOP VFM and ICOADS visibility data found that the present network could accurately identify the foggy area. This work establishes a satellite-based fog detection network, particularly suited for complex marine environments where fog-cloud spectral overlap challenges traditional methods.

title: Bilateral Sharpness-Aware Minimization for Flatter Minima
authors: Jiaxin Deng, Junbiao Pang, Baochang Zhang, Qingming Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) enhances generalization by minimizing max-sharpness (MaxS). Despite its practical success, we empirically found that the MaxS behind SAM’s generalization enhancements faces the “flatness indicator problem” (FIP), where SAM only considers the flatness in the direction of gradient ascent. This leads to high Hessian eigenvalues for the deep neural network (DNN), indicating insufficient flatness in the solution region. Abetter flatness indicator (FI) would lower these Hessian eigenvalues, resulting in a flatter minimum and improved generalization of the network. Because SAM is inherently a greedy search method. In this article, we propose to utilize the difference between the training loss and the minimum loss over the neighborhood surrounding the current weight, which we denote asmin-sharpness (MinS). By merging MaxS and MinS, we create a better FI that indicates a flatter direction during the optimization. Specifically, we combine this FI with SAM into the proposed bilateral SAM (BSAM), which finds a flatter minimum than SAM. The theoretical analysis demonstrates that BSAM converges to a local minimum. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BSAM offers superior generalization performance and robustness compared to vanilla SAM across various tasks, i.e.,classification, transfer learning, human pose estimation, semantic segmentation, and network quantization.

title: Early Warning of Extreme Weather-Induced N-$k$ Contingencies Leveraging Spatio-Temporal Analysis of Historical Event Sequences
authors: Xinsheng Lyu, Jingyu Wang, Jianhua Pei, Dongyuan Shi, Xianzhong Duan
abstract: Traditional N–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${k}$</tex-math></inline-formula> contingency analysis is insufficient for addressing the growing threat of extreme weather to power system reliability and resilience. Existing methods for contingency construction and screening often fail to adequately consider power outage patterns involving simultaneous failures of multiple components caused by disastrous weather, limiting their applicability to high-impact low-probability events. Meanwhile, reliance on short-term weather forecasts makes these methods difficult to leave enough time for implementing preventive measures. To address these limitations, an early-warning framework leveraging spatio-temporal point process modeling of historical failure event sequences is proposed for extreme weather–induced N–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${k}$</tex-math></inline-formula> contingencies. The core component is a Constrained Diffusion Spatio-Temporal Point Process (CDSTPP) model, which learns failure occurrence patterns under spatial, temporal, meteorological, and network constraints, enabling probabilistic forecasts of future line failures. Based on these forecasts, a contingency generation scheme synthesizes high-risk N–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${k}$</tex-math></inline-formula> scenarios via sampling from predicted failure distributions to support preventive operation and anti-risk planning. Given the limited availability of high-resolution historical failure event data, a data augmentation approach that integrates meteorological reanalysis data with line fragility modeling is also proposed. The proposed framework is evaluated on the large-scale ENTSO-E test system. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in forecasting critical contingencies induced by extreme weather events and supporting proactive and resilient operational decision-making to mitigate blackout risks.

title: Emergency-Aware and Frequency-Constrained HVDC Planning for a Multi-Area Asynchronously Interconnected Grid
authors: Yiliu He, Haiwang Zhong, Grant Ruan, Yan Xu, Chongqing Kang
abstract: High-voltage direct current (HVDC) technology has played a crucial role for long-distance transmission of renewable power generation. However, the integration of large-capacity HVDC lines introduces significant frequency security challenges during HVDC fault emergencies. This paper proposes an emergency-aware and frequency-constrained HVDC planning method to optimize the capacity of inter-area HVDC tie-lines in a multi-area asynchronously interconnected grid. Firstly, a coordinated emergency frequency control scheme is proposed to allocate the emergency control resources during HVDC faults. Then, an enhanced system frequency response model integrating event-driven emergency frequency control is developed and a weighted oblique decision tree approach is employed to extract frequency nadir security constraints. The proposed planning model considers all potential HVDC fault emergencies while treating candidate HVDC capacities as decision variables. Simulation results demonstrate superior performance in balancing economic efficiency with frequency security requirements, providing a practical solution for inter-area HVDC planning.

title: Hybrid Lightweight Soft Error Mitigation Techniques for Edge Devices
authors: Jonas Gava, Ricardo Reis, Luciano Ost
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: The increasing deployment of artificial intelligence (AI) at the edge, particularly convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in resource-constrained devices, has created new challenges for ensuring system reliability and safety. Market analysts project a 21% annual growth rate in the edge AI market size over the next five years. These devices are being used in safety-critical applications such as autonomous vehicles, industrial control systems, and medical devices, where malfunctions due to radiationinduced soft errors can have severe consequences, ranging from degraded performance to life-threatening situations. Soft errors, caused by energetic particles, can corrupt data and instructions, resulting in unpredictable system behaviour. To meet safety standards in these domains, reliability engineers must proactively explore and implement efficient mitigation solutions during the initial design cycle.

title: Decentralized Model-Based Policy Optimization for Cooperative Intersection Management
authors: Habiba Adel, Mohammed Ibrahim Awad, Diaa Emad, Omar M. Shehata
publish: 2026 IEEE Forum for Innovative Sustainable Transportation Systems (FISTS)
abstract: Traffic management at road intersections remains a challenge, even with the evolving technologies that enable autonomous vehicles to communicate and cooperate. The challenge switched to managing large amounts of data from driving agents and their surrounding environment. In this paper, we adapted the Decentralized Model-Based Policy Optimization (DMPO) [1], developed for large-scale network control, to address this challenge. DMPO, with its great scalability guarantees, is designed for large-scale static network topologies, while the traffic management problem has a large scale of autonomous vehicle nodes; each node or agent’s action decision alters the graph’s architecture, making it a dynamic graph. Our implementation fixes this by rebuilding the κ-hop communication graph at each time step based on the nodes’ proximity. We test the method in a SUMO simulation of a 4-way signalized intersection with 2 lanes per approach, 30 cars, and mixed traffic (60% CAV penetration). The results show that there were no collisions during the evaluation episodes, that the average wait time was only 0.26 seconds, and that fuel use decreased by 27.4% and emissions decreased by 24.8% compared to driving in a city with stop-and-go traffic. These findings show that DMPO is applicable beyond static networks to transportation scenarios defined by time-varying agent populations.

title: Exploring Enhancements to 1T1C FeMFET Bitcells with a Versatile DTCO Methodology
authors: Rosario Pronsat, Antoine Cauquil, Pascal Vivet, Jean Coignus, Damien Deleruyelle, Cédric Marchand, Lioua Labrak, Ian O’Connor
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: Non-volatile in-memory computing (iMC) has emerged as an energy-efficient paradigm well suited to AI workloads. Its implementation using 1T1C FeMFETs (Ferroelectric Memory Field Effect Transistors), a best-in-class emerging nonvolatile memory technology that integrates BEOL ferroelectric devices with FEOL transistors, is of particular interest. This interest stems from their potential to enable large-scale multiplyaccumulate (MAC) operations in both digital and analog domains. However, realizing tangible performance benefits requires comprehensive cross-layer exploration of both design and technology parameters, extending up to accelerator level. In this work, we propose a bitcell-level multi-objective optimization methodology to identify and extract optimal sizing solutions that provide tractable trade-offs between key performance indicators (KPI). We further demonstrate how this approach facilitates cross-stack exploration of accelerator architectures. Results are presented as Pareto fronts spanning $2-4 \mathrm{KPIs}$: a $2-\mathrm{KPI}$ problem illustrates the methodology, while a $\mathbf{4}$-KPI problem represents a realistic design scenario. Comparison is made between $\mathbf{1 3 0} \mathbf{n m}$ and 28 nm technologies demonstrating a decrease in the average of write energy and area up to 24 X and 30 X respectively.

title: Towards an Optimization-Driven Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Class Brain Tumor Detection in MRI Imaging
authors: Thomas Kidu, Yared Legese, Yared Abraha, Haben Gebrekidan, Akshay Bhuvaneswari Ramakrishnan, Tesfahun Tesfaye
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: The accurate and timely detection of brain tumors from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans remains a critical challenge in medical diagnostics, directly impacting patient treatment outcomes and survival rates. This paper presents an advanced deep learning framework for multi-class brain tumor detection using a customized You Only Look Once version 12 nano (YOLOv12n) architecture. Our approach leverages comprehensive data augmentation, customized hyperparameter optimization, and adaptive learning rate scheduling configured for brain MRI tumor detection, combined with detailed per-class evaluation and training-dynamics analysis. The proposed system detects tumor regions in brain MRI scans across four categories: Glioma, Meningioma, Pituitary Tumor, and No Tumor. Trained on a dataset of 9,973 annotated images, our model achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP @ 0.5) of 89.2%, with individual class performance ranging from 76.3% to 97.3%. Our optimized training configuration, incorporating the AdamW optimizer, cosine learning rate scheduling, and medical imaging-specific augmentation techniques, contributes to robust model generalization. The results highlight the potential of the proposed YOLOv12n-based framework as a practical and efficient tool for automated brain tumor detection in clinical diagnostic workflows.

title: Human–Machine Interface in Orange Technology: A Care-Oriented Framework for Trust in International Medical Tourism
authors: Dina Sekar Vusparatih, Handy Martinus, Hidayat Ahmad Ridzuan, Eshaby Mustafa
abstract: International medical tourism requires more than clinical excellence; it depends on trust fostered through technology-mediated communication. This study examines how Orange Technology, a care-oriented framework that integrates Human Machine Interface (HMI) with Human Computer Interaction (HCI) theory‚ promotes trust in international healthcare delivery. Focusing on Island Hospital in Penang, Malaysia, which serves predominantly Indonesian patients (representing 60% of international cases), we employed a qualitative methodology comprising systematic website observation, semi-structured interviews with 15 patients, 8 family members, and 3 Indonesian consulate officers, conducted between June – July 2024. Thematic analysis revealed five key trust-building mechanisms: (1) multilingual digital interfaces reducing language barriers, (2) transparent treatment and accommodation cost displays, (3) personalized pre-arrival online consultations, (4) culturally sensitive service desk interactions, and (5) continuous digital-physical care integration. These HMI applications operationalize Orange Technology principles by embedding empathy, cultural sensitivity, and humanistic values into both digital systems and human interactions. The study demonstrates that when properly designed, HMI extends beyond information delivery to create emotional connections, reduce patient anxiety, and foster sustainable trust in cross-border medical care. This research contributes to understanding how digital health technologies can advance patient well-being while maintaining human-centered care values.

title: Benchmarking electrical, thermal, and mechanical performance of various industrial REBCO coated conductors for magnet applications
authors: H. Himanshu, J. B. Song, O. Jay, X. Chaud
abstract: Rare-earth barium copper oxide (REBCO) superconducting tapes have experienced a rapid rise in demand and production, driven largely by high-field magnet and fusion applications. With the ongoing push for higher critical current density and long-length deposition, material used and fabrication processes continue to evolve. This makes regular independent evaluation and comparison of commercial products essential. In this work, we benchmarked tapes from four of the major manufacturers, assessing their electrical (at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{77}~\text{K}$</tex-math></inline-formula>), thermal, and mechanical performance (at room temperature). In addition, two batches of THEVA conductors from different production years were compared, highlighting improvements in their overall suitability for high-field magnet applications. The results provide a statistically reliable basis for magnet coil design, improvements in joint fabrication, and the establishment of safer operation margins.

title: Mastering Software Technology: The Journey Ahead
authors: Christof Ebert
abstract: From programming to cloud to AI, software technology keeps evolving. Impact comes when the leading practitioners drive beyond tools. Building cross-domain skills, fostering critical thinking, and steering teams master tomorrow’s tech. Lead the journey ahead.

title: Cascade Computation of Second Order Information for Efficient Optimization of Multi-Argument Objective Functionals
authors: Marcus Carlsson, Jorge Bacca, Herwig Wendt, Viktor Nikitin
publish: 2025 IEEE 10th International Workshop on Computational Advances in Multi-Sensor Adaptive Processing (CAMSAP)
abstract: We present a general technique for efficient computation of second order information for optimization functionals involving many variables of different nature, frequently appearing in modern applications. This technique relies on the bilinear Hessian, and while the main tools for this task have been published elsewhere, we here focus on the problem of how to organize the computations in the multi-argument situation, relying on so called cascade optimization and the chain rule for the bilinear Hessian. For concreteness, we demonstrate our approach by focusing on three concrete problems; phase retrieval, multidistance nano-holotomography and training of neural networks. In the experimental section we present results where the proposed technique significantly speeds up first order methods, and in particular we obtain faster training results than the Adamalgorithm when training the so called SIREN neural network.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>Supported by Swedish Research Council (grant no. 2022-04917 and 202305341)

title: Machine Learning for Intelligent Data Integration in Cloud-Native Architectures: Techniques and Applications for the Retail Domain
authors: Udaya Veeramreddygari, Chirag Agrawal
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The deluge of retail data triggered by social media, e-commerce, and point-of-sale terminals is at the same time a threat and an opportunity. Bringing all this fragmented information together to form one unified picture of the customer lies at the heart of empowering personalization, supply chain optimization, and informed decision-making. In this discussion, the spotlight is thrown on how machine learning (ML) is applied to make data fusion intelligent for cloud-native retail applications. We propose an architecture that applies microservices to ML to perform entity resolution, schema matching, and data quality improvement. A test database, RetailGen, is used in the paper to simulate the complexity of real-world retail data, i.e., customer information, product catalogs, and transactions. The program is in Python using the TensorFlow and Scikit-learn libraries, and the structure is based on a large cloud-based system. Our findings validate the success of the process, as suggested by ML, in significantly enhancing data integration levels and accuracy percentages compared to traditional ETL processes. The results, via performance histograms and latency waterfall plots, show an incredible improvement in data quality with shorter integration times, enabling more agile, data-driven retail firms.

title: The Influence of Big Five Personality Traits on Compulsive Buying Behavior: The Mediating Role of Hedonistic Shopping Motivation Among Indonesian Fashion Consumers
authors: Darren Pangestu, Ardika Suwandi, Melva Hermayanty Saragih
abstract: Compulsive buying behavior, characterized by irresistible urges to purchase unnecessary items, often leads to financial and psychological distress. Although personality traits are established predictors of consumer behavior, the mechanisms linking them to compulsive buying remain underexplored, particularly the role of hedonistic shopping motivation. This study examines how the Big Five personality traits influence compulsive buying behavior through hedonistic shopping motivation among Indonesian fashion consumers. Using a quantitative cross-sectional design, data were collected from 310 consumers aged 13–44 years and analyzed using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM). Validated instruments were employed, including the Big Five Inventory (BFI-S), Hedonistic Shopping Experience Scale (HSE), and Revised Compulsive Buying Scale (RCBS), all demonstrating strong reliability. The model explained 82.7% of the variance in compulsive buying behavior and 89.4% in hedonistic shopping motivation. Hedonistic shopping fully mediated the relationships between all Big Five traits and compulsive buying), with neuroticism showing the strongest effect. These findings highlight pleasure-driven shopping as a key pathway shaping compulsive buying and emphasize the need for interventions promoting responsible consumption and wellbeing, aligning with SDG 12 and SDG 3.

title: Research on DeepEEG Model for Classifying EEG Relaxation States Based on CNN-BiLSTM and Multi-Head Attention
authors: Chang Yu, Jiamin Liu, Xinrong Zhu
abstract: This study proposes a novel hybrid deep learning model, DeepEEG, which effectively addresses the challenge of relaxation state recognition posed by EEG signal complexity and individual variability through the integration of CNN, BiLSTM, and multi-head self-attention mechanisms. The model utilizes CNN to extract spatial features, BiLSTM to capture temporal dynamics, and the multi-head self-attention mechanism to focus on key time frames. Experimental results on the DEAP dataset demonstrate a classification accuracy of 95.8%, significantly outperforming baseline models such as SVM, CNN, and BiLSTM. Ablation experiments validate the effectiveness of each component, particularly the multi-head attention mechanism. This model provides a robust solution for EEG-based psychological state decoding and holds application potential in mental health monitoring.

title: CG-UIE: A Category-Guided Universal Information Extraction Framework for Event Extraction
authors: Runze Liu, Feng Huang, Guangjie Liu, Dehao Tao, Bingkun Wang, Yongfeng Huang
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Universal Information Extraction (UIE) is a fundamental technology for event extraction. However, conventional UIE-based methods exhibit several limitations such as insufficient negative sampling strategy and schema knowledge utilization. In this paper, we propose a novel Category-Guided UIE method (CG-UIE), which introduces an event-type-aware negative sampling method (ETNS) and a category-guided attention fusion method (CGFusion). In detail, ETNS firstly generates highquality negative samples aligned with the distribution of positive ones, and then CGFusion further enhances the interaction between schema prompts and text features. Those two mechanisms serve as a plug-and-play method that activate UIE’s pre-trained knowledge more effectively, as well as improve its representation learning capability. Experiments on two different datasets show significant improvements of $\mathbf{F 1}$ score in argument classification tasks, even with less training data. Codes and data are available at https://github.com/transmoker/CG-UIE.git.

title: Effect of Process Parameters on the Performance of Deep-sea Fiber-optic Hydrophone
authors: Jiaze Zhao, Zhou Meng, Mo Chen, Rui Liang, Haikun Zeng, Yatao Li, Jingjing Feng, Jianfei Wang
abstract: Process parameters affect the performance of fiber-optic hydrophones (FOHs), like sensitivity and hydrostatic pressure resistance. High sensitivity and high hydrostatic pressure resistance are important for deep-sea engineering applications. However, optimizing one typically comes at the expense of the other. We used orthogonal experiments to investigate the effects of several key parameters—including the oxide layer thickness of the elastic tube, the tension pulled on the optical fiber, the adhesive curing temperature, and the cured layer thickness—on both hydrostatic pressure resistance and acoustic pressure sensitivity. The range analysis showed that the oxide layer thickness of the elastic tube has the most significant effect on the hydrostatic pressure resistance, while the fiber tension is the dominant process parameter influencing the sensitivity. The optimal process parameter set for overall performance was determined through a comprehensive scoring method. After optimization, the hydrostatic pressure resistance remained above the experimental average, while the acoustic pressure sensitivity improved from -141.57 dB (re 1 rad/μPa) to -139.70 dB (re 1 rad/μPa). This study provides practical guidance for optimizing the fabrication process of high-sensitivity deep-sea FOHs. It is helpful for fabricating robust, reliable sensors for deep-sea environment.

title: Frequency Selective Absorber-Enhanced Flexible CP MIMO Antenna for mmWave Wearable IoT
authors: Asif Khan, Qingfeng Zhang, Muhammad Zada, Zhonghe Zhang, Yejun He, Qingsha S. Cheng
abstract: Wearable Internet of Things (IoT) devices, used in healthcare, fitness, and industrial safety, rely on compact, streamlined antenna systems to achieve high data rates, reliable connectivity, and seamless multi-device operation. This paper introduces a flexible pulse-nexus MIMO antenna (PN-MIMO) design that achieves wideband CP based on a frequency-selective fractal gate absorber (FGA) for mmWave wearable systems. The single antenna is built on a Rogers RT/Duroid 5880 substrate and features compact dimensions of 0.48λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub>×0.16λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub>×0.02λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub> at 24 GHz. The antenna is further configured into a quad-element MIMO system with a minimal port-to-port spacing of 0.048λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub>. A novel concept is introduced whereby distinct resonance frequencies, one generated by the PN-MIMO antenna (23.4–24.2 GHz) and the other by FGA (24–26 GHz), are combined to achieve a wide impedance bandwidth (IBW) of 3.5 GHz (22.2–25.7 GHz) through constructive interference. The integration also results in low mutual coupling (≤ 25 dB), high gain (≥ 7.5 dBi), and a wider 3-dB axial ratio bandwidth (ARBW) of 2.5 GHz. The on-body performance and bending behavior of the antenna are evaluated on the chest, hand, and leg of a human model, demonstrating stable operation under structural deformation and body loading. Later, the simulation results are validated through measurements, showing good agreement. The proposed PN-FGA-MIMO antenna exhibits excellent radiation characteristics while maintaining SAR levels significantly below the US (1.6 W/kg) and EU (2 W/kg) safety limits, indicating its strong suitability for mmWave wearable applications.

title: Hybridised Stator Characterisation Technique to Accurately Estimate the Peak Performance of Automotive Electric Machines
authors: Gopinath Sengupta, R. M. Ram Kumar, Gaurang Vakil, Karuna Kalita, Jenni Pippuri-Mäkeläinen, Muhammad Raza Khowja, Kuldeep Singh, Chris Gerada
abstract: The rapid expansion of the production of electric vehicles (EV) has increased the demand for high-performance electric motors. The B-H and iron loss of the stator core significantly influence the resulting torque and efficiency of the electric machine. Manufacturing is a major factor that affect the B-H and iron losses of the stator core. Therefore, precise magnetic core characterisation is essential for ensuring reliability and efficiency. End-of-line (EoL) testing a critical validation step, assessing product integrity, defect identification, and regulatory compliance. However, accurate characterisation of the magnetic properties of complex geometries, such as stator cores, is not feasible using standardised measurement methods, which are typically designed for simpler shapes such as toroidal samples. In this paper a hybrid magnetic characterisation method has been developed in which standard experimental data is combined with finite element analysis to improve the estimation of the magnetic characteristics of a stator core material. By compensating for the limitations of conventional stator testing, the proposed method achieves a substantial improvement in material characterisation accuracy by around 69% for the hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) machine and 75% for the electric vehicle (EV) machine. When these refined material properties are employed in finite element motor models, the resulting torque prediction error is reduced by 94.43% in the HEV motor and by 98.39% in the EV motor, relative to predictions based on conventional stator sample testing. The improved agreement between predicted and expected performance demonstrates the relevance of the proposed approach for reliable motor design, particularly in the context of high-volume automotive manufacturing where consistency and repeatability are critical.

title: An Input-Driving-Relaxed Noise-Shaping SAR ADC with Amplifier-Reused In-Loop Buffering and NTF Leakage Reshaping
authors: Tian Xie, Ken Li, Tzu-Han Wang, Wei-En Lee, Dongsuk Kang, Shaolan Li
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Solid-State Circuits Society
abstract: This paper presents a third-order noise-shaping successive approximation register (NS SAR) analog-to-digital converter (ADC) with embedded input buffering capability and a PVT robust error-feedback (EF)-based filter. An Amplifier-Reused In-Loop Buffering (AILB) architecture is proposed to serve multiple purposes: first, it reuses the residue amplifier for input driving, obviating the need for an explicit buffer and its related noise penalty. Second, it inherently suppresses the sampling kT/C noise without explicit noise-cancelling operations. Lastly, it facilitates a simpler bias design compared to using explicit buffers. To enable a PVT-robust EF noise filter, a noise transfer function (NTF) leakage reshaping (NLRS) technique is proposed, allowing the NTF leakage component to be shaped to the first order. Prototyped in 65 nm CMOS technology, this design achieves 82.0 dB signal-to-noise-distortion ratio (SNDR) with 500 kHz bandwidth (BW) under an oversampling ratio (OSR) of 8. The prototype’s total power consumption is 126.8 μW (including the input driving buffer), achieving a Schreier figure-of-merit (FoM) of 178.0 dB.

title: Robust Federated Learning With Double Denoising
authors: Xinglong Wei, Siguang Chen, Xue Li, Songle Chen
abstract: Federated learning (FL), as a representative distributed learning paradigm, has achieved remarkable success. However, most existing FL studies assume that each client holds correctly labeled data, whereas in reality, noisy labels are ubiquitous on the client side. To mitigate the adverse impact of noisy labels on FL performance, we propose a robust FL method with double denoising. Specifically, we first perform primary denoising based on the idea of cross-prediction, where two global models are trained and mutually used on each client to identify and filter out mislabeled samples. Next, after fine-tuning the models, we develop secondary denoising by detecting and removing residual noisy samples through clustering based on loss values. In addition, we design a noise-tolerant local training strategy that dynamically assesses the influence of noisy data on local updates and applies differentiated update rules to prevent overfitting. Finally, experimental results on three benchmark datasets, including the real-world noisy dataset Clothing1M, demonstrate that our method effectively removes label noise, delivering improved performance of the global model.

title: Radar Cross Section Gradient Calculation of Coated Aircraft Based on the Adjoint Method and Improved Self-Dual Integral Equations
authors: Wei-Feng Sun, Zheng-Hong Gao, Ke Zhao, Yi-Ran Gu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This study presents an efficient method for calculating the gradient of the radar cross section (RCS) of coated aircraft. The method is based on adjoint theory and an improved formulation of self-dual integral equations (SDIE), incorporating the multilevel fast multipole algorithm (MLFMA) and impedance boundary conditions (IBC). To address the computational limitations of conventional SDIE methods, such as inaccuracies from extraneous integral terms at coated interfaces and ill-conditioning with nonuniform IBC, we propose an improved SDIE formulation that incorporates MLFMA and IBC. The numerical properties of the adjoint and control equations derived from the improved SDIE are analyzed, and a novel right preconditioning technique is introduced to improve solver convergence and computational efficiency, thus significantly accelerating gradient computation. Numerical experiments conducted on a flying wing model validate the accuracy and efficiency of the proposed method for RCS gradient calculation. The results confirm that the method is both efficient and reliable for computing RCS gradients of coated aircraft.

title: A Self-Decoupled Microstrip Antenna via Inhomogeneous Substrate Manipulation
authors: Yongqi Hu, Jiangwei Sui, Xiangwei Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: In this paper, we introduce a new self‑decoupling technique that leverages an inhomogeneous dielectric substrate to significantly suppress the mutual coupling between closely spaced microstrip antennas. Unlike traditional decoupling strategies that rely on modifications of the patch or ground plane, the proposed method achieves self‑decoupling by exploring the inhomogeneous substrate manipulation, where the spatial distribution of the substrate’s permittivities is deliberately arranged, thereby enabling the mutual cancellation of several distinct coupling paths. The underlying mechanism is revealed through analytical derivation, providing a quantitative guideline for desired designs. To validate the concept, two implementation schemes of the inhomogeneous substrate are investigated. The first scheme (Type I) employs a multi‑column dielectric combination, while the second scheme (Type II) utilizes a monolithic substrate perforated with carefully designed patterns. Several examples for the 1 × 2, 1 × 4, and millimeter-wave scenarios are studied, respectively. The results confirm the superior performance of the proposed approach. To the best of our knowledge, this work constitutes the first demonstration of self‑decoupling achieved through substrate permittivity manipulation, and it showcases the potential of additive manufacturing techniques in the self-decoupling of microstrip antennas.

title: Approximate Inverse Model Explanations as a Model-Agnostic Framework for Linking Musical Scores and Performance
authors: Ayako Minematsu, Takafumi Nakanishi
abstract: Understanding how written musical structures relate to expressive timing decisions is central to computational performance analysis; however, existing explanation methods often rely on predictive models, surrogate learning, or input perturbation. This study proposes approximate inverse model explanations (AIME) as a model-agnostic and data-efficient framework for analyzing expressive piano performance. Unlike perturbation-based approaches such as Shapley additive explanations and local interpretable model-agnostic explanations, AIME derives explanatory contributions directly from observed data by applying a Moore–Penrose inverse to matrices of score-derived features (X) and expressive events (Y) without requiring model training or sampling. Using the Vienna 4×22 corpus, with additional validation on the heterogeneous ASAP dataset, we project score landmarks onto a beat-synchronous grid and extract sparse expressive change points (knees) from MIDI performances. AIME computes the importance of global features (GFIcorpus) and the importance of local features (LFI) within a unified algebraic framework. Experimental results show that boundary-related features, particularly slurs and barlines, consistently dominate structural importance, whereas effect-size analysis indicates a suppression of expressive events near phrase boundaries. Although symbolic structural features alone do not strongly separate individual performers, robustness diagnostics confirm stable and reproducible explanations across datasets. These findings demonstrate that AIME provides transparent, structural interpretations of expressive timing and generalizes to small, heterogeneous performance corpora, establishing a practical explanation framework for symbolic music analysis without reliance on predictive modeling.

title: A Joint Graph-Cut Channel Estimation Method for Multi-user Holographic MIMO
authors: Jiaxin Zhang, Wenqian Shen, Kai Yang, Zhen Gao, Jianping An
abstract: To address the challenges of high-dimensional channel estimation and underutilized spatial correlations among users in holographic MIMO (HMIMO) systems, this paper proposes a joint graph-cut algorithm for multi-user channel estimation in the wavenumber domain. The size of the conventional angular domain channel matrix increases with the number of antennas in densely-spaced HMIMO. Therefore, user channels are projected into the wavenumber domain via a Fourier harmonic transform, revealing their inherent clustered sparsity and exploiting common scatterer clusters among users. Subsequently, a joint graph-cut channel estimation (JGC-CE) algorithm based on multi-user common supports is designed. In each iteration, the algorithm first partitions user clusters to extract shared supports. Then for each user, it performs users' individual graph update and channel estimation to reconstruct the channel matrix. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms independent estimation schemes for individual users in accuracy while reducing pilot length.

title: An Integrated 0.3–145-GHz Dual-Directional Coupler and Its Packaging and Application in VNA Systems
authors: Tingting Sun, Yubin Yang, Keqing Wang, Zixin Liu, Ming Guo, Guanghua Shi, Zhen Wang, Wenhui Liu, Qinglong Zhang, Jianbo Zhao, Jianchao Wang, Qian Yang, Anxue Zhang, Fushun Nian, Cheng Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents an integrated dual-directional coupler chip operating from 300 MHz to 145 GHz, together with a dedicated packaging solution for seamless connection to 1.0- and 0.8-mm coaxial connectors, enabling direct application in next-generation ultrawideband vector network analyzers. The coupler, fabricated using micro metal additive manufacturing (M-MAM), allows high-order impedance-tapered design and a swiss-roll shaped folded topology for dense integration without significantly enlarging the chip area. To guarantee broadband impedance matching, the design combines a tailored multilayer process, time-domain optimization of reflection points, and specially engineered interconnection structures between the chip and coaxial connectors. In addition, the chip itself can also be configured with conventional ground-signal-ground (GSG) interfaces, enabling direct integration with MMIC chips; in terms of performance, with a 1.0-mm connector, the device achieves 120-GHz bandwidth, flat coupling of<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$11.5~\pm ~0.37$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB, directivity >17 dB, and insertion loss below 3 dB. With a 0.8mm connector, the working frequency extends to 145 GHz, achieving >14-dB directivity and <3.7-dB insertion loss. In addition, both couplers can operate down to 10MHz, though the coupling will decrease at lower frequencies. To the best of our knowledge, this represents the widest bandwidth ever reported for a packaged dual-directional coupler, especially notable for its high directivity across nearly three decades of frequency range.

title: Building Resilient ISAC in Maritime IoT: Dynamic Attack Detection and Energy-Efficient Scheduling Under DoS Jamming
authors: Yao Li, Sinan Li, Xinping Guan
abstract: Modern integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) technology enables the tight integration of sensing and transmission within the framework of maritime Internet of Things (IoT) to achieve enhanced performance. However, in actual maritime industrial environments, such maritime IoT systems are often susceptible to various sources of interference, particularly anthropogenic jamming, making the application of antijamming ISAC technology in maritime IoT an imperative challenge. To address this issue, this article proposes a novel algorithm integrating dynamic attack detection for solving transmission scheduling optimization problems under Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks. First, a novel learning-based adaptive Kalman filter (LAKF) algorithm is introduced. Even in time-varying noise environments, the algorithm can still dynamically adjust the covariance matrix of observation noise through reinforcement learning to match the actual noise characteristics. Second, an intelligent detection algorithm is designed to counter time-varying attack patterns, which leverages a temporal window approach incorporating long short-term memory networks and an attention mechanism. Finally, we propose an energy-aware LAKF-attack pattern detection-twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient tracking (APD-TD3T) algorithm, which integrates the LAKF and APD algorithms and uses the improved TD3 algorithm (an improvement of the DDPG algorithm) as the core algorithm. Specially, it also incorporates a delayed reward design, which effectively addresses the anti-DoS transmission scheduling optimization problem in continuous action space. Simulation experiment and comparison results demonstrate that the proposed LAKF-APD-TD3T algorithm achieves superior performance, significantly decreasing both tracking error and energy consumption in target tracking scenarios.

title: Anonymous Messaging Made More Flexible with PingPong
authors: Peipei Jiang, Yihao Wu, Lei Xu, Wentao Dong, Peiyuan Chen, Yulong Ming, Cong Wang, Xiaohua Jia, Qian Wang
abstract: For those seeking end-to-end private communication free from pervasive metadata tracking and censorship, the Tor network has been the de-facto choice in practice, despite its susceptibility to traffic analysis attacks. Recently, numerous metadata-private messaging proposals have emerged with the aim to surpass Tor in the messaging context by obscuring the relationships between any two messaging buddies, even against global and active attackers. However, most of these systems face an undesirable usability constraint: they require a metadata-private “dialing” phase to establish mutual agreement and timing or round coordination before initiating any regular chats among users. This phase is not only resource-intensive but also inflexible, limiting users’ ability to manage multiple concurrent conversations seamlessly. For stringent privacy requirement, the often-enforced traffic uniformity further exacerbated the limitations of this roadblock. In this paper, we introduce PingPong, a new end-to-end system for metadata-private messaging designed to overcome these limitations. Under the same traffic uniformity requirement, PingPong replaces the rigid “dial-before-converse” paradigm with a more flexible “notify-before-retrieval” workflow. This workflow incorporates a metadata-private notification subsystem, PING, and a metadata-private message store, PONG. Both PING and PONG leverage hardware-assisted secure enclaves for performance and operates through a series of customized oblivious algorithms, while meeting the uniformity requirements for metadata protection. By allowing users to switch between conversations on demand, PingPong achieves a level of usability akin to modern instant messaging systems, while also offering improved performance and bandwidth utilization for goodput We have built a prototype of PingPong with 32 8-core servers equipped with enclaves and conducted a case study on a real-world messaging metadata dataset to validate our claims.
