title: A Federated Multi-Task, Multi-View Metatransformer with Causal-Acoustic Attention for Infant Cry Classification
authors: Geofrey Owino, Bernard Shibwabo Kasamani, Edem Wornyo
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Interpreting neonatal distress cries requires precision, robustness, and privacy. We present FACT (Federated Acoustic Causal-attention Transformer), a federated multi-task, multi-view transformer that jointly denoises and classifies infant cries in a strictly causal model. FACT fuses mel spectrograms and mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCCs) with cross-view attention and uses first-order meta learning for fast personalization. On frame-level segments curated from Baby Chillanto, Donate-a-Cry, and primary recordings from a pediatric clinic, FACT attains 95.3% accuracy, 0.943 macro-F1, 0.987 macro- AUC, and mean signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) gain +5.7 dB. Gains over a strong transformer baseline are <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{+ 2. 4 {\%}}$</tex> accuracy, +4.8 macro-F1, and +1.4 dB SNR. Communication per round is 4.1 MB with 8-bit quantization and sparsification.

title: Deepfake Video Detection Using Swin Transformer and LSTM: A Hybrid Approach for Facial Feature Analysis
authors: Jameer Kotwal, Rahul Jawaharlal Pandit, Tanishka Sanjay Dhole, Priyanka Chetwani, Bahar Aslam Inamdar, Sarang Dilip Pawar
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Deepfake technology raises major challenges in digital authenticity, privacy, and trust in society. Although detection techniques based on deep learning, like CNNs, are common, they tend to be less generalizable across datasets and are sometimes incapable of identifying temporal inconsistencies. This indicates the necessity for stronger and more flexible solutions. In return, this paper investigates a hybrid deepfake detection mechanism leveraging Swin Transformer for feature learning and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) for classification. The hierarchical self-attention mechanism of the Swin Transformer well learns multi-scale spatial features, while LSTM can model temporal dependencies to identify inconsistencies in deepfake videos. By using the solution, i.e., Swin-LSTM, generalizability, detection accuracy, and robustness against sophisticated deepfake manipulation techniques are enhanced. Furthermore, the paper considers effective preprocessing methods, including face detection and video frame grabbing, to gain the best out of feature representation. Our experiments demonstrate that combining transformer-based feature extraction with sequential learning highly improves deepfake classification accuracy to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 5. 7 1 \%}$</tex> in testing.

title: Augmented Reality and Mixed Reality in the Factory of the Future: A Bibliometric Analysis
authors: Redney Monteiro, Ana Mara Moreira, André Moreira, Jorge Laranjeira, José Barbosa, Pedro Filipe Oliveira, Carlos R. Cunha, Paulo Leitão
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: Mixed Reality (MR) and Augmented Reality (AR) – technologies which are part of the spectrum of Extended Reality (XR) – are considered emerging technologies and have gained increasing attention across multiple sectors, including Tourism and Healthcare, due to their versatility as tools for training, simulation, and education, as well as their potential to serve as innovative Human-Machine Interfaces (HMI). Simultaneously, other emerging technologies such as Internet of Things (IoT), Artificial Intelligence (AI), and Robotics have become central themes in R&D associated with the Fourth Industrial Revolution, commonly referred to as Industry 4.0, which emphasizes the digital transformation of industrial processes, through the integration of the new technologies. Beyond their relevance to Industry 4.0, XR technologies are also potential technologies in the emerging paradigm of Industry 5.0, which shifts the focus toward human-centric solutions and collaboration between humans and machines, this potential being based on the ability to enhance human interaction with complex systems. This paper presents the results of a bibliometric analysis of 634 peer-reviewed journal articles indexed in Web of Science and Scopus, spanning research from the past decade (2015 – 2025), as contribute to support future research and guide relevant stakeholders by identifying prevailing trends, leading contributors, and key publication sources in the domain of XR applied to industrial contexts.

title: Surface Type Classification Using AIEM-Simulated Radar Scattering and Supervised Machine Learning: A Physics-Aware Approach
authors: Yahya Ali, Marai M. Abousetta, Abdussalam M. Ammar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Accurate classification of surface types using radar remote sensing is essential for applications such as environmental monitoring, terrain interpretation, and defense operations. The Advanced Integral Equation Model (AIEM) has long been used as a deterministic forward scattering model to simulate radar backscatter from rough surfaces. However, its inability to perform inverse inference limits its use in practical classification tasks. In this work, we propose a hybrid physics-informed learning framework that uses AIEM to generate synthetic radar backscatter data from varying surface conditions, which are then used to train supervised machine learning models for surface classification. We generated a labeled dataset by systematically varying surface parameters, including dielectric constant, roughness, and incidence angle, and computed σ<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sup> backscatter coefficients using AIEM for both VV and HH polarizations. Two classifiers—a Support Vector Machine with Error-Correcting Output Codes (SVM-ECOC) and a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)—were trained to map radar features to surface types. Simulation-based experiments show that the MLP achieves 96.7% classification accuracy, while the SVM-ECOC achieves 93.8%, with significantly faster training. These results demonstrate the power of integrating physical models with data-driven learning to enhance surface classification performance in radar systems.

title: REE-ML: A Renewable-Aware and Energy-Efficient Machine Learning Framework for Cloud Environments
authors: Syed Zarak Shah, Mansoor Iqbal, Balaj Khalid, Sarmad Saeed, Syed Mafooq Ul Hassan, Shahid Sultan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The growing energy demands of large-scale machine learning (ML) models in cloud environments raise pressing concerns about sustainability and carbon emissions. Conventional compression methods, such as pruning and quantization-aware training (QAT), improve computational efficiency but rarely align with renewable-aware scheduling, while green cloud strategies lack ML-specific integration. This paper proposes the Renewable-Aware Energy-Efficient Machine Learning (REE-ML) framework, which unifies pruning, QAT, adaptive hyperparameter tuning, and renewable-aware workload scheduling on Amazon Web Services (AWS). The framework employs Auto Scaling, Spot Instances, Sustainability APIs, CodeCarbon, and CloudWatch for real-time energy tracking and carbon estimation. Using the SST-2 dataset with a fine-tuned BERT model, REE-ML achieves a 37.8% reduction in energy use, a 39.3% cut in carbon emissions, and 85% alignment with renewable energy availability, while limiting accuracy and F1-score degradation to only 0.3%. These results establish REE-ML as an effective and scalable framework for sustainable AI deployment in cloud environments.

title: Feedback-Driven AI Workload Emulation for Big Data Cloud Systems
authors: Tahmina Akter Anondi
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This paper introduces a novel methodology for emulating complex computational workloads in Big Data Cloud environments. We present a framework for constructing synthetic AI-driven application models that accurately mirror the performance characteristics of real-world Big Data processes. A core element is the integrated feedback mechanism, allowing iterative refinement of these models to explore system behaviors, identify optimization opportunities, and validate resilience strategies. By enabling precise, controlled experimentation and a continuous feedback loop for system design, this approach significantly simplifies the development and testing of robust and efficient Big Data Cloud AI infrastructures.

title: Cultivating Security: Debug Authentication for Ensuring the Security of SoC’s Root of Trust
authors: Arash Vafaei, Sujan Kumar Saha, Mark Tehranipoor, Farimah Farahmandi
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: Hardware-assisted debugging provides the necessary infrastructure for developers to closely monitor program behaviors at the microarchitectural level in a system-on-chip (SoC). However, debug infrastructure jeopardizes the security of the system by providing a backdoor for accessing crucial assets embedded in the system because of the inevitable increase in observability. While trusted execution environments (TEE) provide an extra level of security and isolate design assets, the security implication of hardware debug integration on TEEs has not been investigated. In this paper, we introduce a multi-level bidirectional access authentication mechanism over the debug module that defines the minimum number of privilege levels needed and the access details at each level so that debug users are authorized and blocked from accessing assets private to other entities. Trust is established by exchanging certificates both from the debugger and SoC sides to implement a bidirectional authorization platform in order to restrict the debugger’s access to SoC assets as well as prevent the debugger’s test data from being accessed by an SoC impersonator through emulation. We provide a prototype of the debug authentication platform on RISC-V architecture that proves the small overhead of the approach while staying compatible with traditional debug efforts.

title: C-EQ-ALINEA: Distributed, Coordinated, and Equitable Ramp Metering Strategy for Sustainable Freeway Operations
authors: Kevin Riehl, Omar Alami Badissi, Anastasios Kouvelas, Michail A. Makridis
publish: 2026 IEEE Forum for Innovative Sustainable Transportation Systems (FISTS)
abstract: Ramp metering is a widely deployed traffic management strategy that improves freeway efficiency without costly infrastructure expansion. However, conventional ramp metering controllers often prioritise aggregate performance at the expense of fairness, leading to highly uneven waiting times across onramps. Such disparities can reduce user compliance, trigger public opposition, and ultimately undermine the long-term sustainability of traffic control systems. This paper proposes C-EQ-ALINEA, a distributed and equity-aware extension of the classical ALINEA ramp metering controller. The approach introduces lightweight information exchange among neighbouring ramps, enabling decentralised coordination that balances congestion impacts without centralised optimisation, detailed traffic models, or additional infrastructure. By preserving the simplicity and robustness of local feedback control, C-EQ-ALINEA offers a directly applicable upgrade path for existing ramp metering installations. The proposed controller is evaluated using calibrated microsimulation of the Amsterdam A10 ring road under highly congested conditions. Results show that C-EQ-ALINEA achieves substantial reductions in total delay and improvements in average speed while maintaining throughput comparable to established coordinated methods. Importantly, the approach delivers a more equitable distribution of delays across ramps under multiple fairness notions, including Harsanyian, Egalitarian, Rawlsian, and Aristotelian perspectives. By improving fairness without sacrificing efficiency or deployability, C-EQ-ALINEA contributes to the sustainable and socially acceptable operation of intelligent freeway traffic management systems.

title: Fairness Evaluation of Client Selection Methods in Federated Learning: A Comparative Study
authors: Naghmeh Khajehali, Jun Yan, Yang-Wai Chow
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a significant paradigm for decentralized model training in Internet of Things applications. In FL, client selection (CS) plays a pivotal role. It can directly influence overall model performance, including convergence speed, communication efficiency, and accuracy. Despite the introduction of many CS methods, there is limited research on the fairness of these CS methods. Securing fairness ensures that certain clients are not consistently underrepresented, and there are fair chances for clients to contribute. To address this gap, this paper presents a rigorous comparative analysis of four widely adopted CS methods (Random, Pow-d, TS, and UCB-GS) and a hybrid method (Pow-MUCB). This work highlights fairness and quantifies it through established mathematical metrics, including Jain’s fairness index (JFI), normalized Shannon entropy (Entropy), and Gini coefficient (Gini). Based on the experimental results, Random is the best in terms of fairness at the cost of its overall performance. Among the compared methods, Pow-MUCB strikes a good balance between fairness and model performance. These findings demonstrate that fairness-aware design in CS is crucial to building an efficient FL system.

title: Broadening Participation in Engineering: A Workshop on Developing Context-Relevant Printed Circuit Board Badges
authors: Vincent Utama, Vincent Jonathan, Erwani Merry Sartika, Archangela Sheilla Haryanto Sundjaya, Oscar Karnalim
abstract: This paper introduces a workshop designed to broaden participation in engineering through the development of Printed Circuit Board Assemblies (PCBA) in the form of culturally inspired badges. The program integrates theoretical instruction with hands-on activities, including electronics fundamentals, schematic design, and Surface Mount Technology (SMT) assembly. To strengthen student engagement, the PCB badges were shaped after Sulawesi Island and embedded with LEDs representing its major cities, linking technical learning with local cultural identity. This contextual approach not only enhanced technical competence. It also fostered collaboration, problem solving, and creativity skills that are essential for success in the 21st century. Survey results indicate that participants found the workshop highly engaging, informative, and motivating, with strong appreciation for both the technical content and cultural relevance. The findings suggest that integrating engineering education with local symbolism can effectively bridge theory and practice, foster a deeper connection to STEM disciplines, and provide an inclusive pathway into engineering for secondary-level students.

title: Comparative Evaluation of State-of-the-art Generative AI Models Using QCA: Performance, Adaptability, and Precision
authors: Edrick Teddyatmaja, Sonya Rapinta Manalu, Sasmoko
abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) has advanced rapidly, producing models capable of generating text, images, audio, and video across diverse domains. Despite sharing foundations in deep learning and neural networks, these systems differ in architecture, optimization, and training methods. This study employs Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA) to systematically evaluate leading generative AI models—focusing on performance, adaptability, and precision—to identify causal configurations that explain their effectiveness. By integrating quantitative benchmarks with qualitative assessments, the study reveals trade-offs between speed, reasoning depth, and contextual sensitivity. Architectural and ethical dimensions, including bias, transparency, and computational efficiency, are also considered. Findings highlight that no single model is universally superior, and balanced capabilities across conditions drive broader utility. The results contribute to understanding how generative AI can be applied responsibly and effectively across academic, creative, and industrial contexts.

title: A Case Study on the Migration of a High-Level PI Controller to ASIC-Compatible HDL Representation
authors: Francisca Donoso, Nelson Salvador, Jorge Marin, Christian A. Rojas, Gonzalo Carvajal
publish: 2025 IFIP/IEEE 33rd International Conference on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI-SoC)
abstract: This article presents a structured methodology for migrating digital control algorithms developed in high-level languages into synthesizable HDL suitable for ASIC implementation. A proportional-integral controller for a three-level flying capacitor converter is used as a representative case study to demonstrate the design flow. The methodology encompasses initial modeling using floating-point arithmetic, conversion to fixed-point representation, generation of reference models for validation, translation to C/C++, and high-level synthesis for HDL generation. Preliminary results consider co-simulation to ensure behavioral consistency between the high-level description and the generated HDL. The HDL was successfully synthesized using the open-source LibreLane tool-chain targeting the SkyWater SKY130 PDK. Ongoing work includes FPGA-based prototyping and detailed characterization of the ASIC. The proposed approach establishes a verified and systematic pathway for transitioning high-level control logic to ASIC-ready hardware implementations, preserving the original algorithmic behavior across abstraction levels.

title: Evaluating Information System Governance in Primary Schools Using COBIT 5 Framework
authors: Carmen Claudia, Ibnu Hamdani, Michael Parhusip, Jarot S. Suroso
abstract: Primary schools are increasingly adopting information systems to support administrative tasks, academic activities, and communication with stakeholders. However, the implementation of these systems often encounters challenges such as inconsistent data management, lack of governance, limited IT resources, and potential security risks. To ensure that information systems deliver value and operate effectively, an audit is required to evaluate their governance and management processes. This study applies COBIT 5, a widely recognized framework for IT governance developed by ISACA, focusing on the Deliver, Service, and Support (DSS) domain, specifically DSS03 Manage Problems. Data were collected through literature review, document analysis, and interviews with school stakeholders, followed by capability level assessment. The results provide an overview of the current capability of the school’s information system processes and offer recommendations to enhance IT governance, ensuring that the system can better support educational objectives.

title: A Hybrid CNN-Machine Learning Framework for Deception Detection Using Facial Landmark Analysis
authors: Muhaimin Hasanudin, Abdi Wahab, Emil R. Kaburuan, Derisma, Indrianto, Suzy Yusna Dewi
abstract: The growing demand for non-invasive deception detection systems has spurred significant interest in AI-driven solutions. Traditional methods, such as polygraphs, are often intrusive and prone to false positives. This paper proposes a hybrid framework that integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with traditional machine learning (ML) classifiers : Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) to identify deception through facial landmark analysis. Utilizing MediaPipe's 468-point facial mesh, we extract geometric features correlated with deceptive behavior, such as mouth width and nose-mouth distance. Our model was evaluated using data on microfacial expressions, consisting of 17,634 training samples (8,375 lies and 9,259 truths) and 5,838 test samples (2,799 lies and 3,039 truths), totaling 23,472 samples. The CNN model achieved 99.96% accuracy, while the KNN and RF models achieved 91.79% and 90.92%, respectively, on the test set. However, the near-perfect CNN performance suggests potential overfitting, which we addressed through an 80/20 stratified train-test split combined with dropout and L2 regularization. The results demonstrate a critical trade-off : the CNN offers superior performance on this dataset, whereas the KNN and RF models provide strong accuracy with greater simplicity and interpretability. This study underscores the viability of facial landmarks as features for deception detection and presents a framework suitable for controlled environments while highlighting the need for validation on diverse, real-world datasets.

title: Industrial Process Root Cause Analysis Method Based on Graph Neural Networks and Knowledge Graphs
authors: Bing Yu, Hong Chen, Yang Yu, Zhiming Yang, Gang Xiang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: Root cause analysis in industrial processes is crucial for ensuring production safety and improving system reliability. Existing methods suffer from limitations in effectively combining domain knowledge with industrial data, making it challenging to provide accurate online root cause diagnosis. This paper proposes a Graph Neural Network and Knowledge Graph-based Root Cause Analysis framework (GNN-RCA) that leverages graph neural networks to learn fault features and incorporates knowledge graphs for fault propagation reasoning. Specifically, we construct a prior industrial knowledge graph to represent physical relationships among equipment, design a temporal graph autoencoder to extract fault contribution scores, and implement root cause reasoning through a fault propagation algorithm. Experimental results on the Tennessee Eastman Process dataset demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms traditional approaches in root cause identification accuracy while maintaining good interpretability and real-time performance.

title: Adaptive Neural Controller Based on PSO Algorithm Applied To Transesterification Reactor
authors: Farhat Yassin, Atig Asma, Ali Zribi, Ben Abdennour Ridha
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: In this study to surmount disadvantages of the classical neural networks such as, slow convergence speed and low precision, an innovative indirect adaptive control approach utilizing a neural network and an optimal learning rate is developed. In this control scheme, an optimal adaptive rate for the Neural Controller (NC) is identified using PSO algorithm. The proposed methodology was evaluated using numerical simulations and experimentally tested on a chemical reactor, demonstrating its effectiveness.

title: Beyond Fair Predictions: Auditing and Mitigating Disparity in Post-Hoc Model Explanations
authors: Ivanie Stella Umuhoza, Rajiv Garg
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal, Control and Communication (SCC)
abstract: Machine learning fairness research primarily focuses on equitable predictions while overlooking explanation fairness, a critical component of trustworthy AI. In high-stakes domains like finance and hiring, biased explanations can perpetuate stereotypes and erode trust even with fair predictions. We make three contributions: First, we formalize explanation fairness and propose the Group Explanation Disparity (GED) metric based on Jensen-Shannon divergence to quantify bias in explanation distributions across demographic groups. Second, we introduce FARET, a model-agnostic post-hoc algorithm that mitigates explanation bias while preserving fidelity through multi-objective optimization. Third, we provide empirical validation on credit scoring and income prediction datasets, revealing significant explanation disparities (GED = 0.045 for education level). FARET reduces explanation bias by 65-70% while maintaining >89% fidelity, with all improvements statistically significant (p < 0.001).

title: U-CMG: U-Shaped Cross-Domain Control Moment Gyroscope Bearing Fault Diagnosis Method
authors: Rongqiang Guan, Qiongying Lv
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology (CAIT)
abstract: To address the problems of insufficient labeled data and distribution differences in single-frame spacecraft control moment gyroscope (CMG) bearing fault diagnosis, a transfer learning method for domain-adaptive CMG bearing fault diagnosis based on the U-Net network structure is proposed. This method improves label reliability and model stability in the cross-domain transfer process through a multi-label adversarial pseudo-label generation strategy. In addition, a feature extractor based on the U-Net network is designed to deeply mine discriminative features of bearing vibration signals, enhancing the model’s fault recognition capability. During the entire training process, model parameters are updated through dynamic calibration of pseudo-labels, ensuring that highconfidence data continuously participate in training, thereby improving the model’s robustness and generalization ability. Experimental results show that this method achieves an average fault diagnosis accuracy of 92.32% on the spacecraft CMG bearing fault dataset, outperforming four other mainstream transfer learning methods, which verifies the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed approach.

title: Vehicle Lateral Control for Intelligent Driving Based on Dynamic Compensation of Stanley Algorithm
authors: Changliang Sun, Hongli Liu, Weichu Xiao, Xing Wen
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, and Artificial Intelligence (RAAI)
abstract: This paper focuses on the lateral control technology for intelligent driving vehicles. Typically, such control systems leverage lane detection or path planning to obtain the centerline of the vehicle's trajectory, while adjusting control strategies dynamically based on real-time vehicle states (e.g., speed, steering angle, yaw rate). Upon lane departure, the lateral control algorithm calculates the required steering angle and actuates the vehicle's steering system via the CAN communication networkensuring smooth correction back to the lane center and effectively enhancing driving safety. To address the limitation of traditional lateral control algorithms (e.g., insufficient precision under varying speeds and turning radii), this paper proposes a Stanley algorithm enhanced with dynamic compensation. Building upon the original Stanley method, this approach introduces compensation factors for the yaw angle and lateral deviation rate during vehicle motion, which optimizes steering angle calculations in dynamic driving scenarios. The effectiveness and robustness of the improved algorithm were validated through real-world tests on two types of passenger buses (6 m and 13.7 m). Comparative experiments with the original Stanley algorithm demonstrate that the proposed method enables more stable and precise control under diverse driving conditions, thereby providing reliable support for core applications in intelligent driving systems, such as lane-keeping assistance, lane centering, and trajectory tracking.

title: How Generative AI Enhances Student Happiness: The Mediating Role of Self-Efficacy in Higher Education
authors: Lidya Agustina, Elyzabet I Marpaung, Erna Erna, Christin Septina Basani, Ai Permanasari
abstract: The rapid development of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) has attracted growing attention to its applications across various fields, including education. However, most existing studies have focused on the perspectives of lecturers and institutions, while students’ views as primary users remain underexplored. This study addresses this gap by examining how GenAI use enhances students’ happiness and whether self-efficacy mediates this relationship. Using a quantitative approach, we surveyed 273 students from six major universities in Indonesia and analyzed the data using structural equation modeling. The results reveal that GenAI use significantly increases self-efficacy (β = 0.465, p < 0.01), which subsequently improves students’ happiness. Moreover, GenAI use directly and significantly enhances students’ happiness (β = 0.469, p < 0.01). These findings may assist educators in developing strategies that strengthen self-efficacy within GenAI-supported learning environments to optimize learning achievement and foster greater student happiness.

title: Adaptive Learning System for Personalizing Secondary Education using Content-Based Filtering and Matrix Factorization
authors: Deivi Estrada, Anghelo Díaz, Roberto Montero
publish: 2025 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
abstract: The current educational context demands personalized solutions that adapt to the individual needs of students, especially at critical levels such as secondary education. This paper proposes the use of adaptive learning supported by learning analytics techniques to personalize education and improve student performance. An approach combining Content-Based Filtering and Matrix Factorization is used to efficiently analyze interactions between students and educational content, providing an experience that is more tailored to the individual needs of each student and optimizing the teaching-learning process. The results show that combining these techniques significantly improves the accuracy of educational recommendations, optimizing academic performance in secondary school students.

title: Generative AI for Technical Writing: Using Google Gemini in Vocational Electrical Engineering Education
authors: Jarot S. Suroso, Andika Candra Jaya, Abdul Ghofir, Ru’yat
abstract: This study examines the integration of Google Gemini, a generative AI tool, in supporting vocational high school students' practicum report writing in electrical power installation at SMKN 1 South Tangerang City. The research aims to understand how students utilize Google Gemini, its impact on learning motivation and report quality, and the pedagogical implications for vocational education. Using a qualitative case study approach, data were collected through semi-structured interviews with 12 students and 3 teachers, classroom observations, and document analysis of practicum reports. Findings reveal that Google Gemini significantly assists students in structuring reports, refining technical language, and validating measurement results, leading to increased efficiency and independent learning. However, concerns emerged regarding potential over-reliance and diminished development of independent writing skills. The study concludes that successful AI integration requires a balanced pedagogical approach incorporating teacher guidance, scaffolded learning activities, and project-based assessment to ensure technology enhances rather than replaces critical thinking and writing competencies.

title: A Torque Synthesis Method Based on Cadence Synchronization Rate for Electric Bikes
authors: Ping-Jui Ho, Yi-Jen Lin, Chen-Pei Yi, Hsin-Tien Yeh, Po-Chien Hsu, Shih-Chin Yang
abstract: This paper proposes a torque synthesis method (TSM) for electric bikes (E-bikes) to improve motor torque distribution by modulating the motor command. Based on crank angle and pedaling torque waveforms, the proposed TSM modulates the motor command according to the instantaneous pedaling phase. Specifically, the motor torque is reduced near peak pedaling torque to avoid torque overlap, while it is increased in low-efficiency regions to compensate for insufficient torque generation. To enable the proposed TSM integrated with commercially available E-bikes, a cadence synchronization rate (CSR) is introduced as a characterization metric. Based on two 2×2 scenario matrices representing typical riding conditions in terms of cadence, gear ratio, and road slope, the CSR characteristics of commercially available E-bike strategies are experimentally identified. Under these CSR strategies, the proposed TSM is superimposed on these assist strategies for commercially available E-bikes. The effectiveness of TSM can be then verified. Finally, the proposed TSM is implemented on a laboratory-built E-bike to experimentally evaluate different riding conditions. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed TSM effectively reduces acceleration ripple and rider pedaling torque across different CSR strategies. These results indicate that the TSM can be practically integrated with existing commercial assist strategies to enhance overall E-bike performance and riding comfort.

title: On Learning the Arithmetic Mean in 4th-Year Secondary Education using Eye-Tracking: A Pilot Study_supp1-3673125.pdf
authors: Adrian Perez-Suay
abstract: his pilot study investigates the learning process of the arithmetic mean through frequency tables in students enrolled in the fourth year of compulsory secondary education (4° ESO) in Spain (ages 15–16).his pilot study investigates the learning process of the arithmetic mean through frequency tables in students enrolled in the fourth year of compulsory secondary education (4° ESO) in Spain (ages 15–16).T The investigation examines the effect of grouped data supported by verbal examples and contextualized problems. Although based on a small sample, preliminary findings suggest that students tend to rely more on numerical examples than on formal algebraic expressions, and that verbal contexts may decrease attention to graphical representations. Using eye-tracking technology enabled the real-time collection of eye-fixation data on specific Areas of Interest (AOIs), providing detailed insights into individual problem-solving behaviours. Error analysis revealed persistent misconceptions related to grouped data calculations and interpretations of the mean as an expected value, highlighting challenges in deep conceptual understanding. Additionally, integrating eye-tracking demonstrated potential to address classroom diversity by informing the development of instructional materials adapted to different learning profiles.

title: Service Placement in Small Cell Networks Using Distributed Best Arm Identification in Linear Bandits_supp1-3670034.pdf
authors: Mariam Yahya
abstract: As users in small cell networks increasingly rely on computation-intensive services, cloud-based access often results in high latency. Multi-access edge computing (MEC) mitigates this by bringing computational resources closer to end users, with small base stations (SBSs) serving as edge servers to enable low-latency service delivery. However, limited edge capacity makes it challenging to decide which services to deploy locally versus in the cloud, especially under unknown service demand and dynamic network conditions. To tackle this problem, we model service demand as a linear function of service attributes and formulate the service placement task as a linear bandit problem, where SBSs act as agents and services as arms. The goal is to identify the service that, when placed at the edge, offers the greatest reduction in total user delay compared to cloud deployment. We propose a distributed and adaptive multi-agent best-arm identification (BAI) algorithm under a fixed-confidence setting, where SBSs collaborate to accelerate learning. Simulations show that our algorithm identifies the optimal service with the desired confidence and achieves near-optimal speedup, as the number of learning rounds decreases proportionally with the number of SBSs. We also provide theoretical analysis of the algorithm's sample complexity and communication overhead.

title: Error-Resilient Semantic Communication for Speech Transmission over Packet-Loss Networks
authors: Zhuohang Han, Jincheng Dai, Shengshi Yao, Junyi Wang, Yanlong Li, Kai Niu, Wenjun Xu, Ping Zhang
abstract: Real-time speech communication over wireless networks remains challenging, as conventional channel protection mechanisms cannot effectively counter packet loss under stringent bandwidth and latency constraints. Semantic communication has emerged as a promising paradigm for enhancing the robustness of speech transmission by means of joint source channel coding (JSCC). However, its cross-layer design hinders practical deployment due to the incompatibility with existing digital communication systems. To address this, we perform JSCC over the network layer to combat packet loss and support real deployment. Inspired by the generative latent modeling, we propose Glaris, a generative latent-prior-based resilient speech semantic communication framework that performs resilient transform coding in the generative latent space. Generative latent priors enable high-quality packet loss concealment (PLC) at the receiver side, well-balancing semantic consistency and reconstruction fidelity. Additionally, an integrated error resilience mechanism is designed to mitigate the error propagation and improve the effectiveness of PLC. Compared with traditional packet-level forward error correction (FEC) strategies, our new method achieves enhanced robustness over dynamic wireless networks while reducing redundancy overhead significantly. Experimental results on the LibriSpeech dataset demonstrate that Glaris consistently outperforms existing error-resilient codecs, achieving JSCC-level robustness while maintaining seamless compatibility with existing systems, and it also strikes a favorable balance between transmission efficiency and error resilience.

title: Perception-Failure-Induced Test Scenario Searching via Online Causal Reinforcement Learning
authors: Chi Zhang, Tingting Long, Zhendong Wang, Linhai Xu, Mingwen Bi, Xingyu Chen, Yuehu Liu, Qing Ye, Li Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Despite compliance with safety standards such as ISO 26262, the perception systems of autonomous vehicles still face numerous challenges during real-world operation. In this work, we propose a framework to identify safety-critical scenarios specifically induced by perception failures, aiming to support more targeted and effective scenario-based testing. Importantly, the key challenge lies in disentangling whether perception failures directly lead to critical outcomes. To address this, we propose an Online Causal Reinforcement Searching (OCRS) framework that simultaneously performs scenario search and causal reasoning. Focusing on visual unawareness and perception degradation, OCRS employs an LSTM-RNN controller to identify accident-prone scenarios linked to perception failures, which are then verified in a counterfactual world to determine causal responsibility. To improve efficiency, an online scenario classifier with passive-aggressive updates is introduced to dynamically filter out non-critical cases. The experimental results demonstrate both the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed approach, achieving a 33% reduction in execution time for searching perception-failure-induced corner cases. Furthermore, we evaluate the method under adverse weather conditions such as snow and fog, confirming that OCRS remains effective in identifying various failure modes.

title: Efficient UAV Swarm-Based Multi-Task Federated Learning with Dynamic Task Knowledge Sharing
authors: Yubo Yang, Tao Yang, Xiaofeng Wu, Ziyu Guo, Bo Hu
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) swarms are extensively used in emergency communications, area monitoring, and disaster relief. Their operations are coordinated by control centers, making them well-suited for federated learning (FL) frameworks. However, current UAV FL methods ignore the rich information contained in UAV images and the potential of using a single dataset to accomplish multiple tasks. For instance, in disaster relief scenarios, images acquired by UAVs can support tasks like crowd detection, road passability analysis, and disaster impact assessment. These tasks exhibit time-varying demands and may have potential correlations. To meet these requirements, this paper introduces two core mechanisms: a dynamic task attention mechanism to evaluate task importance for efficient resource allocation, and a task affinity (TA) metric to capture inter-task correlations for knowledge sharing. Building on these innovations, we propose FedDya, a novel UAV swarm-based one-dataset multi-task FL framework, where ground emergency vehicles (EVs) collaborate with UAVs to accomplish multiple tasks leveraging a single dataset. To optimize resource allocation, we formulate a two-layer optimization problem to jointly optimize UAV transmission power, computation frequency, bandwidth allocation, and UAV-EV associations. For the inner problem, we derive closed-form solutions for transmission power, computation frequency, and bandwidth allocation and apply the block coordinate descent method for optimization. For the outer problem, a novel two-stage algorithm is designed to determine optimal UAV-EV associations. Furthermore, theoretical analysis reveals a trade-off between UAV energy consumption violation and multi-task performance, characterized by an O( √ V , 1/V ) relationship. Extensive simulation results further validate the effectiveness of the proposed scheme.

title: FPGA-based Sensorless PMSM Drive with Sliding Mode Observer for Velocity Tracking
authors: Nguyen Khanh Quang, Quang Phuc Ha
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of sensorless permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drives on field programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) using a sliding mode observer (SMO) for applications requiring highly accurate velocity tracking. To eliminate mechanical position sensors while reducing system cost and vulnerability to harsh environments, a chattering-reduced SMO employing a hyperbolic tangent switching function is proposed. Particular attention is given to practical FPGA feasibility, avoiding excessive algorithmic complexity while ensuring precise tracking performance during speed variations. The proposed estimation scheme is validated through both simulation and real-time experimental studies on an FPGA platform, exploiting its inherent parallel processing capability for deterministic real-time execution. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves high accuracy in rotor speed and position estimation while maintaining stability and robustness against parameter variations and external disturbances. This work represents a significant advancement toward efficient real-time control of PMSM drives, offering a low-cost and reliable solution for industrial applications with diverse velocity profiles. To quantitatively validate the proposed design, extensive evaluations were conducted under identical conditions. Compared with a standard SMO implemented on the same FPGA platform and tested under the same scenarios, the proposed method reduces the speed estimation error by 49.5% in mean absolute error (MAE) and by 47.2% in root mean square error (RMSE). In addition, the rotor flux-angle error is reduced by 4°– 5°, current chattering is suppressed from ±0.15 A to below ±0.05 A, and the back-EMF total harmonic distortion (THD) is reduced by 1.82%. These improvements are achieved with only a modest increase in FPGA resource utilization, from 12.57% to 15.74%, verifying effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: PrivGITD: A High Accuracy and Privacy-preserving Graph Convolutional Network for Insider Threat Detection
authors: Ximing Li, Huizheng Geng, Linghui Li, Fang Liu, Chunmei Li
abstract: Ensuring data security is a fundamental challenge in Internet of Things (IoT) deployments, where massive numbers of interconnected devices continuously transmit, process, and store sensitive information. Among the various security threats, insider threats have emerged as the most critical risk, as malicious insiders with authorized access can directly compromise data integrity and confidentiality. Existing detection approaches often focus solely on improving detection accuracy, while neglecting the structural information embedded in user-device interactions and the imperative need for privacy-preserving mechanisms in sensitive environments. To address these challenges, we proposed PrivGITD, a privacy-preserving insider threat detection framework based on local differential privacy and graph modularization. Specifically, PrivGITD introduces a PageRank-based Structural Reconstruction module that quantifies node importance and reconstructs topological embeddings to capture both local and global interaction patterns. Additionally, a Local Differential Privacy (LDP)-based Feature Encoding module perturbs and calibrates user features, ensuring privacy protection while maintaining statistical utility. Furthermore, a Label Perturbation and Denoising Learning strategy refines randomized labels via multi-hop aggregation and soft supervision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PrivGITD outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving higher detection accuracy while effectively preserving privacy.

title: Noise-resistant Land Cover Classification of SAR Images Based on Automatic Sample Extraction
authors: Ning Li, Jiaming Li, Yue Zhang, Ruirui Huang, Lin Wu, Yabo Huang, Jianhui Zhao
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) can provide stable multi-temporal data for land cover classification (LCC). Nonetheless, existing SAR sample extraction methods are costly, time-consuming, and lack effective temporal-spatial contextual feature representations, leading to suboptimal performance of deep learning algorithms in SAR LCC. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Speckle-robust LCC method for SAR images based on automatic sample extraction. Spatial consistency analysis and morphological processing are performed using multi-temporal historical land cover data, followed by the design of the confidence index (CI) in combination with SAR temporal characteristics for automatic sample extraction. Subsequently, a classification model combining weakly supervised learning and feature fusion is developed to enhance the model's noise resistance and temporal feature extraction capability. Experimental results in Kaifeng City and Yueyang City demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, achieving an Overall Accuracy (OA) exceeding 90% and providing an efficient, automated solution for SAR image LCC.

title: Distributed Predefined-Time Leader-Follower Formation Control for Heterogeneous Wheeled Mobile Robots
authors: Shiyu Yin, Chen Chen, Zhengrong Xiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper investigates the predefined-time (PdT) formation control problem for heterogeneous wheeled mobile robots (WMRs) with complex nonlinear terms. To address the challenges arising from system heterogeneity and distributed coordination, a reference-tracking control framework is proposed. The first layer is a reference signal generation layer, where PdT formation trajectories are generated by signal generators, with local observers embedded in each agent to estimate the leader’s state when direct access is unavailable. The second layer is an agents tracking layer, where each WMR follows its assigned reference trajectories. By coordinating these two layers, the proposed framework enables distributed formation control within a predefined time. To achieve PdT tracking, a sliding mode control strategy enhanced with fuzzy logic control is developed in the tracking layer. Lyapunov-based analysis is conducted for both layers to ensure PdT stability and provide explicit convergence-time guarantees. Simulation results are presented to validate the theoretical analysis and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Rapid Dispersion Compensation and Dispersion Measurement in Optical Coherence Tomography
authors: Duohao Zhao, Haozhe Zhong, Liangqi Cao, Sainan Wang, Jianfeng Huang, Qin Li, Xiao Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: Dispersion mismatch in optical coherence tomography (OCT) systems significantly degrades image quality and the resolution for biological microstructures. Traditional dispersion compensation strategies predominantly rely on exhaustive search (ES) algorithms, i.e. brute-force method, which suffer from low convergence efficiency and high time consumption, necessitating the development of efficient dispersion compensation strategies. Furthermore, the lack of efficient dispersion measurement techniques for biological samples limits clinical applications of OCT systems. This study proposes a rapid dispersion compensation and measurement strategy for OCT systems, systematically optimizing both compensation efficiency and measurement accuracy. For dispersion compensation, a dual stage collaborative optimization framework integrating coarse ES with the Nelder-Mead (NM) simplex algorithm is developed. This approach combines coarse global positioning with efficient local search, significantly accelerating the retrieval of dispersion compensation coefficients without accuracy degradation. Experimental results indicate that the proposed strategy reduced the convergence time by 98.2% and 68.2% compared with ES and golden-section search (GSS) methods, respectively. Regarding dispersion measurement, experiments on optical glasses dispersion extraction were conducted within the same optimization framework. The extracted values agree well with the ground truth. For 1mm thick BaF<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> and H-K9L optical glasses, errors of the extracted dispersion coefficients were 2.97 fs²/mm and 1.45 fs²/mm, respectively. This accuracy is comparable to traditional methods and significantly superior to that achieved by the NM simplex method alone and non-iterative strategies. Further in vivo imaging experiments validated the strategy's rapid applicability in complex biological tissues, with a typical operation time required for dispersion measurement as low as 0.9s in anterior chamber analysis and 0.5s for compensation in retinal imaging on a laptop computer using an unoptimized single-threaded program. In summary, the proposed strategy integrates high compensation efficiency with accurate extraction capabilities, offering a robust solution for clinical OCT imaging and disease screening based on tissue dispersion characterization.

title: Single-Phase Isolated Bidirectional Bipolar Output Buck-Boost AC–AC Converter With Safe-Commutation
authors: Hafiz Furqan Ahmed, Mohsin Jamil, Seyyed Mohammad Javad Mousavi
abstract: This article develops a new single-phase high-frequency transformer (HFT) isolated bidirectional buck-boost AC–AC converter. The proposed topology comprises bidirectional AC switches and passive components and is free of diodes. The proposed topology can provide a symmetric noninverting and inverting buck-boost output with a flexible voltage gain of ±ND/(1-D), providing two degrees of freedom for output voltage control by the HFT turns ratio N and dynamic duty ratio D. The proposed converter integrates a two-winding HFT, providing electrical isolation with better magnetic utilization in comparison with tapped/three-winding transformers, while eliminating the bulky line-frequency transformer for reduction in system size, weight, and cost. The proposed converter utilizes input and output second-order LC filters, providing continuous and low ripple input and output currents. The operation of the proposed converter is reversible, providing bidirectional power flow and support for nonunity power factor loads. A proposed safe-commutation switching strategy is developed for bidirectional AC switches, which provides continuous inductor current path during PWM dead-time, resisting switch voltage overshoots. Moreover, only two switches conduct at high frequency in the proposed modulation, reducing the switching loss. A detailed circuit operation and analysis is provided, along with component design guidelines and loss analysis. The comparisons highlight the advantages of the proposed converter, followed by experimental results on a laboratory-built prototype verifying the circuit operation and features.

title: Resource Allocation for Time-Sensitive Services in Centralized Optical and Wi-Fi Access Networks
authors: Jun Li, Yuxuan Chen, Zhiyuan Zhong, Yongcheng Li, Biswanath Mukherjee, Gangxiang Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: To satisfy the stringent requirements of emerging broadband services in home networks, a novel Centralized optical and Wi-Fi Access Network (C-WAN) has been proposed within the context of Fiber-to-The-Room (FTTR). In C-WAN, centralized management and control of multiple Wi-Fi access points (APs) deployed in each room are facilitated by relocating portions of Wi-Fi protocols from the APs to a centralized entity. This approach significantly enhances network performance, including throughput and roaming capabilities. However, C-WAN also imposes strict demands on the fronthaul networks, specifically requiring high bandwidth and ultra-low latency. In this context, orthogonal frequency division multiplexing passive optical network (OFDM-PON) emerges as a promising solution to support the C-WAN fronthaul network by allocating dedicated subcarriers to each AP. In C-WAN over OFDM-PON, Wi-Fi stations still contend for access to the wireless channel based on existing Wi-Fi protocols, which may result in prolonged wireless access delays. Consequently, the Quality of Service (QoS) requirements for time-sensitive (TS) services may not be met. Additionally, the variation in maximum Wi-Fi throughput due to the contention-based access mechanism presents a significant challenge for the efficient allocation of optical network resources under stringent delay constraints. To address these issues, we propose a priority-based access mechanism that assigns higher priority to TS services for accessing Wi-Fi channels and obtaining wireless resources. Building on this mechanism, we further develop a Wi-Fi throughput prediction model, which is used to optimize the allocation of optical network resources. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme can effectively reduce wireless access delay and jitter for TS services, meeting their performance requirements while also improving the utilization of optical network resources.

title: A 23.4–42.1-GHz Fractional-N Synthesizer With ADC-Based Direct Phase Digitization
authors: Hao Xu, Yizhuo Wang, Ziyang Jiao, Guoyu Li, Yiyun Mao, Shuai Liu, Yan Liu, Jimiao Sang, Qi Guo, Long Kong, Na Yan
abstract: A fractional-N digital phase-locked loop employs a novel analog-to-digital converter (ADC)-based phase detector (PD) to achieve direct phase digitization, thereby eliminating the need for a digital-to-time converter (DTC). The high PD gain reduces in-band phase noise, while its high linearity enables all-digital <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Sigma \Delta $</tex-math> </inline-formula> quantization noise cancellation. Implemented with a dual-mode LC oscillator and followed by ultra-wideband frequency triplers, the complete frequency synthesizer covers a frequency range from 23.4 to 42.1 GHz, achieves a worst case integrated jitter of 175 fs, and consumes 25–30.5-mW power in 40-nm CMOS technology.

title: Algal Blooms in Inland Waters: A Comprehensive Review of Monitoring Techniques, Dataset, Challenges, and Prospects
authors: Jinwei Bu, Shuhui Liu, Hui Yang, Youwen Zhao, Qiulan Wang, Xin He, Xiaoqing Zuo, Hailin Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Harmful algal blooms (HABs) release toxins during growth, posing a significant threat to water quality, ecosystems, and human health. Their formation is closely related to the eutrophication of inland water bodies. With the expansion of urbanization and an increase in wastewater discharge, the problem of eutrophication in inland water bodies, such as lakes, reservoirs, and rivers, has become increasingly prominent, leading to the frequent occurrence of HABs, which in turn pollute water bodies, threaten water supply safety, and cause potential water crises and huge economic losses. Therefore, real-time monitoring of HABs and early warnings in inland aquatic ecosystems are crucial for mitigating these threats. Remote sensing technology, an important tool for monitoring HABs, comprehensively enhances the monitoring capability of HABs through multi-source data fusion. However, the monitoring capabilities and advantages and disadvantages of each remote sensing technology have not been widely discussed or analyzed. This article comprehensively introduces the development process of harmful algal bloom monitoring in inland water bodies, lists the current monitoring technology methods, demonstrates the application progress of remote sensing technology in algal bloom monitoring, and comprehensively examines its current situation and challenges, including (1) the limitations of ground sensors in environmental adaptability; (2) multispectral sensors are easily affected by weather and cloud cover; (3) drone technology is limited by endurance and payload; (4) The Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R) technology faces multiple physical challenges in signal analysis; and (5) multi-source data fusion faces the challenges of spatial and temporal scale mismatch and heterogeneity. In the face of these challenges, we should continue to focus on multi-source data fusion to improve data quality, and develop new sensor algorithms to continuously optimize monitoring and early warning models, thereby promoting the development of inland algae monitoring technology and providing strong support for real-time continuous monitoring of water bodies and ecosystem protection.
